subtitle: 近江町市場 · ベジ・グルテンフリー

places.name: いきいき亭 近江町店 (Ikiikitei Omicho-ten)
places.gf_detail: This is a market seafood-bowl and sushi counter. Sushi rice is seasoned with rice vinegar and the bowls are served with soy sauce, which in Japan is virtually always brewed with wheat. There is no gluten-free menu or any documented allergen accommodation, and the cramped 10-seat counter shares all prep surfaces. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: The entire menu is built around raw fish delivered from Kanazawa Port (seafood bowls and nigiri sushi). There are no vegan or vegetarian options documented, and even the rice is served as part of seafood dishes. Not a viable choice for vegans or vegetarians.
places.hours_raw: 7:00-15:00 (closes earlier when the day's fish sells out). Closed Tuesdays (Wednesday instead if Monday is a public holiday); may also close when seas are rough.
places.neighborhood: Omicho Market (Musashi / Omicho), steps from Hokutetsu Kanaz
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A tiny counter inside Kanazawa's historic Omicho Market, いきいき亭 has become one of the market's most beloved seafood-bowl stops, opening at 7am so visitors and locals alike can start the day with fish delivered straight from Kanazawa Port. With only about ten counter seats, the cra

chef_bio.background: A tiny counter inside Kanazawa's historic Omicho Market, いきいき亭 has become one of the market's most beloved seafood-bowl stops, opening at 7am so visitors and locals alike can start the day with fish delivered straight from Kanazawa Port. With only about ten counter seats, the cramped space naturally turns strangers into companions over a meal.
chef_bio.philosophy: Fresh fish straight from Kanazawa Port, served simply and affordably in a friendly, no-fuss market setting that's easy for solo diners to enter.
chef_bio.specialty: Signature kaisendon (seafood rice bowls) loaded with fresh local catch, including the popular Ikiikitei-don and the Tokusen Hokuriku-don (around 2,500 yen) and a smaller Mini Kanazawa-don (around 1,200 yen), plus per-plate nigiri sushi. In some bowls the fish and the sushi rice are served in separate stacked vessels.

anecdotes.text: Rather than queuing, diners write their desired items and a phone number on an order slip; staff call when a seat opens, so you can wander the market while you wait.
Regulars describe a slip-based ordering system where you note your menu choices and the kitchen fires them in turn, part of the lively market atmosphere.

gf_cross_contamination.text: A roughly 10-seat market counter doing sushi and raw-fish bowls, with shared prep surfaces and no documented allergen separation.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Seafood bowls and nigiri are served with standard Japanese soy sauce, which is brewed with wheat; sushi rice is also vinegar-seasoned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The whole menu is fish-based (Kanazawa Port seafood bowls and sushi), with no vegan or vegetarian dishes offered.

cultural_comfort.note: A welcoming, solo-friendly market counter where you write your order on a slip; a few words of Japanese help, but pointing at the popular bowls works fine for a foreign student.

places.name: かいてん寿し 大倉 (Kaiten Sushi Okura)
places.gf_detail: Traditional sushi counter relying on wheat-based soy sauce and seasoned sushi rice; no gluten-free accommodations are documented and cross-contact is unavoidable. Not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: A seafood-focused sushi restaurant built around fresh local fish, shellfish, shrimp and crab. No vegan menu or plant-based options are documented.
places.hours_raw: 10:30-18:00 (last entry 17:30); a midday pause may occur ~15:00-17:30. Closed Wednesdays. Some sources list weekend hours extending to 19:00.
places.neighborhood: Omicho Market entrance, Shimotsutsumi-cho (near Hokutetsu Ka
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A long-running conveyor-belt sushi shop tucked into Kanazawa's historic Omicho Market, the city's kitchen. Founded around 1969 (roughly 45-50 years before its June 2024 renewal), it has been a local favorite for decades and is often named among the best kaiten-zushi in the market

chef_bio.background: A long-running conveyor-belt sushi shop tucked into Kanazawa's historic Omicho Market, the city's kitchen. Founded around 1969 (roughly 45-50 years before its June 2024 renewal), it has been a local favorite for decades and is often named among the best kaiten-zushi in the market and the city.
chef_bio.philosophy: The master's stated policy is to personally buy and hand-pick the best ingredients every morning at the market, offering only fish he has judged with his own eye - so the flavors can be found nowhere else.
chef_bio.specialty: Local Hokuriku seafood served over a U-shaped counter: seasonal catches such as winter kanpachi, snow crab and cod, spring firefly squid and white shrimp, plus prized gasu-ebi (a sweeter local shrimp), sweet shrimp, and traditional Kanazawa kombu-jime (kelp-cured fish). A tuna nakaochi at around 380 yen is a much-praised bite.

anecdotes.text: Every morning the owner-chef himself goes into Omicho Market to source the day's fish, picking each item by expert eye rather than ordering wholesale.

Though it runs a conveyor belt, regulars order directly from the chef and watch their sushi made fresh, making it feel more like a traditional sushi bar; one local reviewer ranks it among Kanazawa's top three kaiten-zushi.
The shop reopened in June 2024 after a renewal that refreshed its U-shaped counter while keeping its old-school paper-slip ordering.

gf_cross_contamination.text: A traditional sushi counter where wheat-based soy sauce and seasoned sushi rice are used throughout; cross-contact with gluten is unavoidable and no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese soy sauce (containing wheat) is the default condiment at the sushi counter.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All preparation centers on raw fish and shellfish at a shared sushi counter; any non-seafood item would share surfaces and tools with seafood.

cultural_comfort.note: A friendly, casual counter sushi spot inside Omicho Market where ordering is done by paper slip; basic Japanese helps but pointing and a friendly konnichiwa will get you a warm welcome.

places.name: おでん 高砂 (Oden Takasago)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The defining dashi is built on dark soy sauce (wheat-containing) and bonito, classic ingredients include kuruma-fu (wheat gluten), and a ginger-miso topping is used. No gluten-free accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: A seafood-forward oden and izakaya house. The broth is a continuously replenished katsuo (bonito) and dark-soy dashi simmered with meat and fish, and signature items include beef tendon, crab, whelk and dote-yaki. No documented vegan menu, and even vegetable items share the animal-based pot.
places.hours_raw: 16:00-21:00 (may vary with how busy it is)
places.neighborhood: Katamachi (Nomachi-Katamachi area, near the Saigawa river)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Oden Takasago has been simmering its pot in Kanazawa's Katamachi district since 1936 (Showa 11), making it one of the city's long-standing old-guard oden houses. It has stayed in the same neighborhood, and its ivy-draped exterior, red lanterns and noren curtains give it an unmist

chef_bio.background: Oden Takasago has been simmering its pot in Kanazawa's Katamachi district since 1936 (Showa 11), making it one of the city's long-standing old-guard oden houses. It has stayed in the same neighborhood, and its ivy-draped exterior, red lanterns and noren curtains give it an unmistakably nostalgic Showa atmosphere.
chef_bio.philosophy: Keep the founding broth alive by continuously replenishing it (tsugitashi), preserving a consistent, gently rich flavor across the decades.
chef_bio.specialty: Kanazawa oden in an unusually Kanto-style, darker and richer dashi: a continuously replenished broth of dark soy sauce and bonito topped up since the shop's founding. The slow-braised, melt-in-the-mouth beef tendon (gyusuji) served with a secret miso-dare is its signature and most popular item. Other draws include seasonal kani-men (snow-crab oden, roughly November to December), whelk (baigai), kuruma-fu, akamaki and Kaga vegetables, plus less-common dishes like dote-yaki and curry oden. A ginger white-miso is brushed over items such as daikon and tendon.

anecdotes.text: The broth has been continuously topped up (tsugitashi) since the shop opened in 1936, giving it a deep, slightly sweet character locals have loved for generations.

The beef tendon is braised so long it nearly melts in the mouth and, with the house's secret miso-dare, is described as the shop's number-one item; a ginger white-miso is also applied to daikon and tendon.
The proprietor can look stern at first but proves warm and gracious, in a space full of Showa nostalgia with an ivy-covered facade and red lanterns.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All ingredients share one communal oden pot built on a wheat-soy and bonito broth, so cross-contact is unavoidable for gluten-free or vegan diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature dashi is made with dark soy sauce (koikuchi), which contains wheat, simmered with bonito.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The broth is simmered with meat (beef tendon) and seafood; vegetable items such as daikon and Kaga vegetables are cooked in the same animal-based pot.

cultural_comfort.note: A cash-only, Showa-era counter oden shop where little or no English is likely, so some basic Japanese and a friendly, open attitude will help a foreign student feel welcome.

places.name: 東山 みずほ (Higashiyama Mizuho)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional Japanese / Kaga-cuisine eatery built around dashi, soy sauce, and pickled/cured seafood (ikura shoyu-zuke, konka mackerel, koji-zuke). Wheat-based soy sauce and dashi are central to the menu and there is no documented gluten-free handling, so it is not celiac-safe. A traveler avoiding gluten would need to ask in detail and should not assume safety.
places.vegan_detail: The set meals are centered on fish, salmon roe, nodoguro, and other seafood, with dashi-based soups. No vegan or vegetarian set is documented. This is not a suitable destination for vegans.
places.hours_raw: Mon & Fri 11:00-14:00; Tue-Thu 11:00-20:00; Sat/Sun/holidays 11:00-15:00. (Tabelog lists Tue-Thu closing as 21:00; official site lists 20:00 — hours_week uses the official 20:00.)
places.neighborhood: Higashiyama / Higashi Chaya District (across from Sugawara Shrine)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small home-style Japanese eatery tucked into the Higashi Chaya District across from Sugawara Shrine, run by owner Takano Sachi, who carries on the hospitality her grandmother modeled.

chef_bio.origin: Higashiyama, Kanazawa — in the heart of the historic Higashi Chaya teahouse district.
chef_bio.background: A small home-style Japanese eatery tucked into the Higashi Chaya District across from Sugawara Shrine, run by owner Takano Sachi, who carries on the hospitality her grandmother modeled.
chef_bio.philosophy: The owner inherited her grandmother's belief that real hospitality is in genuine care rather than show — captured in the grandmother's words 'なんもないけど、ご飯食べて行きまっし！' ("Nothing fancy, but please come in and eat") and the idea that what truly matters is invisible.
chef_bio.specialty: Generous 'one soup, many sides' set meals built around local Ishikawa ingredients — a soup with six or seven small dishes, plus a nodoguro (rosy seabass) set, served with carefully chosen Noto-area rice (Koshihikari, Hitomebore, Hyakumangoku and other varieties from Suzu) and local sake. Signature small plates include ikura shoyu-zuke (soy-marinated salmon roe), dried nodoguro, firefly squid pickles, konka mackerel and squid koji-zuke.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's spirit traces back to the owner's grandmother, who would invite people in with 'なんもないけど、ご飯食べて行きまっし！' — a humble Kanazawa-dialect welcome that became the shop's guiding ethos.
Rice is taken seriously here: diners can choose among several Ishikawa varieties grown in Suzu on the Noto Peninsula, including Koshihikari, Hitomebore and Hyakumangoku.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu relies on soy sauce, dashi, and cured/pickled seafood; no gluten-free accommodation is documented, so cross-contamination should be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soy-marinated dishes such as ikura shoyu-zuke are signature items, indicating standard (wheat-containing) soy sauce is used.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Set meals and small plates are seafood- and dashi-based; no vegan preparation is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a small family-run local spot with no documented English support, so a foreign student should be ready to order in Japanese or point at the set menu.

places.name: ファーメント カフェ (fArment Cafe)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented anywhere. The vegan offerings center on wheat-based noodle dishes (green curry noodles, tantanmen/担々麺) and the cafe serves cakes, tiramisu and other wheat-flour sweets, so the kitchen handles wheat heavily. There is no mention of dedicated gluten-free preparation, a dedicated fryer, or celiac accommodation. Not safe for celiacs; gluten-free travelers should assume cross-contamination and ask directly.
places.vegan_detail: This is a vegan-friendly omnivore cafe, not a fully vegan venue. It offers a genuine and well-regarded vegan line — spicy keema curry (vegan, topped with fried onion), green curry noodles, taco rice, and a vegan tantanmen (担々麺) — explicitly described as satisfying despite containing no meat. However, much of the menu is not vegan: the signature "ultimate cheesecake" is layered baked-and-cream cheesecake topped with grated Parmesan, and there are dairy lattes, tiramisu and other non-vegan sweets. Vegans can eat well from the savory curry/noodle items but should confirm each dish.
places.hours_raw: 12:00-22:00, closed Tue
places.neighborhood: 乙丸町 (Otomaru-machi), eastern Kanazawa near Higashi-Kanazawa
places.notes: fArment Cafe opened in December 2020 in Otomaru-machi, a quiet pocket of eastern Kanazawa near Higashi-Kanazawa Station. Owner Suzuki began not with a storefront but with a mobile coffee cart called 'madera campana,' and the fixed cafe grew out of that love of coffee. The space i

chef_bio.background: fArment Cafe opened in December 2020 in Otomaru-machi, a quiet pocket of eastern Kanazawa near Higashi-Kanazawa Station. Owner Suzuki began not with a storefront but with a mobile coffee cart called 'madera campana,' and the fixed cafe grew out of that love of coffee. The space is a renovated roadside garage/warehouse, reimagined with high ceilings, moody lighting, mismatched sofas and curated art into what the owner wanted to be 'a cafe unlike anywhere else in Kanazawa' — a sophisticated hideaway that feels like the set of a foreign film.
chef_bio.philosophy: Coffee-first craft (roasting and hand-drip done in-house) paired with an unhurried, welcoming atmosphere; the cafe is also dog-friendly, drawing friends catching up, remote workers and casual meetings alike.
chef_bio.specialty: Self-roasted specialty coffee is the heart of the shop: beans from various single origins are roasted in-house on a large roaster in the corner and hand-dripped to order, alongside playful drinks like tiramisu and prune lattes. The signature dessert is an 'ultimate cheesecake' that layers baked and cream cheesecake and finishes with a cheese-cream top and grated Parmesan. The food side is known for its vegan-friendly cooking — a spicy vegan keema curry crowned with crisp fried onions, green curry noodles, taco rice and a vegan tantanmen — plus seasonal treats like a Korean-style 'pinsu' shaved-ice dessert.

anecdotes.text: Owner Suzuki started out running a mobile coffee cart named 'madera campana' before opening the brick-and-mortar fArment Cafe, which still roasts its own beans in-house.
The cafe is set inside a renovated roadside garage/warehouse with American-industrial styling and an assortment of distinct sofas, and it welcomes dogs indoors.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu is wheat-heavy (noodle-based vegan curries, tantanmen, cakes and tiramisu), so a shared kitchen handling flour and noodles is expected. No gluten-free precautions are documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: This is a mixed vegan / non-vegan kitchen. The signature cheesecake is topped with Parmesan and dairy lattes are served, so vegan dishes are prepared in a kitchen that also handles cheese and dairy; no separate vegan prep is documented.

positives.text: Offers a genuine, well-liked vegan menu (spicy vegan keema curry, green curry noodles, taco rice, vegan tantanmen) described as satisfying despite containing no meat.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed, stylish hideaway cafe where a foreign student can comfortably linger over coffee and a vegan curry; staff likely operate mainly in Japanese, so a few basic phrases help, but the casual menu and counter make it easy.

places.name: 近江町食堂 (Omicho Shokudo)
places.gf_detail: This is a seafood izakaya whose menu centers on sashimi and seafood rice bowls (served with wheat-based soy sauce), tempura, and breaded tonkatsu — all of which contain or are dipped in gluten. Japanese dashi and seasonings here typically contain wheat-derived soy sauce. No gluten-free menu, dedicated preparation, or allergen handling is documented. Not suitable for celiacs or anyone needing strict gluten avoidance.
places.vegan_detail: A seafood-focused restaurant built around fresh fish bought daily at Omicho Market — signature dishes are seafood rice bowls, sashimi, and nodoguro. Stocks and broths are fish/dashi based and there is no documented vegan or vegetarian menu. Not a viable choice for vegans or vegetarians.
places.neighborhood: Omicho Market (Aokusamachi)
places.notes: Omicho Shokudo has operated inside Kanazawa's historic Omicho Market since its founding in 1930 (Showa 5), making it a roughly 95-year-old market institution. It sits right where locals and visitors come to eat the morning's catch.

chef_bio.background: Omicho Shokudo has operated inside Kanazawa's historic Omicho Market since its founding in 1930 (Showa 5), making it a roughly 95-year-old market institution. It sits right where locals and visitors come to eat the morning's catch.
chef_bio.philosophy: A founding principle that has never changed: the proprietor personally inspects and buys the fish, shellfish, and vegetables every morning, serving 'delicious food in generous portions' from ingredients sourced daily within the market.
chef_bio.specialty: Seafood rice bowls (kaisendon) are the long-standing No.1 dish, served since the founding; the kitchen is also known for nodoguro (blackthroat seabream), sashimi platters and boat-style assortments, tempura, and freshly fried tonkatsu, alongside local sake and craft beer.

anecdotes.text: The signature kaisendon (seafood rice bowl) has been the restaurant's most popular dish ever since it opened in 1930.
The owner hand-picks each morning's fish and produce in Omicho Market himself, a practice kept up since the founding.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu is built on sashimi, tempura, and tonkatsu; tempura and tonkatsu are wheat-battered/breaded and fried, with high cross-contamination risk for shared fryers.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Sashimi and seafood bowls are served with standard Japanese soy sauce, which is wheat-based; no wheat-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Kitchen is centered on fish and seafood prepared daily; broths and dishes are dashi/fish based with no documented vegan preparation.

cultural_comfort.note: A busy, long-running market eatery where a few words of Japanese and pointing at the picture menu will get you a great seafood bowl; staff are used to tourists but English may be limited.

places.name: 金沢おでん いっぷくや (Kanazawa Oden Ippukuya)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. This is a Kanazawa oden shop whose dashi is soy-sauce/salt based and whose menu is built around wheat-containing items — the signature 車麩 (kuruma-fu) is literally wheat gluten, and fish cakes, atsuage and ganmodoki also typically contain wheat. All items simmer in a shared communal dashi pot, so cross-contamination is unavoidable. No dedicated gluten-free preparation.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The communal oden broth is a kombu-and-salt/soy dashi that almost certainly includes fish-derived dashi typical of Kanazawa oden, and the pot also simmers seafood (ばい貝 bay shellfish, fish cakes, のど黒/えび seafood items) and beef tendon. Plant items such as tofu, atsuage, ganmodoki, daikon and 車麩 exist but are cooked in the shared animal-based broth, so they are not vegan.
places.neighborhood: Omicho Market, Ippuku Yokocho alley
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Ippukuya is a Kanazawa-oden stall set inside Ippuku Yokocho, a small food-court alley in the heart of Omicho Market, Kanazawa's centuries-old central market. The yokocho gathers a handful of stalls — Ippukuya for oden, a seafood grill stand, and a local-sake counter — under a sha

chef_bio.background: Ippukuya is a Kanazawa-oden stall set inside Ippuku Yokocho, a small food-court alley in the heart of Omicho Market, Kanazawa's centuries-old central market. The yokocho gathers a handful of stalls — Ippukuya for oden, a seafood grill stand, and a local-sake counter — under a shared eat-in space where you take a seat first and then order at each counter.
chef_bio.philosophy: Built around the market itself — tofu items (yakidofu, atsuage, ganmodoki) come from the neighboring Tofuya Shibata and fish cakes from market kamaboko makers, so the oden showcases the produce of the surrounding stalls.
chef_bio.specialty: Kanazawa-style oden simmered in a gentle kombu-and-salt dashi. Signature and seasonal items include 車麩 (kuruma-fu, broth-soaked wheat gluten), ばい貝 (bay shellfish), and the local luxury 'men' pieces — のど黒面 (nodoguro), えび面 (shrimp) and the winter かに面 (crab) — alongside everyday oden like daikon, egg, atsuage, ganmodoki, octopus and beef tendon.

anecdotes.text: A 2026 visitor described the kuruma-fu as soaking up the broth into a juicy bite and the bay shellfish as large and crunchy with natural umami, and finished by mixing leftover local sake with the oden broth for a 'dashi-sake' nightcap.
The stall sources its tofu items from Tofuya Shibata within the market and offers premium seasonal pieces such as nodoguro-men and a winter crab-men.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All oden items are simmered together in one shared dashi pot, and the broth and many items (fish cakes, the signature wheat-gluten 車麩) contain wheat/gluten, so cross-contamination is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Kanazawa oden dashi is a salt/soy-sauce-seasoned kombu broth; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Plant items (tofu, atsuage, daikon, 車麩) are cooked in the same broth as seafood (bay shellfish, fish cakes) and beef tendon and in a likely fish-based dashi, so they are not free of animal contact.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual market food-court stall where you grab a seat first, then point at the oden items you want at the counter — very approachable for a foreign student with only a few words of Japanese, though menus and staff are mostly Japanese.

places.name: 金澤屋珈琲店 近江町市場店 (Kanazawaya Coffee Ten Omicho Ichiba-ten)
places.gf_detail: This is a coffee shop, not a gluten-free destination. Coffee and espresso drinks are naturally gluten-free, but the cafe also serves cakes and sweets and makes no gluten-free claims; there is no dedicated handling and a celiac traveler cannot rely on any safety measures here.
places.vegan_detail: Black coffee is plant-based, but milk-based lattes, the homemade cakes, and the signature gold-leaf soft serve are dairy. No plant-milk option is documented; a vegan would need to ask and would largely be limited to black coffee.
places.hours_raw: 08:30-17:00 (open daily)
places.neighborhood: Omicho / Omicho Market, Jukkenmachi entrance (near Hokutetsu
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 金澤屋珈琲店 is a long-established Kanazawa coffee specialist whose flagship sits right beside Kanazawa Castle's Kuromon gate, overlooking the Otemon moat from a traditional machiya townhouse. This branch carries that roaster into the heart of Omicho Market, the city's centuries-old 'k

chef_bio.background: 金澤屋珈琲店 is a long-established Kanazawa coffee specialist whose flagship sits right beside Kanazawa Castle's Kuromon gate, overlooking the Otemon moat from a traditional machiya townhouse. This branch carries that roaster into the heart of Omicho Market, the city's centuries-old 'kitchen,' at the Jukkenmachi entrance, serving freshly hand-dripped coffee to market shoppers and travelers.
chef_bio.philosophy: The company roasts its own beans and emphasizes carefully, individually hand-brewed coffee meant to reflect Kanazawa's culture and seasons; the flagship received the 35th Kanazawa Urban Beauty and Culture Award.
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-dripped self-roasted coffee, the house Omicho Blend, espresso drinks, and a Kanazawa-style gold-leaf soft serve.

anecdotes.text: The parent shop is celebrated for brewing each cup one at a time through its own net filter in a townhouse with moat views beside Kanazawa Castle, and was honored with the 35th Kanazawa Urban Beauty and Culture Award.
Inside Omicho Market this branch is known for freshly brewed coffee dripped in view of customers and a gold-leaf soft serve, a nod to Kanazawa's famous gold leaf.

positives.text: Black coffee and espresso are naturally gluten-free and plant-based, so a plain hand-dripped or iced coffee is a low-risk choice here.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed market coffee stand where you can point at the menu and order a coffee or gold-leaf soft serve with a smile and a few words of Japanese; English is not guaranteed but the casual takeout setting makes it easy for a foreign student.

places.name: 近江町市場寿し (Omicho Ichiba Zushi)
places.gf_detail: This is a busy conveyor-belt sushi house where the core experience is nigiri and seafood rice bowls served with wheat-based soy sauce. Sushi vinegar, soy sauce, and shared preparation surfaces make this unsuitable for celiac diners; there is no documented gluten-free protocol or dedicated equipment.
places.vegan_detail: The menu is built almost entirely around fresh local fish, shellfish, and seafood bowls. There is no documented vegan or vegetarian offering, and even rice/side items are prepared in a seafood-centric kitchen.
places.hours_raw: 8:30-20:00 daily (last order around 19:30), open year-round
places.neighborhood: Omicho / inside Omicho Market (near Hokutetsu Kanazawa Stati
places.cuisine: Conveyor-belt sushi and seafood rice bowls
places.notes: A lively, easy-to-enter conveyor-belt sushi house set right inside Kanazawa's Omicho Market, the city's centuries-old 'kitchen.' Its location means the fish travels only steps from the market stalls to the counter, and the room draws a cross-section of locals and visitors of ever

chef_bio.background: A lively, easy-to-enter conveyor-belt sushi house set right inside Kanazawa's Omicho Market, the city's centuries-old 'kitchen.' Its location means the fish travels only steps from the market stalls to the counter, and the room draws a cross-section of locals and visitors of every age, from grandparents to small children.
chef_bio.philosophy: An approachable, casual take on sushi: the shop describes itself as a spirited, welcoming place built for everyday enjoyment rather than formal fine dining.
chef_bio.specialty: Local Hokuriku seafood served as nigiri off the rotating belt and as generous kaisendon (seafood rice bowls) — including prized regional catches like nodoguro (blackthroat seaperch) and sweet shrimp (amaebi).

anecdotes.text: The shop sits inside Omicho Market near the produce-street entrance, prizing freshness from its market location.
The restaurant gained wider attention through television coverage in 2009 and features in regional travel guides.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Conveyor-belt sushi setting with shared belts, soy sauce dispensers, and prep surfaces makes cross-contact with wheat highly likely; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese soy sauce (wheat-containing) is the default condiment for nigiri and donburi here; no wheat-free tamari is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is seafood-centric with shared rice and surfaces; no vegan preparation is documented and cross-contact with fish is unavoidable.

cultural_comfort.note: A lively, welcoming conveyor-belt sushi spot inside the market where you can point at plates or photos, so a foreign student gets by easily with a friendly konnichiwa and a few gestures.

places.name: LOVE for ALL（ラブ フォー オール） (Love for All)
places.gf_detail: ⚠ Japanese-first review 2026-07-03 (high→options): Own Japanese coverage says only 「メニューの多くが小麦不使用」(MANY, not all); the GF bun is a paid ¥330 substitution (default bun = wheat); barley/wheat craft beer poured on premises. English 'entire menu is GF' reviews are exaggeration. Shared kitchen with default wheat. — Prior: The kitchen is built around gluten-free cooking and lists gluten-free among its core accommodations; the signature burger offers a gluten-free bun. Tabelog's listing states all menu items are gluten-free, but because the restaurant also handles a broad plant-based menu and no dedicated-fryer or cross-contamination protocol is documented, celiac travelers should confirm preparation directly when ordering.
places.vegan_detail: The restaurant is built on plant-based, animal-product-free cooking. Signature dishes include a Kaga lotus-root vegan burger with plant-based cheese, a vegan Hanton rice, oil-fried-tofu-base pizza with vegan cheese, and cucumber sashimi made from Kaga summer vegetables. It also accommodates soy-free, onion-free, and garlic-free requests.
places.hours_raw: Tabelog lists 10:00-20:30 daily; the official-affiliated guide article lists 12:00-21:00. Hours should be confirmed before visiting due to this discrepancy.
places.neighborhood: 香林坊 (Korinbo), central Kanazawa near the 21st Century Museum
places.notes: LOVE for ALL opened in March 2023 in Kanazawa's central Korinbo district, bringing a Los Angeles-born, health-conscious dining concept to one of Japan's great food cities. It is the sister restaurant of Shojin, a well-known Japanese restaurant in Los Angeles run by the owner's tw

chef_bio.background: LOVE for ALL opened in March 2023 in Kanazawa's central Korinbo district, bringing a Los Angeles-born, health-conscious dining concept to one of Japan's great food cities. It is the sister restaurant of Shojin, a well-known Japanese restaurant in Los Angeles run by the owner's twin brother, which specializes in authentic Japanese cooking for the health-conscious market. The owner saw that Kanazawa's abundance of fresh produce and rich culinary heritage was the perfect foundation for a restaurant blending organic, plant-based cuisine with local tradition.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen's motto centers on connection -- with others, with family and friends -- and on serving 'real food.' Everything is handmade from scratch with carefully chosen organic ingredients, avoiding chemical preservatives, artificial colors, chemical seasonings, and refined sugar, while accommodating gluten-free, vegan, vegetarian, soy-free, onion-free, and garlic-free needs so that, as the owner puts it, more people can dine with peace of mind.
chef_bio.specialty: Plant-based reinterpretations of Kanazawa's iconic comfort foods: a vegan burger built on a Kaga lotus-root patty with a gluten-free bun and plant-based cheese; a vegan Hanton rice (the city's signature omelet-rice dish); an oil-fried-tofu-base pizza with vegan cheese; and a cucumber 'sashimi' presented like fatty tuna using summer Kaga vegetables.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant is the sister establishment of Shojin in Los Angeles, run by the owner's twin brother, and was created to expand the joy of eating and become a place where more people can come with peace of mind.
It took over the central Korinbo space that formerly housed Oriental Brewing's Korinbo location.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The restaurant accommodates a wide range of dietary needs including gluten-free, vegan, vegetarian, soy-free, onion-free, and garlic-free, and all dishes are handmade from scratch with carefully selected ingredients.

positives.text: Tabelog's listing states all menu items are gluten-free.
The menu is plant-based and animal-product-free, with the signature lotus-root burger offering a gluten-free bun and vegan cheese.
Cooking avoids chemical preservatives, artificial colors, chemical seasonings, and refined sugar, with explicit soy-free, onion-free, and garlic-free options.

cultural_comfort.note: The restaurant grew out of an LA dining concept and explicitly welcomes international, health-conscious diners, so a foreign student should feel comfortable being served in English.

gf_review.why: Own Japanese coverage says only 「メニューの多くが小麦不使用」(MANY, not all); the GF bun is a paid ¥330 substitution (default bun = wheat); barley/wheat craft beer poured on premises. English 'entire menu is GF' reviews are exaggeration. Shared kitchen with default wheat.

places.name: SIU.COFFEE +VEG (SIU.COFFEE +VEG (Shiu Coffee and Veg))
places.gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. The signature falafel sandwich and hummus are served in/with pita bread, which contains wheat. There is a genuinely gluten-free option: the rice-flour (米粉) gateau chocolate sweets sourced from a Kaga/Kahoku-area maker (atelier 絲), made with rice rather than wheat flour. No dedicated gluten-free kitchen, fryer, or cross-contamination protocol is documented, so celiac travelers should confirm in person and stick to the rice-flour sweets and naturally GF items rather than the pita-based dishes.
places.vegan_detail: Fully plant-based cafe. The entire menu is vegan: falafel sandwich (chickpea croquettes with vegetables in pita), hummus, rice-flour gateau chocolate with sweet-potato-and-cashew cream, ginger sodas, and oat-milk-based coffee ("Oats Brew"). The owner describes living plant-based herself and built the cafe around sharing those benefits, so vegan diners can order anything on the menu.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–16:00; closed Mondays and Tuesdays
places.neighborhood: 幸町 (Sachimachi), central Kanazawa — within walking distance
places.notes: SIU.COFFEE +VEG opened on April 24, 2025 in central Kanazawa's Sachimachi district, inside a roughly 100-year-old machiya (traditional wooden townhouse) that previously housed a restaurant and was restored over about seven months. The renovation preserved the original wooden beam

chef_bio.background: SIU.COFFEE +VEG opened on April 24, 2025 in central Kanazawa's Sachimachi district, inside a roughly 100-year-old machiya (traditional wooden townhouse) that previously housed a restaurant and was restored over about seven months. The renovation preserved the original wooden beams and lofty vaulted ceilings and added skylights, with the interior softened by herbs and wild plants and a garden view from the seats.
chef_bio.philosophy: Owner Kanako Mizutaka built the cafe around a 'Love yourself' concept and a wish to provide 'the safest possible meals in Ishikawa,' wanting to share the physical and emotional benefits she has experienced through plant-based living and to offer a calm refuge where busy people can reconnect with themselves.
chef_bio.specialty: Vegan Middle Eastern fare: the signature falafel sandwich (chickpea croquettes with vegetables and hummus in pita), hummus, and a rice-flour gateau chocolate with sweet-potato-and-cashew cream sourced from atelier 絲 in the Kaga/Kahoku area. Drinks include house-made ginger syrup sodas and an oat-milk 'Oats Brew' coffee.

anecdotes.text: Owner Kanako Mizutaka said she wants to share the physical and emotional benefits she has experienced through plant-based living, running the space under a 'Love yourself' concept as a refuge for busy people.
The century-old townhouse underwent about seven months of restoration that kept its wooden beams and vaulted ceiling, with the cafe seating overlooking a garden.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat-based pita bread is central to the falafel sandwich and is served alongside hummus, so wheat is handled in the kitchen and the menu is not celiac-safe despite some rice-flour sweets being available.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The cafe is fully plant-based, so there is no animal-product cross-contact risk across the menu.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The owner states a mission to provide 'the safest possible meals in Ishikawa,' indicating attention to ingredient safety, though no specific allergen or cross-contamination protocol is documented.

positives.text: Entirely vegan menu, so all dishes are free of meat, fish, dairy and egg.
Offers genuinely gluten-free rice-flour (米粉) sweets — a gateau chocolate made with rice rather than wheat flour — for diners avoiding gluten.
Uses oat milk for coffee and house-made ginger syrup, supporting dairy-free ordering.

cultural_comfort.note: A welcoming, internationally-minded vegan cafe whose menu (falafel, hummus, oat-milk coffee) is familiar to foreign students; a few words of Japanese or pointing at the menu will be plenty, and the staff are used to health-conscious and overseas visitors.

places.name: 健康食工房 たかの (Kenkoshoku Kobo Takano)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The kitchen cooks with traditional Japanese seasonings including soy sauce (typically wheat-based) and miso, and the official site explicitly states wheat and soy sauce are among allergens used and that there is no gluten-free menu. The signature sprouted/activated brown rice is naturally GF, but dishes overall are not celiac-safe. Guests with severe allergies are asked to inquire in advance.
places.vegan_detail: Fully plant-based. The restaurant cooks entirely without animal products — no meat, fish, eggs, or dairy — and also avoids white sugar and chemical additives. Macrobiotic and shojin-style natural cuisine built around sprouted/activated brown rice (発芽活性玄米) and seasonal vegetables, served as a chef's-choice set menu.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:00), Monday-Saturday. May close earlier if the day's food sells out.
places.neighborhood: Mikuchi-shinmachi (toward Nomachi / Teramachi, near Nomachi Stn)
places.notes: A small natural-food restaurant in Kanazawa run single-handedly by its owner, open since 2000 (more than two decades). It grew out of a simple wish: to create a place where you can eat food that is genuinely delicious while caring for both your body and the earth.

chef_bio.origin: Kanazawa, Ishikawa — in the 三口新町 area toward Nomachi and the Teramachi temple district.
chef_bio.background: A small natural-food restaurant in Kanazawa run single-handedly by its owner, open since 2000 (more than two decades). It grew out of a simple wish: to create a place where you can eat food that is genuinely delicious while caring for both your body and the earth.
chef_bio.philosophy: Everything is cooked without white sugar, animal products (meat, fish, eggs, dairy), or chemical additives, using purified water, with the aim of serving the blessings of nature in as natural a state as possible. The owner sees food choices as directly connected to personal wellbeing and the health of the planet.
chef_bio.specialty: Chef's-choice macrobiotic set centered on sprouted activated brown rice and seasonal vegetables; additive-free bento and vegan osechi.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant has served its plant-based, additive-free cuisine continuously since 2000, run by a single owner, and frames its mission as letting guests 'eat delicious food while caring for your body and the earth.'

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat and soy sauce are listed among allergens used in the kitchen, and there is no gluten-free menu; the food is not celiac-safe.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The kitchen cooks with traditional seasonings including soy sauce, salt, and miso — soy sauce is typically wheat-based.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire kitchen is plant-based — no meat, fish, eggs, or dairy are used anywhere — so vegan cross-contact risk is minimal.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Guests with severe allergies are advised to inquire in advance before ordering.

positives.text: Completely plant-based cooking with no animal products, white sugar, or chemical additives, using purified water.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny owner-run natural-food spot where service is in Japanese only and there is no à la carte menu (chef's-choice set), so a foreign student is best off arriving prepared, perhaps with a translation app or a few key phrases, and asking about allergens up front.

places.name: 山さん寿司 本店 (Yamasan Sushi Honten)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional sushi and kaisen-don shop. Sushi rice is seasoned with vinegar and the bowls are served with wheat-based Japanese soy sauce; no gluten-free accommodation or separate preparation is documented. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: A seafood-focused sushi and raw-fish kaisen-don specialist. Essentially all signature dishes are fish-based and dashi/soy are used throughout; there is no documented vegan offering.
places.hours_raw: 9:00-16:00 (closes when ingredients run out). Open daily.
places.neighborhood: 近江町市場 (Omicho Market), 下近江町 / Musashigatsuji
places.notes: Yamasan Sushi is a long-established sushi house inside Kanazawa's lively Omicho Market, proud of more than 60 years of history. It sits in the heart of the market that has fed Kanazawa for centuries, and draws steady lines of visitors come for fresh local fish.

chef_bio.background: Yamasan Sushi is a long-established sushi house inside Kanazawa's lively Omicho Market, proud of more than 60 years of history. It sits in the heart of the market that has fed Kanazawa for centuries, and draws steady lines of visitors come for fresh local fish.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop emphasizes careful sourcing and handwork: Noto-area rice (a Koshihikari/Notohikari blend), local Noto soy sauce and vinegar, premium Ariake nori, and each piece prepared by hand.
chef_bio.specialty: Its signature is a lavish kaisen-don piled with 18 kinds of local and nearby seafood — including tuna and chu-toro, scallop, squid, sweet shrimp, sea urchin, salmon roe, crab and more — finished with gold leaf. Premium nigiri sushi is the other draw.

anecdotes.text: The house kaisen-don is built from 18 types of fresh local and regional seafood over Noto-grown rice and finished with gold leaf, a presentation that has made it one of the market's most photographed bowls.
The kitchen closes for the day once the day's ingredients run out, so the shop can shut before its posted 16:00 closing.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Sushi and kaisen-don are served with standard Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat; no gluten-free soy sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: An all-seafood sushi and raw-fish kitchen; raw fish and shellfish are handled throughout, so cross-contact with animal products is unavoidable.

cultural_comfort.note: A famous, busy market sushi spot where lines are common; a little basic Japanese and patience help, and pointing at the picture menu for the kaisen-don works fine for a foreign student.

places.name: くろ屋 (Kuroya)
places.gf_detail: This is a Japanese izakaya built around soy sauce, miso, and bonito/seafood dashi, plus tempura and other fried items in shared oil. There is no gluten-free menu and no indication of celiac-safe handling. Not safe for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: The menu centers on local fish (nodoguro/throat perch, sashimi), Noto beef stew, and seafood; even vegetable dishes are typically prepared with fish dashi. There are no documented vegan options.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Thu, Fri, Sat 18:00-23:00 (last order). Closed Wednesday and Sunday. Irregular closures possible. (One listing notes the kitchen runs to last order ~23:00; opening from 17:00 is also reported on Hot Pepper.)
places.neighborhood: Honmachi (near Kanazawa Station east exit / Hokutetsu Kanaza
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Kuroya is an owner-run izakaya near Kanazawa Station, opened by chef Ishiguro, who trained at いたる (Itaru) in Kakigibashi — one of Kanazawa's most famous and beloved izakaya. He brought that pedigree to his own small counter, where the cooking is built around top-quality local fis

chef_bio.background: Kuroya is an owner-run izakaya near Kanazawa Station, opened by chef Ishiguro, who trained at いたる (Itaru) in Kakigibashi — one of Kanazawa's most famous and beloved izakaya. He brought that pedigree to his own small counter, where the cooking is built around top-quality local fish and Kaga vegetables.
chef_bio.philosophy: A focus on 'Kanazawa's deliciousness' — abundant, high-level dishes made from local fish and vegetables, paired with regional sake at an intimate counter.
chef_bio.specialty: Local seafood, above all nodoguro (throat perch) served as melt-in-the-mouth sashimi and as a roasted dish that must be reserved in advance; thick-cut fresh sashimi; double-fried Gosashima sweet potato and other Kaga vegetable dishes; and a daily-limited (about 20 servings) Noto-beef stew, tender enough to cut with chopsticks and served in a hot stone pot.

anecdotes.text: Chef Ishiguro honed his craft at the celebrated Kanazawa izakaya いたる (Itaru) before opening Kuroya near the station.
The signature beef stew is based on a recipe from the chef's parents, who run a Western-style restaurant (洋食店) in Toyama, and uses A4–A5 Noto beef; only about 20 servings are made each day.
Roasted nodoguro is a standout but must be ordered ahead, and the counter is regularly full even on weekday openings, so reservations are strongly advised.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen prepares tempura and other fried seafood/vegetable dishes, so shared frying oil and wheat batter make gluten cross-contact likely.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a traditional Kanazawa izakaya, dishes rely on regular (wheat-containing) soy sauce, miso, and seasonings; no wheat-free soy sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is seafood- and meat-forward (nodoguro, sashimi, Noto-beef stew); vegetable dishes are typically finished with fish dashi, so no reliably vegan preparation is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a small, locally loved counter izakaya where some Japanese and a phone reservation (it fills up fast) will go a long way, so it suits a student comfortable ordering in basic Japanese.

places.name: cafe たもん (Cafe Tamon)
places.gf_detail: ⚠ Japanese-first review 2026-07-03 (high→ask): The venue makes ZERO gluten-free/allergen claim in its own Japanese text — 米粉 is celebrity-cafe (MEGUMI) / SDGs marketing, not a celiac protocol. Barley craft beer AND a barley stout (加賀棒茶スタウト) are on the official menu = gluten in a single shared kitchen. Rice-flour pancakes only by ingredient. — Prior: ⚠ Safety review 2026-07-02: downgraded dedicated→high. Fails 'dedicated': (a) no own-source whole-kitchen GF claim (グルテンフリー never appears; menu only says pancakes use rice flour); (b) gluten served on-premises (4 craft beers) + some sauces/toppings may contain gluten. Still above options: entire food menu in-house rice flour, widely celiac-recommended, strict celiacs report no reactions. — Prior note: Dedicated gluten-free pancake cafe. All pancakes are made from 100% Ishikawa-grown Koshihikari rice flour with no wheat — batter is rice flour plus meringue (whipped egg whites), milk, and a touch of salt. The whole menu is built around gluten-free rice flour, so this is a genuine destination for gluten-avoiders. Note: savory/lunch items (Noto pork roast, bacon eggs benedict, etc.) and toppings have not been individually allergen-verified, so a strict celiac should confirm specific sauces/sides directly; the cafe does not publish a formal cross-contamination protocol.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. The signature rice flour pancakes contain egg whites (meringue) and milk, and most menu items use dairy (cream, butter, ice cream), eggs, or Noto pork. No vegan items are labeled. Gluten-free travelers are well served here, but vegans are not.
places.hours_raw: 08:00–17:00 (L.O. 16:30), open daily including holidays; breakfast menu 8:00–10:00, lunch specials 11:00–14:00
places.neighborhood: Higashiyama / Higashi Chaya District (in front of Utasu Shrine, near Hashiba-cho bus stop)
places.notes: Cafe Tamon occupies a machiya townhouse more than 100 years old in a quiet corner of Kanazawa's Higashi Chaya geisha district, just in front of Utasu Shrine and near the Hashiba-cho bus stop. The interior keeps the old house's exposed beams and nostalgic atmosphere, with table se

chef_bio.background: Cafe Tamon occupies a machiya townhouse more than 100 years old in a quiet corner of Kanazawa's Higashi Chaya geisha district, just in front of Utasu Shrine and near the Hashiba-cho bus stop. The interior keeps the old house's exposed beams and nostalgic atmosphere, with table seating downstairs and traditional tatami seating upstairs.
chef_bio.philosophy: The cafe describes itself as 'people-friendly and earth-friendly,' built on three commitments: sustainable Ishikawa rice sourcing (including rice that would otherwise be discarded), local-production-for-local-consumption ingredient sourcing, and care for natural aromatics.
chef_bio.specialty: Pure rice flour pancakes made from 100% Ishikawa-grown Koshihikari rice flour, folded with meringue (whipped egg whites), milk, and a pinch of salt to create a famously fluffy, mochi-chewy texture. The gold-leaf pancake, dressed in Kanazawa's signature gold leaf, is a standout, alongside Goto Island sweet-potato Mont Blanc pancakes, Noto pork bacon eggs benedict pancakes, black sesame cream pancakes, and savory Noto pork lunches.

anecdotes.text: The cafe draws on sacred water from a historic well at the neighboring Utasu Shrine, which has been cherished since the shrine's founding around 1603.
The gold-leaf rice flour pancake is especially popular with international visitors, pairing Kanazawa's status as Japan's leading gold-leaf producer with a fully gluten-free batter.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The official site and menu publish no formal allergen or cross-contamination protocol; the cafe lists a direct phone line (076-255-0370) for dietary questions.

positives.text: All pancakes are made from 100% Ishikawa-grown Koshihikari rice flour with no wheat — the entire pancake concept is gluten-free, making this a genuine GF destination.
The batter contains only rice flour, meringue (egg whites), milk, and a little salt, so the core product avoids wheat thickeners entirely.

cultural_comfort.note: A foreign student will feel comfortable here — it is popular with international visitors (the gold-leaf pancake is a known draw) and the gluten-free concept is easy to understand, though staff communication is mostly in Japanese so a few polite words help.

gf_review.why: The venue makes ZERO gluten-free/allergen claim in its own Japanese text — 米粉 is celebrity-cafe (MEGUMI) / SDGs marketing, not a celiac protocol. Barley craft beer AND a barley stout (加賀棒茶スタウト) are on the official menu = gluten in a single shared kitchen. Rice-flour pancakes only by ingredient.

places.name: 森八茶寮 近江町 (Morihachi Saryo Omicho)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional Japanese sweets (wagashi) tea salon. Core offerings — fresh seasonal sweets (上生菓子), yokan, dorayaki, anmitsu, kuzukiri, zenzai — are wheat-and-bean-based and made on a confectionery line that handles flour; matcha sweets are dusted and plated together with these. There is no dedicated gluten-free menu, no allergen protocol documented, and cross-contact is essentially unavoidable. Not suitable for celiacs. A few items (plain matcha, some rakugan/agar-based sweets) may incidentally avoid wheat, but staff cannot guarantee this — ask only if you tolerate trace gluten.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu is advertised. Many traditional wagashi here are plant-based by nature (red-bean an, mochi, agar/kanten sweets, kuzukiri, tokoroten, and straight matcha or hojicha/sencha), but other sets include dairy, egg, or honey (cream, ice cream with kakigori, dorayaki, certain confections). Nothing is labeled, so a vegan would need to ask item-by-item; a matcha-with-an-based-wagashi or a kanten/anmitsu order is the most likely fit but not confirmed.
places.hours_raw: Tea salon: 09:30-17:00 daily (per official Tabelog info). Shop counter listed as 9:00-16:30 on the official site. Closed Jan 1-2 only.
places.neighborhood: Omicho Market (Shimotsutsumimachi, Musashigatsuji / Hokutetsu Kanazawa Stn)
places.notes: Morihachi (森八) is one of Kanazawa's most storied confectioners, founded in 1625 and long honored as official sweet-maker (御用菓子司) to the Kaga Domain's Maeda lords. Its Omicho tea salon sits beside Omicho Market, the centuries-old covered market locals call 'Kanazawa's kitchen,' ju

roles: Tea salon and wagashi gallery inside Omicho Market
chef_bio.background: Morihachi (森八) is one of Kanazawa's most storied confectioners, founded in 1625 and long honored as official sweet-maker (御用菓子司) to the Kaga Domain's Maeda lords. Its Omicho tea salon sits beside Omicho Market, the centuries-old covered market locals call 'Kanazawa's kitchen,' just a few minutes from Musashigatsuji. The salon is a calm, light-filled room with a small gallery and table seating that looks out onto the garden of the neighboring historic samurai residence.
chef_bio.philosophy: Roughly four centuries of traditional confectionery craft offered as a quiet pause in a busy market — premium tea and hand-made seasonal sweets enjoyed slowly, in view of a historic garden.
chef_bio.specialty: Matcha-and-seasonal-wagashi sets paired with the house's premium fresh sweets (上生菓子), alongside anmitsu, kuzukiri, zenzai, dorayaki, and Uji-matcha shaved ice (kakigori) in summer. The company's most famous confection is Choseiden (長生殿), a refined rakugan counted among Japan's three great wagashi.

anecdotes.text: Morihachi was founded in 1625 and served as the official confectioner to the Kaga Maeda domain; its signature Choseiden rakugan is regarded as one of Japan's three most famous wagashi.
The Omicho salon overlooks the garden of the adjacent historic samurai residence (Terashima Kuranin-tei) and pairs matcha with seasonal fresh sweets and summer Uji-matcha kakigori.

gf_cross_contamination.text: A wagashi-focused tea salon where flour-, wheat-, and bean-based sweets share preparation and plating; no gluten-free protocol is documented and cross-contact should be assumed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu mixes plant-based wagashi (an, mochi, kanten, kuzukiri) with dairy/egg items (kakigori toppings, dorayaki, certain confections); no vegan labeling, so cross-contact and hidden animal ingredients are possible.

positives.text: Picture menu and clearly priced matcha-and-sweet sets make ordering straightforward; many traditional offerings (matcha, an-based and agar sweets) are incidentally plant-based.

cultural_comfort.note: A welcoming, tourist-accustomed tea salon inside Omicho Market where pointing at the picture menu and a friendly "konnichiwa" will get you a matcha-and-sweet set easily, though detailed allergy questions may be hard to answer in English.

places.name: 久連波 (Kureha)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional Japanese sweets cafe. The signature items are jo-namagashi (seasonal wagashi), matcha, kakigori, and parfaits. Wagashi and matcha sweets here are not gluten-free: dough, fillings, and accompaniments commonly involve wheat/wheat starch, and there are no documented gluten-free offerings or allergen handling. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu is advertised. Some items (matcha with plain wagashi, kakigori with syrup) may be plant-based, but parfaits, cream zenzai, and hot sandwiches typically use dairy/egg. Anyone strictly vegan would need to ask about each item; nothing is documented as certified vegan.
places.hours_raw: Cafe 10:00–17:30 (10:00–18:00 per official site); closed Wednesdays.
places.neighborhood: Higashiyama / Higashi Chaya District
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Kureha occupies a preserved wooden-lattice machiya in Kanazawa's historic Higashi Chaya geisha district. It is run by Eritora, a kimono house dating to the late Edo period that specializes in Kaga Yuzen, the region's traditional hand-dyed silk. The shop pairs a Kaga Yuzen retail

roles: Kaga Yuzen kimono shop and rental
chef_bio.background: Kureha occupies a preserved wooden-lattice machiya in Kanazawa's historic Higashi Chaya geisha district. It is run by Eritora, a kimono house dating to the late Edo period that specializes in Kaga Yuzen, the region's traditional hand-dyed silk. The shop pairs a Kaga Yuzen retail and kimono-rental business with a small tea-cafe, so guests sip matcha surrounded by dyed textile artistry.
chef_bio.philosophy: Embodying the Kaga ethos of tradition, quiet luxury, and attention to detail; tableware is decorated with works by a contemporary Kaga Yuzen artist so the craft of the kimono house extends to the cafe table.
chef_bio.specialty: Seasonal jo-namagashi (upper-grade wagashi) served with matcha, plus Kaga bocha kakigori, parfaits, matcha cream zenzai, and matcha-filled hot sandwiches.

anecdotes.text: The ground floor holds the Kaga Yuzen shop and table seating, while a second-floor tatami zashiki lets you look out over the chaya street while you eat.
Beyond the cafe, Kureha offers Kaga Yuzen kimono rental by advance reservation, blending dining with a dress-up experience in the old quarter.

cultural_comfort.note: A calm, traditional machiya tea-cafe where pointing at the matcha-and-wagashi menu works fine, but staff are unlikely to handle dietary restrictions in English, so come for the atmosphere rather than special requests.

places.name: 菊一 (Kikuichi)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. This is a classic Kanazawa oden house whose dashi is built on Ohno soy sauce (made from wheat) and whose signature items are literally wheat gluten — kuruma-fu (車麩) and nama-fu (生麩). The simmering broth, soy sauce, and miso preparations all contain wheat. Not safe for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. The oden broth is a bonito-and-kombu dashi (fish-based), and the menu centers on seafood and animal items such as crab face (kani-men) made from female snow crab, scallop/whelk (baigai), and white-miso dote-yaki. There are no documented vegan options; the shared simmering pot makes cross-contact with fish dashi unavoidable.
places.hours_raw: 17:30-22:00 (closes earlier when ingredients run out), closed Tue and Wed
places.neighborhood: 片町・香林坊 (Katamachi / Korinbo)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 菊一 (Kikuichi) is a long-established Kanazawa oden house founded in 1934 (Showa 9) near the Korinbo intersection in central Katamachi. It is a small, family-style counter shop that has kept its old-fashioned, Showa-era feel for generations — stepping inside is often described as a

chef_bio.background: 菊一 (Kikuichi) is a long-established Kanazawa oden house founded in 1934 (Showa 9) near the Korinbo intersection in central Katamachi. It is a small, family-style counter shop that has kept its old-fashioned, Showa-era feel for generations — stepping inside is often described as a time slip into mid-century Kanazawa.
chef_bio.philosophy: Preserve traditional Kanazawa oden: a single long-running dashi, local Ohno soy sauce, and seasonal Hokuriku ingredients served honestly across the counter.
chef_bio.specialty: Kanazawa-style oden simmered in a bonito-and-kombu dashi seasoned with local Ohno soy sauce, a broth the shop says it has continuously replenished since its founding. Signature items include kuruma-fu (wheel-shaped wheat gluten) and nama-fu (fresh gluten), baigai (whelk), and white-miso dote-yaki. Its most celebrated dish is kani-men (蟹面) — a whole female snow crab (kobako-gani) packed back into its own shell as an oden ingredient — a dish Kikuichi is widely credited with originating.

anecdotes.text: Kikuichi is widely credited as the birthplace of kani-men (crab face) oden, made by filling a female snow crab's shell with its own meat and roe; the name 面 (face) is said to come from its resemblance to a kendo helmet, and it had already appeared on the menu by around 1954.
The shop's oden broth is a bonito-kombu dashi based on Ohno soy sauce that the restaurant says has been continuously added to since it opened in 1934.
Regulars and visitors describe the interior as an amiable, Showa-era space — 'like slipping back in time' — with counter and a small table, where kuruma-fu and miso dote-yaki pair famously with beer.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Oden is cooked communally in one long-simmering pot; wheat-gluten items (kuruma-fu, nama-fu) share the same broth as everything else, so gluten is throughout the dish.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The dashi is seasoned with Ohno soy sauce, a traditional wheat-containing soy sauce, and has been continuously used since founding.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The broth is a fish-based bonito-and-kombu dashi and the pot also simmers seafood and meat items, so no item avoids animal-derived contact.

cultural_comfort.note: A snug, well-loved Showa-era counter where staff and many regulars speak mainly Japanese — go with a little Japanese or a translation app, be ready to queue, and bring cash.

places.name: 季節料理・おでん 黒百合 (Kisetsu Ryori Oden Kuroyuri)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The food centers on oden simmered in a long-aged dashi broth seasoned with soy sauce, plus fried wheat-based items (e.g. car-fu / wheat gluten cakes) and battered/breaded dishes — all wheat-containing. No gluten-free menu, no dedicated fryer, and no allergen accommodation is documented. Not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The kitchen is built around fish-based dashi, fresh seafood and sashimi from Omicho Market, and oden that includes fish cakes and meat. The broth itself contains fish/bonito stock, so even vegetable items are cooked in animal-derived dashi. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-21:30 (L.O. 21:00), open daily, no regular closing day
places.neighborhood: Kanazawa Station (inside Kanazawa Hyakubangai Anto, station-
places.cuisine: Kanazawa oden and Kaga regional cuisine
places.notes: Kuroyuri is a Kanazawa institution founded in 1953 (Showa 28), originally opening in the basement of the old National Railway Kanazawa Station before moving into the Kanazawa Hyakubangai 'Anto' complex when the station was redeveloped. It is now run by the third generation of the

chef_bio.background: Kuroyuri is a Kanazawa institution founded in 1953 (Showa 28), originally opening in the basement of the old National Railway Kanazawa Station before moving into the Kanazawa Hyakubangai 'Anto' complex when the station was redeveloped. It is now run by the third generation of the founding family, making it one of the city's enduring oden landmarks right at the station gates.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop keeps the broth's flavor unchanged across the decades and aims to be a relaxed, approachable counter where solo diners, lunchtime drinkers, business travelers and tourists can sit enveloped in the aroma of dashi and choose oden one piece at a time.
chef_bio.specialty: Kanazawa-style oden simmered in a 'secret' dashi broth that the family has continuously topped up and carried forward since 1953, served alongside sashimi assortments sourced from nearby Omicho Market, freshly prepared iwana (char) from in-house tanks, Kaga vegetables, Hakusan tofu, and local sake.

anecdotes.text: The oden broth has been continuously replenished and kept to the same flavor since the restaurant's founding in 1953, a recipe passed down through the generations.
Originally opened in the basement of the old Kanazawa Station, the shop relocated into Kanazawa Hyakubangai and is now operated by the third generation of the family.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Oden is simmered communally in a shared dashi pot that includes wheat-based items such as fried gluten/fu cakes, so all oden shares broth with gluten-containing ingredients.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The oden broth and seasonings are soy-sauce based; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, and no wheat-free tamari is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The base broth is a fish/bonito dashi and the menu is seafood-centric, so vegetable items are cooked in animal-derived stock.

cultural_comfort.note: A welcoming, tourist-friendly station spot where you can point at oden at the counter and pay by card or QR; basic Japanese greetings help, but staff are used to visitors and solo diners.

places.name: ローレンス (Lawrence (Junkissa Laurence))
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional coffee house, not a gluten-free destination. The offering is essentially brewed coffee (a special hot blend, no food menu of note). No gluten-free accommodation or celiac-safety information is documented anywhere. Treat as not celiac-oriented.
places.vegan_detail: A coffee-focused kissaten. Black coffee served without milk or sugar is plant-based by default, but there is no documented vegan menu or labeling. Anyone needing dairy-free should confirm directly when reserving.
places.hours_raw: Now operates on a reservation-only basis for a roughly 2-hour window in the evening (reported around 18:00-20:00); historically listed afternoon hours (approx. 15:00-19:00). Hours vary and are confirmed when you phone ahead.
places.neighborhood: 片町 (Katamachi), Korinbo-Katamachi central district
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Lawrence (ローレンス) is a legendary hidden coffee house tucked into central Kanazawa's Katamachi entertainment district, founded in 1966. It is run single-handedly by its madam, an art-school-trained proprietor who turned the small room into an enveloping, otherworldly garden of her

chef_bio.background: Lawrence (ローレンス) is a legendary hidden coffee house tucked into central Kanazawa's Katamachi entertainment district, founded in 1966. It is run single-handedly by its madam, an art-school-trained proprietor who turned the small room into an enveloping, otherworldly garden of her own making. Crossing the threshold feels like stepping out of the present day into a private, dreamlike interior cascading with dried flowers, her own sketches, and sculpture.
chef_bio.philosophy: Keep the place going quietly and on her own terms. To preserve her strength and keep the shop alive, she scaled operation down to a reservation-only, roughly two-hour evening window rather than closing the doors for good.
chef_bio.specialty: A single, carefully sourced cup of coffee. The madam, who reportedly does not even drink coffee herself, hunts down exceptional beans (one account names beans favored by the Colombian embassy) and serves a clean, satisfying brew with no sugar or milk.

anecdotes.text: In 1967 the novelist Hiroyuki Itsuki (五木寛之), who had moved to his wife's hometown of Kanazawa, was a near-daily regular here, sitting for hours over his manuscript paper. It was at this café that he received the news he had won the 56th Naoki Prize for 'Aozameta Uma o Miyo'.

Every element of the space, from the overflowing dried-flower arrangements to the drawings and sculptures on the walls, is the madam's own handiwork, giving the room its singular 'isekai' (otherworldly) atmosphere.

The madam is known for spontaneous, philosophical conversation with guests about life and happiness, and some seats carry 'RESERVED' placards, part of the shop's quietly idiosyncratic charm in its more-than-half-century of operation.

positives.text: The core offering is simply brewed black coffee served without sugar or milk, which is inherently plant-based and free of common food allergens.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a deeply personal, Japanese-speaking space run solo by an elderly madam where you must phone ahead in Japanese to reserve, so go with a Japanese-speaking friend or local help and expect quiet, respectful conversation rather than a quick casual stop.

places.name: 加賀料理 大名茶家 (Kaga Ryori Daimyo Chaya)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional Kaga kaiseki house built around soy-sauce- and dashi-based cooking (jibuni, simmered dishes, grilled fish with tare). No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented, and signature dishes rely on wheat-containing soy sauce and broths. Not suitable for celiacs; a gluten-free diner would need to confirm directly and should not assume cross-contamination control.
places.vegan_detail: A seafood-forward Kaga cuisine restaurant centered on crab, nodoguro, yellowtail, sweet shrimp and fish-and-meat kaiseki, with dashi (bonito/kombu) throughout. No vegan or vegetarian menu is documented. Vegans should look elsewhere; at most, ask in advance whether any plant-only courses can be arranged.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-14:30 (lunch L.O. 13:45), 17:00-22:00 (dinner L.O. 21:00) / open daily (occasional temporary and New Year closures)
places.neighborhood: Around Kanazawa Stn (Konohanamachi)
places.notes: Daimyo Chaya is a long-established Kaga cuisine restaurant founded in 1962 (Showa 37) and located about a three-minute walk from Kanazawa Station. It occupies a three-story house whose interior is decorated with antiques from the old feudal (han) era, giving each floor a distinct

chef_bio.background: Daimyo Chaya is a long-established Kaga cuisine restaurant founded in 1962 (Showa 37) and located about a three-minute walk from Kanazawa Station. It occupies a three-story house whose interior is decorated with antiques from the old feudal (han) era, giving each floor a distinctly Kanazawa atmosphere of private tatami rooms, gardens, and halls used according to the occasion.
chef_bio.philosophy: The house describes its aim as drawing out the ingredient's own flavor and umami and elevating it into a dish unique to the restaurant ('素材本来の美味しさや旨味を引き出し、当店ならではの逸品へと昇華させていく').
chef_bio.specialty: Kaga kaiseki built on fish from the Sea of Japan and Kaga vegetables: the local classic jibuni, plus nodoguro (rosy seabass), and a strong winter focus on Kanazawa-port brand crab and yellowtail (buri-shabu hot pot). The kitchen is known for crab in many preparations and for the very rare 'golden crab,' said to appear in only one of every one to two thousand crabs landed (by reservation). Seasonal highlights run from firefly squid in spring to pike conger (hamo) in summer and nodoguro and crab in autumn and winter.

anecdotes.text: Founded in Showa 37 (1962) and continuously patronized ever since, per the restaurant's own statement '昭和三十七年に創業して以来、多くのお客様にご愛顧いただいています'.
Known for the rare 'golden crab' (黄金蟹), said to appear in only about one of every 1,000-2,000 crabs caught, offered November through April by reservation.
The interior displays antiques from the feudal-domain era across rooms on the first, second, and third floors, used flexibly by occasion.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No gluten-free handling or dedicated equipment is documented; the cuisine is soy-sauce- and dashi-based, so cross-contamination with wheat should be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Traditional Kaga dishes such as jibuni and simmered/grilled preparations use Japanese soy sauce and tare, which normally contain wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: A seafood- and dashi-centered kaiseki kitchen; no separate vegan preparation is documented, so animal-derived broth and cross-contact should be expected.

cultural_comfort.note: A formal traditional kaiseki house oriented toward set courses and groups; a foreign student would be most comfortable reserving ahead and choosing a seasonal course rather than ordering à la carte.

places.name: おでん居酒屋 三幸 本店 (Oden Izakaya Miyuki Honten)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free or celiac-safe. This is a Kanazawa oden house: the simmering dashi is wheat-laden (oden in Japan is cooked with soy sauce / mirin), and signature oden ingredients are wheat gluten — 車麩 (kuruma-fu / Kaga fu) is made of wheat. Tempura items (corn tempura, みゆき揚げ fried fishcake) are battered/breaded. No gluten-free menu, no dedicated fryer, and no documented allergy handling.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians. The house dashi is built on chicken bones, bonito (katsuo) and dried sardines (niboshi), and the oden pot simmers fish cakes, バイ貝 (whelk), 梅貝, and other animal ingredients. The menu centers on seafood sashimi, oden, and izakaya dishes. No vegan options are documented.
places.hours_raw: 16:00–22:00 (L.O. 21:30); closes when the oden runs out. Closed Sundays, public holidays, and the 2nd Monday of each month.
places.neighborhood: Katamachi (back streets of Korinbo / Katamachi)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Founded in 1967, 三幸 (Miyuki) is a long-running, family-run oden izakaya tucked into a back lane of Katamachi, Kanazawa's liveliest nightlife district. Over more than half a century it has become a neighborhood institution — the kind of place locals send visitors to for a true tas

chef_bio.background: Founded in 1967, 三幸 (Miyuki) is a long-running, family-run oden izakaya tucked into a back lane of Katamachi, Kanazawa's liveliest nightlife district. Over more than half a century it has become a neighborhood institution — the kind of place locals send visitors to for a true taste of Kanazawa oden.
chef_bio.philosophy: The owners describe evolving their dashi day by day — 'better today than yesterday, better tomorrow than today' — by continually topping up and refining the same broth.
chef_bio.specialty: Kanazawa oden simmered in a continuously replenished dashi built on chicken bones, bonito and dried sardines, kept light and refined. The pot features Kanazawa classics: 車麩 (Kaga wheat-gluten fu), バイ貝/梅貝 (whelk), and 源助大根, a Kaga heirloom daikon. The house signatures are みゆき揚げ, a freshly made deep-fried fishcake with a deep fish flavor, and a とろろの鉄板焼 (grilled grated yam). The menu rounds out with seasonal obanzai home-style dishes and an oden-dashi tea rice to finish.

anecdotes.text: Locals call it a pilgrimage site for Kanazawa oden, and reviewers note it draws daily lines; a long-running blog marks the shop's 56th year and calls it the spot locals genuinely recommend.
Despite the crowds, it's described as a comfortable bar even for first-timers and solo diners, with counter seats that look straight onto the bubbling oden pot.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Oden is simmered communally in one shared dashi pot containing wheat-gluten fu (車麩) and soy-sauce-based broth, so all oden items share gluten-contaminated liquid.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a Kanazawa oden house, the simmering broth is built on dashi seasoned with soy sauce (standard wheat-containing shoyu); no gluten-free tamari is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The base dashi is made from chicken bones, bonito and dried sardines, and the oden pot simmers fishcakes and whelk, so even vegetable items absorb animal-based broth.

cultural_comfort.note: A bustling, locally-loved counter izakaya where some Japanese will help a foreign student a lot; solo diners and pairs queue rather than reserve (reservations are for groups of 3+), it's cash only, and an English menu is not guaranteed.

places.name: 手打蕎麦 こより (Teuchi Soba Koyori)
places.gf_detail: A handmade soba restaurant. Soba noodles contain buckwheat but the dipping broth (tsuyu) is made with bonito/mackerel/kombu dashi and locally produced soy sauce (which contains wheat), and tempura is on the menu. There is no documented gluten-free preparation or dedicated handling, so this is not celiac-safe.
places.vegan_detail: The dashi-based broth uses bonito and mackerel, and signature dishes include duck (kamo) soba and tempura. No vegan menu or plant-based dashi is documented, so this is not suitable for strict vegans.
places.hours_raw: Tue-Sat 11:00-14:00 (last entry). Closed Sun, Mon, and holidays
places.neighborhood: Nagata, Kanazawa (residential area near Korinbo / Katamachi)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The owner comes from Togakushi in Nagano, a region famous for soba, where his family still runs a soba restaurant. He moved to Kanazawa and converted his wife's family home, a former farmhouse in the Nagata/Takaoka area, into this hideaway shop set in a building over 100 years ol

chef_bio.background: The owner comes from Togakushi in Nagano, a region famous for soba, where his family still runs a soba restaurant. He moved to Kanazawa and converted his wife's family home, a former farmhouse in the Nagata/Takaoka area, into this hideaway shop set in a building over 100 years old.
chef_bio.philosophy: A refined, craft-focused approach to soba: the dipping broth departs from Edo style, blending bonito, mackerel and kombu dashi softened with local round-soybean soy sauce for umami without harshness. The interior displays a ceramic work bearing Kenji Miyazawa's poem 'Be Not Defeated by the Rain,' reflecting the owner's quiet philosophy.
chef_bio.specialty: Togakushi-style handmade soba served in 'botchi-mori' presentation (five small bundles of very thin under-1.2mm noodles arranged on a basket), hand-milled daily from Shinshu buckwheat. Signature dishes include duck (kamo) broth soba with mountain pepper and tempura soba.

anecdotes.text: The renovated farmhouse keeps stone-paved entryways, heavy overhead beams, and vintage wooden shelves displaying seasonal pottery, and the owner personally sees departing guests off in a gesture of genuine hospitality.
Now regarded as one of Kanazawa's representative soba shops, it was selected for a national soba “Top 100” list in 2025.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Soba noodles are buckwheat-based but the kitchen also fries tempura and uses wheat-containing soy sauce in the broth; no gluten-free protocol is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The dipping broth is finished with local round-soybean soy sauce, which contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The broth is built on bonito and mackerel dashi, and duck dishes are a specialty, so animal products are central to the menu.

cultural_comfort.note: A reservation-only hideaway soba house in a 100-year-old converted farmhouse; little English is documented, so basic Japanese (or a Japanese-speaking friend to phone-book) will make the visit much smoother.

places.name: 宇宙軒食堂 (Uchuken Shokudo)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The house is built around a wheat-and-miso "secret tare" (made with three kinds of miso, most of which contain wheat) that coats the signature grilled pork belly and yakisoba. Tonkatsu, curry roux, yakisoba noodles and soy-based seasonings all contain wheat. There is no gluten-free menu, no allergen handling, and shared griddle/sauce surfaces. Not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. This is a meat-centered set-meal shop whose entire identity is grilled pork belly (とんバラ) and pork-based dishes (tonkatsu-don, pork soup), all cooked on a shared central iron griddle. Even side items are prepared in a pork-fat environment, and the secret tare is the only sauce. No vegan or vegetarian set is offered.
places.neighborhood: Katamachi (back alley of the Korinbo / Katamachi entertainment district, beside APA Hotel)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Uchuken Shokudo is one of Kanazawa's most beloved no-frills set-meal shops, tucked into a back alley of the Katamachi entertainment district beside an APA Hotel. Locals say nearly everyone in the city has eaten here at least once, and it is counted among Kanazawa's heavyweight 's

chef_bio.background: Uchuken Shokudo is one of Kanazawa's most beloved no-frills set-meal shops, tucked into a back alley of the Katamachi entertainment district beside an APA Hotel.
