places.gf_detail: Several signature sweets are starch-based rather than wheat-based — honkuzukiri is made from premium Yoshino arrowroot (kudzu) starch and the honwarabimochi from bracken starch with kinako and kuromitsu — so they are inherently low in wheat. However, the kitchen documents no celiac-safe handling, no dedicated equipment, and items like anmitsu/zenzai and seasonal toppings may involve shared utensils or wheat-containing components. Celiacs should confirm each item directly; this is not a certified gluten-free venue.
places.vegan_detail: Many items are plant-leaning — kuzukiri, warabimochi, sweet-potato zenzai and anmitsu are built around starches, red beans, kinako and kuromitsu — but parfaits and cream anmitsu include dairy, and no dish is labeled vegan. Dashi or other animal-derived elements in some items are not ruled out. Vegans should ask item by item.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–18:00 (L.O. 17:30), closed Wednesdays
places.neighborhood: Kakinokibatake / Hirosaka, near the 21st Century Museum of A
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Tsubomi opened in 2004 in the Kakinokibatake district near the 21st Century Museum of Art, founded by the long-established Kanazawa kaiseki ryotei Honami (穂濤). At the time, a ryotei operating a dedicated sweets shop was said to be the first attempt of its kind in Japan. The café

chef_bio.background: Tsubomi opened in 2004 in the Kakinokibatake district near the 21st Century Museum of Art, founded by the long-established Kanazawa kaiseki ryotei Honami (穂濤). At the time, a ryotei operating a dedicated sweets shop was said to be the first attempt of its kind in Japan. The café grew out of the popularity of the kuzukiri dessert served at the parent restaurant, conceived as an approachable way to introduce guests to kaiseki culture.
chef_bio.philosophy: Owner Koichiro Koshikawa describes the aim as serving the finest ingredients in the finest setting, personally visiting producers and selecting specific farms — for example a particular grower of Goroshima-kintoki sweet potatoes — rather than accepting any supplier.
chef_bio.specialty: Honkuzukiri — delicate, translucent arrowroot noodles made from the highest-grade Yoshino kudzu, served with kuromitsu black molasses — alongside honwarabimochi from premium bracken starch with kinako, white-dumpling cream anmitsu, kintoki sweet-potato zenzai, and limited summer kakigori such as the popular white-peach shaved ice. Every sweet comes with complimentary Kanazawa bocha roasted tea.

anecdotes.text: The shop's earthen walls were finished by Kanazawa plasterers over roughly three months, and the space is built around a single large wooden slab table.

The courtyard garden borrows the stone walls of Kanazawa Castle's outer moat as scenery, with the sound of the historic Tatsumi waterway running nearby and seasonal mountain plants marking the seasons.
Honami's kuzukiri was so popular at the main restaurant that the owner created Tsubomi to share it more accessibly — reportedly the first time a ryotei in Japan opened its own sweets shop.

positives.text: Several signature sweets are naturally starch-based rather than wheat-based: honkuzukiri is made from Yoshino arrowroot (kudzu) and honwarabimochi from bracken starch, with kinako and kuromitsu.

cultural_comfort.note: A calm, traditional sweets café where a few words of Japanese and a relaxed manner go a long way; menus are simple and pointing works, but English support is limited.

places.name: 和菓子 村上 金沢本店 (Wagashi Murakami Kanazawa Honten)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe by default. While some wagashi are rice-based (mochi/anko), the signature ふくさ餅 has a soft baked skin made with wheat flour, and many traditional sweets and the café menu (mont blanc, soft serve) involve wheat and dairy. No celiac-safe protocols are documented; cross-contamination in a shared confectionery kitchen is likely. A traveler avoiding gluten would need to ask item-by-item and should not assume safety.
places.vegan_detail: Some core wagashi (mochi with red bean paste, 黒糖ふくさ餅, わり氷) are plant-based by tradition, but ingredients are not labeled vegan and the eat-in menu includes dairy items (soft serve ice cream, mont blanc). No vegan certification or stated egg/dairy-free assurances are documented, so a vegan should confirm ingredients per item before ordering.
places.hours_raw: 09:15-17:00 (open daily)
places.neighborhood: Nagamachi (toward Nishi Chaya District / Nomachi)
places.notes: 和菓子 村上 was founded in Kanazawa in 1911 (Meiji 44) and has spent more than a century devoted to a single path of sweets-making. The Nagamachi shop in central Kanazawa sits near the historic samurai district, pairing a confectionery counter with a sit-down café (菓ふぇ MURAKAMI) where

chef_bio.background: 和菓子 村上 was founded in Kanazawa in 1911 (Meiji 44) and has spent more than a century devoted to a single path of sweets-making. The Nagamachi shop in central Kanazawa sits near the historic samurai district, pairing a confectionery counter with a sit-down café (菓ふぇ MURAKAMI) where visitors can rest between sightseeing.
chef_bio.philosophy: The house holds that 'the foundation of wagashi is delicious bean paste,' insisting on homemade filling and traditional technique — 'not taking shortcuts, avoiding complacency, and doing ordinary things extraordinarily well.'
chef_bio.specialty: The signature ふくさ餅 wraps mochi and house-made bean paste in a soft baked skin; the black-sugar 黒糖ふくさ餅 is a classic Kanazawa souvenir. The eat-in café is also known for a half-gold-leaf soft serve, mont blanc, and seasonal wagashi such as 垣穂 (kinako and sesame) and the crystalline dry sweet わり氷.

anecdotes.text: Founded in 1911 (Meiji 44), the maker has dedicated itself to a single path of confectionery for over a century, prizing homemade bean paste above all.
The black-sugar fukusa mochi — mochi and house-made anko wrapped in a fluffy black-sugar skin — is treated as a quintessential Kanazawa souvenir.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Shared confectionery kitchen producing wheat-based sweets (the baked-skin ふくさ餅) alongside other items; no gluten-free protocol documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Eat-in menu mixes plant-based wagashi with dairy items (soft serve, mont blanc); no separation or vegan labeling documented.

positives.text: Some traditional offerings such as mochi with red bean paste and the dry sweet わり氷 are naturally plant-based.

cultural_comfort.note: A welcoming traditional sweets shop with an eat-in café where a smile and a few words of Japanese go a long way; pointing at the display cases works fine.

places.name: ロサンゼルス（organic&natural Los Angeles） (Los Angeles (Organic & Natural Los Angeles))
places.gf_detail: Not a dedicated gluten-free kitchen, but there are real GF options: the daily-baked vegan muffins are made with rice flour and described as gluten-free, and gluten-free sweets are highlighted. The savory menu (soy-based "meat" burgers, teriyaki/ginger items) likely involves wheat-containing seasonings such as soy sauce and breaded/wheat-based mock meats, so celiac diners should ask item-by-item. No statement about a dedicated fryer or cross-contamination controls was found.
places.vegan_detail: Fully vegan / 100% plant-based. The owner states the food contains absolutely no meat, eggs, dairy, or animal-derived fats, and all dressings are homemade and animal-free. Centered on Kaga vegetables (Kanazawa shungiku chrysanthemum greens, Kaga lotus root) and other organic/pesticide-free local vegetables, with soy-based meat alternatives and pesticide-free Isehikari rice.
places.hours_raw: Mon & Thu 10:00-16:00; Tue, Wed, Fri, Sat 10:00-17:00; Sun closed. Lunch service roughly 11:30-14:00. (A local guide lists broader 10:00-18:00 with irregular closures, so confirm before visiting.)
places.neighborhood: Otemachi (near Omicho Market / Kanazawa Castle Park)
places.cuisine: Vegan / plant-based cafe using Kaga vegetabl
places.notes: Los Angeles is a fully plant-based cafe in central Kanazawa, opened in 2014. Owner Mikihiro Morishima evacuated from Iwaki City in Fukushima to Ishikawa after the disaster and built the cafe around a wish to share the safe, delicious vegetables that local farmers work hard to gro

chef_bio.background: Los Angeles is a fully plant-based cafe in central Kanazawa, opened in 2014. Owner Mikihiro Morishima evacuated from Iwaki City in Fukushima to Ishikawa after the disaster and built the cafe around a wish to share the safe, delicious vegetables that local farmers work hard to grow. Everything served is vegan, containing no meat, eggs, dairy, or animal fats.
chef_bio.philosophy: The cafe describes a 'New Smart Life Style' where dining is about more than taste - caring for your own and your family's wellbeing, the environment, and connection with others, summed up in the line 'Luxury is satisfaction of the soul.' Vegetables are sourced from pesticide-free local growers such as Tomofarm and Eco Farm Okuno, cultivated in living, microbe-rich soil to bring out the vegetables' true sweetness.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature Veggie Combination Lunch is a large wooden bowl piled with Kaga vegetables such as Kanazawa shungiku chrysanthemum greens and Kaga lotus root alongside many seasonal organic greens, paired with soy-based 'meat' dishes like a ginger steak bowl or burger and pesticide-free Isehikari rice. Soy-meat ginger steak and teriyaki burgers are popular, and the daily handmade gluten-free rice-flour vegan muffins (2-3 kinds) often sell out as soon as they hit the counter.

anecdotes.text: Owner Mikihiro Morishima relocated from Fukushima's Iwaki City to Ishikawa after evacuating, opening Los Angeles to introduce people to the safe, tasty vegetables grown by local farmers.
The gluten-free rice-flour vegan muffins are baked fresh every day in 2-3 varieties and frequently sell out right after they appear on the counter.
The logo artwork was created by designer Sarah Williams, who previously worked on art for Cirque du Soleil's 'Viva Elvis!' production.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Savory items include teriyaki and ginger 'steak' preparations made with soy-based mock meat; these typically involve wheat-containing soy sauce/seasonings, so celiac diners should confirm each dish rather than assume it is gluten-free.

positives.text: Fully vegan kitchen with no meat, eggs, dairy, or animal fats, and homemade animal-free dressings - very low risk of accidental animal ingredients.
Daily handmade gluten-free vegan muffins are made with rice flour, and gluten-free sweets are a regular offering.
Vegetables come from named pesticide-free local farms (Tomofarm, Eco Farm Okuno) and include Kaga vegetables, with pesticide-free Isehikari rice.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, welcoming plant-based cafe where a little Japanese helps; the fully vegan menu and takeout make it easy for a foreign student, though staff English may be limited so a few polite phrases go a long way.

places.name: 市場めし あまつぼ (Ichiba-meshi Amatsubo)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The kitchen is built around Kanazawa oden simmered in a soy-and-dashi broth, plus tempura, fried fish cakes (satsuma-age, chikuwa) and signature kuruma-fu (wheat gluten), all of which contain wheat. Soy sauce and oden dashi are central to nearly every dish. There is no gluten-free menu and no indication of allergen accommodation, so this is not safe for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: This is a seafood and Kaga-cuisine eatery: sashimi, seafood rice bowls (uni, botan shrimp, salmon roe, tuna), and oden built on fish/bonito dashi. Even vegetable items (daikon, konnyaku) are simmered in a fish-based broth, so there is no reliable vegan option.
places.hours_raw: 8:00-21:00 daily; Wednesday 8:00-15:00 (lunch only). Sources vary slightly on Wednesday — the Omicho Market and official pages list Wednesday 8:00-15:00, while Tabelog lists Wednesday as closed. Occasional multi-day/year-end closures.
places.neighborhood: Omicho Market (近江町市場), near the M'za/Emza entrance, Musashig
places.notes: Amatsubo was founded in 1964 (Showa 39) by the current owner's parents, who first set up in Kanazawa's Korinbo district before moving to Kakinokibata in 1984. Curiously, oden was not on the menu at the start: the shop originally ran with two entrances and two signboards, operatin

chef_bio.background: Amatsubo was founded in 1964 (Showa 39) by the current owner's parents, who first set up in Kanazawa's Korinbo district before moving to Kakinokibata in 1984. Curiously, oden was not on the menu at the start: the shop originally ran with two entrances and two signboards, operating as both a popular diner (taishu-shokudo) and a Western-style restaurant. The owner recalls that oden was added later, when his father put it on the menu in response to customers' requests. This market-side branch, 市場めし あまつぼ, brings the honten's flavors right into Omicho Market, Kanazawa's centuries-old 'kitchen.'
chef_bio.philosophy: Showcasing fresh Kaga and Noto ingredients in hearty, approachable Kanazawa home-cooking, open from early morning so visitors can enjoy oden and local sake any time of day.
chef_bio.specialty: Kanazawa oden (signature items include kuruma-fu / wheat-gluten and well-simmered daikon in a soy-and-dashi broth), plus generous market-fresh kaisendon seafood rice bowls topped with uni, botan shrimp, salmon roe and tuna, sashimi, and Kaga dishes such as duck jibu-ni.

anecdotes.text: The shop did not serve oden at first — it originally had two entrances and operated as both a popular diner and a Western restaurant; the founder added oden only after customers asked for it.
Amatsubo belongs to the Kanazawa Oden 50-Year Veterans Association, whose two rules are that oden ingredients use local Kanazawa products and that the oden is available year-round.
A longtime community fixture beloved by Kanazawa University alumni and Ishikawa prefectural staff, the shop has run for over 50 years.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Oden broth is soy-sauce and dashi based and shared across all simmered items; fried items (tempura, satsuma-age, chikuwa) and wheat-gluten kuruma-fu are core menu items, so wheat contact is pervasive.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese soy sauce (contains wheat) is central to the oden broth and seafood-bowl seasoning; no wheat-free tamari indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Even vegetable oden items (daikon, konnyaku) are simmered in a fish/bonito-based dashi shared with seafood and meat items.

cultural_comfort.note: A bustling, casual market eatery where pointing at the picture menu and a friendly "konnichiwa" will carry you through; staff are used to tourists but English is limited, so a few basic Japanese words help.

places.name: グリルオーツカ (Grill Otsuka)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The kitchen is built around breaded/fried items (marlin and shrimp fries), omelette-over-rice with ketchup, hamburg steak, cream croquettes and flour-based cream/white sauces — all wheat-heavy. No gluten-free menu, no dedicated fryer, and shared frying oil makes this unsafe for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The menu centers on seafood (fried marlin/swordfish, shrimp), eggs (the signature dish uses about three eggs per plate), meat (hamburg, pork steak) and dairy-based cream sauces and soups. No vegan options are documented.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-15:20 (L.O. 14:45), 17:00-20:30 (L.O. 19:50). Closed Wednesday (open if Wed is a holiday, with Thursday as substitute holiday). One additional temporary closure per month.
places.neighborhood: 片町 (Katamachi)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A brick-fronted Western-style diner (yoshoku) founded in 1957 by Taduzou Otsuka. It is now run by the third generation, Masaki Otsuka, who took over from his father and second-generation owner Shoji Otsuka. The restaurant is widely regarded as the most famous home of Kanazawa's l

chef_bio.background: A brick-fronted Western-style diner (yoshoku) founded in 1957 by Taduzou Otsuka. It is now run by the third generation, Masaki Otsuka, who took over from his father and second-generation owner Shoji Otsuka. The restaurant is widely regarded as the most famous home of Kanazawa's local specialty, Hunton Rice.
chef_bio.philosophy: Second-generation owner Shoji Otsuka held that 'diligently making delicious food matters most,' prioritizing quality and consistency over flash.
chef_bio.specialty: Hunton Rice — a plate of plain ketchup rice topped with silky, partly soft-set egg, two fried marlin (swordfish) cutlets and two fried shrimp, lavished with house tartar sauce and ketchup. The restaurant also has a devoted following for its beef-consomme-and-milk cream soup, pork steak on an iron plate, cream croquettes and omelette hayashi rice.

anecdotes.text: Hunton Rice was born here in the 1960s when the head chef under second-generation owner Shoji recreated a dish he remembered from his Tokyo apprenticeship; the name blends 'Han' (Hungarian) and 'ton' (French for tuna). The original choice to spread the egg over the rice rather than wrap it was unconventional at the time and drew attention.
Today roughly 70-80% of customers order the Hunton Rice, and long queues regularly form outside the shop.
A lesser-known house favorite is the velvety cream soup, built on a sauce veloute base with beef consomme and milk that turns subtly sweet as you eat.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core dishes are breaded and deep-fried (marlin and shrimp fries, cream croquettes) in shared oil, with flour-based cream and white sauces throughout the menu — high gluten cross-contamination risk.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Kitchen works extensively with eggs, seafood, meat and dairy; the signature dish alone uses about three eggs per plate, so vegan cross-contact is unavoidable.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual, no-reservation family-run diner where pointing at the famous Hunton Rice and a few basic Japanese greetings will carry you through; bring cash and be ready to queue.

places.name: 刺身屋 (Sashimiya)
places.gf_detail: This is a seafood and Kaga-cuisine izakaya built around sashimi, seafood rice bowls, soy-sauce-based dishes, jibuni (which uses wheat-thickened sauce and soy), and tempura. Soy sauce, dashi, and wheat are pervasive, and no gluten-free menu or allergen handling is documented. Not safe for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: A seafood-focused restaurant whose entire identity is fresh fish, sashimi, seafood bowls, and Noto beef. No vegan or vegetarian menu is documented, and dashi/fish stock underlies most cooked dishes. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
places.hours_raw: Weekdays (Mon-Fri): 10:30-21:00 (L.O. 20:30). Saturdays, Sundays & holidays: 09:30-21:00 (L.O. 20:30). Closed Jan 1-4.
places.neighborhood: Omicho Market (Aokusamachi)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Sashimiya sits right inside Kanazawa's Omicho Market, the city's bustling fish market, and is run by a former fish merchant who turned decades of handling the day's catch into a kitchen. The connection to the surrounding stalls is direct: the seafood comes from fishmongers within

chef_bio.background: Sashimiya sits right inside Kanazawa's Omicho Market, the city's bustling fish market, and is run by a former fish merchant who turned decades of handling the day's catch into a kitchen. The connection to the surrounding stalls is direct: the seafood comes from fishmongers within the same market, so the sashimi reaches the plate while it is still firm and glossy.
chef_bio.philosophy: The owner's stated aim is to draw out the natural umami of each ingredient through expert preparation rather than heavy seasoning, leaning on the freshness that comes from buying within the market.
chef_bio.specialty: The kitchen is known for generously piled seafood rice bowls (kaisen-don) heaped with tuna, crab, yellowtail, nodoguro/black-throat seaperch, salmon roe, and sea urchin, alongside sashimi platters, nodoguro prepared several ways, tempura of Kaga vegetables, and the local Ishikawa specialty jibuni.

anecdotes.text: The owner is a former fishmonger who applies skills honed over many years in the fish trade to select and cut the day's catch.
Its signature seafood bowl is loaded with luxurious toppings like tuna, crab, yellowtail, nodoguro, salmon roe, and sea urchin, all sourced from fishmongers inside Omicho Market.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Menu centers on sashimi, seafood bowls, tempura, and jibuni, all of which rely on soy sauce and dashi; tempura adds wheat batter. No gluten-free options are documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Entirely seafood- and meat-driven (sashimi, seafood bowls, Noto beef), with dashi-based cooked dishes; no vegan or vegetarian items are documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A busy, welcoming market izakaya used to tourists and accepting cards and QR payments, so a few words of Japanese and pointing at the seafood bowls will get you a great meal comfortably.

places.name: チャルダ (Cialda)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional Showa-era kissaten whose signature dishes are wheat-based: napolitan pasta, pizza toast, ham pilaf, and toast. There is no gluten-free menu and no indication of allergen accommodation. Not safe for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: A classic coffee shop serving meat- and dairy-based fare (ham pilaf, pizza toast, napolitan). Beverages include coffee and tea typically taken with milk. No documented vegan options.
places.hours_raw: 10:00-18:30, open daily
places.neighborhood: 下堤町 (Shimotsutsumicho), near Omicho Market / Hokutetsu Kanaz
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Cialda is a long-running retro coffee shop tucked away just steps from Omicho Market in central Kanazawa. Reported in 2024 to be 61 years old, it dates to roughly the early 1960s. Its sign is almost hidden behind a curtain of potted plants, giving it a hidden-away, easy-to-miss c

chef_bio.background: Cialda is a long-running retro coffee shop tucked away just steps from Omicho Market in central Kanazawa. Reported in 2024 to be 61 years old, it dates to roughly the early 1960s. Its sign is almost hidden behind a curtain of potted plants, giving it a hidden-away, easy-to-miss charm even for locals who pass nearby.
chef_bio.specialty: Napolitan pasta is the standout dish (served with Tabasco and grated cheese on the side), alongside other kissaten classics like ham pilaf and pizza toast, plus coffee and tea served in delicate floral-patterned cups.

anecdotes.text: The deep interior blends Showa retro with Western vintage: arches and tiles, an amber-toned glow, brick walls in the back room with a striking double-arched wall, shelves of vintage collectibles (Hills Coffee cans, antique pitchers, dog figurines), and even an actual telephone booth inside.

A blogger noted the shop is so screened by abundant potted plants that its signage is nearly invisible from a distance, and that the master, like many old kissaten owners, joked that the cups and saucers gradually stop matching in number over the years.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Signature menu is wheat-heavy (napolitan pasta, pizza toast, ham pilaf, toast); shared kitchen with no documented allergen handling.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes are built on ham, cheese, butter and dairy; no documented dairy-free or plant-based preparation.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, time-capsule local kissaten run in Showa style where little or no English is likely spoken and the menu is in Japanese, so a foreign student will be most comfortable with a guide or a translation app and a respectful, quiet approach. Note: smoking is permitted inside.

places.name: ピノ (Pino)
places.gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. The menu centers on wheat-based items — waffles, spaghetti, doria, curry roux and breaded/sauced Western dishes — and there is no celiac protocol or gluten-free labeling documented. Even the omurice is typically served in a wheat-flour-thickened sauce. Not suitable for strict celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: A classic yoshoku kissaten built around egg-heavy omurice, cream sauces (mentaiko cream, butter chicken curry, béchamel), parfaits and dairy-topped waffles. No vegan menu and no documented plant-based options; the core dishes rely on eggs, butter, cream and dairy.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-20:30 (L.O. 20:00). Closed Wednesdays per cafe sources; one listing also notes Tuesday closure, so confirm before a Tuesday visit. Popular with younger diners — 30+ minute waits are possible.
places.neighborhood: Katamachi (near the Korinbo / Katamachi 1-chome intersection)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Pino is a Showa-era kissaten just a minute from the Katamachi 1-chome intersection in central Kanazawa, open for over 40 years with a look that has barely changed since it debuted — stained-glass windows, brick walls and red sofas all original to the space, which was once a UCC c

chef_bio.background: Pino is a Showa-era kissaten just a minute from the Katamachi 1-chome intersection in central Kanazawa, open for over 40 years with a look that has barely changed since it debuted — stained-glass windows, brick walls and red sofas all original to the space, which was once a UCC coffee franchise location. It is run day to day by a husband-and-wife team.
chef_bio.philosophy: All sauces and dressings are made in-house, and coffee is brewed individually by siphon rather than batch-prepared, keeping the hand-made, unhurried character of a traditional kissaten.
chef_bio.specialty: Western-style comfort food anchored by a signature omurice served under double sauce (tomato and béchamel), with variations like mentaiko cream and butter-chicken-curry omurice, plus crispy waffles available in around 16-20 topping combinations and roughly 11 styles of parfait. The popular 'Yokubari Set' pairs a main with salad, a drink and a mini parfait or waffle.

anecdotes.text: The cafe draws multiple generations — some regulars first came as high schoolers and now bring their grandchildren — and on busy days the owner couple are said to walk roughly 10km inside the shop keeping up with the crowd.
Its enduring Showa-retro atmosphere has made it popular again with younger diners and tourists, with waits of 30 minutes or more possible at peak times.

positives.text: Sauces and dressings are all made in-house, so staff can describe what goes into a dish if you ask, though no formal allergen handling is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A welcoming, busy old-school Showa-era kissaten where ordering is point-and-eat simple, but staff likely speak little English, so a few basic Japanese phrases or pointing at the picture menu will make the visit smooth.

places.name: 割烹 むら井 (Kappo Murai)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional Kaga-cuisine kappo built around tempura, soy-seasoned simmered dishes (jibuni), grilled fish and dashi-based cooking. Wheat (tempura batter, soy sauce) and dashi are central to the menu, and there is no documented gluten-free handling or dedicated equipment. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: A seafood-focused kappo specializing in nodoguro (blackthroat seaperch), sashimi, crab, oysters and the soy/dashi-stewed Kaga dish jibuni. Dishes rely on fish, dashi and meat throughout, with no documented vegan menu or options.
places.hours_raw: 16:30-22:00 (dinner only per official site and Tabelog). One tourism listing also notes a 11:30-14:00 lunch service; dinner-only confirmed by official site.
places.neighborhood: Korinbo (Seseragi-dori)
places.notes: A long-established kappo on Korinbo's Seseragi-dori, an area renowned as one of Kanazawa's culinary streets and within easy reach of the historic samurai district. The restaurant has been serving for several decades (described as a 40- to 50-year establishment), spread over three

chef_bio.background: A long-established kappo on Korinbo's Seseragi-dori, an area renowned as one of Kanazawa's culinary streets and within easy reach of the historic samurai district. The restaurant has been serving for several decades (described as a 40- to 50-year establishment), spread over three floors with a ground-floor counter and upstairs private tatami rooms suited to small groups and larger parties.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen's stated approach values seasonality, carefully chosen local ingredients, and thoughtful presentation, putting heart and technique into each dish; the house motto emphasizes care for the guest and sincerity in cooking ('after, no regrets').
chef_bio.specialty: Kaga cuisine and seasonal seafood: salt-grilled nodoguro (blackthroat seaperch), the soy-and-dashi simmered Kaga specialty jibuni, assorted sashimi, the house 'Murai special' sweet-shrimp tempura, seasonal natural Noto oysters, crab and pufferfish, and Kaga-vegetable tempura. Signature courses include Kaga kaiseki and a nodoguro course.

anecdotes.text: Located on Seseragi-dori, a celebrated dining street in central Kanazawa, the place is known as a discreet spot favored by visiting celebrities.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu centers on tempura (wheat batter), soy-seasoned jibuni, and dashi-based dishes; no gluten-free handling is documented, so cross-contamination should be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Kaga dishes such as jibuni and accompanying sauces use Japanese soy sauce, which typically contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Cooking is built around fish, shellfish, meat and dashi; no vegan preparation or separation is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a traditional Japanese kappo where some Japanese language and familiarity with multi-course Kaga dining will help; reservations by phone and a basic grasp of the menu make for a smoother visit.

places.name: 中谷とうふ (Nakatani Tofu)
places.gf_detail: The shop's signature items are soy-based: soy milk soft serve and tofu ice cream, plus tofu, fried tofu (age) and okara. Tofu and soy milk are naturally wheat-free, so the core soy desserts are likely suitable for gluten-avoiders. However, this is a traditional neighborhood tofu shop with no documented allergen handling or celiac protocol, fried items (age) share frying, and cones/toppings are not confirmed gluten-free. A celiac should ask and stick to the plain soy soft serve or tofu rather than fried products.
places.vegan_detail: A handmade tofu shop whose draw for plant-based eaters is its 豆乳ソフトクリーム (soy milk soft serve) and とうふアイスクリーム (tofu ice cream), both made from soybeans rather than dairy, along with fresh tofu, fried tofu (age) and okara to take away. The soft serve and tofu products are plant-based by nature, though the shop is a tofu maker, not a self-declared vegan business, so individual recipes (e.g. any added ingredients in flavored items) are not certified. The soy milk soft serve is seasonal, typically sold April through October.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Sat 10:00-19:00 / Sun soy-milk soft serve only
places.neighborhood: Nishi Chaya District (Nomachi)
places.cuisine: Handmade tofu shop / soy-milk soft serve / tofu ice cream
places.notes: A small, long-established handmade tofu shop tucked into the historic Nishi Chaya geisha district near Nomachi. It keeps the unassuming feel of an old neighborhood tofu maker, selling fresh tofu, fried tofu (age), okara and other soy goods, with an elderly proprietor who minds th

roles: 手造り豆腐店（handmade tofu shop）
chef_bio.background: A small, long-established handmade tofu shop tucked into the historic Nishi Chaya geisha district near Nomachi. It keeps the unassuming feel of an old neighborhood tofu maker, selling fresh tofu, fried tofu (age), okara and other soy goods, with an elderly proprietor who minds the shop.
chef_bio.philosophy: Carefully selected soybeans and good water turned into honest, traditional tofu — the soy desserts let visitors taste that tofu character in a cone.
chef_bio.specialty: 豆乳ソフトクリーム (soy milk soft serve), a rich, lightly sweetened cone made from the shop's own soybeans, and とうふアイスクリーム (tofu ice cream), a light frozen dessert that locals say even pairs with a drizzle of soy sauce. Alongside the desserts the shop sells handmade tofu, age and okara.

anecdotes.text: Visitors describe the soy milk soft serve as having a concentrated soybean flavor with restrained sweetness and a smooth, almost tofu-like texture rather than ordinary dairy ice cream.

One reviewer noted the elderly proprietor sits outside and comes when customers ring a bell, underlining how intimate and family-run the shop is, and that the soft serve is only sold in the warmer months.
The shop's tofu ice cream is described as light in flavor and said to go well with a touch of soy sauce.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The tofu ice cream is sometimes eaten with a touch of soy sauce; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, so gluten-avoiders should skip the soy sauce option.

positives.text: The signature items — soy milk soft serve and tofu ice cream — are made from soybeans rather than dairy, making the core offerings plant-based.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a tiny old neighborhood tofu shop run by an elderly proprietor with essentially no English, so point at the soft serve, have cash ready, and a few words of Japanese will help.

places.name: 落雁諸江屋 西茶屋菓寮 味和以 (Rakugan Moroeya Nishichaya Karyo Ajiwai)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. Rakugan is traditionally pressed rice-flour/wasanbon sugar candy and is often wheat-free, and zenzai (red bean soup) is rice-and-bean based, but namagashi (上生菓子) and seasonal items can contain wheat, and no allergen information or cross-contact controls are published. Celiac diners must ask about each specific item.
places.vegan_detail: Traditional Kaga wagashi here (rakugan, zenzai, namagashi) are typically plant-based, made from rice, sugar (wasanbon/wasanbon-tou), and adzuki beans without dairy or egg, so vegan-suitable options likely exist. However, the shop publishes no vegan labeling and kakigori syrups/toppings and some namagashi may vary, so confirm ingredients per item before ordering.
places.hours_raw: 10:00-17:00 (L.O. 16:30); closed Tuesdays (open on public holidays, with Wednesday taken as substitute closure)
places.neighborhood: Nishi Chaya District (Nomachi)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Rakugan Moroeya is a Kanazawa wagashi house founded in 1849 (late Edo period), describing itself as having walked the same path as the city for over 170 years. In 2012 it opened the Nishichaya Karyo cafe, named Ajiwai, in the historic Nishi Chaya geisha district of Nomachi, combi

chef_bio.background: Rakugan Moroeya is a Kanazawa wagashi house founded in 1849 (late Edo period), describing itself as having walked the same path as the city for over 170 years. In 2012 it opened the Nishichaya Karyo cafe, named Ajiwai, in the historic Nishi Chaya geisha district of Nomachi, combining a retail shop with a bright, welcoming tea space where travelers can shop and rest.
chef_bio.philosophy: The house aims to honor the tradition of Kaga meika (famous Kaga sweets) and traditional manufacturing methods while continuing to refine flavors and products beloved in contemporary times.
chef_bio.specialty: Rakugan (pressed wasanbon-sugar candy) is the house specialty, alongside seasonal Japanese sweets, zenzai (sweet red-bean soup), kakigori (shaved ice) served in summer (June-September), and jonamagashi (premium fresh sweets). Signature confections include Hojo-gashi, Kaga Hosho, and Hana-usagi.

anecdotes.text: Founded in 1849 near the end of the Edo period, Moroeya says it has shared Kanazawa's path for more than 170 years.
The Nishichaya Karyo cafe opened in 2012 as a combined shop and tea house in the Nishi Chaya district, designed as a bright place to shop and take a break while traveling.

positives.text: Traditional menu of rakugan, zenzai, kakigori and namagashi is largely rice-, bean- and sugar-based, which often suits plant-based diners, though no allergen labeling is published.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed traditional sweets cafe in a historic geisha district where a smile and a little Japanese go a long way; menus are seasonal and mostly in Japanese, so pointing and basic phrases help.

places.name: 地場料理と酒肴 季節会席 金澤 斉や (Jiba Ryori to Shuko Kisetsu Kaiseki Kanazawa)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature Kaga jibuni is made with wheat-flour-coated meat and Kanazawa fu (sudare-fu / nama-fu, which are wheat gluten), and the kaiseki menu relies on wheat-based soy sauce and dashi throughout. No gluten-free or allergy accommodations are mentioned anywhere. Celiac travelers should treat this as unsafe.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan or vegetarian-friendly. This is a seasonal Kaga kaiseki house centered on fish, eel, duck/chicken/pork, and seafood, with dashi-based broths throughout. No vegan or vegetarian menu is offered.
places.hours_raw: Official site: Lunch 11:00-15:00 (last seating 13:00, reservation-only, larger groups), Dinner 18:00-22:00. Tabelog: 12:00-14:00 and 18:00-23:00 (L.O. 21:30). Reservation-only operation. Irregular days off.
places.neighborhood: Kanazawa Stn east exit / Konohanamachi (near Hokutetsu Kanazawa Stn)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Kanazawa Saiya is an owner-run seasonal kaiseki restaurant a short walk from the east side of Kanazawa Station, working with fresh local fish, Kaga vegetables, and seasonal ingredients sourced from around Japan, paired with local sake.

chef_bio.background: Kanazawa Saiya is an owner-run seasonal kaiseki restaurant a short walk from the east side of Kanazawa Station, working with fresh local fish, Kaga vegetables, and seasonal ingredients sourced from around Japan, paired with local sake.
chef_bio.philosophy: The owner emphasizes uncompromising, meticulous preparation paired with seasonal ingredients to create hospitality (妥協のない仕込みと旬の食材が織り成すおもてなし).
chef_bio.specialty: Its signature is Kaga jibuni served as a small individual hot pot (加賀治部煮小鍋仕立て). While the classic Kanazawa dish uses wheat-flour-coated duck or chicken with sudare-fu and nama-fu (wheat gluten) and root vegetables, this house makes its version with slow-braised pork belly alongside the Kaga fu and root vegetables. Other seasonal dishes include white-soy-glazed eel, flounder tempura, and seasonal rice cooked in clay pots.

anecdotes.text: The kaiseki is offered at the more approachable prices typical of an individually owned restaurant, drawing both Kanazawa locals and visitors who return as regulars.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The signature jibuni uses wheat-flour-coated meat and Kanazawa fu (sudare-fu and nama-fu, which are wheat gluten), so wheat is central to the kitchen and shared prep is likely.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Seasoning is built on traditional Japanese soy sauce and dashi (including a white-soy eel glaze), which contain wheat; no wheat-free soy alternative is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes are dashi-, fish-, and meat-based throughout, so even vegetable items are not prepared vegan.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a reservation-only owner-run kaiseki house where some Japanese ability or a Japanese-speaking helper to book and communicate dietary needs will make the visit much smoother for a foreign student.

places.name: そば処WAY (Sobadokoro WAY)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The house soba is nihachi (二八), made with 20% wheat flour blended into the buckwheat, and is served with wheat-based soba dashi/tsuyu. There is no gluten-free option and no indication of celiac accommodation. Not safe for those with celiac disease or wheat allergy.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The fixed "osoba-zen" set is built around fish-dashi-based soba broth and seasonal small dishes; the owner is a fisherman who serves his own-caught sashimi when available, and add-on options include herring (nishin) and nameko soba. No documented vegan set.
places.hours_raw: Sun & Mon 11:00-14:00 (open Sun and Mon only; check Instagram for latest info)
places.neighborhood: Teramachi (corner of the Teramachi 1-chome intersection)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Sobadokoro WAY opened in autumn 2024 on the corner of the Teramachi 1-chome intersection, inside a renovated building that previously housed a hair salon and a remake shop. The interior was designed by the local architecture and design studio SWAY DESIGN, giving the small space a

chef_bio.background: Sobadokoro WAY opened in autumn 2024 on the corner of the Teramachi 1-chome intersection, inside a renovated building that previously housed a hair salon and a remake shop. The interior was designed by the local architecture and design studio SWAY DESIGN, giving the small space a modern, stylish feel complete with a vacuum-tube amplifier audio system.
chef_bio.philosophy: The owner wants guests to eat things freshly made with real care and time ('手間ひまかけた出来たてを食べて欲しい').
chef_bio.specialty: The signature is the 'osoba-zen' (おそば膳), a 1,500 yen set built around freshly hand-cut nihachi soba made from Nagano-grown buckwheat flour, served with a seasonal small dish and rice (and, per one account, a post-meal coffee and small dessert). The soba can be swapped for grated-radish oroshi soba, warm nameko soba, or nishin (herring) soba for an extra 300 yen.

anecdotes.text: Owner Fujita is a soba craftsman with more than 50 years of experience, hand-cutting each batch of nihachi soba himself.
He also runs a main shop in Komatsu and is a fisherman who serves his own freshly caught sashimi when the previous day's catch allows.

gf_cross_contamination.text: House soba is nihachi (二八), made with 20% wheat flour, so the noodles themselves contain gluten and there is no gluten-free option.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soba is served with a traditional dashi/tsuyu, which in soba shops is wheat-soy-sauce based; no gluten-free tsuyu is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The set centers on fish-dashi broth and seafood (sashimi, herring), so vegan separation is not offered.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny 7-seat counter run by a veteran soba craftsman with limited Sunday/Monday-only hours, so a little Japanese and patience help; come ready to order the set course and enjoy the quiet, music-filled space.

places.name: 喫茶 ノバール (Kissa Nobar)
places.gf_detail: A retro coffee shop whose signature items are hotcakes, pudding parfaits and Napolitan-style dishes — all wheat-based or served with wheat sides. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented, and this is not a celiac-safe venue.
places.vegan_detail: The menu centers on dairy-and-egg desserts (homemade pudding, parfaits with whipped cream, hotcakes) and coffee. No vegan options are documented.
places.neighborhood: Nomachi (near Arimatsu intersection, south of Nishi Chaya District)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A nostalgic Showa-era pure coffee house that has been welcoming locals for more than 40 years. The cafe is run as a husband-and-wife operation: the master handcrafts the desserts one by one while his wife decorates the plates with carefully cut fruit and whipped cream. The interi

roles: master (owner-chef, makes the puddings and desserts)
mama (owner's wife, plates and decorates the dishes)
chef_bio.origin: Kanazawa, near the Arimatsu intersection just south of the Nishi Chaya geisha district
chef_bio.background: A nostalgic Showa-era pure coffee house that has been welcoming locals for more than 40 years. The cafe is run as a husband-and-wife operation: the master handcrafts the desserts one by one while his wife decorates the plates with carefully cut fruit and whipped cream. The interior has barely changed since it opened, with a stained-glass wall, a chandelier, wine-red sofas, brickwork and framed paintings preserving a gorgeous, slightly bubble-era Western look.
chef_bio.philosophy: Seating and sight lines are arranged so guests never feel watched by other customers, making it an unusually relaxing place to linger over coffee.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature dish is the homemade pudding (自家製プリン, around 500 yen), a firm, not-too-sweet custard topped with decoratively cut fruit and cream; it also appears as a pudding parfait. Other beloved items include Ogura hotcakes (小倉ホットケーキ) and coffee.

anecdotes.text: The cafe is locally famous because actress Yoh Yoshida (吉田羊) worked here as a part-time staff member before her acting career.

Soft light filtering through the stained glass makes the menus and desserts a popular photo subject, and the master-and-mama team handcraft each pudding with restrained sweetness so it pairs well with coffee or tea.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional Showa-era kissaten run by an older couple with no English menu and cash-only payment, so a foreign student would be most comfortable bringing a Japanese-speaking friend or a translation app.

places.name: 鮨 木場谷 (Sushi Kibatani)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional Edomae sushi counter and is not safe for celiacs. Sushi is built on vinegared rice and seafood brushed with nikiri/tsume sauces and served with soy sauce, all of which are wheat-based. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented. Travelers with celiac disease should not rely on this restaurant.
places.vegan_detail: A seafood-focused omakase sushi restaurant centered entirely on fish sourced daily from Himi and Shinminato ports (yellowtail, abalone, uni, kohada, crab). There is no vegan or vegetarian menu and the entire concept is animal-based. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
places.hours_raw: 17:00-21:00 (seatings reported around 17:00 and 20:30); closed Sundays plus irregular holidays. Completely reservation-based.
places.neighborhood: Hikoso-machi (near Omicho Market / Hokutetsu Kanazawa Stn)
places.notes: After starting out as a salaryman, Kibatani found his calling working at a fish shop in Tokyo's Tsukiji market, which sparked a passion for seafood. He trained in Edomae technique at respected sushi houses including Yoshiura in Funabashi and the celebrated Sushi Aoki in Ginza, th

chef_bio.origin: Born 1973 in Toyama Prefecture; his family runs a fresh-fish business in Oyabe, giving him fish-sourcing in his blood.
chef_bio.background: After starting out as a salaryman, Kibatani found his calling working at a fish shop in Tokyo's Tsukiji market, which sparked a passion for seafood. He trained in Edomae technique at respected sushi houses including Yoshiura in Funabashi and the celebrated Sushi Aoki in Ginza, then spent years as a traveling (catering) sushi chef before opening his own counter, Sushi Kibatani, in Kanazawa's Hikosan-cho district in 2016. The restaurant has since earned a Michelin one-star and become one of Kanazawa's hardest tables to book.
chef_bio.philosophy: Kibatani speaks with gratitude about his hard years as a traveling chef and treats running his own place as a privilege, devoting himself to letting guests experience the full richness of the Hokuriku sea. He keeps learning even after success - in summer 2022 he closed for about three months to apprentice in uni handling in Hokkaido.
chef_bio.specialty: Edomae sushi built on premium Hokuriku fish landed daily at Himi and Shinminato ports - yellowtail, abalone, horse mackerel, uni and crab. His signature is kohada, cured with meticulous Edomae technique adjusted to the weather and each individual fish. Dashi is shaved fresh from katsuobushi at the counter, and he uses binchotan charcoal for precise grilling; one memorable course pairs Uozu crab with a rich, almost French-style uni sauce over rice.

anecdotes.text: Despite running one of Kanazawa's most sought-after sushi counters, Kibatani took a roughly three-month hiatus in summer 2022 to train in uni preparation in Hokkaido.
A signature course pairs Uozu crab with a rich uni sauce over rice, described as enveloping the crab's delicate sweetness in a French-style richness.
His signature kohada is cured with Edomae technique that he adjusts to the day's weather and the character of each fish.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Edomae sushi is built on vinegared rice, soy-based nikiri/tsume sauces, and soy sauce, so gluten is pervasive throughout the meal; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese soy sauce (wheat-containing) and brushed nikiri/tsume sauces are integral to the sushi served here.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire omakase is seafood-based with fish-derived dashi (freshly shaved katsuobushi at the counter); there are no vegan dishes.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a high-end, completely reservation-only omakase sushi counter where the chef serves you directly, so a foreign student should expect a formal Japanese-only dining experience and a substantial budget (roughly 20,000 yen and up).

places.name: Natural Grocery Store midoriya（ミドリヤ） (Natural Grocery Store midoriya)
places.gf_detail: ⚠ Safety review 2026-07-02: downgraded high→options. BOTH a grocery (sealed GF goods, low CC) AND a café with an OPEN kitchen that bakes WHEAT scones alongside rice-flour items, zero documented cross-contact controls; sources even contradict on whether scones are wheat. Sealed goods are fine, prepared café food is not celiac-safe. — Prior note: Most baked goods are made with rice flour and are gluten-free; the owner makes them daily without wheat. Scones are the documented exception (not gluten-free), so confirm per item. Not a certified celiac-dedicated kitchen, but rice-flour baking is the core focus, so confidence is high for the muffins/pound cakes/florentines.
places.vegan_detail: The cafe's handmade baked goods (muffins, scones, pound cakes, florentines) and drinks are entirely plant-based, made without eggs, butter or dairy. The attached shop also stocks vegan-certified products. Sweets and house-made items are fully vegan.
places.hours_raw: 10:00-17:00. Closed Tue and irregular days off.
places.neighborhood: 安江町・横安江町（金澤表参道）商店街 — about 3 min south of Omichō Market (近江町
places.notes: midoriya is run by Takahashi Tomoko, a former high-school teacher who, after becoming a mother, reconsidered the importance of what we eat and decided to open a natural-foods shop. It first opened in November 2020 on the second floor of her family's longstanding boutique. When th

chef_bio.background: midoriya is run by Takahashi Tomoko, a former high-school teacher who, after becoming a mother, reconsidered the importance of what we eat and decided to open a natural-foods shop. It first opened in November 2020 on the second floor of her family's longstanding boutique. When the boutique closed, she moved the shop down to the ground floor and reopened in May 2023 with a larger sales floor and a new cafe space.
chef_bio.philosophy: She personally curates every product from a parent's point of view, choosing additive-free, organic and vegan-certified items that are genuinely good for body, mind and the environment. The cafe extends that ethic with reusable-tumbler discounts and sustainable sourcing.
chef_bio.specialty: House-made vegan, rice-flour (gluten-free) baked goods — muffins, pound cakes, scones and florentines — plus herbal teas, Japanese black tea and coffee.

anecdotes.text: The cafe occupies the family's former boutique building (described as roughly 90 years old), and the renovation kept the antique furniture and vintage fabric upholstery from the boutique era, giving the eat-in space a retro charm.
A signature item is a rice-flour muffin made with pesticide-free organic lemon and a vegan custard, served alongside 100%-natural herbal tea.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Baked goods are made fresh in an open kitchen; most are rice-flour gluten-free, but scones are not gluten-free, so wheat is present in the same kitchen. No celiac-grade cross-contamination protocol is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All house-made baked goods and drinks are stated to contain no animal-derived ingredients (no eggs, butter or dairy), so vegan cross-contact risk in the cafe items is low.

positives.text: Sweets are made with rice flour and are vegan, with gluten-free options clearly the focus of the menu.
Egg-free and dairy-free options are available, with explicit vegan and gluten-free framing throughout the menu.

cultural_comfort.note: A warm, small solo-run natural-foods cafe where a few words of Japanese and pointing at the rice-flour sweets will get you a friendly welcome; English service is not guaranteed.

gf_review.why: BOTH a grocery (sealed GF goods, low CC) AND a café with an OPEN kitchen that bakes WHEAT scones alongside rice-flour items, zero documented cross-contact controls; sources even contradict on whether scones are wheat. Sealed goods are fine, prepared café food is not celiac-safe.
red_flags: Wheat scones baked in shared open kitchen — airborne-flour hazard
Sources contradict on which items contain wheat
Only celiac FMGF reviewer 'didn't ask about cross-contact'
gf_review.plain_rice: N/A — bakery/café; sells raw rice as grocery only.

places.name: 主計町お料理 いち凛 (Kazuemachi Oryori Ichirin)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering documented. This is a traditional Japanese seasonal/kaiseki house built around dashi and soy sauce, with courses centered on seafood and Kaga vegetables. Soy sauce (typically wheat-based) and wheat-containing seasonings are integral to the cooking, so it is not celiac-safe. No dedicated fryer or allergen protocol is published; a celiac traveler would need to inquire directly in advance.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian menu is documented. The kitchen is "particular about fish" and courses are built on seasonal seafood plus fish/kombu dashi, so standard courses are not vegan or vegetarian. The restaurant features Kaga vegetables and local sake, so a plant-forward request might be accommodated for a reserved course, but this is not confirmed and must be arranged with the restaurant ahead of time.
places.hours_raw: [Tue] 17:00-22:30 (dinner only); [Wed-Fri] 11:30-13:30, 17:00-22:30; [Sat/Sun/holidays] 11:00-14:30, 17:00-22:30; [Mon] closed (open if a public holiday).
places.neighborhood: Kazuemachi (主計町茶屋街), Asano River side
places.notes: Ichirin occupies a renovated Kanazawa machiya on the Asano River in the historic Kazuemachi tea district, a townhouse that was the later-life residence of the American printmaker Clifton Karhu. The interior still displays pieces from his collection, so diners eat surrounded by hi

roles: part of the local MOMIJI GROUP
chef_bio.background: Ichirin occupies a renovated Kanazawa machiya on the Asano River in the historic Kazuemachi tea district, a townhouse that was the later-life residence of the American printmaker Clifton Karhu. The interior still displays pieces from his collection, so diners eat surrounded by his artwork while taking in the Edo-period atmosphere of the riverside teahouse quarter.
chef_bio.philosophy: An emphasis on Kanazawa's seasonal local ingredients and regional sake, served in a setting that preserves the heritage of a Kazuemachi townhouse.
chef_bio.specialty: Seasonal multi-course Japanese cuisine built on local seafood and Kaga vegetables, paired with local Ishikawa sake; the kitchen is noted for being particular about its fish. Lunch sets such as the Hanakago box make the cuisine approachable at midday.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant is set in the former home of American woodblock printmaker Clifton Karhu, who spent his final years in this Kazuemachi townhouse; his art is shown throughout the dining space, including a distinctive blue-plaster-walled Japanese room.
It is the riverside Kanazawa restaurant operated by the local MOMIJI GROUP, which also runs other Japanese and Western dining venues in the city, opened in this Kazuemachi location.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Traditional Japanese kaiseki-style kitchen relying on soy sauce and dashi-based seasonings; no gluten-free protocol or dedicated equipment is documented, so cross-contamination with wheat should be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Cuisine is built around standard Japanese seasonings including soy sauce, which is typically brewed with wheat; no wheat-free soy sauce is advertised.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Courses center on seafood and almost certainly use fish/kombu dashi; no separate vegan preparation line is documented, so animal-derived stocks should be assumed throughout.

positives.text: Reservations are required and arranged by phone or an online form, giving diners a clear channel to communicate dietary restrictions in advance.

cultural_comfort.note: A polished course-style Japanese restaurant where a reservation and a few words of Japanese (or a translation app) go a long way; staff are used to guests but the experience is formal, so book ahead and flag any dietary needs when you reserve.

places.name: 割烹 魚常 (Kappo Uotsune)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. This is a Kaga kappo built around dashi, soy sauce and seasonal fish. The signature jibuni is served with sudare-fu (wheat gluten) and a soy-and-mirin broth, and dishes use wheat-based soy sauce throughout. No dedicated gluten-free preparation. The kitchen does say it accepts allergy/dietary inquiries by direct phone, so a celiac diner would need to call ahead and discuss in detail — but it should not be considered celiac-safe.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The menu is centered on fish (nodoguro, sashimi) and duck (jibuni), and nearly everything is built on bonito/fish dashi. Vegetables are seasonal but prepared with animal-based broths and soy seasonings. The kitchen accepts allergy/dietary inquiries by phone, but there is no documented vegan offering.
places.hours_raw: 17:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:30); some listings give 17:00-23:00 (L.O. 22:00). Closed Sundays and holidays (open for groups of 10+ by reservation).
places.neighborhood: Namikimachi, by the Asano River near Higashi Chaya / Kazuema
places.cuisine: Family-run kappo serving Kaga local cuisine
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Uotsune is a family-run kappo set in a Taisho-era building on the embankment of the Asano River, just across from the Higashi Chaya District and near the Ume-no-hashi and Asanogawa-ohashi bridges. The weathered old structure is part of the draw: second-floor tatami rooms look out

chef_bio.background: Uotsune is a family-run kappo set in a Taisho-era building on the embankment of the Asano River, just across from the Higashi Chaya District and near the Ume-no-hashi and Asanogawa-ohashi bridges. The weathered old structure is part of the draw: second-floor tatami rooms look out over the river, framing cherry blossoms in spring and colored leaves in autumn.
chef_bio.philosophy: The house has long run on a simple motto carried down from the previous owner: 'oishikute yasui' — delicious and affordable — pairing seasonal Kaga ingredients with the changing views of the Asano River.
chef_bio.specialty: Kaga local cuisine led by duck jibuni — duck simmered with sudare-fu wheat gluten and seasonal vegetables in a soy-and-mirin broth — alongside salt-grilled nodoguro (blackthroat seaperch), sashimi, and seasonal fish and vegetables.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant sits in a building that has kept its Taisho-era character, with upstairs river-view rooms that change with the four seasons of the Asano River.
The kitchen states it does not use automated reservation systems and prefers direct contact, and welcomes inquiries about allergies and dietary needs.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Signature jibuni is made with sudare-fu (wheat gluten) and a soy-sauce-based broth, and the kitchen relies on wheat-based soy sauce and dashi across the menu; not celiac-safe.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Kaga kappo cooking here uses standard wheat-based soy sauce in jibuni and seasonings throughout.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes are built on fish/bonito dashi and feature duck and seafood; vegetable items are not prepared vegan.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The official site states the restaurant accepts inquiries about allergies and dietary restrictions and prefers direct phone contact over automated systems.

positives.text: Kitchen is open to allergy/dietary questions when contacted directly by phone before the visit.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional family-run riverside kappo where some Japanese helps; reservations are taken by phone and the kitchen prefers direct contact, so calling ahead (ideally in Japanese) and being ready to discuss dishes makes for a smoother visit.

places.name: 蕎麦処 杏庵 (Sobadokoro Anan)
places.gf_detail: This is a hand-made soba shop. The noodles are made with Hokkaido buckwheat flour but, as with virtually all Japanese soba, are not certified gluten-free and almost always contain wheat flour binder; broths and dipping sauces are soy-sauce based (wheat-containing). Not suitable for celiacs. No gluten-free accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Menu centers on duck (Zao duck seiro), egg yolk, Parmigiano-Reggiano cheese, and seafood/vegetable tempura, with dashi- and soy-sauce-based broths. No vegan offerings are documented.
places.neighborhood: Musashi-machi, near Omicho Market
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A casual hand-made soba house run by a welcoming female proprietor. The noodles are hand-cut daily using buckwheat flour from the Isoya region of Hokkaido, prized for its aroma and smooth, easy-to-eat texture, and the kitchen leans on local Ishikawa vegetables and seasonal ingred

chef_bio.origin: Opened March 1, 2022 in Kanazawa's Musashi-machi, a short walk from Omicho Market, on the site of a former soba shop (元たんば屋).
chef_bio.background: A casual hand-made soba house run by a welcoming female proprietor. The noodles are hand-cut daily using buckwheat flour from the Isoya region of Hokkaido, prized for its aroma and smooth, easy-to-eat texture, and the kitchen leans on local Ishikawa vegetables and seasonal ingredients.
chef_bio.philosophy: The owner wants guests to enjoy the meal all the way through without tiring of it (最後まで飽きずに楽しんでほしい), which shapes the varied, lightly inventive menu.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature dish is tsuke-kamo seiro (duck seiro, ~1,800 yen): freshly hand-cut soba dipped in a warm, mellow, refined broth made with free-range Zao duck. Other standouts include a playful 'cheese soba' piled with Parmigiano-Reggiano and egg yolk (~1,300 yen) and bukkake oroshi soba with seasonal Ishikawa grated daikon (~1,000 yen).

anecdotes.text: The shop sits on the former site of the soba restaurant Tanbaya, behind the hotel in the Musashi area near Omicho Market.
Run solo by a kind female owner, the casual counter is described as especially comfortable for women dining alone.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Hand-made soba is made and cut in a flour-heavy kitchen; wheat-based binder and shared surfaces make cross-contamination effectively certain.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dipping broths and sauces are soy-sauce based, which standardly contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Kitchen handles duck, egg, cheese, and seafood/vegetable tempura; no vegan-segregated preparation is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A warm, solo-female-owner counter shop where dining alone is easy and welcome; a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu will go a long way, as English is unlikely.

places.name: 武右衛門 (Buemon)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. Soba noodles here are made with sarashina (refined buckwheat) flour blended with wheat and roasted-tea powder, and soba is typically served with a wheat-based soy-sauce dashi tsuyu. No gluten-free menu or precautions are documented. Not safe for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu is documented. Soba is traditionally served with a katsuo/dashi-based dipping sauce and may include egg or wheat in the noodle binding. A plain tea-soba or vegetable item might be possible but would need to be confirmed in person; assume animal-based dashi by default.
places.hours_raw: Daytime soba cafe (Buemon): 11:30-16:00. Evening soba bar (Otoyu): 18:30-01:00, closed Sun & Mon (open Sun if Mon is a holiday).
places.neighborhood: 東山・ひがし茶屋街 (Higashiyama / Higashi Chaya District)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Buemon occupies a renovated townhouse in a back alley of Kanazawa's Higashi Chaya geisha district. The building was once the private residence of the proprietor of 'Toyu' (東湯), a public bathhouse that the district's geiko once frequented. It has been modernized while keeping the

chef_bio.background: Buemon occupies a renovated townhouse in a back alley of Kanazawa's Higashi Chaya geisha district. The building was once the private residence of the proprietor of 'Toyu' (東湯), a public bathhouse that the district's geiko once frequented. It has been modernized while keeping the chic character of the chaya district, with a high ceiling and exposed beams, an open counter for solo diners, and a courtyard garden visible through large glass windows.
chef_bio.philosophy: A focus on hometown ingredients and Kanazawa's food culture, expressed through the marriage of soba with local Kaga roasted tea.
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-made soba, with the signature 'Kaga-bocha soba' (加賀棒茶そば, around 880 yen) made by kneading powdered Kaga roasted tea into fine sarashina-flour noodles. By day it is a soba cafe; in the evening it runs as a soba bar (音ゆ).

anecdotes.text: The soba chef is a son of the owner of the 'Toyu' bathhouse; he was involved in launching several soba shops in Tokyo before returning home to open Buemon in the former bathhouse-owner's residence.

The signature Kaga-bocha soba was born from trial and error: the chef gathered roasted teas from across Kanazawa and repeatedly experimented before arriving at this dish, which he calls something you can only taste here.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a soba (buckwheat) shop whose noodles are blended with sarashina flour and roasted-tea powder; wheat is standard in soba dough and in the dipping sauce. No gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soba is served with a traditional soy-sauce-and-dashi tsuyu, which is normally wheat-based; no wheat-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Traditional soba tsuyu is based on katsuobushi/animal dashi; no vegan dashi or menu is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed, modern soba cafe in a renovated townhouse where a friendly greeting and pointing at the photo menu will get you by, though staff may have limited English.

places.name: 大正浪漫喫茶 金魚庵 (Taisho Roman Kissa Kingyoan)
places.gf_detail: This is a classic kissaten built around hot cakes, homemade pudding and other flour-, egg- and dairy-based sweets, with no gluten-free menu or allergy program documented. The signature white hot cakes are wheat pancakes. Not suitable for celiac diners; there is no dedicated gluten-free preparation.
places.vegan_detail: The menu centers on egg- and dairy-rich sweets (custard pudding with strong egg flavor, hot cakes with honey and butter, cream sodas with ice cream). No vegan options are documented. Some drinks such as plain coffee or the jelly/fruit punch may be incidentally plant-based, but you would need to ask; this is not a vegan-friendly destination.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-17:00 (L.O. 16:30); food service until around 14:00. Closed on the 2nd and 4th Wednesdays of the month.
places.neighborhood: 東山・ひがし茶屋街 / Higashi Chaya District
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Kingyoan opened in September 2022 inside a renovated Kanazawa machiya more than a century old, tucked just off the main lane of the Higashi Chaya District. The whole cafe is built as a 'time machine' back to Japan's Taisho era (1912-1926), when Western culture was first taking ro

chef_bio.origin: The name 金魚庵 (Kingyoan, 'goldfish retreat') is drawn from the goldfish that appears in Kanazawa-born novelist Muro Saisei's fairy-tale work, and goldfish motifs are hidden throughout the interior like a treasure hunt; hanging goldfish lamps mark the vermillion lattice entrance.
chef_bio.background: Kingyoan opened in September 2022 inside a renovated Kanazawa machiya more than a century old, tucked just off the main lane of the Higashi Chaya District. The whole cafe is built as a 'time machine' back to Japan's Taisho era (1912-1926), when Western culture was first taking root: original red carpets, a working phonograph, period refrigerator, stained glass and antique telephones fill the rooms, and staff serve in kimono with frilly aprons.
chef_bio.philosophy: The cafe recreates Meiji- and Taisho-era recipes and decor to let guests step back in time and experience the romance of early-modern Japan.
chef_bio.specialty: Signature items include cream soda served in custom goldfish-bowl glasses in red, indigo and blue; the summer-only Kingyoan goldfish-bowl soda whose decorations mimic water plants and bubbles; Haikara Punch, a colorful jelly-and-fruit soda with marble-like gel spheres; a dense old-fashioned homemade pudding with strong egg flavor and bitter caramel; and pale 'white' hot cakes recreated from Taisho-era recipes using honey and jam instead of sugar.

anecdotes.text: Cream sodas come in three colors served in goldfish-bowl-shaped glasses, and the summer-limited goldfish soda is designed so that, viewed from above, the bubbles and grass-like decorations look like a real goldfish bowl, with chocolate 'pebbles' at the bottom.
The building is a 100-plus-year-old machiya preserved with its Taisho-era furnishings, and the cafe doubles as a photography studio that can be rented after hours and on closed days.

cultural_comfort.note: A welcoming themed cafe popular with tourists where a smile and a few words of Japanese go a long way; staff are used to visitors but English menus or fluent English service are not guaranteed.

places.name: いたる 本店 (Itaru Honten)
places.gf_detail: This is a classic Japanese izakaya built around soy sauce, miso, and bonito/kombu dashi, with sashimi served with wheat-based soy sauce and many fried items (tempura, fried white shrimp). There is no documented gluten-free menu or allergen handling, so it is not safe for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: The kitchen is centered on Sea-of-Japan seafood (sashimi platters, nodoguro, crab, yellowtail) with dashi and seafood used throughout. While some Kaga-vegetable dishes exist, they are typically prepared with dashi or other animal products, and there is no documented vegan menu.
places.hours_raw: 17:30–23:30 (L.O. 23:00); closed Sundays (and the following Monday when Sunday is a national holiday)
places.neighborhood: Kakinokibatake / Katamachi, near the 21st Century Museum (Kō
places.notes: Founded in 1988 in the Kakinokibatake quarter near Kanazawa's Katamachi district, Itaru grew from a neighborhood spot into what locals call one of Kanazawa's defining izakaya, especially after the Hokuriku Shinkansen reached the city in 2015 brought a wave of visitors alongside i

chef_bio.background: Founded in 1988 in the Kakinokibatake quarter near Kanazawa's Katamachi district, Itaru grew from a neighborhood spot into what locals call one of Kanazawa's defining izakaya, especially after the Hokuriku Shinkansen reached the city in 2015 brought a wave of visitors alongside its loyal regulars.
chef_bio.philosophy: The restaurant builds its menu around top local ingredients — day-boat fish from Toyama's Shinminato and Noto, and Kaga vegetables such as Gorojima-kintoki sweet potato, kinjiso red amaranth, Kaga futo-kyuri cucumber, and Noto taro — with chefs cooking in full view of the counter so guests can watch the craft.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature is the kioke-mori otsukuri — a generous sashimi platter heaped in a wooden barrel with bold seasonal cuts of Sea-of-Japan fish. Other standouts include aji no namero (minced horse mackerel), salt-grilled nodoguro (blackthroat seaperch), and shime-saba. Winter brings yopparai-gani (sake-marinated kobako crab) and kan-buri (cold-season yellowtail).

anecdotes.text: Itaru pours Tengu-mai sake drawn straight from a brewery mini-tank without bottling, a kura-dashi pour the shop describes as available nowhere else.
The counter is set up as a live-cooking stage where the head chef works alongside apprentices in front of diners.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu relies on soy sauce, miso, and dashi with fried items (tempura, fried white shrimp) sharing the kitchen; no gluten-free accommodation is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Sashimi and izakaya dishes are served with standard Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Seafood and dashi are central to nearly every dish; vegetable preparations are typically dashi-based.

cultural_comfort.note: A bustling, hugely popular local izakaya where a few words of Japanese and a reservation go a long way; counter seats let you watch the chefs, but service is fast-paced and not heavily English-oriented.

places.name: 加登長 横安江町店 (Kadochō Yokoanjemachi-ten)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core menu is wheat udon and soba (buckwheat is typically blended with wheat flour) served in a soy-and-dashi broth, plus fried dishes (tempura, katsu). There is no indication of gluten-free options or any allergen handling, so this is not safe for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. Broths are made with fish/bonito dashi, and signature plates include saba (mackerel) miso, tempura rice bowls, ramen, and katsu. No documented vegan options.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–15:00; closed Tuesdays
places.neighborhood: 横安江町商店街・安江町 (Yokoanjemachi shopping street, between Kanazawa
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] An old-school Kanazawa soba and udon shokudō tucked into the Yokoanjemachi shopping street, on the walking route between Kanazawa Station and Ōmichō Market. It has the calm, lived-in feel of a Shōwa-era neighborhood diner, serving home-style set meals at lunchtime prices that loc

chef_bio.background: An old-school Kanazawa soba and udon shokudō tucked into the Yokoanjemachi shopping street, on the walking route between Kanazawa Station and Ōmichō Market. It has the calm, lived-in feel of a Shōwa-era neighborhood diner, serving home-style set meals at lunchtime prices that locals have long relied on.
chef_bio.philosophy: Warm, home-style cooking in an unhurried, settled atmosphere.
chef_bio.specialty: Sweet-broth udon and soba alongside hearty teishoku (set meals) — diners single out the saba miso-ni teishoku (mackerel simmered in miso), and the menu also features hayashi katsu rice, tempura rice bowls (ten-toji-don), ramen, and nabe-udon.

anecdotes.text: Described as a 'warmhearted set-meal restaurant' offering traditional Kanazawa flavors and home-style, comforting food in a calm atmosphere, with the saba miso-ni teishoku singled out for its firm seasoning and never-tiring deliciousness.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat udon and soba share kitchen and likely boiling water; fried items (tempura, katsu) use wheat batter/breading. No allergen separation is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese soy-sauce-and-dashi broths are used throughout; conventional soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths rely on fish/bonito dashi and the menu centers on fish, egg, and meat dishes; no vegan preparation is indicated.

cultural_comfort.note: A no-frills local lunch spot with no English menu or English-speaking staff expected, so a foreign student is best off going with a guide or someone who reads Japanese.

places.name: 魚旨 (Uouma)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. This is a sushi and seafood-bowl counter built around vinegared rice served with house sushi soy sauce (blended on a Kanazawa Ohno shoyu base), plus typical accompaniments like tamagoyaki — all wheat-containing. There is no documented gluten-free menu, no dedicated preparation, and no allergy program. Not safe for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The entire concept is fresh local seafood — sashimi bowls and nigiri. The signature dish is a 12-topping all-seafood kaisendon. No plant-based options are documented.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-16:00 (approx.); irregular closures, some sources note closed Wed.
places.neighborhood: 近江町市場 (Omicho Market), Kanazawa
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The owner comes from an established sushi-restaurant background and personally selects the fish, bringing a trained sushi craftsman's eye to a casual market counter in the heart of Omicho Market.

chef_bio.background: The owner comes from an established sushi-restaurant background and personally selects the fish, bringing a trained sushi craftsman's eye to a casual market counter in the heart of Omicho Market.
chef_bio.philosophy: A committed local-production, local-consumption ethos: Oku-Noto Koshihikari rice cooked with Hakusan spring water, blended with pure rice vinegar from the old Tsurugi town, and finished with a house sushi soy sauce built on Kanazawa Ohno shoyu — rice, vinegar, water and soy sauce all sourced locally for a balanced bite.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature is the kaisendon (around 2,480 yen) piled with twelve different all-seafood toppings, served over mild, lightly-tart vinegared rice meant to bring out the natural sweetness of the fish. Hand-pressed nigiri of seasonal local fish and the owner's own tamagoyaki round out the menu.

anecdotes.text: The shop leans into a homey, welcoming feel and has built a loyal following of both tourists and locals who return for the generously heaped seafood bowls.
Every element of the rice is deliberately local — Oku-Noto Koshihikari cooked with Hakusan sacred spring water, Tsurugi-town pure rice vinegar, and a Kanazawa Ohno shoyu-based sushi soy.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: House sushi soy sauce is blended on a Kanazawa Ohno shoyu base; conventional Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, friendly market counter where pointing at the photo menu and a cheerful greeting will carry you through; little to no English, but the staff are noted as warm and attentive.

places.name: おでん よし坊 (Oden Yoshibou)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. Oden is simmered in a soy-sauce-and-dashi broth, and many ingredients (fish cakes/hanpen, chikuwa, ganmodoki, fried tofu) contain or are bound with wheat. The signature dishes lean on soy sauce and fish dashi. No gluten-free menu or allowance is documented, and the kitchen is not celiac-safe.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The broth is a fish/seafood dashi, signature items include bai-gai (whelk) simmered whole in the stock and local fish dishes, and most oden ingredients sit in the same animal-based pot. No vegan menu or preparation is documented.
places.hours_raw: 17:30-21:30. Closed Sundays; holidays are an irregular closure.
places.neighborhood: 香林坊・木倉町 (Kōrinbō / Kikuramachi, central Kanazawa)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Yoshibou is a long-running Kanazawa oden shop in the Kōrinbō district. It first opened in 1957, and after the previous owner passed away the shop went quiet for a time before the family revived it; today it is carried on by proprietress Yoshie Koshida together with her son Kōta,

chef_bio.chef_name: Yoshie Koshida (越田良枝) and her son Kōta (皓太)
chef_bio.background: Yoshibou is a long-running Kanazawa oden shop in the Kōrinbō district. It first opened in 1957, and after the previous owner passed away the shop went quiet for a time before the family revived it; today it is carried on by proprietress Yoshie Koshida together with her son Kōta, who together set a warm, welcoming counter atmosphere favored by local businesspeople and people on work assignment in the city.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen measures the salinity of the broth daily and adjusts the seasoning to the weather and temperature, aiming for a stock with lingering, deep flavor. The cooking avoids chemical seasonings in favor of a gentle, ingredient-forward taste, and the shop is a member of the Kanazawa Oden veteran shops association.
chef_bio.specialty: Kanazawa-style oden simmered in a clear golden dashi drawn from local ingredients. A signature is bai-gai (whelk) simmered whole in the broth and shucked to order, prized for its briny aroma; the shop is also known for obanzai side dishes and a sake list chosen to match the food.
