anecdotes.text: The shop opened in 1957 and was revived by the founding family's son after the previous owner passed away, returning Yoshibou's oden to its longtime Kōrinbō spot.
Proprietress Yoshie Koshida and her son Kōta run the counter together, creating the warm atmosphere the shop is known for.

The golden dashi is drawn from local ingredients, and bai-gai (whelk) is simmered whole in the broth then shucked just before serving; salinity is measured daily and the seasoning tuned to the day's climate.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Oden is cooked communally in a single soy-sauce-and-dashi pot containing wheat-based items such as fish cakes, chikuwa and fried tofu, so heavy cross-contact with gluten is inherent to the dish.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is a soy-sauce-based dashi; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, so the broth and most simmered items are not gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The shared simmering broth is a fish/seafood dashi and includes seafood such as whole bai-gai (whelk), so vegan separation is not possible.

positives.text: Cooking avoids chemical seasonings, with broth salinity measured daily and seasoning adjusted to the day's conditions for a gentle, ingredient-forward taste.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, locally loved counter oden shop where some basic Japanese and pointing at the simmering pot will help; an English menu is not documented, so come open to a little adventure.

places.name: 大國鮨 (Daikoku Sushi)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional sushi counter where soy sauce (typically wheat-containing) is central and dishes rely on standard sushi seasonings; there is no indication of gluten-free accommodation. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: A seafood-focused sushi house built around Hokuriku fish (nodoguro, white shrimp, anago). There is no documented vegan offering; even rice and broths likely involve fish-based dashi. Not suitable for vegans.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Tue, Thu-Sat: 11:00-13:30 and 17:00-19:00; Sun: 11:00-13:30 (lunch only, no dinner); Wed: closed. (Some listings show slightly later dinner end around 19:30.)
places.neighborhood: 近江町市場 十間町口 (Omichō Market, Jukkenmachi exit)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, tucked-away counter sushi shop just outside Omichō Market at the Jukkenmachi exit, where the master forms each piece by hand at the counter while the front-of-house hostess attends to guests in a calm, unhurried room.

roles: Itamae (master sushi chef) at the counter
Okami-san (hostess) handling the front of house
chef_bio.background: A small, tucked-away counter sushi shop just outside Omichō Market at the Jukkenmachi exit, where the master forms each piece by hand at the counter while the front-of-house hostess attends to guests in a calm, unhurried room.
chef_bio.philosophy: Sushi made to the diner's pace: the master shapes the pieces as you eat, serving roughly two at a time so each arrives fresh from his hands.
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-formed nigiri showcasing Hokuriku seafood — seared nodoguro (rosy seabass), white shrimp (shiroebi), sweet shrimp, anago and seasonal local fish. The popular 'Ume' omakase course pairs about a dozen pieces of nigiri with a roll and soup.

anecdotes.text: The master hand-forms each piece with precise timing at the counter while the hostess carefully presents the dishes, giving the room a calm, personal feel.
The 'Ume' omakase course of about twelve nigiri plus a roll and soup is praised as outstanding value, with seared nodoguro and white shrimp as highlights.
Sushi is served in pairs, paced to how quickly the diner eats, so each piece is freshly made.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a traditional sushi counter, soy sauce (typically brewed with wheat) is used throughout; no gluten-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is built entirely around fish and shellfish (nodoguro, white shrimp, anago); broths and rice seasoning likely include fish-based ingredients.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny traditional counter sushi shop run by an older master and front-of-house hostess; little to no English is likely, so basic Japanese or a translation app and a phone reservation will make your visit smooth.

places.name: 香林坊らくや (Korinbo Rakuya)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The house specialty jibuni (治部煮) is made by dusting duck/chicken in wheat flour and simmering it with Kanazawa fu (wheat gluten) in a wheat-soy-sauce-based broth, and the izakaya menu relies on soy sauce and dashi throughout. There is no indication of gluten-free accommodation, so it is not celiac-safe.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. This is a Kaga-cuisine izakaya built around fresh Hokuriku seafood, duck/chicken jibuni, and bonito/kombu dashi. No vegan menu or documented plant-based accommodation; vegans would have very little to eat.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Sat & day before public holidays: Lunch 11:30–13:30 (food L.O. 13:30); Dinner 17:00–22:00 (food L.O. 21:00). Closed Sundays and public holidays.
places.neighborhood: Korinbo (香林坊)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Korinbo Rakuya is a Kanazawa machiya (traditional townhouse) izakaya tucked into the central Korinbo district, where the weathered wooden architecture, tatami zashiki rooms and a serene inner garden give it the feel of an old Kanazawa merchant's home. It is one of the comparative

chef_bio.background: Korinbo Rakuya is a Kanazawa machiya (traditional townhouse) izakaya tucked into the central Korinbo district, where the weathered wooden architecture, tatami zashiki rooms and a serene inner garden give it the feel of an old Kanazawa merchant's home. It is one of the comparatively few local spots where you can order Kaga regional dishes individually rather than only as part of a set course.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature dish is jibuni (治部煮), the classic Kaga stew of wheat-flour-dusted duck simmered with Kanazawa's distinctive sudare-fu (wheat gluten) and seasonal vegetables in a glossy thickened broth, finished with a sharp dab of wasabi. Rakuya is noted for serving jibuni as an affordable single à-la-carte plate, alongside seasonal Hokuriku seafood and Kaga vegetables paired with local sake.

anecdotes.text: The menu centers on Ishikawa seafood, Kaga vegetables and local sake served amid the atmosphere of a Kanazawa machiya townhouse, with a first-floor tatami room looking onto an inner garden.
Diners can enjoy jibuni featuring duck, Kanazawa fu, a distinctive broth and wasabi offered as an inexpensive single dish, a relatively rare way to sample the dish in the city.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The signature jibuni is made with wheat-flour-coated meat and wheat-gluten fu in a soy-sauce broth, so gluten is integral to the kitchen and the menu is not suitable for celiac diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Kaga cuisine such as jibuni is built on wheat-containing soy sauce and dashi; no wheat-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is centered on seafood, duck/chicken and bonito/kombu dashi, so plant-only dishes cannot be assumed free of animal-based broth.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional Kanazawa machiya izakaya where staff likely speak limited English, so a few basic Japanese phrases and pointing at the menu will help a foreign student feel at ease.

places.name: 茶房 素心 (Sabo Soshin)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional sweets cafe whose menu centers on wheat- and dairy-based items: cheesecake, parfaits, Japanese confections (wagashi/上生菓子), and zenzai. There is no documented gluten-free menu, no dedicated preparation, and no allergen handling described. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: Plant-based options are not advertised. Plain matcha, hojicha, and brewed coffee/tea are likely vegan as drinks, but the sweets (parfaits, cheesecake, wagashi, zenzai) typically contain dairy, eggs, or refined ingredients. Kakigori syrups may be acceptable but ice/sweets are not verified vegan. A vegan diner would need to ask in person and limit to plain beverages.
places.hours_raw: 10:00 until varies (closing time changes day to day). Closed Wed (with occasional irregular closures). Guests are asked to order at least one item per person.
places.neighborhood: Higashiyama / Higashi Chaya District
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, independent tea-and-sweets cafe that has quietly operated for over a decade (around 13 years as of the mid-2020s), set inside a renovated traditional townhouse (machiya) right on the main street of Kanazawa's Higashi Chaya geisha district. The owners describe their aim s

chef_bio.origin: 金沢・東山（ひがし茶屋街）
chef_bio.background: A small, independent tea-and-sweets cafe that has quietly operated for over a decade (around 13 years as of the mid-2020s), set inside a renovated traditional townhouse (machiya) right on the main street of Kanazawa's Higashi Chaya geisha district. The owners describe their aim simply: to keep the spirit of an old-fashioned kissaten (Japanese coffeehouse) alive, refining the craft day by day.
chef_bio.philosophy: Doing the simple things carefully — grinding and brewing each cup of coffee by hand, and preserving the calm, unhurried feel of a classic neighborhood coffeehouse rather than chasing trends.
chef_bio.specialty: Stone-ground, cup-by-cup hand-brewed coffee and Viennese coffee; matcha and hojicha parfaits; cheesecake; zenzai (sweet red-bean soup); premium seasonal wagashi (上生菓子) with matcha; and, in summer, around eight kinds of Japanese-style kakigori (shaved ice) served with the ice and syrup separate so guests sweeten to taste.

anecdotes.text: The shop sums up its own ethos as 'in our 13th year, striving daily to be an old-fashioned coffeehouse.'
On the second floor, window seats look out over the historic Higashi Chaya streetscape, making it a favorite rest stop between sightseeing.
In summer the cafe serves about eight varieties of Japanese kakigori at around 900 yen, with the shaved ice and syrup brought separately so you can adjust the sweetness yourself.

positives.text: Kakigori is served with the ice and syrup separate, so guests can control sweetness and see what they are adding.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed, tourist-friendly old-townhouse tea cafe where a few words of Japanese and pointing at the sweets menu will get you a warm welcome, but plan to order at least one item per person as the shop requests.

places.name: 土家 (Tsuchiya)
places.gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. The café serves wagashi and toast-style items with no celiac protocols documented; the complimentary sweets are chosen by the house. No dedicated equipment or allergen handling is described, so it is not suitable for someone needing strict gluten avoidance.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu is advertised. The core offering is coffee and seasonal wagashi (Japanese sweets such as yokan, zenzai/sweet red-bean soup, wasanbon-sugar items); many wagashi are plant-based, but ingredients (e.g. dairy in some items, sweets served as a complimentary set you cannot choose) are not specified. A vegan visitor would need to ask in Japanese about each item.
places.hours_raw: Listings vary: standard listing shows 10:00–17:00, closed Mon & Tue. A 2023 update reports a change (from Aug 2023) to weekends/holidays only — Sat & Sun 11:00–18:00, holidays 11:00–17:00. Irregular closures noted.
places.neighborhood: 主計町茶屋街 (Kazuemachi chaya district), beside the Asano River;
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Tsuchiya occupies a wooden townhouse built in 1913 (Taisho 2) in Kazuemachi, one of Kanazawa's historic teahouse districts, on the banks of the Asano River between the Asanogawa-ohashi and Nakano-bashi bridges. The building is a Kanazawa-registered traditional structure, recogniz

chef_bio.background: Tsuchiya occupies a wooden townhouse built in 1913 (Taisho 2) in Kazuemachi, one of Kanazawa's historic teahouse districts, on the banks of the Asano River between the Asanogawa-ohashi and Nakano-bashi bridges. The building is a Kanazawa-registered traditional structure, recognizable by its deep red bengara-pigment walls. It has been converted into a small café that keeps the spirit of the old chaya intact.
chef_bio.philosophy: A quiet, gracious tea-time: guests are welcomed with complimentary tea and a seasonal sweet while their order is prepared, and the second-floor tatami room is dressed with seasonal hanging scrolls and alcove crafts reflecting Kanazawa's four seasons.
chef_bio.specialty: House 'Tsuchiya coffee' (about 500 yen) and Vietnamese Dalat coffee, each accompanied by seasonal wagashi; seasonal specialties include wasanbon iced coffee in summer and zenzai (sweet red-bean soup) in winter, served on Wajima lacquerware.

anecdotes.text: The large second-floor windows frame the cherry-lined Asano River so closely that blossoms feel within reach in spring, while in winter white migratory birds gather on the water below.
Reviewers single out the value: a modestly priced coffee comes with included sweets and a serene riverside view from the old tatami room.

positives.text: Coffee is paired with seasonal Japanese sweets like yokan and zenzai, several of which are traditionally plant-based, though the specific items are chosen by the house and not selectable.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny owner-run townhouse café where service is in Japanese with little English; lovely but best approached with a phrasebook or a Japanese-speaking friend, and patience for irregular opening days.

places.name: 金沢おでんとおばんざい酒場 さんさん (Kanazawa Oden to Obanzai Sakaba Sansan)
places.gf_detail: A Kanazawa oden and obanzai izakaya. The cuisine is built on dashi (typically fish/kelp/soy-based broth), soy sauce, and wheat-based ingredients — Kanazawa oden famously includes 車麩 (wheel-shaped wheat gluten) and many simmered items soaked in soy-flavored broth. There is no documented gluten-free menu, dedicated preparation, or allergen handling. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: This is a seafood-and-meat-forward izakaya specializing in Kanazawa oden, obanzai, karaage and mapo tofu, with dashi-based broths throughout. Obanzai (seasonal home-style vegetable side dishes) may include some plant-based items, but almost all are prepared with fish dashi and soy seasoning, so they are not reliably vegan. No documented vegan menu or accommodation; a vegan would need to ask and options would be minimal.
places.hours_raw: Tabelog: Mon-Tue, Thu-Sun & holidays 20:00-03:00 (Food L.O. 02:00, Drinks L.O. 02:30); closed Wednesdays. Official site frames it as dinner from 18:00 with a late-night bar continuing afterward.
places.neighborhood: Korinbo / Katamachi
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] さんさん is a small, late-night oden-and-obanzai izakaya tucked on the third floor of a building in Katamachi, the heart of Kanazawa's Korinbo-Katamachi nightlife district. A relatively recent arrival to the scene, it sets out to offer 'local flavors and drinks enjoyed casually at a

roles: 金沢おでん・おばんざいの居酒屋
chef_bio.background: さんさん is a small, late-night oden-and-obanzai izakaya tucked on the third floor of a building in Katamachi, the heart of Kanazawa's Korinbo-Katamachi nightlife district. A relatively recent arrival to the scene, it sets out to offer 'local flavors and drinks enjoyed casually at a bar,' with counter seats designed so that solo guests can drop in comfortably.
chef_bio.philosophy: A relaxed neighborhood spot pairing Kanazawa's home-cooking traditions with easygoing late-night drinking, welcoming everyone from solo diners to groups.
chef_bio.specialty: Kanazawa oden simmered in a regional broth (including local touches like 車麩 wheel-shaped wheat gluten), a daily-changing platter of obanzai home-style side dishes built around seasonal ingredients, plus crowd-pleasers like karaage and mapo tofu. By the late hours it shifts into bar mode, known for its tea-based shochu highballs alongside sake and cocktails.

anecdotes.text: The kitchen leans into Kanazawa oden's signature local ingredients and rotates its obanzai daily with the season, while the late-night counter is built around tea-infused shochu highballs that are something of a house specialty.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Kanazawa oden is built on soy-and-dashi broth and includes wheat-based items such as 車麩 (wheel-shaped wheat gluten); fried items like karaage are wheat-battered, so shared broth and fryer cross-contact should be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The menu centers on oden and obanzai seasoned with soy sauce and dashi, which in Japan are virtually always wheat-containing; no wheat-free seasoning is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths and obanzai are typically made with fish dashi and the menu features karaage, mapo tofu and seafood, so vegetable dishes likely share animal-based broth and preparation surfaces.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a small, late-night local izakaya where you order in Japanese and there is no documented English support, so it is most comfortable if you can manage basic Japanese or come with a Japanese-speaking friend.

places.name: サロン・ド・テ・カワムラ (Salon de The Kawamura)
places.gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. The menu centers on Japanese sweets; while items like warabi mochi (bracken starch) and amanatto (candied beans) can be naturally wheat-free, the café also serves wagashi and dessert items that may contain or be cross-contacted with wheat, and no gluten-free protocol is documented. A celiac visitor would need to ask carefully about each item.
places.vegan_detail: Several plant-based-leaning sweets exist: warabi mochi served with kinako (soybean flour) and kuromitsu (black sugar syrup), amanatto (candied beans), yokan, and bean-based jellies and parfait layers are typically dairy-free. However, parfaits and kakigori include ice cream and condensed milk, and some shiratama/dessert components may use dairy. Vegan items are available but not guaranteed; confirm dairy-free options when ordering.
places.hours_raw: Weekdays (Mon–Sat) 10:00–17:30 (L.O. 17:00); Sundays & holidays 10:00–16:30 (L.O. 16:00). Entry ages 6 and up; one order minimum per person.
places.neighborhood: Nishi Chaya District (Nomachi)
places.notes: Salon de The Kawamura is the second-floor tea salon above 甘納豆かわむら (Amanatto Kawamura), a specialty shop in Kanazawa's Nishi Chaya geisha district devoted to amanatto — candied azuki and other beans — along with yokan and jelly confections. The salon was created so visitors can si

chef_bio.background: Salon de The Kawamura is the second-floor tea salon above 甘納豆かわむら (Amanatto Kawamura), a specialty shop in Kanazawa's Nishi Chaya geisha district devoted to amanatto — candied azuki and other beans — along with yokan and jelly confections. The salon was created so visitors can sit and savor the same carefully sourced ingredients freshly made on the premises, with large windows looking out over the historic Nishi Chaya streetscape.
chef_bio.philosophy: The salon is conceived as a quiet, serene space (静寂なる空間) emphasizing freshly made sweets from carefully selected ingredients, with the bean as the protagonist of each dish.
chef_bio.specialty: Signature offerings include the Kawamura parfait built around the shop's amanatto with layers of matcha and Kaga bo-cha, 本黒わらび餅 (warabi mochi made with rare domestic black bracken starch, served with kuromitsu from Hatahama black sugar and kinako), and seasonal kakigori such as a hojicha shaved ice with house-made concentrated hojicha syrup, local tofu shiratama, and house red bean paste, plus a matcha version with condensed milk.

anecdotes.text: The salon admits guests aged 6 and older, asks for one order per person, and notes that its handmade sweets require preparation time — a deliberate framing of the experience as an unhurried, grown-up tea break.
The hojicha kakigori hides hojicha ice cream inside the shaved ice and is topped with daidai azuki beans and house-made red bean paste, with shiratama from a neighborhood tofu maker.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Parfaits and kakigori include ice cream and condensed milk, so shared scoops and prep surfaces could contact dairy even on otherwise plant-based bean and warabi items.

positives.text: Warabi mochi is served with kinako (soybean flour) and kuromitsu (black sugar syrup), and amanatto, yokan and bean jellies are typically dairy-free options for plant-based guests.

cultural_comfort.note: A serene Japanese tea salon where a few words of Japanese and patience help, since handmade desserts take time and English support is limited; note children under 6 are not admitted.

places.name: 金沢和食 みんなのまなぶ (Kanazawa Washoku Minna no Manabu)
places.gf_detail: A traditional Kanazawa washoku spot built around dashi, soy sauce, sake and fried/simmered dishes (e.g. crab cream croquette, beef tendon stew). No gluten-free menu or handling is documented, and wheat-based soy sauce is central to Japanese cooking here — not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: The menu centers on fresh fish (daily-sourced sashimi, nodoguro), seafood, and meat dishes, with dashi-based seasoning throughout. No vegan or vegetarian options are advertised; this is a seafood- and meat-forward Kaga washoku restaurant.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Sat 17:00-00:00 (food L.O. 23:00, drinks L.O. 23:30). Closed Sun.
places.neighborhood: Katamachi / Korinbo (Kakinokibatake / near Saigawa Ohashi bridge)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Chef Manabu Maeda trained for about ten years at the well-regarded kappo restaurant Takeshi, then worked at a fresh-fish shop before going independent and opening his own place in August 2018. The shop's name, 'Minna no Manabu' (everyone's Manabu), reflects its friendly, unpreten

chef_bio.background: Chef Manabu Maeda trained for about ten years at the well-regarded kappo restaurant Takeshi, then worked at a fresh-fish shop before going independent and opening his own place in August 2018. The shop's name, 'Minna no Manabu' (everyone's Manabu), reflects its friendly, unpretentious spirit.
chef_bio.philosophy: Showcase fresh local Kanazawa and Kaga ingredients in honest, well-crafted Japanese cooking at accessible prices, served in a relaxed, welcoming atmosphere rather than a formal high-end setting.
chef_bio.specialty: Daily-sourced sashimi platters with six or seven varieties of fish, grilled nodoguro (rosy seabass), and a much-loved beef tendon stew simmered two to three hours in a salt-based broth with daikon and green onion; seasonal Kaga vegetables and winter kobako crab also feature.

anecdotes.text: Maeda spent roughly a decade honing his craft at the popular kappo restaurant Takeshi, then worked at a fishmonger's before opening his own restaurant in August 2018 — experience that shows in the morning-fresh fish sourced daily from a trusted local fish vendor.
The kitchen brings contemporary plating to traditional Japanese techniques and leans on Kaga vegetables, pairing dishes with highballs and locally made Kaga plum wine.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No gluten-free handling is documented; fried dishes such as crab cream croquette are on the menu, implying shared frying and breading with wheat.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Traditional Kanazawa washoku relying on soy sauce and dashi seasoning; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat and no GF substitute is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dashi (typically fish/bonito based) and fresh seafood are central to the cooking, so animal products pervade the menu.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a warm, no-frills local izakaya-style washoku counter where some basic Japanese will help you order and chat with the chef; an English menu is not advertised, so a translation app is handy.

places.name: 甘納豆かわむら (Amanatto Kawamura)
places.gf_detail: A traditional wagashi (Japanese confectionery) maker. Many sweets like daifuku and shitatari-mochi are made from glutinous rice (mochi/gyūhi) and azuki, which can be naturally wheat-free, but yōkan, sweet beans, jellies and shared kitchen equipment mean nothing is verified gluten-free. There is no celiac protocol; ask in person about specific items before ordering. Not a dedicated gluten-free destination.
places.vegan_detail: The core products are plant-based by tradition: amanatto (sugar-candied beans), yōkan, bean-paste daifuku and fruit jellies are typically made from beans, sugar, agar and rice with no animal products. However, some confections and the 2F tea salon menu may use dairy, eggs or honey, and none of this is labeled vegan. Confirm individual items in person.
places.hours_raw: Shop: weekdays & Saturday 9:30-18:00; Sundays/holidays 9:00-17:00. 2F tea salon (Salon de The Kawamura): weekdays 10:00-17:00 (closes 17:30), Sundays/holidays 10:00-16:00 (closes 16:30).
places.neighborhood: Nishi Chaya District (Nomachi)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Kawamura began as a wholesale bean and confectionery business in Kanazawa with a history said to stretch back roughly a century. The family opened this retail shop in the historic Nishi-Chaya geisha district in 2001, set in a traditional wooden machiya directly across from the di

chef_bio.background: Kawamura began as a wholesale bean and confectionery business in Kanazawa with a history said to stretch back roughly a century. The family opened this retail shop in the historic Nishi-Chaya geisha district in 2001, set in a traditional wooden machiya directly across from the district's old geisha registry office, and renovated it in November 2013.
chef_bio.philosophy: Handcrafted quality using local, seasonal Kanazawa ingredients, served in a calm, serene setting, with sweets made without artificial colors or preservatives even at the cost of a short shelf life.
chef_bio.specialty: Amanatto (sugar-candied beans made without artificial coloring or preservatives), alongside yōkan, seasonal fruit jellies, strawberry daifuku, and the beloved shitatari-mochi, a sweet-bean filling wrapped in gyūhi mochi so tender it seems to drip, sold with just a three-day shelf life.

anecdotes.text: The signature shitatari-mochi is named for being 'so soft it seems to drip,' and is sold with a shelf life of only three days from purchase, prioritizing freshness over longevity.

The shop sits in the Nishi-Chaya geisha quarter in a traditional machiya opposite the old kennibanshō (geisha registry office), and the founder's wife contributed the gentle, calming typeface used on the packaging.

positives.text: Amanatto and other sweets are made without artificial coloring or preservatives, following traditional handcrafted methods.

cultural_comfort.note: A welcoming traditional sweets shop and quiet tea salon; staff likely speak limited English, so a translation app or a few Japanese phrases will help you order and ask about ingredients.

places.name: 海鮮酒処 あげは (Kaisen Sakedokoro Ageha)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The kitchen specializes in surimi (satsuma-age / fish cakes) and seafood manju, and serves sashimi with soy sauce — fried items and fish-paste products commonly contain wheat starch/flour and the soy sauce contains wheat. No gluten-free menu or celiac accommodation is documented. Not safe for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. This is a seafood and fish-paste specialist — sashimi, satsuma-age, seafood manju, and fish-based courses are the entire concept. No vegan options are documented.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:30–until sold out (approx. 15:00); Dinner 18:00–22:00 (reservation only). Closed Mon, Tue, Wed; occasional irregular closures.
places.neighborhood: Hirosaka / Kakinokibatake (near Korinbo / 21st Century Museum of Contemporary Art)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Before opening Ageha, owner Shigeru Ueda spent some 40 years working at a kamaboko (fish-cake) maker that supplied traditional Japanese restaurants, rising to factory manager. After leaving, he opened this small counter shop to share what most people never taste — surimi and sats

chef_bio.origin: Kanazawa (Hirosaka / Kakikibatake area, near the 21st Century Museum)
chef_bio.background: Before opening Ageha, owner Shigeru Ueda spent some 40 years working at a kamaboko (fish-cake) maker that supplied traditional Japanese restaurants, rising to factory manager. After leaving, he opened this small counter shop to share what most people never taste — surimi and satsuma-age eaten the moment they are made.
chef_bio.philosophy: Ueda wants diners to discover that fish cakes and satsuma-age are not the supermarket-processed versions they know — that freshly fried surimi is something exceptional, served light, fluffy, and at its peak the instant it leaves the fryer.
chef_bio.specialty: The hugely popular lunch 'Ageha Gozen' brings together sashimi, just-fried satsuma-age, and a choice of seasonal seafood manju (sea urchin tofu, clam, pumpkin, sweet shrimp, sablefish, and more), with rice, soup, and small dishes. Dinner is reservation-only chef's courses of fish-paste and seafood dishes, including an occasional 'raw kamaboko' made from fresh fish marinated about a week until it sets.

anecdotes.text: Ueda spent about 40 years at a kamaboko manufacturer serving traditional restaurants, finishing as factory manager, before opening this 10-seat counter to let people taste freshly made surimi directly.
The lunch 'Ageha Gozen' is so sought-after that customers regularly line up before opening, and lunch runs until it sells out.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Surimi/satsuma-age (fried fish paste) and seafood manju are the core dishes; fish-paste products and fried items typically contain wheat, and no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Sashimi is served as part of the set, normally eaten with soy sauce, which in Japan generally contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire menu is built around fish, seafood, and fish paste; no plant-based dishes are documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny 10-seat owner-run counter where lunch sells out and dinner is reservation-only courses; it's cash-only with little English, so come early for lunch, expect to be greeted in Japanese, and have basic phrases or a translation app ready.

places.name: 八郎すし (Hachiro Sushi)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. This is a traditional sushi counter where soy sauce (wheat-based) is used throughout, and dishes like sushi rice seasoning, dashi, and accompaniments commonly contain wheat or soy. No gluten-free accommodation is documented and there is no celiac-safe protocol. Not suitable for someone with celiac disease.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. This is a seafood-focused sushi restaurant centered on local fish, crab, and yellowtail, with omakase courses built around raw and prepared seafood. No vegan options are documented.
places.hours_raw: 12:00-14:00 / 17:30-22:00 (lunch and dinner), closed Thu
places.neighborhood: 並木町 (Namikimachi) — near the Asano River and Umebashi Bridge
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A neighborhood sushi shop founded more than 60 years ago (around 65 years) and run by the same family across three generations. The name 八郎 (Hachiro, literally 'eighth son') comes from the founding grandfather, who was the eighth of twelve siblings. Today the third-generation che

chef_bio.origin: Kanazawa, Namikimachi — at the foot of Umebashi Bridge along the Asano River, near the Higashi Chaya District
chef_bio.background: A neighborhood sushi shop founded more than 60 years ago (around 65 years) and run by the same family across three generations. The name 八郎 (Hachiro, literally 'eighth son') comes from the founding grandfather, who was the eighth of twelve siblings. Today the third-generation chef shapes the sushi, having trained at the well-regarded Taihei Sushi in Nonoichi; his father, the second generation, still works the counter beside him, and his mother handles the service. It keeps the easygoing, good-natured feel of an old-fashioned Showa-era sushi shop in a neighborhood that has otherwise modernized.
chef_bio.philosophy: Showcasing fresh local (jimono) ingredients through skilled, unpretentious craftsmanship while welcoming guests with warm, family hospitality.
chef_bio.specialty: Omakase nigiri and sushi-and-sashimi assortments built on local Ishikawa, Kaga and Noto seafood — seasonal fish, crab, and yellowtail — paired with local sake.

anecdotes.text: The shop's name comes from the first-generation founder (the chef's grandfather), who was the eighth of twelve siblings, hence 'Hachiro' — eighth son.
Three generations work together: the third-generation son shapes the sushi (after training at Taihei Sushi), the second-generation father stands at the counter, and the mother handles service.
Praised by a local writer as 'a treasure of Kanazawa' and one of the most quintessentially Kanazawa sushi shops.
The restaurant appeared in the film 'Inochi no Teishajo' featuring Sayuri Yoshinaga.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Traditional sushi counter where wheat-containing soy sauce is central to the meal; no gluten-free handling is documented, so cross-contact with gluten should be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese soy sauce (typically brewed with wheat) is used for nigiri and sashimi; no wheat-free tamari is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Seafood-centric omakase; dishes are prepared on shared surfaces with fish, so no vegan-safe preparation is available.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, traditional three-generation neighborhood sushi counter where some spoken Japanese and familiarity with omakase sushi etiquette will help; the family is known for warm hospitality, so a polite greeting goes a long way.

places.name: 香りん寿司 (Kourin Sushi)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional sushi restaurant where dishes are served with regular Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat. Nigiri, rolls, miso soup, and homemade sweet glazes (such as on the anago/sea eel) are not gluten-free, and there is no indication of gluten-free accommodation or a celiac-safe kitchen. Not suitable for those who must avoid gluten.
places.vegan_detail: The menu is built entirely around fresh seafood sourced daily from Omicho Market — nigiri, sashimi, grilled fish, and seafood sets. There are no documented vegan or vegetarian dishes; dashi-based soup and fish are central to nearly everything served.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-13:30, 17:30-22:00 (last order 21:15). Closed Wednesdays and Sunday evenings (lunch-only Sundays per one source; Tabelog lists Sunday as closed). Lunch sets such as the Ichihan are available weekday lunches.
places.neighborhood: 博労町 (Hakuro-cho), central Kanazawa
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A family-run sushi shop in central Kanazawa's Hakuro-cho, founded around 70 years ago by the current owner's grandfather and now run by the fourth generation of the Yoshikawa family. It sits a short walk from Omicho Market, where the chef buys fish fresh each morning rather than

chef_bio.origin: Kanazawa, with founding-family roots in the Kansai region
chef_bio.background: A family-run sushi shop in central Kanazawa's Hakuro-cho, founded around 70 years ago by the current owner's grandfather and now run by the fourth generation of the Yoshikawa family. It sits a short walk from Omicho Market, where the chef buys fish fresh each morning rather than in bulk, keeping the catch consistently fresh.
chef_bio.philosophy: Buying fish daily from Omicho Market to ensure freshness, and offering approachable, affordable sushi that keeps both locals and travelers coming back across four generations.
chef_bio.specialty: Local Kanazawa fish platters and the value-priced Ichihan lunch set (nine pieces of nigiri plus a thin roll and miso soup for around 1,100 yen, weekday lunch only). A quiet specialty is the anago (sea eel), finished with a sweet homemade glaze. Reflecting the family's Kansai roots, the menu also includes battera and grilled-mackerel sushi, plus a choi-nomi tasting set with sashimi, grilled fish, pickles and soup.

anecdotes.text: The interior walls are lined with messages and photographs left by international customers over the years, reflecting the shop's long-standing welcome to visitors from abroad.
The family's Kansai roots show up on the menu through dishes like battera (pressed mackerel sushi) and grilled-mackerel sushi.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a traditional sushi restaurant, dishes are served with standard Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat; this is the default and there is no documented gluten-free or wheat-free soy sauce option.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen centers on seafood from Omicho Market and dashi-based soup, so cross-contact with fish is unavoidable; no separate vegan preparation is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: The walls are covered with notes and photos from international visitors, an English menu is available, and the family welcomes foreign guests, so a foreign student can dine comfortably here.

places.name: わごめ (WAGOME)
places.gf_detail: Dedicated 100% rice flour (国産コシヒカリ米粉) bakery with no wheat, eggs, dairy, or nuts used anywhere in the workshop. Bread is leavened with house-made apple yeast instead of using gluten or thickeners, and the shop deliberately avoids the seven mandatory allergen ingredients. As an entirely wheat-free production environment, it is one of the safest options in Kanazawa for celiac and gluten-sensitive travelers. No explicit certified-celiac/PPM testing was documented, but the no-wheat-on-premises policy is stated clearly.
places.vegan_detail: Many items are egg- and dairy-free by design, and the shop explicitly offers vegan cakes (soy-milk based) and a range of rice flour breads and muffins. Not everything is guaranteed vegan (some pizzas use additive-free cheese), so vegans should confirm the specific item, but a substantial vegan selection is available.
places.hours_raw: 11:30-16:30 (closes when sold out). Closed Thu and Fri.
places.neighborhood: Eki-nishi-shinmachi (~10 min walk from JR Kanazawa Stn west exit)
places.cuisine: Gluten-free, additive-free, natural-yeast domestic rice-flour bread specialist
places.notes: Founder Junko Kobayashi worked in Ishikawa restaurants and bakeries and later as a TV and radio reporter before becoming a natural-sweets planner. While presenting at a university hospital event she was struck by how many more people live with food allergies than she had imagined

chef_bio.background: Founder Junko Kobayashi worked in Ishikawa restaurants and bakeries and later as a TV and radio reporter before becoming a natural-sweets planner. While presenting at a university hospital event she was struck by how many more people live with food allergies than she had imagined, and that experience inspired her to open a bakery that people with dietary restrictions could enjoy without worry.
chef_bio.philosophy: Everything is made with 100% domestic rice flour and no wheat gluten, avoiding the seven mandatory allergen ingredients and additives such as thickeners, and leavened with house-made apple yeast so that people with allergies can eat freely. After opening she spent a full year refining the recipes.
chef_bio.specialty: 100% domestic Koshihikari rice flour bread with a crisp crust and chewy (mochi-like) interior; salt bread 'Solte', seven to eight kinds of muffins, rice flour chiffon cakes, cheesecakes and tarts, and soy-milk vegan cakes.

anecdotes.text: Kobayashi decided to open the shop after realizing at a university hospital event how many people live with food allergies, opening WAGOME in the Kanazawa station-west area in 2014 after a year of recipe development.
The shop achieves the rice-flour bread's signature 'crispy outside, chewy inside' texture using house-made apple yeast instead of gluten or additives.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No wheat, eggs, dairy, or nuts are used anywhere in the workshop, making it a fully wheat-free production environment.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Many items are egg- and dairy-free by design and dedicated vegan (soy-milk) cakes are offered, but some products such as pizzas use additive-free cheese, so confirm the specific item.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The shop was founded specifically to serve people with food allergies and deliberately avoids the seven mandatory allergen ingredients, so staff are highly allergy-aware, though no individual-order cross-contamination protocol was documented in reviews.

positives.text: 100% domestic Koshihikari rice flour, additive-free, leavened with house-made apple yeast; no wheat gluten used.
Entire workshop is free of wheat, egg, dairy, and nuts, which is rare and reassuring for celiac and allergy travelers.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, friendly neighborhood takeout bakery where a smile and a few basic Japanese words go a long way, though the all-allergen-free concept makes it welcoming and easy to point at what you want.

places.name: midoriya（ミドリヤ） (midoriya (Natural Grocery Store midoriya))
places.gf_detail: Most of the baked goods are made with rice flour and are gluten-free (e.g. the organic-lemon vegan custard rice flour muffin), and the shop markets itself as gluten-free. However it is NOT a dedicated GF facility: the scones are made with wheat flour, and all items are produced in the same open kitchen, so wheat is handled on-site and cross-contamination is possible. Celiac travelers should confirm which specific items are wheat-free before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: The baked goods are all made without eggs or butter (卵・バター不使用), and the retail products are largely vegan-certified / free of animal-derived ingredients. The cafe menu (rice flour muffins, pound cakes, herbal teas, house-made craft cola, organic banana smoothie, vegan gateau chocolat) is plant-based. Verify individual items, but this is effectively a fully vegan cafe.
places.hours_raw: 10:00-17:00; closed Tuesdays plus irregular closures
places.neighborhood: 安江町／横安江町商店街 (Yasue-cho / Yokoyasue-cho arcade)
places.notes: midoriya was founded by Takahashi Tomoko, who first opened it in November 2020 as an additive-free natural food shop on the second floor of her family's former boutique building in the Yokoyasue-cho arcade. The idea grew out of motherhood: after having her child she rethought wha

chef_bio.background: midoriya was founded by Takahashi Tomoko, who first opened it in November 2020 as an additive-free natural food shop on the second floor of her family's former boutique building in the Yokoyasue-cho arcade. The idea grew out of motherhood: after having her child she rethought what she fed her family and decided to seek out and sell only items she had personally, carefully chosen.
chef_bio.philosophy: Choosing natural, additive-free foods is good for the body, the mind, and the earth; everyday items deserve genuine quality, and the shop curates everything through a mother's eye so that even children can eat safely.
chef_bio.specialty: Freshly baked rice-flour sweets made daily in an open kitchen — rice flour muffins (including an organic-lemon vegan custard muffin), scones, caramel-nut and other pound cakes, and florentines — all made without eggs or butter, alongside herbal tea blends, house-made craft cola, and a vegan gateau chocolat.

anecdotes.text: In May 2023 midoriya moved down from the second floor to the first floor and was renovated to add a proper cafe space, furnished with antique pieces re-upholstered in new fabric and counter seats looking out onto the shopping street, deliberately keeping the retro character of the old boutique.
The cafe encourages customers to bring their own tumblers and reusable containers, offering a discount on drinks as part of its low-waste, earth-friendly outlook.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Not a dedicated gluten-free kitchen: scones are made with wheat flour while most other pastries use rice flour, and everything is baked in the same open kitchen, so cross-contact with wheat is possible.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All baked goods are made without eggs or butter and the retail products are largely vegan-certified, so the risk of accidental animal-derived ingredients is low.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The owner curates products with a focus on items children can safely enjoy and labels baked goods as egg/butter/wheat-free where applicable, but no formal allergy protocol is documented; ask about specific items in person.

positives.text: Marketed explicitly as a vegan & gluten-free sweets cafe using organic/pesticide-free ingredients, with many rice-flour, egg- and butter-free options.
Daily-made rice flour muffins and pound cakes are gluten-free and vegan, e.g. the organic-lemon vegan custard rice flour muffin.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, friendly mother-run natural-foods cafe where a basic greeting in Japanese goes a long way; menus and labels are in Japanese, so a translation app helps, but the relaxed counter setting is easy for a foreign student to enjoy.

places.name: 金沢カレー＆ハントンライス だいだい（橙） (Kanazawa Curry & Hunton Rice Daidai)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The entire menu centers on wheat-based dishes: Kanazawa curry uses a flour-thickened roux served over a breaded (panko) cutlet, and the Hunton rice is topped with deep-fried breaded white fish and breaded shrimp. There is no gluten-free menu, no dedicated fryer, and no documented allergy accommodation. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan or vegetarian friendly. Signature dishes are built around fried fish, shrimp, pork cutlet, egg (omurice base) and meat-based homemade demi-glace and curry roux. No plant-based options are documented.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-15:00 (L.O. ~14:30-14:45). Closed Tuesdays and the day after public holidays.
places.neighborhood: Katamachi (Korinbo)
places.cuisine: Kanazawa curry and Hunton rice Western-style
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Daidai opened on April 29, 2023 in Kanazawa's lively Katamachi district, just behind Korinbo Tokyu Square. The shop's whole concept is to serve the city's two great local specialties under one roof: Kanazawa curry and Hunton rice.

chef_bio.background: Daidai opened on April 29, 2023 in Kanazawa's lively Katamachi district, just behind Korinbo Tokyu Square. The shop's whole concept is to serve the city's two great local specialties under one roof: Kanazawa curry and Hunton rice.
chef_bio.philosophy: Owner Isao Shimabukuro makes the demi-glace, tartar sauce and curry roux from scratch and refuses to cut corners, saying he simply wants delicious food to make people smile.
chef_bio.specialty: The flagship 'Daidai Hunton Rice' (around 2,000 yen) is a fluffy omurice crowned with fried white fish, tartar sauce and two oversized shrimp fries; the tartar sauce hides a note of daidai (bitter orange) - the citrus that gives the shop its name - for a refreshing finish. The other pillar is Kanazawa-style cutlet curry (around 1,400 yen), a thick, spiced dark roux over a crisp breaded cutlet and shredded cabbage, served on the classic stainless boat dish.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's name and its signature tartar sauce both come from daidai (bitter orange), which is folded into the sauce to lift the richness of the fried shrimp and fish.
The retro, wood-toned dining room also serves local Kanazawa craft beer and a house yuzu soda alongside the food.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core dishes use flour-thickened curry roux, panko-breaded fried fish, shrimp and pork cutlet, all fried together; no dedicated fryer or gluten-free handling is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes are built on egg, fish, shrimp, pork and meat-based demi-glace/curry roux; no plant-based preparation is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, casual lunch counter where pointing at the picture menu and a friendly "konnichiwa" will carry you through; bring cash, as cards are not accepted and reservations are not taken.

places.name: Cajon to Seed（カホントゥシード） (Cajon to Seed)
places.gf_detail: No documented gluten-free program. The kitchen does serve brown rice and many vegetable-forward dishes, but published menus include soy-sauce-style additive-free seasonings, Thai/Asian preparations and muffins, and there is no statement about gluten avoidance, separate fryers, or cross-contamination control. A celiac diner should not assume safety and would need to ask in detail; this is not a gluten-free destination.
places.vegan_detail: Strongly plant-forward: organic salads, seasonal Ishikawa vegetables, pesticide-free brown rice, vegetable soup, smoothies, fresh-pressed juices and hand-dripped coffee. However the kitchen is omnivore — the signature weekly Seed Lunch and Asian plates routinely include seafood (shrimp, tuna), chicken and egg/fish components, so it is not fully vegan. Vegan diners should ask which of that week's dishes are plant-only; the salad/brown-rice/vegetable-soup base is the safest bet.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–21:00 (lunch L.O. 14:00, dinner L.O. 20:00). Closed Mondays and the 2nd Sunday of each month.
places.neighborhood: Fujie-kita (Eki-nishi / prefectural office area, Kanazawa)
places.notes: Cajon to Seed began life simply as "Cajon," named after the cajón — the wooden box-drum that founding members fell for at a live concert. The instrument became the shop's metaphor: different parts coming together to make one harmony. The current representative, Okada, who hails f

chef_bio.background: Cajon to Seed began life simply as "Cajon," named after the cajón — the wooden box-drum that founding members fell for at a live concert. The instrument became the shop's metaphor: different parts coming together to make one harmony. The current representative, Okada, who hails from Nomi City in Ishikawa, joined as part of that founding team roughly 18 years ago and took the reins as owner about nine years in, during a major renovation that reshaped the place into the airy, plant-filled organic café-dining it is today.
chef_bio.philosophy: Okada wants to retire the old idea that organic food has to taste dull — the kitchen chases genuinely delicious organic cooking and treats natural, additive-free eating as an everyday way to look after one's health, while respecting that diners make their own choices. The brown rice is grown pesticide-free in the restaurant's own paddies and cooked with cold Kawachi-village water kept out of direct sun; seasonings are additive-free, including products from Nakashō, a 129-year-old Ishikawa maker.
chef_bio.specialty: The weekly-changing Seed Lunch is the heart of the menu — a colorful plate of four seasonal dishes built around pesticide-free brown rice, an organic salad, a vegetable soup and a drink. It regularly sells out within about an hour of opening. Other favorites include the Seed Taco Rice, chicken-basil stir-fry rice, organic smoothies and fresh-pressed juices, single-origin hand-dripped coffee, and house-made muffins.

anecdotes.text: The name comes from the cajón drum that inspired the original founders at a live show — chosen to symbolize how different parts create one harmony.
The pesticide-free brown rice is grown on the restaurant's own rice paddies and prepared with cold water from Kawachi Village; refills of rice are offered.
The Seed Lunch is the number-one seller and typically sells out roughly an hour after the doors open.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Published menus describe additive-free seasonings (including soy-sauce-style products from a traditional Ishikawa maker) and Asian/Thai preparations, so wheat-containing sauces are likely present; no gluten-free assurance is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The omnivore kitchen plates seafood (shrimp, tuna), chicken and egg components alongside vegetable dishes on the same changing lunch set, so plant-only diners should confirm which items are free of animal products that week.

positives.text: Strong base of plant-based items — organic salad, pesticide-free brown rice, vegetable soup, smoothies and fresh juices — gives vegetarians and vegans workable options.
Ingredients are emphasized as organic, pesticide-free and additive-free, including brown rice from the restaurant's own paddies.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed, plant-forward organic café in the quieter Ekinishi/Fujie area west of the station — easy and welcoming for a foreign student, though it sits in a residential pocket with little English, so a translation app and a willingness to ask which dishes are meat- or fish-free will help most.

places.name: キッチンユキ 武蔵店 (Kitchen Yuki Musashi-ten)
places.gf_detail: A classic Japanese yoshoku (Western-style) restaurant whose signature is Kanazawa Black Curry served over rice with breaded, deep-fried cutlets (katsu). Roux-based Japanese curry is thickened with wheat flour, the cutlets are coated in wheat breadcrumbs, and items like hayashi rice and spaghetti are also wheat-based. There is no documented gluten-free menu or allergen handling. This is not a celiac-safe place.
places.vegan_detail: The menu is built around meat: pork/chicken cutlet curries, hayashi rice and other yoshoku staples, with the curry roux and sauces typically meat-based. No documented vegetarian or vegan options. Not a vegan destination.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Sat 11:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:00); Sun 11:00-19:00 (L.O. 18:30). Located inside Kanazawa M'ZA B1F; follows the department store's calendar.
places.neighborhood: Musashigatsuji (Kanazawa M'za / right by Omicho Market)
places.notes: Kitchen Yuki is a long-established yoshoku (Japanese Western-style) restaurant with more than 40 years of history, widely regarded as one of the roots of 'Kanazawa Curry' — the regional style of dark, glossy, thick curry served over rice and eaten with a fork or spoon, usually to

chef_bio.background: Kitchen Yuki is a long-established yoshoku (Japanese Western-style) restaurant with more than 40 years of history, widely regarded as one of the roots of 'Kanazawa Curry' — the regional style of dark, glossy, thick curry served over rice and eaten with a fork or spoon, usually topped with a crisp cutlet. This Musashi branch brought the storied name into the basement food floor of the Kanazawa M'ZA department store at the Musashi crossing, a short walk from Omicho Market.
chef_bio.philosophy: The house emphasizes a long-simmered, homemade curry sauce aiming for richness and complexity rather than simple spiciness.
chef_bio.specialty: Kanazawa Black Curry (金沢ブラックカレー) — a deeply colored, near-black curry prized for its rich, mellow depth rather than sharp heat. The most popular order is the Kanazawa Black Katsu Curry, alongside pork-cutlet curry, chicken-cutlet curry, Camembert cheese curry, and hayashi rice.

anecdotes.text: Kitchen Yuki is described as one of the origins of Kanazawa Curry, with a black curry sauce so dark it is nearly the color of its name.
The revered Kitchen Yuki name opened a branch inside the Kanazawa M'ZA department store, bringing the old-school yoshoku curry to the city center.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Japanese curry roux is thickened with wheat flour and the signature cutlets are coated in wheat breadcrumbs and deep-fried, so the core menu is inherently wheat-based.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The curry sauce and menu are meat-based (pork and chicken cutlets, hayashi rice), with no documented vegetarian or vegan preparation.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual, easy department-store-basement curry counter where you order a clearly named dish (point at the popular Kanazawa Black Katsu Curry) and pay by card or QR, so a foreign student can manage without much Japanese, but staff likely speak little English and there is no allergen support.

places.name: キュリオ エスプレッソ ヴィンテージ デザイン (Curio Espresso and Vintage Design)
places.gf_detail: No dedicated or documented gluten-free menu. Core offerings (BBQ pork sandwiches, breakfast sandwiches, carrot cake, pastries) are wheat-based, so the kitchen is not gluten-free. A celiac traveler should not assume any item is safe; ask staff directly (English-speaking) before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: No documented vegan menu. The cafe centers on espresso drinks (dairy milk) plus meat/egg/cheese sandwiches and dairy/egg baked goods. Plant-milk substitution for espresso drinks is plausible at a Seattle-style cafe but is not documented, so ask. Few if any fully vegan food options.
places.hours_raw: 8:00-15:00; closed Tuesday and Wednesday (some listings show 9:00-18:00; current Tabelog and cafe coverage indicate 8:00-15:00 with Tue/Wed closed)
places.neighborhood: Yasuemachi (Yokoanjemachi shopping street / Kanazawa Omotesando entrance, right by Omicho Market / Musashigatsuji bus stop)
places.cuisine: Seattle-style cafe / espresso / house-made American sandwiches
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small independent cafe opened by a Seattle native and his Japanese partner who previously worked in the Seattle coffee scene. It occupies a renovated traditional machiya townhouse at the entrance of the Yokoyasuecho shopping street (Kanazawa Omote-Sando), a short walk from Omic

chef_bio.background: A small independent cafe opened by a Seattle native and his Japanese partner who previously worked in the Seattle coffee scene. It occupies a renovated traditional machiya townhouse at the entrance of the Yokoyasuecho shopping street (Kanazawa Omote-Sando), a short walk from Omicho Market.
chef_bio.philosophy: Bring genuine Seattle specialty-coffee culture and homemade American cafe food to Kanazawa, using beans sourced from Seattle and roasted locally then blended from five varieties for depth.
chef_bio.specialty: Seattle-style espresso drinks (cinnamon latte, the coffee-jelly Crystal latte), house-made barbecue pork sandwiches, breakfast sandwiches, and spiced carrot cake

anecdotes.text: The shop is decorated with the owners' eclectic vintage collection - antique cameras and sewing machines - and still rings up orders on a working cash register from 1943.
Beans are sourced from Seattle, roasted in Kanazawa, and blended from five varieties to build complexity in the espresso.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Staff speak English, which makes it easier for a foreign visitor to ask about ingredients and allergens directly.

positives.text: English-speaking owners and staff at an American-style cafe make communicating dietary needs straightforward.

cultural_comfort.note: Run by a Seattle native and his Japanese partner with English-speaking staff and an American-cafe menu, so a foreign student can order entirely in English and feel at home here.

places.name: カフェ 安江町ジャルダン (Cafe Yasuecho Jardin)
places.gf_detail: The signature items are thick wheat pancakes and freshly baked scones, both made from wheat flour, so the menu is wheat-centric and not celiac-safe. No gluten-free menu or dedicated handling is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Core offerings rely on dairy and eggs — pancakes and scones served with rich Hokkaido cream, and a kuzu milk pudding made with milk and cream. No vegan or plant-based options are documented.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–18:30, closed Tuesdays (per official site)
places.neighborhood: Yasuemachi
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Yasuecho Jardin opened in 2014, tucked at the end of a narrow corridor inside Machiya5 — a mixed-use complex of design studios and IT offices set in a restored Meiji-era townhouse, just minutes from Kanazawa Station yet feeling like a step into another world. The owner, Rieko Kos

chef_bio.background: Yasuecho Jardin opened in 2014, tucked at the end of a narrow corridor inside Machiya5 — a mixed-use complex of design studios and IT offices set in a restored Meiji-era townhouse, just minutes from Kanazawa Station yet feeling like a step into another world. The owner, Rieko Koshi, made the bold choice to open the café in her 50s rather than slow down, pouring her love of food into a small, personal space.
chef_bio.philosophy: Koshi serves only what she herself finds genuinely delicious and refuses to compromise on ingredient quality. The name 'Jardin' is French for 'garden,' reflecting the lush green view from the windows; even the lounge chairs are chosen with care (sourced from Osaka's TRUCK) for comfort and longevity, and sweets are paired with drinks to deepen the experience.
chef_bio.specialty: Freshly baked scones (about 800 yen, baked to order over ~30 minutes and served warm with rich Hokkaido cream and homemade blueberry jam), thick crisp-edged, fluffy pancakes, and a made-to-order kuzu milk pudding using traditional kuzu, kibi sugar, milk and cream topped with kinako and black syrup. Coffee is a self-roasted blend extracted by cloth drip.

anecdotes.text: The owner readily chats with first-time visitors and happily shares dining recommendations, giving the hidden café a warm, neighborly feel.
Guests walk through narrow machiya corridors that create an 'otherworldly' transition before reaching the soft-lit room overlooking a green garden, with quiet jazz playing.

positives.text: Sweets such as scones and the kuzu milk pudding are prepared fresh to order, so the owner is closely involved in each dish and could be asked about ingredients in person.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, warmly personal owner-run café in a hidden machiya; the owner happily chats with first-time guests, but there's no indication of English service, so a few basic Japanese phrases will help a foreign student feel at ease.

places.name: 割烹 松由 (Kappo Matsuyoshi)
places.gf_detail: Traditional Kaga kappo built around dashi, soy sauce, and simmered/braised preparations. No gluten-free menu or accommodations are documented, and wheat-based soy sauce is integral to the cuisine (e.g. jibuni, simmered dishes). Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: A seafood-forward Kaga cuisine restaurant; signature dishes include grilled fish, snow crab, and jibuni (a meat/poultry stew). Dashi is fish/kombu based. No vegan or vegetarian menu is documented.
places.hours_raw: Monday-Saturday 17:30-22:00; closed Sundays (plus occasional irregular closures).
places.neighborhood: 大工町 (Daikumachi), near Katamachi / Korinbo / Omicho
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Founded in 1979, Matsuyoshi is a kappo tucked onto Daikumachi, a quiet side street one block back from busy Katamachi and within walking distance of Korinbo and the Omicho market. The restaurant was built by first-generation owner Akira Matsushita, who set famously strict standar

chef_bio.background: Founded in 1979, Matsuyoshi is a kappo tucked onto Daikumachi, a quiet side street one block back from busy Katamachi and within walking distance of Korinbo and the Omicho market. The restaurant was built by first-generation owner Akira Matsushita, who set famously strict standards for taste and presentation. After his passing, his sons carried it on; the current chef trained at kappo restaurants in Kyoto and Osaka before returning home to continue the family tradition.
chef_bio.philosophy: An uncompromising commitment to delicate, gentle flavors and refined seasonal presentation, letting Ishikawa's abundant ingredients speak for themselves.
chef_bio.specialty: Seasonal Kaga cuisine sourced daily from the market and prepared through slow simmering, aging, and flavor-infusion. Signature dishes include cedar-plank grilled fish, steamed lotus root, jibuni (Kaga's traditional stew), and snow crab in season.

anecdotes.text: First-generation owner Akira Matsushita was known for an exceptionally strict, no-compromise approach to flavor, and his sons trained in Kyoto and Osaka before returning to preserve that reputation among regulars and connoisseurs.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Traditional Kaga cuisine relies on wheat-based soy sauce and dashi in simmered and braised dishes such as jibuni; no gluten-free preparation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is seafood- and meat-forward (grilled fish, snow crab, jibuni stew) with fish/kombu dashi throughout; no vegan offerings are documented.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a refined, reservation-only kappo where most communication is in Japanese and dinner runs around 10,000 yen and up, so it suits a student comfortable ordering in Japanese for a special occasion rather than a casual drop-in.

places.name: 川木商店 (Kawaki Shoten)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The signature kabayaki sauce is built on Ohno soy sauce (regular Japanese soy sauce contains wheat) plus malt syrup, brushed onto the grilled eel and loach. This is a soy-sauce-and-seafood shop with no gluten-free accommodation documented, so it is not celiac-safe.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. The entire shop is built around animal products: charcoal-grilled eel and loach, crab, oysters, sea urchin, scallops, and shrimp. There are no documented plant-based options.
places.hours_raw: Approx. 9:00–15:30 (shop ~8:30–16:00 for crab; eel grilling from around 9:00–9:30). Eel not served on Wednesdays.
places.neighborhood: Omicho Market (Omicho Ichiba-kan 1F, near Hokutetsu Kanazawa Stn)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Kawaki Shoten is one of the long-standing crab-and-eel specialists in Kanazawa's Omicho Market, run as a family business (有限会社 川木商店). It sits on the ground floor of the Omicho Ichiba-kan building, where the family grills eel and loach over charcoal right at the stall and sells cr

chef_bio.background: Kawaki Shoten is one of the long-standing crab-and-eel specialists in Kanazawa's Omicho Market, run as a family business (有限会社 川木商店). It sits on the ground floor of the Omicho Ichiba-kan building, where the family grills eel and loach over charcoal right at the stall and sells crab, oysters, sea urchin, scallops and shrimp from the same counter.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop is particular about origin, sauce and grilling method: eels with firm flesh are grilled hot and fast over domestic charcoal to seal in the umami, rested for about an hour, then dipped and grilled again so the sauce soaks in — leaving crisp skin and fluffy flesh.
chef_bio.specialty: Charcoal-grilled unagi (eel) kabayaki and the more unusual, very affordable dojo (loach) kabayaki at around 100-120 yen a skewer, alongside boiled crab and seasonal Kanazawa seafood like nodoguro and rock oysters.

anecdotes.text: The kabayaki sauce has been kept going and replenished for over 50 years since the shop's founding, made with Ohno soy sauce and malt syrup for a rounded umami without an aggressive edge.
Eels are sourced from Aichi Prefecture for their firm flesh and grilled over domestic charcoal at high heat for a short time, then rested and re-grilled with the sauce.
The loach kabayaki is glazed with a slightly sweeter sauce than the eel and sold for about 120 yen a skewer, making it an easy, cheap taste of an old Kanazawa specialty.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Everything is grilled and glazed on a shared charcoal kabayaki setup with a wheat-containing soy-sauce glaze; no gluten-free preparation is offered.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature kabayaki sauce is made with Ohno soy sauce and malt syrup; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, so the eel and loach glaze is not gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: This is a crab, eel, loach and shellfish stall with no plant-based menu items, so cross-contact with animal products is unavoidable.

cultural_comfort.note: A bustling market stall where you point at what you want and eat grilled eel or loach skewers standing up — easy and welcoming, but expect Japanese-only service, so it helps to know the dish names or come with a guide.

places.name: おでん ゑびす (Oden Ebisu)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The house oden is built on a continuously-replenished bonito-and-kombu dashi typically seasoned with wheat-based soy sauce, and the menu centers on oden, sashimi, and obanzai such as char siu — all high cross-contamination risk. No gluten-free accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. This is a seafood-and-meat oden/izakaya: bonito dashi broth, sashimi, shellfish oden, and char siu are core offerings. Some vegetable obanzai or oden items (daikon, ginkgo) exist but are simmered in fish-based dashi. No vegan menu is documented.
places.hours_raw: 17:30-22:30 (closed Sun). Tabelog lists 17:30-22:00.
places.neighborhood: Nishicho / near Omicho Market
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Ebisu has stood near Omicho Market, Kanazawa's centuries-old kitchen, since 1966 (Showa 41), making it one of the city's long-running oden institutions — well over half a century of service to local regulars.

chef_bio.origin: 西町藪ノ内通, a few minutes from Omicho Market in central Kanazawa.
chef_bio.background: Ebisu has stood near Omicho Market, Kanazawa's centuries-old kitchen, since 1966 (Showa 41), making it one of the city's long-running oden institutions — well over half a century of service to local regulars.
chef_bio.philosophy: Daily selection of quality ingredients from neighboring Omicho Market and a generations-deep dashi are the heart of the kitchen; the small counter is meant to feel, as one writer put it, warming to the heart.
chef_bio.specialty: Kanazawa-style oden simmered in a bonito-and-Rishiri-kombu dashi that has been continuously replenished since the shop opened, with notably large oden pieces — daikon, shinoda-maki, shellfish, ginkgo, beef tendon — alongside an abundant, seasonally rotating lineup of home-style obanzai dishes and fresh sashimi sourced daily at the market.

anecdotes.text: The oden broth carries the shop's whole history: a bonito and kombu dashi continuously topped up since the 1966 opening, soaking into each oversized oden piece.
An intimate counter-only room that fills daily with knowledgeable local regulars seeking specific seasonal obanzai — grilled pork, vinegared vegetables, tempura — beyond the oden itself.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu is oden, sashimi, and obanzai cooked in a shared bonito-kombu dashi with wheat-based soy sauce and served in a tiny counter kitchen; cross-contamination risk is high and no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Kanazawa oden and obanzai are conventionally seasoned with wheat-containing soy sauce; no wheat-free tamari is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Core broth is bonito (fish) dashi and the menu features sashimi, shellfish, and char siu, so even vegetable items are simmered in animal-based stock.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, regulars-filled local oden counter where some Japanese helps and a phone call ahead is wise, since seating is limited and the place is sometimes described as favoring familiar faces.

places.name: むすび 近江町市場店 (Musubi Omicho Ichiba-ten)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. This is a handmade onigiri (rice ball) stand. While plain salt, umeboshi (pickled plum), or kombu rice balls are naturally rice-based, the shop also sells croquettes (wheat breading) and uses soy sauce-based fillings, and there is no allergen separation or dedicated equipment at this small open stall. No gluten-free handling is documented. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: A few onigiri may be plant-based (e.g. plain salt, umeboshi pickled plum, or kombu seaweed), but most fillings are fish, meat, or mayonnaise-based, and rice may be seasoned with dashi (fish/bonito stock). The shop also sells Noto-beef croquettes and other meat items. No vegan menu is documented; vegans should ask which onigiri are free of fish/meat/dashi, and a language barrier should be expected.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Fri 08:00-16:00 / Sat, Sun & holidays 10:00-17:00 (closed Wed). Note: the official site lists the Omicho Market branch as 11:00-17:00 with irregular days off.
places.neighborhood: Omicho Market / Omicho Ichiba-kan (Hokutetsu Kanazawa Stn)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Musubi is a handmade rice ball (omusubi) shop that has been making onigiri in Kanazawa for over 20 years. Its main store sits inside JR Kanazawa Station, and this smaller branch stands right at the lively entrance of Omicho Market, the city's centuries-old fresh-food market.

chef_bio.background: Musubi is a handmade rice ball (omusubi) shop that has been making onigiri in Kanazawa for over 20 years. Its main store sits inside JR Kanazawa Station, and this smaller branch stands right at the lively entrance of Omicho Market, the city's centuries-old fresh-food market.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop's motto, '真心むすびます' (rolled with heartfelt sincerity), reflects its craft approach: rice is cooked over strong gas heat with soft water and washed by hand, then each ball is shaped gently by a craftsperson's hands alone, never with a mold.
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-shaped onigiri made from rice specially chosen for rice balls, alongside croquettes (including a Noto-beef heart-shaped croquette) and gold-leaf soft-serve ice cream that nods to Kanazawa's famous gold leaf.

anecdotes.text: At its peak the company has hand-rolled as many as 8,000 onigiri in a single day, all made for the counter rather than pre-ordered.
The Omicho Market branch leans into local Kanazawa treats, offering a gold-leaf soft serve and a Noto-beef croquette shaped like a heart.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat-breaded croquettes are sold and fried at the same small stall as the rice products, with no documented separation.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soy sauce-based onigiri fillings are common at such shops and standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat; no wheat-free soy sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Fish, meat, and mayonnaise fillings are prepared alongside any plain rice balls at the same counter; no separation is documented.

positives.text: Naturally simple options like plain salt, umeboshi (pickled plum), or kombu onigiri may suit some diners, though fillings should be confirmed in person.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny cash-only grab-and-go onigiri stand at the market entrance with little or no English, so point at what you want and have small bills ready; easiest with a guide or translation app if you need to check fillings.

places.name: むすびそば (Musubi Soba)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. The house soba is 二八 (nihachi) — a buckwheat-and-wheat blend, so the noodles themselves contain wheat. The dashi-based tsuyu broth, tempura batter, and likely soy sauce all add further gluten. There is no documented gluten-free menu or handling, and cross-contamination from shared wheat-flour soba prep is essentially unavoidable. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu is documented. The soba broth is a bonito-and-kombu dashi (contains fish), the onigiri fillings include salmon and Noto pork, and tempura is fried in a shared setting. A plain rice onigiri (e.g., umeboshi) might be obtainable, but there is no confirmed plant-based offering — a vegan would need to ask directly and verify the broth/fillings.
places.neighborhood: Omicho Market (Kanazawa Hakomachi 2F)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] むすびそば opened in April 2025 on the 2nd floor of かなざわはこまち, a shopping complex about a one-minute walk from the historic Omicho Market in central Kanazawa. It is a specialist shop built around two simple staples: hand-rolled soba and freshly made rice balls (omusubi).

chef_bio.background: むすびそば opened in April 2025 on the 2nd floor of かなざわはこまち, a shopping complex about a one-minute walk from the historic Omicho Market in central Kanazawa. It is a specialist shop built around two simple staples: hand-rolled soba and freshly made rice balls (omusubi).
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop's stated aim is 素朴で優しい美味しさ — a plain, gentle deliciousness in its soba and onigiri. The fuller set menu reportedly grew out of customer requests for something more substantial than soba alone.
chef_bio.specialty: Self-described as 自家製二八蕎麦とおむすびのお店 — a house-made nihachi (80% buckwheat / 20% wheat) soba kneaded and cut in-store using domestic buckwheat flour, served in a bonito-and-kombu dashi. Signature plates include the 海老天そば (shrimp tempura soba) and the むすびそば御膳 set of half-portion soba, onigiri, tempura and a small side. The onigiri come in about six varieties made with Ishikawa-grown rice, including Noto pork with shiso-miso, salmon, and umeboshi.

anecdotes.text: The soba is a nihachi blend made from domestic buckwheat flour and hand-rolled inside the shop each day.
Onigiri are made with Ishikawa-grown rice and cooked fresh daily, in small handheld portions across about six fillings such as Noto pork with shiso-miso.

gf_cross_contamination.text: House soba is nihachi (buckwheat + wheat), kneaded and cut in-shop, so wheat flour is present throughout soba preparation and surfaces.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soba is served in a dashi-based tsuyu; typical Japanese soba tsuyu and soy sauce contain wheat. No wheat-free seasoning is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is a bonito-and-kombu dashi (contains fish), and onigiri fillings include salmon and Noto pork; tempura is fried in a shared setting.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed, modern, family-friendly counter-and-table shop inside a shopping building — easy to walk into, point at the picture menu, and order soba or an onigiri even with only a few words of Japanese.

places.name: 加登長 総本店 (Kadocho Sohonten)
places.gf_detail: This is a wheat-noodle house: udon and soba (soba is typically blended with wheat flour), plus tempura, tonkatsu, and a dashi/sweet-soy broth that contains wheat. There is no gluten-free menu and the kitchen is wheat-heavy, so it is not safe for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: The broth is a Kansai-style fish dashi made from urume sardine, mackerel and bonito, and signature dishes feature duck (kamo jibuni), so the menu is built around animal products. No documented vegan options.
places.neighborhood: 近江町市場周辺・下近江町 (near Omicho Market)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Kadocho is widely called Kanazawa's oldest udon shop. The first generation, Wada Chohei, opened the original stall in front of the railway station in Komatsu, and as the rail line pushed north the family followed the tracks and resettled in Kanazawa, where the main shop now stand

chef_bio.background: Kadocho is widely called Kanazawa's oldest udon shop. The first generation, Wada Chohei, opened the original stall in front of the railway station in Komatsu, and as the rail line pushed north the family followed the tracks and resettled in Kanazawa, where the main shop now stands a short walk from the bustling Omicho Market. Across four generations the shop has been a humble neighborhood fixture for well over a century; the third generation, Yoshida Tarokichi, revived the business and founded the Kadocho association that at its peak counted more than thirty branches around the city. The main shop reopened in July 2020 after a renovation that added evening service and sake alongside the long-standing lunchtime trade.
chef_bio.philosophy: Hand-made noodles using domestic wheat and an inherited 'kaeshi' base, kept affordable so that locals and travelers can share the same simple, warming bowl.
chef_bio.specialty: Soft, springy 'Kanazawa udon' in a sweet Kansai-style broth blended with Kanazawa's sweet soy sauce; the signature plate is the kamo jibuni set (duck and vegetables simmered with sudare-fu wheat gluten in a sweet-savory sauce, served with udon, salad and mixed rice). Other favorites include mizore udon with grated daikon, bonito and yuzu-kosho, tanuki udon, and Kaga-vegetable tempura.

anecdotes.text: The shop is the root of what is now called Komatsu udon, having literally migrated up the rail line from Komatsu to Kanazawa with the spread of the railway.
Its mochi-mochi soft Kanazawa udon, served near Omicho Market, has been celebrated as a noodle beloved even by literary figures.

gf_cross_contamination.text: A wheat-noodle kitchen serving udon, soba, tempura and tonkatsu; flour and wheat-based broth are pervasive, making cross-contamination unavoidable for gluten-sensitive diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth combines Kansai dashi with Kanazawa sweet soy sauce; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Base broth is a fish dashi (urume sardine, mackerel, bonito), so even vegetable dishes are prepared in an animal-stock kitchen.
