cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed old-style noodle shop where pointing at the menu and a friendly "konnichiwa" will carry you through; English support is limited and payment is cash only.

places.name: 不室茶屋 (Fumuro Chaya)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free at all — in fact the opposite. The entire shop is built around namafu (生麩), which is fresh WHEAT gluten. The signature shira-tama namafu is essentially pure wheat protein. This is one of the least celiac-safe places imaginable and should be avoided entirely by anyone with celiac disease or wheat allergy.
places.vegan_detail: Many of the namafu sweets are plant-based: the signature shira-tama namafu is served with anko (red bean paste), kinako (roasted soybean powder), and mitarashi (soy-sauce-based sweet sauce), all of which are vegan. However some items are not — e.g. the soft-serve version (しら玉生麩ソフト) uses soy-milk soft serve which may be vegan, but standard soft serve and certain seasonal items may contain dairy. Vegans should confirm individual toppings/soft-serve. Note: nothing here is gluten-free.
places.hours_raw: 10:00-17:00 (L.O. 16:15); may close earlier when crowded. Closed year-end/New Year.
places.neighborhood: 東山 / ひがし茶屋街 (Higashiyama, Higashi Chaya district)
places.notes: Fumuro Chaya is the tea-house and sweets outpost of Fumuroya (不室屋), one of Kanazawa's most storied makers of Kaga-fu (wheat gluten). The family traces its origin to Fumuro village; Kyuemon settled in Owari-cho (today's main shop) and his heir Rokuemon began making fu around Febru

chef_bio.background: Fumuro Chaya is the tea-house and sweets outpost of Fumuroya (不室屋), one of Kanazawa's most storied makers of Kaga-fu (wheat gluten). The family traces its origin to Fumuro village; Kyuemon settled in Owari-cho (today's main shop) and his heir Rokuemon began making fu around February of Keio 1 (1865), naming the house 'Fumuroya' after the founding village. For over 150 years the company has carried on Edo-period techniques for traditional Kaga-fu — including its famous sudare-fu — while developing new products, and this Higashi Chaya shop lets visitors taste that craft as fresh-gluten sweets in a restored townhouse with a tatami room, veranda, and Kanazawa gold-leaf decoration.
chef_bio.philosophy: A place to rest and savor a single, refined traditional sweet while strolling the historic tea streets — the shop frames itself as a quiet pause in the middle of sightseeing.
chef_bio.specialty: Shira-tama namafu (しら玉生麩) — chewy, mochi-like fresh wheat-gluten dumplings served with a choice of anko (red bean), kinako (roasted soybean flour), or mitarashi (sweet-savory) sauce; plus seasonal namafu sweets such as chilled namafu zenzai in summer and hot kinako-and-black-sugar versions in winter.

anecdotes.text: Fumuroya was founded in Keio 1 (1865) when Rokuemon began producing fu, naming the business after the family's ancestral village of Fumuro.

The shop occupies a restored old townhouse in the Higashi Chaya district and presents its signature shira-tama namafu with three toppings (red bean, roasted soybean flour, and mitarashi sauce) in a traditional tatami setting.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The shop's entire concept is namafu — fresh wheat gluten — so wheat is the core ingredient throughout. There is no gluten-free path here; cross-contamination is moot because the food itself is wheat protein.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The mitarashi sauce topping is a soy-sauce-based sweet glaze, which typically contains wheat-derived soy sauce.

positives.text: Several signature options are plant-based: shira-tama namafu with anko (red bean), kinako (soybean flour), or mitarashi are all dairy- and egg-free.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed, tourist-friendly tea-district sweet shop with a ticket/QR wait system and table seating, where a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu will get you a beautiful plate of traditional sweets with ease.

places.name: ひらみぱん (Hiramipan (Boulangerie et Bistro Hiramipan))
places.gf_detail: This is a wheat-flour boulangerie whose core products are bread, croissants, quiche, croque madame and similar wheat-based dishes. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented, and it is not safe for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: The kitchen is built around bread, eggs, cheese, béchamel and meat (e.g. croque madame with homemade chicken ham and fried egg, quiche). The morning and lunch sets are vegetable-rich but not vegan — they include egg, dairy and often meat. A vegetarian could likely find some plant-forward sides (salad, carrot rape, ratatouille) or plain bread, but no vegan menu is documented; ask in person.
places.hours_raw: Morning 8:00–11:00 (L.O. 10:30), Lunch & Cafe 12:00–15:30 (some sources note cafe until 18:00; bread retail sales 8:00–18:00). Closed Mondays plus irregular closures announced via SNS.
places.neighborhood: Nagamachi / Seseragi-dori
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Hiramipan was founded in March 2011 in Kanazawa's Nagamachi district, along the willow-lined Seseragi-dori canal walk. The shop occupies a renovated Taisho-era ironworks (鉄工所跡) wrapped in climbing ivy, with vintage and antique furnishings inside that give it a storybook, Studio-G

chef_bio.background: Hiramipan was founded in March 2011 in Kanazawa's Nagamachi district, along the willow-lined Seseragi-dori canal walk. The shop occupies a renovated Taisho-era ironworks (鉄工所跡) wrapped in climbing ivy, with vintage and antique furnishings inside that give it a storybook, Studio-Ghibli-like atmosphere. Founder Takahiro Hirame grew the brand over the years and incorporated it as Hiramipan Co., Ltd. in September 2017, later expanding into related ventures including a creperie, gallery and pizza venue.
chef_bio.philosophy: The team explicitly resists being 'just an ordinary bakery,' treating bread and pastry as a medium for personal vision and design — comparable to how an illustrator uses a picture book or a designer uses clothing — and aiming to avoid generic, 'tacky' bread.
chef_bio.specialty: House-baked bread (croissants, melon pan, scones, canelés including the local 金澤カヌレ) and vegetable-rich morning sets — the croque madame set with homemade chicken ham, bechamel and cheddar topped with a fried egg is the signature, alongside quiche and avocado toast sets; lunch plates pair French bistro fare with salad, carrot rape and ratatouille.

anecdotes.text: The shop is set in a renovated Taisho-era ironworks covered in ivy on Seseragi-dori, an aesthetic so distinctive it is often compared to a Studio Ghibli film.
The croque madame is a destination dish — guests come specifically for it at the 8:00 morning service.

gf_cross_contamination.text: A working boulangerie where wheat flour is used throughout for bread, croissants, quiche crust and bechamel; airborne flour and shared surfaces make this unsuitable for celiac diners.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Core dishes use egg, dairy (cheddar, bechamel) and meat (homemade chicken ham); shared kitchen and bakery equipment, no documented vegan separation.

positives.text: Morning and lunch sets are notably vegetable-forward, including salad, carrot rape and ratatouille alongside the mains.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual, popular cafe-bakery where ordering is simple and pointing at the bread case works; a little Japanese helps but a friendly greeting and gestures will get you through.

places.name: 手打ち蕎麦 ほやさけ (Teuchi Soba Hoyasake)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core menu is soba (buckwheat noodle flour is typically cut with wheat) and wheat udon, served with dashi/soy-based tsuyu that normally contains wheat. No gluten-free preparation or allergen handling is documented; not safe for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan destination. Soba and udon are served in bonito/kombu dashi-based broths (typically containing fish stock) with wheat-based soy sauce, and tempura is offered. A plain dish such as cold zaru soba might be possible but the dipping sauce almost certainly contains fish dashi, so vegans must ask in detail and should not assume anything is suitable.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-18:00 daily; closes when sold out
places.neighborhood: Higashiyama / Higashi Chaya District, central Kanazawa
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, owner-run handmade-soba shop tucked into a quaint traditional machiya (wooden townhouse) in Kanazawa's historic Higashi Chaya geisha district, a short walk from Kenrokuen and Kanazawa Castle.

chef_bio.background: A small, owner-run handmade-soba shop tucked into a quaint traditional machiya (wooden townhouse) in Kanazawa's historic Higashi Chaya geisha district, a short walk from Kenrokuen and Kanazawa Castle.
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-cut soba and Kanazawa-style udon; the kitchen is noted for thin, light-textured wheat udon made from komugi, alongside cold soba, mountain-yam (toro) dishes, and tempura.

anecdotes.text: Set in a quiet, somewhat tucked-away corner of the Higashi Chaya District, the shop is recognizable by its old-fashioned machiya facade and serves noodles only until the day's batch sells out.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No gluten-free measures documented; soba and udon plus wheat-based tsuyu and tempura share the same kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Broths and dipping sauces are dashi/soy-based and not specified as wheat-free; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Noodle broths typically use bonito (fish) dashi; no vegan or plant-based broth is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, walk-in-only neighborhood soba house in a traditional machiya; staff likely speak little to no English, so point at the menu, bring cash, and be ready for the simple counter-and-tatami atmosphere.

places.name: 近江町食処 鮮彩えにし (Omicho Shokusho Sensai Enishi)
places.gf_detail: This is a seafood restaurant whose dishes rely heavily on soy sauce (which contains wheat) for sashimi, kaisen-don and hitsumabushi, plus the nodoguro hitsumabushi tea-broth course. No gluten-free menu or celiac handling is documented. Not suitable for strict gluten-free or celiac diners without direct confirmation, and even then cross-contact in a busy market kitchen is likely.
places.vegan_detail: A fresh-fish-shop-run seafood specialist (sashimi bowls, sushi, nodoguro). Core offerings are fish and shellfish, and dashi/soy-based seasonings are used throughout. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented; not a suitable choice for plant-based diners.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Fri 11:00-14:00, 17:00-22:00; Sat-Sun 10:00-14:00, 17:00-22:00 (Tabelog). Official site lists lunch 11:00-15:00 (L.O. 14:30) and dinner 17:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:00); hours may vary with ingredient availability.
places.cuisine: Seafood rice bowls / sashimi / sushi / nodoguro hitsumabushi
places.notes: Sensai Enishi is a seafood eatery on the 2nd floor of the Omicho Ichiba building inside Kanazawa's lively Omicho Market. It is directly run by Okuguchi Suisan, a fresh-fish wholesaler and shop within the same market, so the restaurant draws on the company's daily access to local

roles: Operated by Okuguchi Suisan Group, a fresh-fish vendor in Omicho Market
chef_bio.origin: Located within 近江町いちば館 in Omicho Market, the centuries-old fish-and-produce market that locals call Kanazawa's kitchen.
chef_bio.background: Sensai Enishi is a seafood eatery on the 2nd floor of the Omicho Ichiba building inside Kanazawa's lively Omicho Market. It is directly run by Okuguchi Suisan, a fresh-fish wholesaler and shop within the same market, so the restaurant draws on the company's daily access to local catch from the Sea of Japan.
chef_bio.philosophy: Reflecting the market's hospitality tradition, the operator emphasizes おまけ (generous complimentary extras) and offering high freshness at good value to both tourists and locals.
chef_bio.specialty: Two signature dishes define the menu: the Kaisen Enishi-don, a bowl piled with around 13 kinds of seafood such as tuna, nodoguro (blackthroat seaperch), uni, salmon roe, yellowtail, crab and sweet shrimp; and the Nodoguro Hitsumabushi, which uses a whole prized nodoguro and is eaten three ways - first with wasabi and soy, then mixed with uni and garnishes, and finally as ochazuke with a special broth made from the nodoguro bones.

anecdotes.text: The Kaisen Enishi-don is recommended to be eaten in stages: first enjoy the sashimi toppings dipped in soy sauce, then drizzle wasabi-mixed soy over the rice and seafood for the rest of the bowl.
The nodoguro hitsumabushi uses an entire nodoguro and ends with a tea-over-rice course using broth simmered from the fish's bones.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Sashimi bowls, sushi and the hitsumabushi are served and eaten with soy sauce, which standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat; no wheat-free soy alternative is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is a dedicated seafood operation run by a fish wholesaler; all signature dishes are fish or shellfish, so cross-contact with animal products is unavoidable.

positives.text: English and Korean menus are available, which can help diners ask about ingredients.

cultural_comfort.note: Easy for foreign students: it is a tourist-friendly market restaurant with English and Korean menus, card and e-money payment, and walk-ins welcome, though signature dishes run higher than a casual budget.

places.name: 金澤ななほしカレー (Kanazawa Nanahoshi Curry)
places.gf_detail: ⚠ Safety review 2026-07-02: downgraded high→ask. Legitimately skips wheat-flour roux (rice flour instead) — good — so GF options plausible. BUT base curry seasoned with soy sauce AND miso per their own page (standard shoyu=wheat, miso may be barley), unverified. No allergen menu, no tamari sourcing, no CC protocol. Zero independent celiac confirmation — every 'all GF' claim is own marketing. Fails 2-source 'high'. — Prior note: The shop's entire curry concept is built around using no wheat flour and no sugar — the roux is thickened without flour and all curries are described as gluten-free. The official site and multiple write-ups state the curry is made 砂糖・小麦粉不使用 (no sugar, no wheat flour). This makes it one of the most gluten-aware spots in central Kanazawa. However, it is not a certified dedicated celiac kitchen; toppings, side items (pickles, soup) and cross-contamination handling are not explicitly documented, so highly sensitive celiacs should confirm specific items and shared-surface risk directly with staff.
places.vegan_detail: Has clear vegan-friendly options rather than being a fully vegan restaurant (chicken and beef curries are on the regular menu). The 豆カレー (bean curry) is made for vegetarian, macrobiotic, vegan and Muslim diners and excludes the five pungent roots (五葷: garlic, scallions, rakkyo, nira, asatsuki). A newer vegan curry, カカオとナッツのごちそうカレー (cacao & nut curry), is dairy-free, gluten-free and plant-based using coconut milk, nuts and okara-konjac. Vegan diners should confirm which curries and toppings are plant-based on the day, as the menu rotates seasonally.
places.hours_raw: Mon 11:00-14:00; Tue 11:00-15:00; Wed-Sat 11:00-19:00; Sun 11:00-16:00 (hours vary by day across sources; official site notes 11:00-19:30, closed Mondays). Lunch-focused; afternoon/evening availability varies.
places.neighborhood: Hirosaka / Shiinoki Geihinkan, near Kanazawa City Hall (Kori
places.notes: An independent curry specialist in central Kanazawa. The owner spent more than 15 years researching curry, including time overseas (such as Canada), before realizing a long-held dream of opening the shop around 2015. The result is a personal, ingredient-driven take on Kanazawa cu

chef_bio.background: An independent curry specialist in central Kanazawa. The owner spent more than 15 years researching curry, including time overseas (such as Canada), before realizing a long-held dream of opening the shop around 2015. The result is a personal, ingredient-driven take on Kanazawa curry.
chef_bio.philosophy: Food that is delicious and makes the body feel good — gently made, light on the stomach, and accessible to people of every dietary style. The shop frames itself as offering 'the most Kanazawa-like Kanazawa curry in the world' with a spirit of omotenashi (hospitality).
chef_bio.specialty: A refined Kanazawa-style curry made with no sugar and no wheat flour, seasoned with 27 spices. The signature 金澤ななほしカレーセット lets diners choose two curries from options such as chicken, bean, and a seasonal curry, served with a nut-milk soup, original Japanese-style pickles, and a dessert of the day. Seasonal curries (e.g. pork with bamboo shoot and bitter melon, or squid in squid-ink base) rotate through the year. The bean curry is built for vegetarian/vegan/macrobiotic/Muslim diners with the five pungent roots omitted, and a cacao-and-nut vegan curry has been added.

anecdotes.text: The owner researched curry for over 15 years, including overseas study such as in Canada, before opening the shop around 2015 to realize a long-held dream.
The bean curry is made so vegetarian, macrobiotic, vegan and Muslim diners can all eat it, omitting the five pungent vegetables (garlic, scallion, rakkyo, nira, asatsuki).
Describes its curry as containing no sugar and no wheat flour and uses 27 spices, positioning itself as a body-friendly, healthy Kanazawa curry.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The shop is explicitly oriented toward dietary needs, stating it wants to serve diners of every food style, with a dedicated five-pungent-free bean curry for vegetarian/vegan/macrobiotic/Muslim guests.

positives.text: All curries are made with no wheat flour and no sugar, making the core dishes inherently gluten-free rather than relying on a single modified menu item.
Offers a dairy-free, gluten-free, plant-based cacao-and-nut vegan curry alongside the five-pungent-free bean curry.
The curry is described as additive-free and oil-light (no-oil/ノンオイル, 無添加 in the shop's own positioning), aimed at being easy on the stomach.

cultural_comfort.note: A welcoming, health-focused curry shop used to explaining its gluten-free and vegetarian options; a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu will get a foreign student a comfortable, no-reservation lunch.

gf_review.why: Legitimately skips wheat-flour roux (rice flour instead) — good — so GF options plausible. BUT base curry seasoned with soy sauce AND miso per their own page (standard shoyu=wheat, miso may be barley), unverified. No allergen menu, no tamari sourcing, no CC protocol. Zero independent celiac confirmation — every 'all GF' claim is own marketing. Fails 2-source 'high'.
red_flags: Soy sauce AND miso in the curry base, wheat/barley status unverified
Venue says wheat-allergic diners should double-check
'No wheat flour' scoped to chicken curry only
No independent celiac 'ate here and was fine' report
gf_review.plain_rice: Curry-over-plain-white-rice; rice is GF — the risk is the sauce, not the rice.

places.name: わ食場 はす家 本店 (Wa Shokuba Hasuya Honten)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free program. This is a Kaga-cuisine seafood izakaya built around soy sauce, dashi, and fried/simmered dishes; jibuni is thickened with flour and seasoned with soy-based seasonings, and the menu relies heavily on wheat-containing soy sauce. No dedicated gluten-free menu, fryer, or allergen handling is documented. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: The restaurant is centered on fresh seafood from Kanazawa Port (nodoguro, sashimi) and Kaga dishes like duck jibuni; nearly everything involves fish, meat, or fish/bonito dashi. No vegan or vegetarian menu is documented. A vegetarian could perhaps ask about plain Kaga vegetable preparations, but dashi exposure is likely throughout.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Thu 17:00-23:00 (L.O. 22:30); Fri-Sun 17:00-24:00 (L.O. 23:30). Closed Wednesday (open by reservation only).
places.neighborhood: 片町・木倉町 (Katamachi / Kiguramachi, near Korinbo)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Hasuya is a Kaga regional-cuisine seafood izakaya in the heart of Kanazawa's Katamachi/Kiguramachi nightlife district, just off Korinbo. The kitchen works on a local-production-for-local-consumption principle, building its menu around seasonal Kaga vegetables and fish landed at K

chef_bio.background: Hasuya is a Kaga regional-cuisine seafood izakaya in the heart of Kanazawa's Katamachi/Kiguramachi nightlife district, just off Korinbo. The kitchen works on a local-production-for-local-consumption principle, building its menu around seasonal Kaga vegetables and fish landed at Kanazawa Port.
chef_bio.philosophy: Local production for local consumption: showcasing the seasonal vegetables and seafood of Ishikawa in dishes meant to be both beautiful to look at and delicious to eat.
chef_bio.specialty: Signature dishes include nodoguro (rich, fatty throat-fish) preparations, seasonal sashimi platters, and Kaga lotus root cooked many ways. House favorites are hassu-mushi (steamed lotus root with a chewy texture and gentle flavor) and kamo no jibuni, the classic Kaga simmered-duck dish. A set such as the 'Gold Coast Umami Set' bundles nodoguro, local simmered items, and steamed lotus root.

anecdotes.text: The shop's name plays on renkon (lotus root), which the kitchen treats as a signature ingredient across the menu.
There is a sister location, Hasuya Kanazawa Ekimae-ten, near Kanazawa Station, making the Katamachi shop the honten (main store).

gf_cross_contamination.text: Kaga cuisine such as jibuni is thickened with flour and seasoned with wheat-based soy sauce; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The menu is built on Japanese seasonings (soy sauce, dashi); standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat. No wheat-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes rely on fish/bonito dashi and the kitchen centers on seafood and meat, so plant-only dishes would likely still contact animal-based dashi.

cultural_comfort.note: A lively local Kaga-cuisine izakaya where a few words of Japanese and pointing at dishes will go a long way; little English is documented, but staff accept cards and cashless payment so ordering is manageable.

places.name: 茶房&Bar ゴーシュ (Sabou & Bar Gauche)
places.gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination and not celiac-safe. The food menu centers on house cheesecake, gateau chocolat, cake, and an original eggplant curry over saffron rice (curry roux/sauce typically contains wheat). There is no documented gluten-free menu, gluten-free preparation, or allergen handling. A celiac traveler should not assume any dish here is safe.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan or vegetarian destination. The signature sweets (cheesecake, gateau chocolat) contain dairy and eggs. The Gauche-style eggplant curry on saffron rice may be vegetable-forward but is not documented as vegan and could contain dairy/meat-based stock. Siphon coffee (Toraja blend) and herbal teas are available and can be enjoyed black/plant-based. Vegans would need to ask directly; no plant-based menu is documented.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Wed-Sat 11:00-01:00 (next day); Sun 11:00-00:00. Tea time 11:00-19:00, Bar time 19:00 onward. Closed Tuesdays.
places.neighborhood: 東山・ひがし茶屋街 (Higashi Chaya District)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Tucked into a quiet back lane of Kanazawa's historic Higashi Chaya geisha district, Gauche occupies a charming old machiya (traditional wooden townhouse). Step off the bustling main avenue and the atmosphere shifts dramatically: a latticed-window facade keeps the interior hidden

chef_bio.origin: The name 'Gauche' (ゴーシュ) is widely understood to reference Kenji Miyazawa's beloved story 'Gorsch the Cellist' (セロ弾きのゴーシュ); copies of Miyazawa's books are kept on the shelves for guests, reinforcing the literary connection, though the establishment does not explicitly state this in the sources reviewed.
chef_bio.background: Tucked into a quiet back lane of Kanazawa's historic Higashi Chaya geisha district, Gauche occupies a charming old machiya (traditional wooden townhouse). Step off the bustling main avenue and the atmosphere shifts dramatically: a latticed-window facade keeps the interior hidden from passersby while those inside enjoy a soft view out. The space leads a double life, opening as a tranquil tea house from late morning and transforming into an intimate adult bar after 19:00.
chef_bio.philosophy: An intentionally intimate, unhurried 'adult time' aesthetic. The owner curates the mood down to the music, with Edith Piaf on the sound system and shelves of Kenji Miyazawa's books to read by the window, encouraging guests to linger and slow down.
chef_bio.specialty: By day, siphon-brewed coffee is the star: made with a Toraja blend and brewed cup by cup, served with a refill pot for roughly three cups while you settle by the lattice window. Sweets include a house cheesecake and gateau chocolat, and the kitchen's original 'Gauche-style eggplant curry' layers plenty of sliced eggplant over saffron rice scented with cumin. By night it becomes a connoisseur's bar with roughly 300 whiskies, 150 wines, 80 calvados and 25 sherries, plus calvados highballs and cigars.

anecdotes.text: Each cup of siphon coffee is brewed individually and comes with a refill pot for about three cups, and guests can read Kenji Miyazawa's books from the shelves while seated by the latticed window.
Unusually for the Higashi Chaya area, it stays open late into the night, with a bar selection of around 300 whiskies, 150 wines and 80 calvados.

positives.text: Siphon-brewed coffee (Toraja blend) and herbal teas are available and can be ordered plain, which are naturally suitable options for plant-based and gluten-avoiding guests.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, atmospheric machiya hidden on a back lane where the owner curates a quiet adult mood, so come relaxed and ready to communicate mostly in Japanese; daytime siphon coffee is the easiest entry point for a foreign student.

places.name: 蕎味 櫂 (Kyomi Kai)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The restaurant's signature is buckwheat soba, and Japanese soba is almost always cut with wheat flour (nihachi style); the kaiseki courses also use wheat-based tempura, soy sauce, and dashi seasonings. There is no documented gluten-free menu or accommodation. This is not celiac-safe.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. The fixed soba-kaiseki courses are built on seasonal seafood (sashimi, uni, conger eel), duck, dashi, and other animal ingredients. No vegan course or documented plant-based accommodation. As completely-reservation-based course-only dining, options are fixed by the kitchen.
places.hours_raw: 12:00-14:00 (L.O. 13:00), 17:30-20:00 (L.O. 19:30). Closed Sundays and the first Monday of the month. Completely reservation-based (advance booking required).
places.neighborhood: 東山・ひがし茶屋街 (Higashi Chaya District)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] He opened Kyomi Kai in August 2015 in Kanazawa, his wife's hometown. The couple runs the small townhouse together.

chef_bio.origin: The chef is from Sendai in northeastern Japan; he trained at a culinary school in Tokyo and honed his craft in Yamagata and Tokyo.
chef_bio.background: He opened Kyomi Kai in August 2015 in Kanazawa, his wife's hometown. The couple runs the small townhouse together.
chef_bio.philosophy: Quiet, understated elegance: delicate course cooking that threads soba through each stage rather than serving it as a standalone bowl, letting seasonal local ingredients lead.
chef_bio.specialty: Soba kaiseki: refined seasonal Japanese courses with house-made buckwheat woven throughout, including coarse-ground sobagaki (buckwheat dumpling/cake), hand-cut soba noodles to finish, soba sushi, and dishes pairing soba with seafood such as conger eel, crab, and karasumi (bottarga).

anecdotes.text: The restaurant occupies a roughly century-old townhouse from the early Taisho era, fronted by Kanazawa's characteristic kimusuko wooden lattice, tucked in a back lane behind the Higashi Chaya geisha district.
A reviewer who balked at the per-person price before visiting came away astonished at the value compared with big-city dining, praising the airy, sake-lees-seasoned coarse-ground sobagaki.
Kyomi Kai earned a Michelin star (Toyama-Ishikawa 2016 and Hokuriku 2021) and is counted among western Japan’s standout soba-and-kappō counters.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen centers on buckwheat soba (typically cut with wheat) plus tempura and soy/dashi seasonings, so wheat is pervasive across the fixed courses.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Courses are built on seafood, duck, and dashi; no plant-based course is documented and the menu is fixed by the kitchen.

cultural_comfort.note: A serene, formal course-only counter and table restaurant run by a married couple where reservations are essential and ordering is fixed; basic Japanese (or a Japanese-speaking helper to book) will make a foreign student's visit far smoother.

places.name: ゲッコ洞 (Gecko-do (Curry to Osake Gecko-do))
places.gf_detail: A spice curry shop where the curry base (clam broth with coconut milk, achaar, etc.) and accompaniments are not gluten-free and no gluten-free accommodation is documented. Spice curries here typically contain wheat-based thickeners and seasonings, and there is no published allergen handling. Not suitable for celiacs; if strictly gluten-free, ask in advance, but assume it is not safe.
places.vegan_detail: The rotating menu centers on seafood (shrimp, clams, scallops, salmon), chicken, egg, dairy (blue cheese), and clam/fish-based broths. No vegan or vegetarian option is documented. Not a vegan destination.
places.hours_raw: Sources differ after the 2025 reopening. Tabelog: Tue–Sat 11:00–14:00 & 18:00–21:00, Sun 11:00–14:00 (until sold out), closed Mon. A curry-guide source lists post-reopening lunch-only hours 11:00–15:00 (L.O. 14:30), closed Mon & Tue. Hours are subject to change; confirm via the shop's Instagram.
places.neighborhood: Ibaraki-machi (residential area near Korinbo / Katamachi, within walking distance of Tatemachi)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Gecko-do began as a curry shop in March 2020, born from a bar called GECKOS that the owner previously ran in Kanazawa's Shintenchi area. The owner runs the place solo out of a small converted house with a Showa-era exterior and an exotic, foreign-feeling interior tucked into a re

chef_bio.background: Gecko-do began as a curry shop in March 2020, born from a bar called GECKOS that the owner previously ran in Kanazawa's Shintenchi area. The owner runs the place solo out of a small converted house with a Showa-era exterior and an exotic, foreign-feeling interior tucked into a residential maze near Korinbo/Katamachi. In May 2025 the shop temporarily closed, but devoted regulars rallied to revive it, and it reopened in July 2025 keeping the original style and flavors.
chef_bio.philosophy: The owner cooks 'without attachment to the concept or conventional form of curry' — the guiding question is simply what tastes delicious, with each plate prepared individually rather than batch-cooked.
chef_bio.specialty: Spice curry that ignores convention, with a rotating lineup (often two varieties at a time). The standout is the Seafood Coconut Curry — shrimp, clams, scallops and butter-seared salmon over long-grain rice in a clam-broth-and-coconut-milk base, served with onion achaar and a boiled egg. Other rotating curries have included squid-ink seafood, chicken blue cheese, and chickpea-and-chicken. Guests are welcomed with hibiscus tea and offered several vinegars to vary the flavor mid-meal.

anecdotes.text: After the shop closed in May 2025, fans of Gecko-do came together to revive it, and it reopened on July 11, 2025 — just two months later — preserving the original taste and style.
The owner works alone and prepares each curry plate one at a time rather than in pre-made batches, so meals take extra time to come out.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, owner-run hideaway in a converted house with little English signage and contact mainly by Instagram, so basic Japanese and an easygoing attitude help a lot.

places.name: 近江町旬彩 (Omicho Shunsai)
places.gf_detail: This is a fish-market standing-drink counter run by a seafood wholesaler. Core items are sashimi, torched nodoguro (blackthroat seaperch), seafood bowls and a senbero (cheap drink) set, all of which lean on Japanese soy sauce — which is brewed with wheat — and there is no documented gluten-free menu, dedicated equipment, or allergen handling. Not celiac-safe; a celiac traveler would at minimum need to bring their own GF tamari and confirm in person.
places.vegan_detail: A seafood specialist whose entire menu is built around raw and grilled fish, oysters, sea urchin and scallops. There are no documented vegan or vegetable-only dishes; this is not a vegan-friendly destination.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Thu, Fri 10:00-15:00 / Sat, Sun & holidays 09:00-15:30. Closed Wed.
places.neighborhood: Omicho Market (Kami-omicho)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Tucked into a corner of Kanazawa's famous Omicho Market, this is the direct-run counter of Daimatsu Suisan, a long-standing local fish merchant. Because the same company sources the fish, the counter can put genuinely fresh seafood in front of you at market prices rather than res

chef_bio.background: Tucked into a corner of Kanazawa's famous Omicho Market, this is the direct-run counter of Daimatsu Suisan, a long-standing local fish merchant. Because the same company sources the fish, the counter can put genuinely fresh seafood in front of you at market prices rather than restaurant markups. Until the summer of 2023 it operated as a robatayaki-style grill called Omicho Shunsai-yaki; that July the grill format was retired and the shop was renamed simply Omicho Shunsai, reframed as a casual standing spot so that, in the company's words, more visitors could taste what Daimatsu Suisan at Omicho has to offer.
chef_bio.philosophy: Let a working fish wholesaler's freshness speak for itself at an everyday, approachable price.
chef_bio.specialty: Torched nodoguro (blackthroat seaperch) aburi-sashi, raw oysters, sashimi platters, seafood rice bowls, and a signature senbero set pairing a draft beer with assorted sashimi and a small side for around 1,000 yen.

anecdotes.text: The popular senbero set comes with a draft beer, three kinds of sashimi and a small side dish for around 1,000 yen, making it an ideal quick market drink, with the torched nodoguro's fat melting just enough to pair beautifully with sake.
Open from around 9am, it's a stand-and-eat spot for oysters, sea urchin and scallops, where two older women run the counter and look after customers.

With the July 2023 change of format the shop dropped its robatayaki grill and renamed itself Omicho Shunsai, centering the menu on the popular senbero set alongside raw oysters and torched nodoguro sashimi.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The menu centers on sashimi, torched nodoguro and seafood bowls, which are eaten with Japanese soy sauce (brewed with wheat); no wheat-free soy alternative is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: An entirely seafood-based market counter (oysters, sea urchin, scallops, sashimi, nodoguro) with no documented plant-based options; cross-contact with fish is unavoidable here.

cultural_comfort.note: A bustling, no-frills market stand where two older women serve the counter and little to no English is expected, so a smile, pointing at the senbero set, and a few Japanese phrases will carry you a long way.

places.name: 第7ギョーザの店 (Dai-7 Gyoza no Mise)
places.gf_detail: This is a gyoza (dumpling) specialty shop. The signature White Gyoza is defined by its thick, chewy wheat-flour skin, so the core menu is fundamentally wheat-based and not safe for celiacs. Dipping uses regular (wheat-based) soy sauce/vinegar. No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or allergen accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: The gyoza filling is meat-based (pork). This is a meat-dumpling specialist with no documented vegan or vegetable-only options; not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
places.hours_raw: 1F 11:00-21:00 / 2F 11:00-21:00 / takeout 11:00-21:00 (shortened due to staff shortage). Official site lists 11:00-02:00, but Tabelog notes current shortened hours to 21:00.
places.neighborhood: Mori-no-sato (near Kanazawa University, along the Saigawa River)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A beloved Kanazawa gyoza institution affectionately known to locals simply as Dai-nana ("the 7th"). It belongs to the White Gyoza (ホワイト餃子) technical-chain lineage whose head shop is in Noda, Chiba, but it has become a soul-food landmark of Kanazawa in its own right, drawing long

chef_bio.origin: Kanazawa (もりの里, near Kanazawa University, beside the river)
chef_bio.background: A beloved Kanazawa gyoza institution affectionately known to locals simply as Dai-nana ("the 7th"). It belongs to the White Gyoza (ホワイト餃子) technical-chain lineage whose head shop is in Noda, Chiba, but it has become a soul-food landmark of Kanazawa in its own right, drawing long lines even on weekdays.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop frames itself as a specialist that can offer this distinctive product at low prices precisely because it focuses on gyoza alone, urging diners to eat them piping hot, fresh off the griddle.
chef_bio.specialty: Its signature is the White Gyoza: round, plump dumplings with a thick, chewy wheat skin that turns crisp and golden when deep-fried in oil. Around 90% of customers order them. The shop also serves steamed and boiled (water) dumplings and gyoza set meals at specialist-shop prices.

anecdotes.text: Locals call it simply 'Dai-nana' (the 7th), and roughly 90% of customers order the White Gyoza; the round, thick-skinned dumplings deep-fried to a crisp are its defining dish, often with waits of an hour even on weekdays.
The riverside upper-floor rooms offer seasonal views — cherry blossoms in spring, ayu fishing in summer, autumn foliage, and snow in winter.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Gyoza are fried/cooked in shared oil and equipment; the entire core menu is wheat-flour dumplings, so heavy gluten cross-contact is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard gyoza dipping sauce (soy sauce / vinegar / chili oil) is used; no gluten-free soy sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dumplings are pork-based and cooked on shared meat equipment; no vegan options documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual, no-reservation local gyoza counter where you can point at the famous round dumplings and order with a few words of Japanese, though expect a wait at peak times and little English.

places.name: 焼肉ホルモン 芝生 (Yakiniku Horumon Shibafu)
places.gf_detail: This is a yakiniku/horumon (grilled beef offal) restaurant whose signature dishes revolve around house white and black sauces — the black sauce is a sweet-savory miso preparation — and a finishing bowl of wheat noodles. Soy- and miso-based marinades plus wheat noodles make this menu unsuitable for celiacs, and no gluten-free accommodation is documented. The site mentions allergy information is available, so a GF or wheat-sensitive diner should ask directly, but it is not a celiac-safe destination.
places.vegan_detail: The entire concept is grilled beef and beef offal (horumon, tongue, throat, heart). There are no documented vegan or vegetarian set offerings; the menu is built around meat. Not a vegan-friendly destination.
places.hours_raw: Tuesday–Sunday & holidays: 17:00–00:00 (L.O. 23:00); Monday closed
places.neighborhood: Kanazawa Stn east exit / Konohanamachi / Nanatsuya area
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A compact horumon-yakiniku counter about three minutes' walk from the east exit of Kanazawa Station, just past the Tsuzumi-mon gate. The shop is built around a single idea: pursuing what it calls the ultimate way to eat meat.

chef_bio.background: A compact horumon-yakiniku counter about three minutes' walk from the east exit of Kanazawa Station, just past the Tsuzumi-mon gate. The shop is built around a single idea: pursuing what it calls the ultimate way to eat meat.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen frames good horumon as the mastery of five elements: the freshness of the meat, the sauce, the griddle, the grilling technique, and the finishing noodles.
chef_bio.specialty: The house Shibafu course is served in three movements — a 'white sauce' stage featuring premium cuts such as upper tongue and throat, a 'black sauce' stage of beef heart and offal dressed in a sweet-savory miso preparation, and a finishing bowl of special thick noodles designed to soak up the accumulated meat juices. Staff grill each piece individually in front of the diner using a tilted-griddle technique to lock in the juices.

anecdotes.text: The signature service is a tilted-griddle grilling method: rather than letting diners cook freely, staff grill each cut individually and tilt the plate so the flavorful juices are preserved and served at the optimal moment.
Sourcing prioritizes freshness, so the available cuts on the menu can change with what arrives.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Signature dishes center on house white and black sauces, the black being a sweet-savory miso preparation, and the meal ends with wheat finishing noodles — indicating soy/miso/wheat are integral and not avoidable.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is entirely grilled beef and beef offal cooked on shared griddles; no plant-based offerings documented.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The official site notes that allergy information is available (linked on the site), so diners with allergies can request details.

positives.text: An English menu and international reservation support are offered, easing communication about ingredients for non-Japanese diners.

cultural_comfort.note: The shop provides an English menu and supports reservations from international visitors, so a foreign student can dine here comfortably with minimal Japanese.

noodles.notes: Specially-formulated thick noodles (特製太麺) built to grab and balance the concentrated meat-juice sauce rather than carry soup. No documented detail on shape, hydration, or whether they are handmade.

ramen.signature_bowl: 極〆そば (Goku-Shime Soba) — the course-finishing thick noodles tossed in the rendered meat juices and the house sauce, ordered at the end of the grilled-meat meal.
ramen.chashu: None. This is a grill finisher, not a ramen — no chashu component is documented.
notable_toppings: soft egg / onsen-style egg (documented on the finishing noodles)

gf.note: Wheat thick noodles plus a miso/soy-style house sauce that almost certainly contains wheat — not safe for celiacs, and this is a yakiniku house with no documented gluten-free noodle option.

vegan.note: The entire dish is built on rendered beef/pork meat juices from the grill plus an animal-derived sauce and egg topping — there is no plant-based version.

ramen.pantheon: This is not a ramen bowl at all but the soul of a Kanazawa horumon house's grand finale: thick noodles dragged through the rendered fat and juices of premium wagyu and offal, dressed in a house white sauce and a multi-miso black sauce. It belongs to the brothless shime-soba / abura-soba lineage — you taste the grill, not a simmered soup.

places.highlight: This is not a ramen bowl at all but the soul of a Kanazawa horumon house's grand finale: thick noodles dragged through the rendered fat and juices of premium wagyu and offal, dressed in a house white sauce and a multi-miso black sauce. It belongs to the brothless shime-soba / abura-soba lineage — you taste the grill, not a simmered soup.

places.name: 似故礼 (Nigokorei)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free information is documented. As a traditional Japanese tea house serving sweets such as zenzai (anko/red bean) with nama-fu (wheat gluten cake) and souffle (wheat flour), most items are not gluten-free. A celiac traveler should not assume any safe options; ask directly.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan information is documented. Some menu items (matcha, certain anko-based sweets) may be plant-based, but souffle/cream items contain dairy and eggs, and no claims about vegan preparation exist. Ask staff about specific items.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Fri 09:00-13:00; Sat/Sun/Holidays 09:00-14:00
places.neighborhood: Nagamachi samurai district area
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Nigokorei is a quiet, antique-styled tea house tucked into Kanazawa's Nagamachi samurai-residence district, set inside a wooden machiya estimated to be more than 140 years old. The space is filled with the atmosphere of old Japan, with seating arranged so guests can look out over

chef_bio.background: Nigokorei is a quiet, antique-styled tea house tucked into Kanazawa's Nagamachi samurai-residence district, set inside a wooden machiya estimated to be more than 140 years old. The space is filled with the atmosphere of old Japan, with seating arranged so guests can look out over a traditional interior garden (中庭).
chef_bio.philosophy: The proprietor places special emphasis on fine ceramics, particularly local Kutani ware, presenting tea and sweets in carefully selected vessels so the tableware is part of the experience.
chef_bio.specialty: Tea-house sweets served on carefully chosen pottery, including chestnut zenzai (栗入りぜんざい) made with nama-fu, a souffle set (スフレセット) with rich whipped cream, and ceremonial matcha.

anecdotes.text: The cafe is known for striking, eye-catching hina dolls displayed inside, adding to its distinctive retro atmosphere.
Visitors describe the chestnut zenzai's chewy nama-fu against rich, sweet anko, and a moist, gently sweet souffle that pairs well with coffee.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, traditional owner-run kissaten in a 140-year-old house; little to no English is likely, so basic Japanese phrases and a calm, quiet manner will help.

places.name: 茶房 やなぎ庵 (Saboh Yanagian)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. The menu centers on wagashi, parfaits and matcha sweets that typically involve wheat or shared kitchen prep, and many sweet/sauce components use gluten-containing ingredients. Some items (e.g. anmitsu with agar and azuki) may be naturally wheat-light, but cross-contact is unaddressed — a celiac visitor would need to ask in detail and should not assume safety.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan venue and no vegan menu is advertised. Some signature items are plant-based by nature (the gold-leaf cream anmitsu uses domestic agar, red bean paste and matcha warabimochi), but parfaits and afternoon-tea sets typically include dairy/cream and the kitchen is not vegan-oriented. A vegan would need to ask which items omit dairy/eggs.
places.hours_raw: 10:00-17:00 (L.O. 16:30), daily. 1F gallery shop Kanazawa Shitsurae open 9:00-18:00.
places.neighborhood: ひがし茶屋街 (Higashi Chaya District)
places.notes: Saboh Yanagian is a second-floor wa-café inside 金澤しつらえ, a gallery-shop run by the renowned Kanazawa gold-leaf brand Hakuichi, set within a tea-house (chaya) building over 200 years old in the Higashi Chaya District. The space was restored on the floor plan of the former 'Kyū-Moro

roles: Operated by Hakuichi (箔一) gold-leaf brand
chef_bio.background: Saboh Yanagian is a second-floor wa-café inside 金澤しつらえ, a gallery-shop run by the renowned Kanazawa gold-leaf brand Hakuichi, set within a tea-house (chaya) building over 200 years old in the Higashi Chaya District. The space was restored on the floor plan of the former 'Kyū-Moroeya' era, when geisha performed their arts there, and blends Edo-period architectural design with contemporary touches — including a glass connecting corridor finished in gold leaf.
chef_bio.philosophy: The café aims to offer 'a quiet moment separated from everyday life,' inviting guests to feel as if they are dissolving into a Japanese painting while gazing at the historic streetscape.
chef_bio.specialty: Cream anmitsu crowned with a single sheet of gold leaf (over domestic agar, red bean paste and matcha warabimochi), seasonal fruit parfaits, matcha served with Kanazawa wagashi, and seasonal afternoon-tea sets.

anecdotes.text: The café is named after the 'Mikaeri Yanagi' (backward-glance willow), the iconic tree standing right in front of the building and a symbol of the Higashi Chaya geisha district; table seats look out onto the swaying willow.
The building is a restored 200-year-old chaya, and the second-floor connecting corridor was rebuilt in glass and finished with gold leaf, reflecting Hakuichi's gold-leaf craft heritage.

cultural_comfort.note: A polished, tourist-friendly café in a famous gold-leaf brand's gallery; an English Tabelog page and card/QR payment exist, so a foreign student can visit comfortably with little Japanese.

places.name: ターバンカレー 総本店 (Turban Curry Sohonten)
places.gf_detail: Kanazawa curry is built on a dark, thick roux thickened with wheat flour and topped with breaded pork cutlet, so the food is wheat-heavy and not celiac-safe. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: This is a meat-centric curry shop; signature dishes feature pork cutlet, sausage, hamburg steak, and a roux made with meat stock. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented.
places.neighborhood: Korinbo / Hirosaka
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Turban Curry opened in 1971 (Showa 46) in Kanazawa's Korinbo district and has served its signature Kanazawa curry for over half a century, celebrating its 50th anniversary in August 2021. This Hirosaka storefront, near the 21st Century Museum of Contemporary Art, is the original

chef_bio.background: Turban Curry opened in 1971 (Showa 46) in Kanazawa's Korinbo district and has served its signature Kanazawa curry for over half a century, celebrating its 50th anniversary in August 2021. This Hirosaka storefront, near the 21st Century Museum of Contemporary Art, is the original flagship (sohonten). The shop relocated to its current spot in 2005.
chef_bio.philosophy: A streamlined, efficient counter operation focused on consistently turning out its rich, dark, medium-spiced curry rather than frills.
chef_bio.specialty: Classic Kanazawa-style curry: a thick, dark, glossy roux made with more than twenty spices, served over firm rice on a stainless-steel plate. Signature plates include the L Set (pork cutlet, sausage, and hamburg steak together), pork cutlet (roskatsu) curry, and a house-made cream croquette curry, all available as takeout bento too.

anecdotes.text: The founder of the now-famous Go Go Curry chain is said to have worked at Turban Curry before starting his own brand, placing this shop at the roots of the Kanazawa curry scene.
The shop marked its 50th anniversary in August 2021, half a century of continuous service in Korinbo.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Curry roux is wheat-flour based and the signature toppings are breaded/fried cutlets and croquettes, so wheat is pervasive throughout the kitchen.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is centered on pork cutlet, sausage, hamburg steak and a meat-stock roux; no plant-based items are documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A no-frills local counter curry shop with a quick, efficient ordering flow and cash-only payment, so come with yen and be ready to order promptly; a little guidance helps but the menu of cutlet/sausage/hamburg curries is easy to point at.

places.name: 能加万菜 と成屋 (Noukabanzai Tonariya)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. The menu centers on nodoguro kamameshi finished as dashi-chazuke (broth poured over rice), and Japanese dashi/seasonings here typically involve soy sauce, which contains wheat. No gluten-free menu, dedicated preparation, or celiac accommodation is documented. Celiac travelers should avoid.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan or vegetarian. This is a seafood specialist whose signature dish is nodoguro clay-pot rice topped with ikura and finished with fish dashi; no plant-based options are documented.
places.neighborhood: Omicho Market (Shimozutsumi-machi / Musashi entrance)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Tonariya is the nodoguro-kamameshi specialty shop of the local Noukabanzai brand, a Kanazawa group built around Ishikawa regional ingredients. It puts the spotlight on nodoguro (blackthroat seabream, akamutsu) — a fatty, refined white fish so prized it is nicknamed 'the toro of w

chef_bio.origin: Opened May 1, 2021 inside Kanazawa's historic Omicho Market, near the Musashi entrance.
chef_bio.background: Tonariya is the nodoguro-kamameshi specialty shop of the local Noukabanzai brand, a Kanazawa group built around Ishikawa regional ingredients. It puts the spotlight on nodoguro (blackthroat seabream, akamutsu) — a fatty, refined white fish so prized it is nicknamed 'the toro of white fish.'
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop describes its signature as 'a beauty so simple that nothing can be hidden' — meaning the pared-back preparation leaves nowhere to mask anything but the quality of the fish and rice.
chef_bio.specialty: Nodoguro kamameshi: each clay pot is cooked to order from raw rice, taking about 20 minutes, and is served with a nodoguro broth carefully drawn from the bones. The dish is designed to be eaten in three stages — first plain to taste the fish, then dressed up with ikura and condiments, and finally as dashi-chazuke with broth poured over the rice.

anecdotes.text: Nodoguro is described as a premium white fish whose fat is so refined it is called 'the toro of white fish,' and the kamameshi is meant to be enjoyed three ways: as-is, with condiments, and as tea-soaked rice.
The nodoguro broth served alongside the rice is carefully made from the fish bones.

cultural_comfort.note: A friendly single-dish counter spot inside the bustling Omicho Market where a basic greeting and pointing at the famous nodoguro kamameshi will get you through comfortably, though the staff and menu are primarily Japanese.

places.name: PETRA BAKE&COFFEE（ペトラ ベイク&コーヒー） (Petra Bake & Coffee)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. The bakery openly uses wheat, including spelt wheat (スペルト小麦) in items such as the carrot cake. While some items use rice flour (米粉, e.g. banana bread), everything is baked in the same small shop with no dedicated gluten-free facility or stated allergen protocol, so cross-contamination with wheat is likely. Coeliac travelers should avoid the baked goods. Plain espresso/coffee drinks themselves contain no gluten.
places.vegan_detail: Vegan-friendly options exist but the shop is not fully vegan. The bakery lists "animal-product-free" (動物性不使用) baked goods alongside conventional items so customers can choose what suits them, and soy milk is available for lattes (e.g. iced soy latte). However many baked goods contain dairy/eggs and the carrot cake contains walnuts, so ask which specific items are animal-free on the day. No claim of a fully separate vegan prep area.
places.neighborhood: Omicho Market entrance (Aokusamachi / Omicho Ichiba-kan, Musashi side)
places.notes: Petra Bake & Coffee is a small, design-forward coffee stand that opened on May 21, 2023 right at the entrance of Kanazawa's Omicho Market. It is the second venture from the team behind the well-loved MORON CAFE in the Nagamachi samurai district, bringing the same organic, homemad

chef_bio.origin: 金沢市・長町のMORON CAFE系列の2号店
chef_bio.background: Petra Bake & Coffee is a small, design-forward coffee stand that opened on May 21, 2023 right at the entrance of Kanazawa's Omicho Market. It is the second venture from the team behind the well-loved MORON CAFE in the Nagamachi samurai district, bringing the same organic, homemade ethos into a compact grab-and-go space styled like an overseas cafe.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop deliberately bakes with as much organic flour as possible and offers a mix of rice-flour, spelt-wheat and animal-product-free items so that customers can pick what fits their own lifestyle and diet.
chef_bio.specialty: Homemade baked goods built around organic ingredients - a spelt-wheat carrot cake with walnuts, banana bread made with rice flour, plus cookies, muffins and scones - paired with espresso drinks. Signatures include the self-made spiced cafe mocha and an iced soy latte using aromatic espresso and smooth soy milk.

anecdotes.text: The espresso is a light-roast original blend sourced from the GOOD4LIFE 36CAFE coffee stand, chosen for a clean, gently sweet flavor.
It is the second location of Nagamachi's popular MORON CAFE, opened at the Omicho Market entrance to serve shoppers a quick, stylish break.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat, including spelt wheat (used in the carrot cake), is baked on-site alongside rice-flour items in a single small shop, so cross-contamination is likely; no dedicated gluten-free area is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Animal-product-free baked goods are offered on the same counter as dairy/egg items; no separate vegan prep is documented, and the carrot cake contains walnuts.

positives.text: The bakery explicitly offers animal-product-free (vegan) items and soy milk for lattes, so plant-based diners have real choices.
Some items are made with rice flour (米粉), such as the banana bread, giving non-strict diners lighter-wheat options.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual, stylish takeout coffee stand at the market entrance where pointing at the counter pastries and a few words of Japanese will get you through easily; staff likely have limited English but the order is simple.

places.name: つば甚 (Tsubajin)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional Kaga kaiseki ryotei built around wheat-based soy sauce, bonito/kombu dashi, and dishes like jibu-ni (which uses flour-dusted meat/fish) and tai no karamushi. There is no documented gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or celiac accommodation. Not safe for celiacs. A formal multi-course kaiseki cannot reasonably be made gluten-free here without prior arrangement, and no such accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: A 1752-founded ryotei specializing in Kaga cuisine built on fresh seafood (crab porridge, tai no karamushi, sashimi) and dashi-based dishes. Nothing vegan or vegetarian is documented on the official site or other sources. As a fully reservation-based kaiseki house with chefs who prepare bespoke courses, a vegan/vegetarian course might be possible only by arranging well in advance when booking, but it is not offered as standard and is not confirmed.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-14:00 (L.O. 13:00), 17:00-21:00 (L.O. 19:00); closed Wednesdays and year-end/New Year. Completely reservation-based.
places.neighborhood: Teramachi (Nomachi / Nishi Chaya District, toward Saigawa Ohashi bridge)
places.notes: Tsubajin is Kanazawa's oldest ryotei, founded in 1752 (Horeki 2). Its roots go back further: the Tsuba family served the Maeda clan of Kaga as hereditary tsuba (sword-guard) craftsmen from the late 16th century, and the third-generation Jinbei opened a small teahouse called Tsuba

chef_bio.origin: Kanazawa (Teramachi, near Nomachi and the Saigawa river), Ishikawa Prefecture, Japan
chef_bio.background: Tsubajin is Kanazawa's oldest ryotei, founded in 1752 (Horeki 2). Its roots go back further: the Tsuba family served the Maeda clan of Kaga as hereditary tsuba (sword-guard) craftsmen from the late 16th century, and the third-generation Jinbei opened a small teahouse called Tsubaya alongside that trade, which grew into the restaurant known today as Tsubajin. The house has carried its craft of Kaga cuisine for roughly 260 years on its present footing as a restaurant.
chef_bio.philosophy: The house describes its enduring aim across 350 years as flavors that satisfy and a heart that thinks of the guest, served entirely in private rooms.
chef_bio.specialty: Kaga regional cuisine served as kaiseki: signature dishes include jibu-ni (the representative Kanazawa stew), tai no karamushi (sea bream stuffed and steamed), renmushi (steamed lotus dishes), and a celebrated crab rice porridge (kani-gayu) breakfast. Courses use fresh local seafood and Kaga vegetables.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant has hosted many leading historical and literary figures, including the statesman Ito Hirobumi and the writer Akutagawa Ryunosuke.
Its name and lineage come from the Tsuba family's former trade as sword-guard craftsmen for the Maeda lords of Kaga before the family turned to cooking.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Traditional Kaga kaiseki built on wheat-based soy sauce and bonito/kombu dashi; signature jibu-ni traditionally uses flour-dusted protein, so gluten is intrinsic to the cuisine. No gluten-free preparation is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Kaga cuisine here relies on standard Japanese soy sauce and dashi-based seasonings, which contain wheat; no wheat-free soy alternative is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menus center on seafood (crab porridge, sea bream, sashimi) and dashi; no vegan or vegetarian course is documented, so animal-derived stock and seafood are pervasive.

positives.text: Fully reservation-based with bespoke multi-course service, so dietary requests can in principle be raised when booking well in advance, though no specific accommodation is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a formal, expensive, reservation-only historic ryotei with private-room kaiseki service and Japanese etiquette expectations; a foreign student should come with a host or guide, dress appropriately, and arrange any dietary needs far in advance.

places.name: 喫茶オリーブ (Kissa Olive)
places.gf_detail: A traditional kissaten whose menu centers on toast and sandwich sets (wheat bread) plus おしるこ; nothing indicates gluten-free options or any allergen handling, so it is not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: Old-style coffee shop serving buttered toast, sandwiches and milk-based drinks; no vegan menu or plant-milk options are documented.
places.hours_raw: 7:30-17:00, closed Wednesdays
places.neighborhood: Central Kanazawa (around Nagamachi / Omicho)
places.cuisine: Retro coffee house (junkissa) / old-fashioned coffee house
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A long-running, old-fashioned kissaten tucked into the residential Nagamachi area near Kanazawa's samurai-house district and within reach of Omicho Market. It opens early at 7:30 in the morning, giving the neighborhood a quiet, unhurried place to start the day.

chef_bio.background: A long-running, old-fashioned kissaten tucked into the residential Nagamachi area near Kanazawa's samurai-house district and within reach of Omicho Market. It opens early at 7:30 in the morning, giving the neighborhood a quiet, unhurried place to start the day.
chef_bio.philosophy: A calm, classic kissaten atmosphere that welcomes both families and solo visitors.
chef_bio.specialty: Coffee is the heart of the shop, with around ten kinds of beans the owner selects and offers, and toast and sandwich sets are the signature plates. House-made おしるこ (sweet red-bean soup) and a homemade honey-lemon drink round out the menu.

anecdotes.text: Regarded among Kanazawa's beloved retro kissaten, the master is particular about coffee — pairing carefully chosen beans with roast — and the sandwiches are singled out as especially good.
Known for toast sets generously topped with vegetables and fruit, plus homemade おしるこ and a complimentary honey-lemon service.

cultural_comfort.note: A quiet neighborhood kissaten run in the old-fashioned way with little to no English, cash only — easiest to enjoy if you bring a Japanese-speaking friend or are comfortable ordering by pointing.

places.name: 大衆割烹 大関 (Taishu Kappo Ozeki)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional oden and kappo izakaya built around dashi-simmered broth, eel with sauce glaze, and Kaga local dishes. The oden broth, the kabayaki-style eel sauce, and the simmered dishes all rely on wheat-containing soy sauce, and there is no dedicated gluten-free preparation. It is not a safe choice for celiacs. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: The kitchen is centered on seafood, eel, crab, and fish-based dashi (the oden broth is made from herring/iwashi dashi). Nearly every dish involves animal stock or seafood, and there is no documented vegan or vegetarian menu. Not suitable for vegans or strict vegetarians.
places.hours_raw: 16:30-21:30 (Tabelog lists close 21:30; one local source lists last hours to 22:00). Closed Tuesdays (open on the eve of holidays).
places.neighborhood: Kiguramachi (near Katamachi / Korinbo)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Founded in December 1957 (Showa 32) in Kanazawa's Kikuramachi quarter near Katamachi and Korinbo, Ozeki is a long-loved neighborhood kappo-izakaya that still carries an authentic Showa-era atmosphere with its long oden counter and warm, homey feel.

chef_bio.background: Founded in December 1957 (Showa 32) in Kanazawa's Kikuramachi quarter near Katamachi and Korinbo, Ozeki is a long-loved neighborhood kappo-izakaya that still carries an authentic Showa-era atmosphere with its long oden counter and warm, homey feel.
chef_bio.philosophy: A welcoming, unpretentious neighborhood place where regulars gather at the counter; the proprietress is noted for creating a warm, homey atmosphere.
chef_bio.specialty: Kanazawa-style oden simmered in a refined, deep dashi broth (including seasonal items like crab face in November-December), and live-ordered freshwater eel prepared to the customer's choice of white-grilled, sauce-glazed, or combined. Kaga local dishes such as jibuni also feature.

anecdotes.text: The proprietor Heikichi Hirayasu prepares eel that is ordered live by the fish, combining Kanto-style back-cutting with Kansai-style grill-only cooking for a fragrant, crispy exterior and sweet fat.
On Mondays nearly all oden items are offered at a special low price, drawing crowds of regulars.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Oden, simmered dishes, and sauce-glazed eel are built on wheat-based soy sauce and fish dashi; there is no separate gluten-free preparation.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Eel is offered sauce-glazed (kabayaki style) and dishes use standard soy-based seasonings, which contain wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The oden broth and most dishes are made with fish/seafood dashi, and the kitchen centers on eel, crab, and seafood; vegan separation is not possible.

cultural_comfort.note: A cozy, old-school Showa-era counter izakaya where a little Japanese and an easygoing attitude go a long way; menus and conversation are mostly in Japanese, so a translation app helps for a foreign student.

places.name: おでん 若葉 (Oden Wakaba)
places.gf_detail: A traditional oden house built on a continuously replenished dried-sardine dashi seasoned in Japanese style; soy sauce (wheat-based) and miso are central, and items like tsumire and dote-yaki are not gluten-free. No gluten-free accommodation is documented. Not celiac-safe.
places.vegan_detail: The entire menu rests on a fish (niboshi/sardine) dashi, and signature dishes include sardine tsumire, pork-belly dote-yaki, octopus and bay snails. There are a few plant-based oden items (daikon, taro, konnyaku, grilled tofu) but they are all simmered in the fish broth, so the place is not suitable for vegans.
places.hours_raw: 17:00–22:00 (some listings note up to 22:30). Closed Mondays (and Tuesdays per Tabelog); first-come, first-served.
places.neighborhood: 石引 (Ishibiki) — east of Kenrokuen / Hondamachi
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Founded in 1935 (Showa 10), Wakaba is one of the great old-school Kanazawa oden houses, tucked away in the residential Ishibiki neighborhood east of Kenrokuen rather than in the tourist quarters. It is run today by third-generation owner Yoshikawa Masashi, working alongside his f

chef_bio.background: Founded in 1935 (Showa 10), Wakaba is one of the great old-school Kanazawa oden houses, tucked away in the residential Ishibiki neighborhood east of Kenrokuen rather than in the tourist quarters. It is run today by third-generation owner Yoshikawa Masashi, working alongside his father, the second-generation proprietor.
chef_bio.philosophy: Rather than chasing the modern 'Kanazawa oden' boom, the family deliberately keeps everything the same — their stated ethos is that they have never changed and never will, and that constancy is exactly why regulars keep coming back across generations.
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-made iwashi (sardine) tsumire — firm on the outside, remarkably fluffy inside, blended with onion and ginger and drinking up the broth; dote-yaki, simmered pork belly crowned with sweet white miso and a heap of minced green onion; plus Kanazawa classics like daikon, taro, octopus and bay snails. Everything bathes in a clear, lightly seasoned dashi built on a niboshi (dried sardine) base that has been continuously topped up since the shop opened.

anecdotes.text: The second-generation owner is the one who created the now-signature sardine tsumire, a recipe carried on by his son.
Prep starts around 9:10 in the morning so the pot is perfectly ready by opening, and the same broth has been replenished rather than replaced for decades.
The late novelist Ikenami Shotaro, who spent time in Kanazawa, was a regular and later wrote about his visits here.

Diners sit at a single U-shaped counter around the bubbling pot, choosing items by sight; the atmosphere is unpretentious and mixes elderly regulars with younger groups, and solo guests are made to feel at ease.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All oden, including otherwise plant-based items like daikon and tofu, is simmered together in a shared fish-and-soy dashi, so cross-contact with wheat and fish is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth and seasoning are traditional Japanese soy- and miso-based, which means wheat-containing soy sauce; no wheat-free alternative is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The base dashi is made from dried sardines (niboshi), and every item shares that fish broth, so no dish is free of animal products.

cultural_comfort.note: A welcoming, populist old-school counter where even solo diners are made comfortable, but it is a traditional Japanese-only local spot, so a little Japanese (or pointing at the pot) will go a long way.

places.name: COYA.（コヤ） (COYA.)
places.gf_detail: This is a wheat-flour bakery; bread is the entire product line. No gluten-free items, dedicated equipment, or allergy accommodations are documented. Not suitable for celiac or gluten-avoidant diners.
places.vegan_detail: A few of the signature items lean plant-forward — hard breads such as the raisin/currant loaf and Dainagon (red bean) stick may be made without dairy or egg — but the bakery also sells dairy/egg-rich goods (lemon cake, scones, cookies) and publishes no vegan labeling. Vegans would need to ask about each item; nothing is confirmed.
places.hours_raw: Thu–Fri 7:00–sold out; Sat–Sun 9:00–sold out; Sunday café 11:00–17:00 (L.O. 16:00). Closed Mon/Tue/Wed. (An older source listed 10:00 open and closed Mon/Tue, so opening times may vary — confirm via Instagram.)
places.neighborhood: 尾山町 (Oyama-cho) — beside Oyama Shrine, within walking distan
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] COYA. is a small bakery in the Oyama-cho neighborhood beside Oyama Shrine, run single-handedly by baker Ayako Tajiri. Its plain white-washed facade blends into the retro streetscape around the shrine. A 2019 renovation added a Sunday-only café space on the second floor, where cof

chef_bio.background: COYA. is a small bakery in the Oyama-cho neighborhood beside Oyama Shrine, run single-handedly by baker Ayako Tajiri. Its plain white-washed facade blends into the retro streetscape around the shrine. A 2019 renovation added a Sunday-only café space on the second floor, where coffee from a Toyama roaster is served alongside café-exclusive breads.
chef_bio.philosophy: Tajiri puts quality over quantity, baking only as much as she can make well each day and closing once it sells out. In her words she wants to keep making 'unpretentious bread you won't tire of even eating it every day,' letting the natural sweetness of the ingredients come through.
chef_bio.specialty: Hard-crust breads are the heart of the shop: a raisin-and-currant loaf, a Dainagon sweet-red-bean stick, and a chocolate baguette (choco-france). The house pain de mie comes in three styles — plain, sesame, and walnut — and draws daily regulars. The most talked-about item is the lemon cake, a French-style pound cake folded with lemon juice.

anecdotes.text: The owner refined her craft at Hirami Pan, a celebrated Kanazawa bakery, before opening her own small shop.
A 2019 renovation introduced a Sunday-only second-floor café pouring coffee from a Toyama roaster, popular with both locals and tourists.
Decorative red peppers hang at the storefront as a charm to ward off misfortune.

gf_cross_contamination.text: A wheat-flour bakery where bread is the entire product line, so airborne flour and shared surfaces make cross-contamination unavoidable; not celiac-safe.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dairy- and egg-rich items (lemon cake, scones, cookies) are made in the same small kitchen as the breads, so shared equipment is likely; no vegan separation is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny neighborhood bakery run single-handedly with no English signage — point at what you want, pay cash, and go early before the popular loaves sell out.

places.name: シンタテベイクストア (Shintate Bake Store)
places.gf_detail: This is a wheat bakery — its entire identity is bread made from home-milled organic wheat and whole wheat flour. There is no gluten-free offering and it is not safe for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: No documented vegan labeling. Breads use natural yeast and organic wheat; some items (canelé, cherry pie, pastries) clearly contain dairy/eggs. Simple lean breads may be plant-based but this is not confirmed, so vegans should ask about specific loaves.
places.hours_raw: 9:00-16:00 (closes earlier when sold out). Closed Monday and Tuesday.
places.neighborhood: 新竪町 (Shintatemachi)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Shintate Bake Store opened on April 12, 2023, in the Shintatemachi shopping street as the sister shop of the much-loved Kanazawa bakery Sakanoue Bakery (坂の上ベーカリー), run by the Wakai couple. In September 2023 the original Sakanoue Bakery closed and its bread workshop moved here, ma

chef_bio.background: Shintate Bake Store opened on April 12, 2023, in the Shintatemachi shopping street as the sister shop of the much-loved Kanazawa bakery Sakanoue Bakery (坂の上ベーカリー), run by the Wakai couple. In September 2023 the original Sakanoue Bakery closed and its bread workshop moved here, making Shintate Bake Store the continuation of that bakery's craft.
chef_bio.philosophy: The theme is 'healthy and delicious' (健やかで美味しく) — bread and sweets made with ingredients gentle on children, adults, and the environment, using pesticide-free organic wheat that is milled in-house and natural yeast. The owners deliberately do not serve coffee in the shop, encouraging customers to wander the shopping street and pair their bread with a drink from a neighboring cafe.
chef_bio.specialty: More than 20 kinds of bread and pastries are laid out daily from around 9 a.m., centered on breads made from home-milled organic whole wheat. Signature items include the Fukumitsuya canelé made with rice shochu from the local Fukumitsuya sake brewery, and the playful 'SS Cherry Pie' (a Twin Peaks reference, where SS stands for Shintate Street).

anecdotes.text: The shop intentionally avoids selling coffee so that customers will explore the Shintatemachi shopping street and combine the bread with offerings from nearby coffee shops.
It is the successor to Sakanoue Bakery: when that shop closed in September 2023, its bread workshop was relocated into Shintate Bake Store.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a flour-based bakery using home-milled organic wheat and whole wheat as its core ingredient; airborne flour and shared surfaces make it unsafe for celiacs.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Many items contain dairy and eggs (e.g. canelé, cherry pie); no separate vegan production is documented.

positives.text: Ingredients emphasize pesticide-free organic wheat and natural yeast with a 'healthy and delicious' philosophy, so the bread is made from carefully chosen, transparent ingredients.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed neighborhood bakery where you simply point at the bread you want and pay (cash or QR codes like PayPay accepted), so almost no Japanese is needed.

places.name: 味処 大工町 よし村 (Ajidokoro Daikumachi Yoshimura)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. This is a traditional Kaga kappo built on soy sauce and dashi, and the signature jibu-ni is thickened and includes fresh wheat gluten (sudare-fu/fu). No gluten-free menu or dedicated handling is documented; not safe for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The menu is centered on Sea of Japan seafood (sashimi, sweet shrimp, nodoguro, snow crab) and meat (duck in jibu-ni), with dashi-based seasoning throughout. No vegan options are documented.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-14:00 (reservation required), Dinner 17:00-22:30. Closed Sundays (and the following Monday when Sunday-Monday fall on consecutive holidays).
places.neighborhood: Daikumachi, Katamachi/Korinbo area (near the Saigawa river)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Founded in 1977 in Kanazawa's Daikumachi quarter, a small street historically lined with kappo restaurants near the Saigawa river. The kitchen is now led by the second-generation proprietor, Yoshimura Ryoichiro, who cooks at the counter alongside a head chef in the classic kappo

chef_bio.background: Founded in 1977 in Kanazawa's Daikumachi quarter, a small street historically lined with kappo restaurants near the Saigawa river. The kitchen is now led by the second-generation proprietor, Yoshimura Ryoichiro, who cooks at the counter alongside a head chef in the classic kappo style.
chef_bio.philosophy: A 'quality over quantity' ethos: the chef goes to market daily to hand-pick fresh fish, Kaga vegetables and local ingredients, keeping more than 70 seasonal dishes available so guests can eat omakase-style at the counter.
chef_bio.specialty: Kaga cuisine and Sea of Japan seafood. Signatures include jibu-ni (duck and Kaga vegetables with fresh wheat gluten in a thick sauce), Kaga lotus-root dishes such as hasu-mushi (steamed lotus) and lotus-root soup, sashimi of squid, sea urchin, sweet shrimp and konbu-cured fish, and seasonal specialties like hamo with matsutake and snow crab in winter.

anecdotes.text: A reviewer budgeting around 10,000 yen reported a course that ran about 11,000 yen yet far exceeded expectations in portion size and ingredient quality, singling out an exceptional abundance of matsutake with hamo and a creamy Kaga lotus-root soup.
The signature first-floor seating is a vermilion lacquered counter, where the appeal is watching the chef work and chatting casually while seasonal Kaga dishes are served.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Traditional Kaga kappo cooking relies heavily on soy sauce and dashi, and the house signature jibu-ni includes fresh wheat gluten (fu) and a flour-thickened sauce, so gluten is pervasive in the kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese soy sauce (wheat-containing) is used throughout Kaga cuisine and its signature dishes here; no wheat-free soy sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is centered on seafood and meat with dashi-based seasoning; no separate vegan preparation is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a traditional family-run kappo where you sit at the counter and chat with the chef; reservations and some basic Japanese (or a translation app) will make the experience much smoother for a foreign student.

places.name: のど黒めし本舗 いたる (Nodoguro-meshi Honpo Itaru)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The food centers on grilled fish over seasoned rice eaten with condiments and a finishing dashi broth, which typically rely on wheat-containing soy sauce and dashi seasonings. No gluten-free menu or celiac handling is documented; not suitable for strict celiacs.
