places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. This is a fish specialty shop built around nodoguro (blackthroat seaperch), yellowtail and other Kaga seafood, with fish-bone dashi broth. There are no documented vegan dishes.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 12:00-14:30, Dinner 17:30-20:30 (L.O. 20:00). Note: some listings show lunch-focused hours of 11:30-16:30/17:00 (L.O. 16:30). Hours vary by source/season — confirm by phone.
places.neighborhood: Korinbo / Katamachi (near Korinbo intersection)
places.notes: A specialty shop devoted almost entirely to one dish — nodoguro-meshi, a bowl of premium blackthroat seaperch. It belongs to the locally beloved Kanazawa group '酒と人情料理 いたる' (Sake and Heartfelt Cuisine Itaru), and the original nodoguro-meshi shop opened in 2015 to coincide with th

chef_bio.origin: Kanazawa, Ishikawa — a homegrown offshoot of the city's Itaru izakaya group.
chef_bio.background: A specialty shop devoted almost entirely to one dish — nodoguro-meshi, a bowl of premium blackthroat seaperch. It belongs to the locally beloved Kanazawa group '酒と人情料理 いたる' (Sake and Heartfelt Cuisine Itaru), and the original nodoguro-meshi shop opened in 2015 to coincide with the Hokuriku Shinkansen reaching Kanazawa. In February 2021 it relocated from Kakinokibatake to the Korinbo area, into a building originally constructed in 1964 (the year of the Tokyo Olympics) and lovingly renovated.
chef_bio.philosophy: Owner Ishiguro Itaru wanted the relocated shop to feel like a welcoming hatago (traditional travelers' inn), evoking an inn his family once ran. He filled the interior with heirlooms, including Inami wood carvings and paintings left by his late father.
chef_bio.specialty: Nodoguro-meshi (3,300 yen): generously piled, aromatically grilled nodoguro over seasoned rice, eaten in three stages — first plain, then with condiments, and finally as ochazuke with a rich, cloudy broth simmered from nodoguro bones. Other dishes include a sesame yellowtail bowl (ゴマぶり丼), and handmade sweets such as umeboshi-filled 梅つつみ.

anecdotes.text: When the shop moved to Korinbo in 2021, the owner designed it as a nostalgic 'hatago'-style retreat and decorated it with family heirlooms — Inami wood carvings and his late father's paintings.
The signature nodoguro-meshi is meant to be savored in three escalating stages, finishing with a cloudy soup extracted from the nodoguro's own bones poured over the rice.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes are seasoned-rice fish bowls finished with condiments and a fish-bone dashi broth; these preparations conventionally use wheat-containing soy sauce and dashi seasonings, so the menu is not safe for strict gluten avoidance.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Entirely seafood-based (nodoguro, yellowtail, Kaga fish) with fish-bone broth; no vegan options are documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A popular, tourist-friendly seafood-bowl shop with tablet ordering and card/QR payments; a little Japanese helps but you can manage with basics and pointing at the famous nodoguro bowl.

places.name: コーヒー処 ノバール (Coffee-dokoro Nobaru)
places.gf_detail: A classic Showa-era kissaten whose menu centers on pudding, parfaits, cakes, napolitan pasta, and sandwiches, all wheat- and dairy-based. No documented gluten-free offering and no allergy accommodation. Not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: The signature items (custard pudding with fruit and whipped cream, pudding parfaits, cakes) rely heavily on eggs and dairy, and the cafe also serves napolitan and sandwiches. No vegan or plant-based options are documented.
places.hours_raw: 9:00-19:00; closed Wednesdays. Hours and holidays may change, so check before visiting.
places.neighborhood: Arimatsu (south of Korinbo / Katamachi, near Arimatsu intersection)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A long-loved retro coffee house near the Arimatsu intersection, Nobaru has been operating for more than 40 years. It is run by a husband-and-wife team, the master and the mama, and its brick exterior and Showa-era interior, with stained-glass partitions casting soft light, have r

roles: mama (front of house, fruit and cream decoration)
chef_bio.background: A long-loved retro coffee house near the Arimatsu intersection, Nobaru has been operating for more than 40 years. It is run by a husband-and-wife team, the master and the mama, and its brick exterior and Showa-era interior, with stained-glass partitions casting soft light, have remained almost unchanged since it opened.
chef_bio.philosophy: The seating is laid out so that sight lines between customers are calculated to keep others unobtrusive, creating a relaxed space where guests can linger comfortably.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature is the homemade firm-set custard pudding: the master makes the pudding by hand and the mama decoratively cuts fruit and adds whipped cream on top. It is gently sweetened so it pairs well with coffee or tea. The pudding parfait (around 750 yen) is praised for its generous volume, and the cafe also serves classic kissaten fare like napolitan, sandwiches, cakes and breakfast sets, with most items under 1,000 yen.

anecdotes.text: Actress Yoshida Yo is said to have worked part-time at this cafe when she lived in Kanazawa in her younger years, which has added to its local fame.
The interior is nearly unchanged from when it opened, with stained-glass partitions that cast soft, reflective light, making it a popular photography spot.

cultural_comfort.note: A warm, family-run retro coffee house where the master and mama greet regulars; little to no English, so a few words of Japanese or pointing at the menu will make your visit smooth.

places.name: 特別名勝兼六園 堤亭 (Tokubetsu Meishō Kenrokuen Tsutsumitei)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The signature dishes are jibu-ni (a stew thickened with wheat flour and seasoned with wheat-based soy sauce), plus udon and soba, all containing wheat. Sweets like dango and ankoro-mochi use rice flour but are served with soy-based or sweet sauces. No gluten-free menu, labeling, or accommodation is documented. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: This is a Kaga-cuisine teahouse centered on jibu-ni (duck/meat stew) and seafood (sweet-shrimp rice bowl), so the savory menu is not vegan. However, as a 甘味処 (sweets house) it offers matcha tea, plum juice, soft-serve, and dango/ankoro-mochi sweets — some of which may be plant-based but are not verified vegan (sauces and dashi unconfirmed). No declared vegan menu. A vegan visitor could likely have matcha and certain sweets only; confirm ingredients with staff.
places.hours_raw: 9:00–16:30 daily (lunch service to ~14:30). Seasonal: 9:00–17:00 (Mar 1–Oct 15), 9:00–16:00 (Oct 16–end of Feb). Dinner by reservation only, extended during illumination and cherry-blossom seasons. Tabelog lists 08:15–17:00.
places.neighborhood: Kenrokuen Garden (Chaya-dori teahouse street, near Katsurazaka entrance)
places.notes: Tsutsumitei was founded in 1888 (Meiji 21) and occupies a building more than a century old on the historic tea-shop street of Kenrokuen, one of Japan's three great landscape gardens. With its calm, russet-walled rooms and second-floor tatami chambers overlooking the garden, it ha

chef_bio.origin: Kanazawa, Ishikawa — within the grounds of the Special Place of Scenic Beauty, Kenrokuen Garden.
chef_bio.background: Tsutsumitei was founded in 1888 (Meiji 21) and occupies a building more than a century old on the historic tea-shop street of Kenrokuen, one of Japan's three great landscape gardens. With its calm, russet-walled rooms and second-floor tatami chambers overlooking the garden, it has served generations of visitors near the Katsurazaka entrance and Kanazawa Castle's Ishikawa Gate.
chef_bio.philosophy: A traditional teahouse experience: enjoy regional Kaga cuisine and Japanese sweets in a historic garden setting, with veranda seating for a quick matcha and dango or upstairs rooms for a full meal.
chef_bio.specialty: Kanazawa's signature Kaga dish jibu-ni (a stew of duck/meat and vegetables), served on its own or as jibu-ni udon and soba; also a sweet-shrimp rice bowl. As a sweets house it is known for matcha, homemade-sauce dango, ankoro-mochi (sweet bean-paste rice cakes), seasonal parfaits, soft-serve, and house-made plum juice.

anecdotes.text: The shop safeguards carved wooden statues of the prosperity deities Daikoku and Ebisu as family heirlooms, made from timber salvaged from the historic Saikawa Bridge during its 1918 reconstruction.
On the first floor the shop sells local souvenirs, crafts, Kaga-yuzen textiles, and regional sake, while the second-floor tatami rooms serve meals with garden views.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Jibu-ni is traditionally thickened with wheat flour and seasoned with wheat-based soy sauce, and the menu prominently features udon and soba; assume wheat is present throughout the savory offerings.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen centers on duck/meat jibu-ni and seafood (sweet-shrimp bowl); dashi and broths likely contain animal products, so even vegetable or sweet items may share preparation surfaces.

positives.text: As a 甘味処, matcha tea, plum juice, and rice-based sweets (dango, ankoro-mochi) are available, giving non-meat diners some options alongside the savory Kaga dishes.

cultural_comfort.note: A welcoming tourist-oriented teahouse inside Kenrokuen where you can point at picture menus and order matcha or jibu-ni with just a few words of Japanese; a friendly greeting goes a long way.

places.name: 兼六亭 (Kenrokutei)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The signature dishes are built on wheat: jibu-ni dusts chicken/seafood in wheat flour before simmering in dashi, and the house "jibu soba" uses noodles that contain wheat (standard soba is a buckwheat-wheat blend). Soy-based dashi and seasonings are used throughout. No gluten-free menu or accommodations are documented. Celiac travelers should not consider this safe.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan destination. The cooking centers on chicken, seafood (sea urchin, nodoguro, sushi), Noto beef, and dashi stock. No vegan or vegetarian set menus are documented. A vegan diner could potentially ask about the cafe (sweets, matcha lattes, local teas), but wagashi and dairy lattes are not reliably plant-based and nothing is confirmed. Best to treat as ask-only with very limited prospects.
places.hours_raw: Breakfast 9:00–11:00 (reservation required, by 3pm previous day); Lunch 11:00–14:00; Cafe 9:00–16:30 (to 16:00 in winter); Evening "Genan" dining 18:00+ by reservation, light-up season only. Open year-round per one listing; official site notes irregular closures.
places.neighborhood: Kenrokuen Garden (inside the garden, in front of Japan's old
places.notes: Kenrokutei occupies one of the most enviable addresses in Kanazawa: inside Kenrokuen Garden, looking out over what is said to be Japan's oldest fountain. The kitchen is led by a chef who trained at a well-regarded Kyoto establishment, and the place leans on that pedigree while st

chef_bio.background: Kenrokutei occupies one of the most enviable addresses in Kanazawa: inside Kenrokuen Garden, looking out over what is said to be Japan's oldest fountain. The kitchen is led by a chef who trained at a well-regarded Kyoto establishment, and the place leans on that pedigree while staying rooted in Kaga's own food culture.
chef_bio.philosophy: The cooking emphasizes handmade, additive-free dishes built on seasonal local vegetables and seafood, served in a calm room with garden views.
chef_bio.specialty: The house specialty is an original "jibu soba" — the classic Kaga dish jibu-ni reimagined over noodles, with crisp roasted chicken and Kaga renkon (lotus root) worked into the soba. The broader menu runs through jibu-ni sets, seafood rice bowls (sea urchin, nodoguro), Noto beef sukiyaki, and hand-rolled sushi, alongside a garden cafe of wagashi, matcha lattes and local teas.

anecdotes.text: Beyond daytime dining, the same address transforms in the evening into a private dining room called Genan (幻庵), where guests can take a kaiseki course while looking out over the illuminated garden during light-up season.
The signature jibu soba kneads crisp roasted chicken and Kaga lotus root into the noodles, turning the regional braise jibu-ni into a noodle dish unique to this house.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Jibu-ni is prepared by dusting chicken and other ingredients in wheat flour before simmering, so wheat is integral to the signature cooking and shared prep surfaces.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes rely on dashi and Japanese seasonings (soy-based), and the signature soba contains wheat alongside buckwheat; no wheat-free preparations are documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen centers on chicken, seafood, beef and dashi; vegetable and cafe items are prepared in the same kitchen with no documented vegan separation.

positives.text: An English menu is available, which can help travelers communicate basic needs even though no formal allergen program is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: An English menu is available and the garden setting draws many overseas visitors, so a foreign student can order comfortably here.

places.name: ジャルダン ポール・ボキューズ (Jardin Paul Bocuse)
places.gf_detail: No documented gluten-free program. This is classic French cuisine where flour-thickened sauces, bread, and wheat-based preparations are central, so it is not celiac-safe. Allergy consultations are accepted at the time of reservation, so a guest could ask, but cross-contamination cannot be assumed to be controlled.
places.vegan_detail: A vegetarian menu is available, but it must be arranged in advance at reservation. There is no documented fully vegan menu, and as a French kitchen built around butter, cream, dairy, and seafood/meat stocks, vegan diners should confirm a fully plant-based course directly when booking rather than assume it.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:30–15:00 (L.O. 13:30); Dinner 17:30–22:00 (L.O. 20:00). Closed Mondays (or the following day if Monday is a holiday).
places.neighborhood: Hirosaka / Korinbo (inside Shiinoki Cultural Complex)
places.notes: Jardin Paul Bocuse occupies the second floor of Shiinoki Geihinkan, the former Taisho-era Ishikawa Prefectural Government building in central Kanazawa, restored as an elegant guesthouse near Kenrokuen. It opened in 2010 and is one of only two places (alongside Tokyo) carrying the

chef_bio.background: Jardin Paul Bocuse occupies the second floor of Shiinoki Geihinkan, the former Taisho-era Ishikawa Prefectural Government building in central Kanazawa, restored as an elegant guesthouse near Kenrokuen. It opened in 2010 and is one of only two places (alongside Tokyo) carrying the Paul Bocuse name in Japan, inheriting specialties from the celebrated main restaurant in Lyon, France.
chef_bio.philosophy: A fusion of Lyonnaise gastronomic tradition with the bounty of Ishikawa, set in a historic building accented with Kanazawa cultural touches such as gold leaf.
chef_bio.specialty: Classic Lyonnaise French specialties handed down from the Paul Bocuse main restaurant, combined with seasonal French dishes built around Ishikawa and Hokuriku seafood and produce.

anecdotes.text: It is described as one of only two restaurants in the world outside Lyon carrying the Paul Bocuse name in Japan, alongside its Tokyo counterpart.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Allergy consultations are accepted at the time of reservation.

positives.text: A vegetarian menu is available with advance arrangement.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a polished fine-dining French restaurant inside a historic guesthouse that hosts international guests and offers online reservations in English, so a foreign student would be comfortable, though the price point makes it a special-occasion choice.

places.name: 料亭 大友楼 (Ryotei Otomoro)
places.gf_detail: Traditional Kaga kaiseki built on dashi and soy sauce (which contains wheat), with wheat-thickened jibu-ni and various wheat-containing components. No gluten-free menu, no dedicated handling, and nothing about celiac safety is documented. Not suitable for those who must strictly avoid gluten.
places.vegan_detail: A seafood- and dashi-centric formal Kaga cuisine ryotei; signature dishes are sea-bream and the duck/seafood stew jibu-ni, and stocks are bonito/fish based. No vegan or vegetarian menu is documented. As a fully reservation-based ryotei it may be possible to request a customized course in advance by phone, but this is not advertised and would need to be arranged ahead.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-15:00, 17:00-21:00 (completely reservation-based; phone reservations 9:00-18:00)
places.neighborhood: Oyama-cho, near Oyama Shrine (Korinbo / Hokutetsu Kanazawa a
places.notes: Otomoro is a long-established Kanazawa ryotei founded in 1828 (Tenpo era), whose family long served as cooks to the Maeda lords of the Kaga domain. Over generations it has preserved the refined, ceremonial cooking of the castle town. When Kanazawa Station opened in 1898 the house

roles: Maker of historic Kanazawa railway bento
chef_bio.background: Otomoro is a long-established Kanazawa ryotei founded in 1828 (Tenpo era), whose family long served as cooks to the Maeda lords of the Kaga domain. Over generations it has preserved the refined, ceremonial cooking of the castle town. When Kanazawa Station opened in 1898 the house began producing railway bento, and it continues to run a bento shop and prepared-foods counter alongside its formal dining rooms.
chef_bio.philosophy: To carry forward Kanazawa's culinary heritage by pursuing the elegance, aroma and beauty of traditional Kaga cuisine, using carefully selected seasonal ingredients and meticulous handwork while offering heartfelt hospitality.
chef_bio.specialty: Traditional Kaga cuisine, above all jibu-ni (a duck/seafood stew thickened and simmered in a savory broth) and tai no karamushi (steamed sea bream), served in seasonal kaiseki courses.

anecdotes.text: The family served as cooks to the Maeda lords of the Kaga domain, and the house has carried that castle-town cuisine forward since its founding in 1828.
Its tea room Isseian occupies a roughly 250-year-old building that once served as a place of tea instruction for Kaga-domain samurai.
The restaurant began selling station bento when Kanazawa Station opened in 1898, beginning a long railway-food tradition.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Traditional Kaga kaiseki here relies on soy sauce and dashi-based seasoning; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, and the signature jibu-ni is traditionally thickened with wheat flour.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Cooking is centered on fish/bonito dashi and seafood (sea bream, jibu-ni), so plant-only dishes are not part of the documented menu and shared stocks would be used throughout the kitchen.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a formal, completely reservation-based heritage ryotei with multi-course kaiseki at high prices, so a foreign student would be most comfortable visiting with a Japanese-speaking guide or host who can book and explain the courses.

places.name: 味処 高崎 (Ajidokoro Takasaki)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional Japanese restaurant whose cooking relies heavily on soy sauce, dashi, and Kaga local dishes like jibuni (which is typically thickened/seasoned with soy and often dusted with wheat flour). No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented, and there is no dedicated fryer. Not safe for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: The menu is built around seafood (nodoguro, crab, oysters, gas shrimp), duck (jibuni), and fish-based dashi. No vegan or vegetarian offering is documented; this is a seafood-and-meat washoku destination, not suitable for vegans.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Sat 17:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:30). Closed Sundays (except three-day holiday weekends), year-end/New Year.
places.neighborhood: Korinbo/Katamachi (Nagamachi samurai district)
places.notes: A long-running Kanazawa washoku restaurant, established in 1971, set near the Nagamachi samurai district just off Korinbo. Over more than half a century it has built its reputation on Kaga regional cooking and the seafood of the Sea of Japan.

chef_bio.background: A long-running Kanazawa washoku restaurant, established in 1971, set near the Nagamachi samurai district just off Korinbo. Over more than half a century it has built its reputation on Kaga regional cooking and the seafood of the Sea of Japan.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen keeps preparation as simple as possible to let the natural flavor of carefully sourced ingredients come through (素材本来の味を生かす為、調理はできるだけシンプルにして), aiming to make refined Kanazawa cuisine approachable.
chef_bio.specialty: Nodoguro (blackthroat seaperch, grilled or as sashimi), Ishikawa snow/Kano crab, natural rock oysters, white gas shrimp sashimi, the house crab-cream croquette, and Kaga local dishes including jibuni (duck simmered in soy-seasoned broth). Seasonal lineup spans firefly squid and gas shrimp in spring, rock oysters and Kaga vegetables in summer, hairy crab and sweet shrimp in autumn, and Kano crab and winter yellowtail in winter.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant frames its food around the seasons of the Sea of Japan and the Kaga region, rotating its centerpiece dishes through firefly squid in spring, rock oysters in summer, hairy crab in autumn, and prized Kano crab in winter.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Cooking is centered on Kaga local dishes such as jibuni and a range of simmered and grilled preparations that conventionally use soy sauce and dashi; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is dominated by seafood, crab, duck and fish dashi, so vegan dishes and the avoidance of animal-based stock are not accommodated.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional, well-established kappo-style washoku restaurant where a few words of Japanese and a phone reservation will smooth your visit; staff are used to tourists but it is an evening dining destination rather than a casual drop-in.

places.name: 食道 月 (Shokudo Tsuki)
places.gf_detail: This is a Japanese seasonal cuisine izakaya built around sashimi, simmered and grilled seafood, and sake. Dishes rely heavily on dashi and Japanese soy sauce (which contains wheat), and there is no documented gluten-free menu or allergy program. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: The menu centers on fresh fish and seafood (sashimi, anago, uni hand rolls) with daily market catch as the core draw, and dashi-based seasoning throughout. No vegan or vegetarian offering is documented.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Sat 17:00-00:00; Sun 17:00-22:00. Irregular closures.
places.neighborhood: Katamachi (Katamachi 2-chome), Kanazawa
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Shintani grew up around cooking — his father was a professional chef and his siblings also went into the trade — and he entered the kitchen himself at age 15. He trained in hotels and traditional ryokan and worked his way up to head chef at the popular Katamachi izakaya 'Sho' bef

chef_bio.background: Shintani grew up around cooking — his father was a professional chef and his siblings also went into the trade — and he entered the kitchen himself at age 15. He trained in hotels and traditional ryokan and worked his way up to head chef at the popular Katamachi izakaya 'Sho' before going independent and opening Shokudo Tsuki in April 2022. He is a certified sake sommelier (kikisake-shi).
chef_bio.philosophy: The name 食道 ('the path of food') reflects his wish to roam freely across genres rather than be boxed into formal washoku, balancing respect for tradition with playful creativity. He wants guests to drop in casually and spend a relaxed, unfussy evening.
chef_bio.specialty: Daily-changing seasonal Japanese cooking driven by what he hand-picks at the market each morning, with fresh fish at the heart of the menu. Signature plates include seasonal sashimi (季節の鮮魚のお造り), a chilled simmered-anago bowl, grilled yanagi-sawara finished with sudachi, and a raw Hokkaido (Nemuro) uni hand roll that regulars have followed him from his 'Sho' days as a favorite closing dish. He pairs these with local sake sourced from across Japan.

anecdotes.text: The raw uni hand roll using Hokkaido Nemuro uni has been a customer favorite since Shintani's time at the izakaya 'Sho', and longtime guests still order it as their finishing dish.
Shintani came from a cooking family — his father was a professional chef and his siblings also pursued culinary careers — and he started in the kitchen at 15.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The cooking is traditional Japanese seafood cuisine relying on dashi and soy-sauce-based seasoning, which in Japan normally contains wheat; no gluten-free accommodation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is built around fresh fish and seafood with dashi-based seasoning; no vegan or plant-only dishes are documented.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a local counter izakaya where ordering and conversation happen in Japanese, so a little Japanese or a translation app will help a foreign student feel comfortable.

places.name: 大口水産 ヤキヤキコーナー (Ohguchi Suisan Yakiyaki Corner)
places.gf_detail: This is a grilled-seafood and sushi counter where items like salt-grilled nodoguro, scallops and squid are likely fine on their own, but no gluten-free handling is documented, soy-based sauces and shared grills are in use, and staff are not set up for celiac-level allergy management. Not safe for strict celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: A seafood-focused grilled counter run by a fishmonger; the menu is built entirely around fish, squid, scallops, shrimp and eel. There are no vegan options.
places.hours_raw: 9:00–15:00 (per Omichō Market official page); some listings show 9:00–17:00. Closed Wednesdays.
places.neighborhood: 近江町市場 (Omichō Market), Musashigatsuji
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The Yakiyaki Corner is the grilled-seafood standing counter of Ohguchi Suisan, one of the largest and longest-running fishmongers in Kanazawa's 300-year-old Omichō Market, which holds the biggest sales floor in the market. Alongside its fresh-fish cases, the shop runs an open gri

roles: grilled-seafood counter operated by Ohguchi Suisan
chef_bio.background: The Yakiyaki Corner is the grilled-seafood standing counter of Ohguchi Suisan, one of the largest and longest-running fishmongers in Kanazawa's 300-year-old Omichō Market, which holds the biggest sales floor in the market. Alongside its fresh-fish cases, the shop runs an open grill where market-fresh seafood is skewered and cooked right in front of customers to eat on the spot.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop sums up its approach as 安くてうまい — cheap and delicious — leaning on ingredients pulled straight from the market's own fish counters.
chef_bio.specialty: Charcoal-grilled skewers from about 200 yen each — striped (mongo) squid legs, plump scallops, salt-grilled nodoguro (blackthroat seaperch), round whelk shellfish, eel liver, Noto-region fugu and shrimp — plus fresh sashimi and sushi packs, often washed down with a 350-yen draft beer.

anecdotes.text: Skewers such as scallop, squid and eel liver are grilled to order right in front of you, making it a favorite stop for eating while strolling the market.
A 350-yen draft beer alongside 200-yen skewers makes it an easy, affordable pause in the middle of the market.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Skewers are cooked on a shared open grill alongside marinated and sauced items, so cross-contact with wheat-containing sauces is likely.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Sashimi and sushi are served, which are typically eaten with standard (wheat-containing) soy sauce; no gluten-free soy sauce is documented.

positives.text: Salt-grilled items such as nodoguro shioyaki and plain salted scallops are grilled simply with salt rather than sauce.

cultural_comfort.note: A bustling open-air market counter where you point at the skewer you want and eat standing up; a few words of Japanese and pointing go a long way, and English is unlikely.

places.name: 御料理 五十嵐 (Goryori Igarashi)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional Kaga kaiseki/kappo kitchen built around dashi, soy sauce, and seasonal seafood — all of which involve wheat-based seasonings (regular soy sauce contains wheat). There is no gluten-free menu and no documented allergen handling. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: A seafood- and Kaga-vegetable-focused seasonal kaiseki restaurant serving fixed omakase courses. Fish, dashi (typically bonito/kombu), and animal products are central to every course; there is no vegan menu and dishes cannot meaningfully be made vegan. Not a vegan destination.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Sat 18:00-22:00 (dinner only); closed Sundays.
places.neighborhood: Korinbo / Seseragi-dori
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The chef behind this small Seseragi-dori counter spent nine years training at the celebrated Asadaya ryotei in Kanazawa, then served nine more years as head chef at Lamp no Yado in Suzu, the famed seaside ryokan on the Noto Peninsula, before opening his own place in May 2014.

chef_bio.background: The chef behind this small Seseragi-dori counter spent nine years training at the celebrated Asadaya ryotei in Kanazawa, then served nine more years as head chef at Lamp no Yado in Suzu, the famed seaside ryokan on the Noto Peninsula, before opening his own place in May 2014.
chef_bio.philosophy: He cooks for contrast and season — temperature, flavor and texture, seasonality, and the cleverness of combinations — and deliberately keeps prices approachable so that this level of craft stays within reach.
chef_bio.specialty: Seasonal Kaga kaiseki built on local seafood and Kaga vegetables, served as fixed omakase courses — refined cooking that plays with temperature, texture, and seasonal pairings.

anecdotes.text: He runs the whole kitchen single-handedly, turning out a long, intricately composed sequence of dishes alone — a feat regulars marvel at.
Tucked into a single-story wooden house on Seseragi-dori, where a clear canal runs past year-round, the room is just a six-seat counter facing the open kitchen and a handful of small tables.
Despite elaborate plating and unexpected touches, the courses remain modestly priced — a point reviewers single out as unusually generous.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Traditional kaiseki built on dashi and soy-based seasonings; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, and there is no documented gluten-free handling.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Kaga kaiseki cooking relies on soy sauce and dashi throughout the courses; assume wheat-containing soy sauce is used.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Courses are seafood- and dashi-centric with animal products in nearly every dish; no plant-only option is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, quiet owner-run counter where the chef serves set omakase courses — comfortable for a student with some Japanese who can phone ahead to reserve, but there's likely little English and no menu choices to point at.

places.name: ヒッコリー (Hickory)
places.gf_detail: A Showa-era kissaten whose signature dishes are curry omurice, fried rice, cheese toast and parfaits — wheat-based bread, roux, and flour are central to the menu. No gluten-free accommodation is documented and the kitchen is a single owner-run operation. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: The menu centers on egg-topped curry, omelette-based dishes, butter chicken curry, cheese toast, and dairy-rich parfaits and coffee drinks. No vegan options are documented. Animal products (egg, dairy, meat) are core to nearly every dish.
places.hours_raw: Tabelog lists Mon–Sat 10:00–21:00, closed Sunday. An owner interview states 11:00–19:00, closed Sundays and Mondays. Hours have varied between sources — confirm by phone (076-221-0946).
places.neighborhood: Kakinokibatake (Katamachi / Korinbo / near 21st Century Museum of Contemporary Art)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Tucked down a lane in the 柿木畠 (Kakinokibatake) quarter just off Katamachi — reached by crossing a small bridge over the Kurazuki canal, near the 21st Century Museum — Hickory opened in 1988 as 珈琲 ヒッコリー. The owner first took over his father's dry-cleaning shop on this spot before

chef_bio.background: Tucked down a lane in the 柿木畠 (Kakinokibatake) quarter just off Katamachi — reached by crossing a small bridge over the Kurazuki canal, near the 21st Century Museum — Hickory opened in 1988 as 珈琲 ヒッコリー. The owner first took over his father's dry-cleaning shop on this spot before converting it into a coffee house a few years later. In its early years it filled with students, when Kanazawa University still occupied the nearby castle grounds (until 1995).
chef_bio.philosophy: The owner, a former smoker, deliberately made the cafe non-smoking so families would feel welcome. There is no Wi-Fi, no power outlets and no BGM — it is a quiet, unhurried room built for coffee and conversation.
chef_bio.specialty: A Showa-retro kissaten known for siphon-brewed coffee, curry topped with a soft omelette (curry omurice), parfaits, cheese toast and a hazelnut coffee. Its most personal dish is 太洋軒の焼きめし, a nostalgic fried rice the owner revived from the long-closed local restaurant Taiyoken — 'only I can make it,' he says.

anecdotes.text: Opened in 1988 as 珈琲 ヒッコリー; the owner inherited his father's dry-cleaning business on the site before turning it into a cafe, and it thrived with students while Kanazawa University was still on the nearby castle grounds.
The owner revived 太洋軒の焼きめし, a fried-rice recipe from a defunct local restaurant, saying 私しか作られんからね ('only I can make it').
Reached by crossing a small bridge over the Kurazuki canal in the 柿木畠 district; the siphon-brewed coffee is praised as remarkably mellow.

cultural_comfort.note: This is an old-school, owner-run Showa kissaten with no English menu, Wi-Fi, or BGM, so a little Japanese and an easygoing attitude will help you settle into its quiet, time-capsule atmosphere.

places.name: 鮨処 あさの川 (Sushidokoro Asanogawa)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional sushi house. Sushi is served with wheat-based soy sauce, and nimono/marinades and dashi typically contain soy sauce and other wheat-containing seasonings. No gluten-free menu or celiac protocol is documented. Not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: A seafood-focused omakase sushi restaurant built around fish, shellfish and Ishikawa coastal catch (e.g. nodoguro). There is no vegan menu and the cuisine is fundamentally animal-based. Not suitable for vegans.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-14:00, Dinner 17:30-22:00. Closed Wednesday and Thursday.
places.neighborhood: Kazuemachi / Owaricho (along the Asano River, near Omicho Ma
places.cuisine: Sushi / Edomae-style sushi and seafood, Ishikawa
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Sushidokoro Asanogawa opened in the summer of 2016 in Kanazawa's Kazuemachi geisha district, on a quiet lane beside the Asano River near Omicho Market. Its name was bestowed by Morita Kazuo, the revered sushi master who ran the celebrated 'Komatsu Yasuke,' and the shop carries on

chef_bio.chef_name: Trained successor of Morita Kazuo (the legendary sushi chef known as "Komatsu Yasuke")
chef_bio.background: Sushidokoro Asanogawa opened in the summer of 2016 in Kanazawa's Kazuemachi geisha district, on a quiet lane beside the Asano River near Omicho Market. Its name was bestowed by Morita Kazuo, the revered sushi master who ran the celebrated 'Komatsu Yasuke,' and the shop carries on his techniques. The current head chef, born in 1974, grew up in a restaurant family, studied Japanese cuisine in Kyoto for four years after high school, then returned to Kanazawa to deepen his knowledge of local seafood before inheriting Morita's methods when Komatsu Yasuke closed.
chef_bio.philosophy: 妥協しない技で寿司に向き合い、心を込めておもてなし致します — to face the sushi with uncompromising technique and serve with wholehearted hospitality.
chef_bio.specialty: Counter omakase sushi built on carefully selected fish, with Ishikawa coastal catch such as nodoguro (rosy seabass); a marinated red-tuna (maguro no akami-zuke) preparation is a signature touched by the Komatsu Yasuke lineage.

anecdotes.text: The shop's name was given by Morita Kazuo, the master behind the legendary 'Komatsu Yasuke,' who continued to guide and cooperate with the chef here.
Morita Kazuo, the mentor whose techniques live on at this counter, was recognized in 2019 by Japan's Ministry of Health, Labour and Welfare as a 'Contemporary Master Craftsman' (現代の名工).

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a traditional sushi house, dishes are served with standard (wheat-based) soy sauce and seasonings; no gluten-free soy sauce or celiac accommodation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is entirely seafood-based (raw fish, shellfish, fish dashi), so vegan diners cannot avoid cross-contact; no plant-based menu exists.

cultural_comfort.note: This is an intimate, reservation-only counter sushi house where the meal is an omakase course at a high price point, so a foreign student should book ahead and be ready for a quiet, Japanese-speaking setting where following the chef's lead is the norm.

places.name: 小料理とおでん 燗九郎 (Koryori to Oden Kankuro)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. Oden and the broths rely on Japanese dashi and almost certainly wheat-based soy sauce, and the kitchen is a small shared izakaya space. No gluten-free accommodation or dedicated equipment is documented; not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The food is built on animal stocks (duck-bone broth, bonito/dried-fish and shiitake dashi) and the signatures are fish, scallop, egg, smoked salmon, steak and roast beef. No vegan options are documented.
places.hours_raw: 18:00-02:00 (until 2 a.m. next day), last order 23:30. Closed Sun.
places.neighborhood: Kiguramachi / Seseragi-dori (Katamachi / Korinbo)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Run by a chef who trained in French cuisine and channels that experience into a new-style 'bistro oden' specialty shop. The place opened in July 2020 just behind the Katamachi Kira building, in the space that previously housed a yakitori shop, and quickly became known around Kana

chef_bio.background: Run by a chef who trained in French cuisine and channels that experience into a new-style 'bistro oden' specialty shop. The place opened in July 2020 just behind the Katamachi Kira building, in the space that previously housed a yakitori shop, and quickly became known around Kanazawa for its French-meets-Japanese take on a humble winter classic.
chef_bio.philosophy: Bringing French technique and ingredients to the everyday comfort of oden, so a simmered-pot standard becomes something new while still feeling at home in Kanazawa.
chef_bio.specialty: Two oden lineups side by side: a Western-leaning set built on a slowly simmered duck-bone broth (tomato, a creamy Western-style egg whose broth regulars like to drink, a rich scallop surimi), and a more traditional Japanese oden in a bonito-kelp-shiitake dashi (including a half-rare sardine dumpling). Rounded out with a French bar menu of smoked salmon, steak and roast beef, paired with both sake and wine.

anecdotes.text: Opened July 21, 2020 in Katamachi as a 'new-sensation bistro oden' shop, taking over a former yakitori location behind Katamachi Kira.

Local coverage credits a French-trained chef for 'revolutionary' French oden such as a duck-dashi base and a sweet-sour tomato oden, alongside Kanazawa-style standards, with both sake and wine pairings.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Oden and small-plate izakaya cooking in Japan standardly uses wheat-containing soy sauce and dashi; no gluten-free preparation is documented for this kitchen.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths are animal-based (duck-bone stock; bonito and dried-fish/shiitake dashi) and signature items center on fish, scallop, egg and meat, so vegan dishes and avoidance of animal cross-contact are not provided.

cultural_comfort.note: A small late-night local oden and small-plates spot where a little Japanese helps; warm and welcoming but not set up for English, so come ready to point at the menu and try a few words.

places.name: 手打そば 更科藤井 (Teuchi Soba Sarashina Fujii)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. The house standard is nihachi soba (80% buckwheat, 20% wheat flour), and the signature pale "sarashina" soba uses refined buckwheat but is still made in a wheat-handling kitchen. A 100% buckwheat (juwari) soba appears on the menu, but it is prepared in the same kitchen alongside wheat-bound noodles, so cross-contamination is essentially certain. Dipping sauces (tsuyu) and many side dishes also use wheat-based soy sauce. Celiac travelers should avoid.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan destination. The dipping broth is bonito-dashi based, and the menu leans on duck, scallop, sea urchin and oysters. Plain noodles and vegetable tempura exist but most accompaniments are not vegan; ask before ordering.
places.hours_raw: [Tue-Sat] 11:30-14:00 / 17:30-20:30 [Sun & holidays] 11:30-19:30. Closes when soba sells out. Closed Mon (next day if a holiday) plus irregular days off.
places.neighborhood: Kakinokibatake (Korinbo / behind the 21st Century Museum of Contemporary Art / near City Hall)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Owner-chef Fujii Makoto trained for more than a decade in Nagano and Tokyo, including time in the Edo soba tradition, before opening his own shop in 1999 - an era when dedicated soba specialists were still rare in Kanazawa. The restaurant moved from its original Katamachi locatio

chef_bio.background: Owner-chef Fujii Makoto trained for more than a decade in Nagano and Tokyo, including time in the Edo soba tradition, before opening his own shop in 1999 - an era when dedicated soba specialists were still rare in Kanazawa. The restaurant moved from its original Katamachi location to the quiet Kakigibahake lane behind the 21st Century Museum of Art in March 2011, settling into a calm, machiya-like townhouse space.
chef_bio.philosophy: Fujii treats raw material as everything - 'ingredients are the lifeblood of soba' - and travels each year to meet his buckwheat producers in person, sourcing directly rather than settling for what is convenient.
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-cut soba in several styles - the everyday nihachi (80% buckwheat) zaru, a coarse-ground (arabiki) version, a 100% buckwheat juwari, and the elegant pale 'sarashina' (gozen) soba made from highly refined buckwheat, prized for its clean throat-feel and delicate aroma. Alongside the noodles, Fujii serves refined small plates such as roast duck, scallop-filled tamagoyaki, vegetable tempura, oysters and salted sea urchin, all meant to pair with sake or buckwheat shochu.

anecdotes.text: Fujii's soba became such a Kanazawa benchmark that, back in the late-night Katamachi days, even rival soba shop owners would come in to eat his noodles, and locals credit him with helping raise the whole city's soba culture.
He spent over ten years apprenticing in Nagano and Tokyo before going independent in 1999, when soba specialists were still uncommon in Kanazawa.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen hand-cuts wheat-bound nihachi soba (80% buckwheat / 20% wheat) as its standard, so any 100% buckwheat (juwari) option is made in the same wheat-handling environment - cross-contamination is essentially unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soba tsuyu and the small side dishes rely on standard Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat; no wheat-free seasoning is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The dipping broth is built on bonito (katsuo) dashi and the menu centers on duck and seafood, so plant-only diners cannot assume the noodles, broth, or shared kitchen are animal-free.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional owner-run soba shop where a little Japanese and quiet, respectful dining go a long way; English service is not assured, so point at the menu and keep it simple.

places.name: 居酒屋割烹 源左ェ門 木倉町本店 (Izakaya Kappo Genzaemon Kiguramachi Honten)
places.gf_detail: This is a Kaga regional cuisine and local-fish kappo where dashi, soy sauce, miso (hoba miso), tempura, and sake-based seasonings are central to nearly every dish. There is no documented gluten-free menu or allergen accommodation, and the kitchen relies heavily on wheat-containing soy sauce and miso. Not safe for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: The restaurant is built around fresh fish from Kanazawa Port (sashimi, nodoguro, etc.), Noto beef sukiyaki, and dashi-based regional dishes. While Kaga vegetable dishes exist (e.g., Kaga thick cucumber, lotus-root dumplings), they are prepared with fish/bonito dashi and are not vegan. No vegan menu is documented.
places.hours_raw: Mon/Tue/Thu/Sun & holidays 17:00-23:00 (L.O. 22:30); Fri/Sat & day before holidays 17:00-23:30 (L.O. 23:00); closed Wednesdays. Irregular closures during Golden Week, Obon, and year-end/New Year.
places.neighborhood: Kiguramachi (Katamachi / Korinbo)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Genzaemon sits in Kiguramachi, a Kanazawa dining quarter with over 300 years of history. The restaurant takes its name from Genzaemon Sano, said to be a Kamakura-era samurai whose descendants carried on a tradition of hospitality. Owner Yohei Sano runs the house in a traditional

chef_bio.background: Genzaemon sits in Kiguramachi, a Kanazawa dining quarter with over 300 years of history. The restaurant takes its name from Genzaemon Sano, said to be a Kamakura-era samurai whose descendants carried on a tradition of hospitality. Owner Yohei Sano runs the house in a traditional farmhouse-style (kominka) setting, with counter seats that look out onto Kaga Yuzen textiles.
chef_bio.philosophy: Owner Sano describes his consistent approach as responding to things from the customer's point of view, pairing local fish and Kaga vegetables with a deep selection of Hokuriku and nationwide sake.
chef_bio.specialty: Sashimi and dishes built around fresh fish delivered from Kanazawa Port (nodoguro/blackthroat seaperch is a highlight), Kaga regional cooking using Kaga vegetables, Noto beef sukiyaki, and a signature lotus-root dumpling in dashi broth that combines grated lotus root with mountain yam for a fluffy, chewy texture. A hidden specialty is hoba miso yaki (miso grilled on a magnolia leaf) using beef or seasonal items like scallop and firefly squid.

anecdotes.text: The shop is named after Genzaemon Sano, said to be a Kamakura-era samurai whose descendants served generals, establishing a tradition of hospitality the restaurant continues today.
A signature lotus-root dumpling combines grated lotus root with mountain yam for a fluffy, chewy texture, served in dashi broth.
Counter seats look out onto displays of Kaga Yuzen textiles, and a hidden specialty is hoba miso yaki featuring beef or seasonal seafood like scallop and firefly squid.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Cooking centers on regional Kaga dishes, sashimi with soy sauce, miso-grilled hoba miso, and dashi-based preparations, all typically using wheat-containing soy sauce and miso.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is fish- and meat-forward (Kanazawa Port sashimi, nodoguro, Noto beef sukiyaki) with dashi used throughout, so vegetable dishes are not free of animal products.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional, locally loved evening izakaya-kappo where some Japanese language ability and comfort with regional Japanese food (raw fish, dashi, sake) will help a foreign student feel at ease.

places.name: 御料理 貴船 (Oryori Kifune)
places.gf_detail: Traditional Kaga kaiseki built on dashi and Japanese soy sauce, with no documented gluten-free menu, dedicated equipment, or allergen handling. Wheat-containing soy sauce is integral to this style of cuisine, so it should not be considered celiac-safe.
places.vegan_detail: Course menus are seafood- and fish-dashi-centered (sashimi, scallop/squid salads, white fish and crab in winter, beef hot pot at the Sunday breakfast). No vegan or vegetarian course is documented. As a reservation-only, fixed-course kaiseki, plant-based diners would need to inquire well in advance and should not assume accommodation.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Thu, Fri, Sat: 11:30-15:00, 17:30-22:30 (lunch L.O. 13:30 / dinner L.O. 20:30) / Sun: 07:00-09:00 (Kanazawa asage breakfast, reservation only), 11:30-15:00, 17:30-22:30 / closed Wed. Fully reservation-based, limited to 4 groups per day.
places.neighborhood: Kazuemachi / Hikoso-machi (along the Asano River / near Omicho Market)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Chef-owner Nakagawa Seiichi trained at hotels and specialty restaurants in Kanazawa before opening Kifune in 2008 in the Kazue-machi/Shukeimachi teahouse district along the Asano River.

chef_bio.origin: Opened 2008 in the riverside teahouse quarter near Omicho Market, in a quiet building whose second-floor window seats look out over the Asano River and whose first-floor tables face a small inner garden (tsuboniwa).
chef_bio.background: Chef-owner Nakagawa Seiichi trained at hotels and specialty restaurants in Kanazawa before opening Kifune in 2008 in the Kazue-machi/Shukeimachi teahouse district along the Asano River.
chef_bio.philosophy: Nakagawa caps service at four groups per seating so that every dish can be prepared and timed perfectly for each table — an expression of his ideal of offering 'the finest hospitality possible today' while keeping the essence of traditional Japanese cuisine approachable across ages and cultures.
chef_bio.specialty: Monthly-changing seasonal kaiseki that turns over on the 15th of each month, with winter highlights of crab and white fish, beautifully composed multi-course meals, and a celebrated Sunday-only 'Kanazawa asage' breakfast where rice is cooked tableside in individual clay pots using premium Ishikawa rice.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant deliberately limits itself to four groups per service because that is the most Nakagawa can fully prepare for and serve with perfectly timed courses.

Its Sunday-morning 'Kanazawa asage' breakfast for only a few groups is so beloved that over 90% of guests return, and reservations open on the 1st of each month at 10:00 yet often fill the slots up to six months ahead.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Traditional Kaga kaiseki and Japanese cuisine of this style is built on dashi and wheat-based Japanese soy sauce; no gluten-free soy sauce or alternative is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Courses are seafood- and meat-inclusive (sashimi, scallop and squid, white fish, crab, beef hot pot), prepared with fish dashi; no vegan preparation is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: This is an intimate, reservation-only kaiseki ryotei where bookings often fill six months out and service is paced personally for each group, so some Japanese ability (or a Japanese-speaking friend to phone-reserve) will make the visit far smoother for a foreign student.

places.name: 豆月 (Mamezuki)
places.gf_detail: The signature sweets are agar-and-bean based (黒豆かん, 豆しるこ) and many items are naturally wheat-free, but there is no documented gluten-free program, no dedicated equipment, and items like 餅 (mochi) and seasonal dishes vary. Not a celiac-safe destination; a celiac diner would need to ask about each item directly in Japanese.
places.vegan_detail: The menu is plant-forward and bean-centered: 黒豆かん (black beans, agar, and a syrup reduced from the beans' own cooking liquid), 四色福豆しるこ (heirloom beans), seasonal 豆スープ with simply-dressed vegetables and pickles, ume juice and house-made ginger drinks. Many items appear vegan, but it is not a certified vegan kitchen — mochi, broths, or syrups could vary seasonally, so confirm specific items. No documented use of dashi/animal products in the headline sweets.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-18:00 (closed Wed & Thu; Sun open irregularly)
places.neighborhood: Higashiyama / Higashi Chaya District
places.notes: Mamezuki was opened in 2016 by Kitade Miyuki and her husband Kitade Takesuke, a first-class architect, who relocated to Kanazawa from Oiso in Kanagawa. Searching for a traditional Japanese way of living, they were drawn to Kanazawa's active machiya-preservation scene and fell for

chef_bio.background: Mamezuki was opened in 2016 by Kitade Miyuki and her husband Kitade Takesuke, a first-class architect, who relocated to Kanazawa from Oiso in Kanagawa. Searching for a traditional Japanese way of living, they were drawn to Kanazawa's active machiya-preservation scene and fell for an 80-plus-year-old townhouse in the Higashiyama district, which Takesuke renovated by hand while preserving its courtyard garden, tea room, and alcove.
chef_bio.philosophy: The couple cook at 'the pace of the beans' — black beans are simmered slowly over roughly three days, never with a pressure cooker — and live by a motto of doing things 'within their means' (身の丈に合っている), honoring local culture and preparing each dish with care. The shop typically keeps around 15 varieties of beans on hand and serves them in ceramics by regional and national artisans.
chef_bio.specialty: Beans are the whole story. The signature 黒豆かん (¥700) is built on black beans cooked deliberately firm for an edamame-like crunch, set with agar and finished with a syrup reduced from the beans' own cooking liquid rather than sugar. Other staples include the seasonal 四色福豆しるこ made with four heirloom bean varieties and locally pounded mochi, a weekly-rotating 豆スープ served with simply dressed vegetables and pickles, and drinks like ume juice and house-made ginger.

anecdotes.text: The recipe for the firm, crisp-textured black beans comes from Kitade's childhood: her grandmother in Hokkaido would cook black beans for her, and she set out to recreate that 'ideal bean' of memory by learning to simmer them herself.
Rather than sweetening with black sugar, the syrup over the 黒豆かん is made by reducing the liquid the beans were cooked in, so the flavors stay in harmony with the beans themselves.

positives.text: Headline sweets are naturally plant-based and bean/agar-based: black beans set in agar with a syrup made from the beans' own cooking liquid, and bean shiruko made from heirloom beans.
Seasonal bean soup is served with simply prepared vegetables and pickles, indicating a strongly plant-forward kitchen.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny owner-run machiya café where Japanese will go furthest; the menu is short and bean-focused, so a foreign student should be comfortable but shouldn't expect English service or a printed English menu.

places.name: 甘味処 漆の実 (Kanmidokoro Urushi no Mi)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional Japanese sweets shop. The menu centers on wagashi, mochi, anko (red bean), kuzukiri and toast — items that typically involve wheat-based confections and shared kitchen prep. No gluten-free accommodation or dedicated handling is documented, and a celiac traveler should not assume safety here.
places.vegan_detail: Some sweets are plant-based by nature (kuzukiri, anko, matcha syrup), but dairy is used in items like matcha cream zenzai, and no vegan labeling or accommodation is documented. A vegan would need to ask which items contain milk, cream or egg. Nothing specific is published.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-18:00 (L.O. 17:30). Closed Tuesdays and Wednesdays (irregular closures in August; open on public holidays).
places.neighborhood: 広坂・柿木畠 (Hirosaka, near Kenrokuen Garden, the 21st Century Mu
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Urushi no Mi is the tea room on the 4th floor of Nosaku, a Kanazawa lacquerware house founded in 1780 that has carried on Kaga maki-e and Wajima-nuri traditions for more than two centuries. The cafe opened over twenty years ago, conceived as a quiet, elegant salon — the kind you

chef_bio.background: Urushi no Mi is the tea room on the 4th floor of Nosaku, a Kanazawa lacquerware house founded in 1780 that has carried on Kaga maki-e and Wajima-nuri traditions for more than two centuries. The cafe opened over twenty years ago, conceived as a quiet, elegant salon — the kind you might find tucked inside a luxury boutique — where guests rest with a sweet after browsing the lacquerware below.
chef_bio.philosophy: The room is built around savoring the seasons of Kanazawa — cherry blossoms, fresh greenery, autumn color and snow seen from the windows over Hirosaka — paired with refined, traditional Japanese sweets in a hushed, unhurried setting.
chef_bio.specialty: Matcha cream zenzai (matcha cream over mochi dumplings and sweet red beans) and made-to-order matcha kuzukiri are the signature offerings, alongside high-grade Yoshihashi wagashi served with matcha. Dumplings and kuzukiri are prepared fresh to order.

anecdotes.text: The cafe sits four floors above the Nosaku lacquerware shop, so a visit doubles as a window onto 1780-founded Kaga maki-e and Wajima lacquer craft before you sit down to dessert.

From the 4th-floor windows guests look out over Hirosaka toward Kenrokuen and the surrounding cultural landmarks, with the view changing through cherry blossoms, greenery, autumn leaves and snow across the year.

cultural_comfort.note: A calm sit-down sweets cafe inside a lacquerware shop where a few words of Japanese and pointing at the picture menu will get you a lovely matcha dessert with garden-side views; little English is documented.

places.name: 桜桃 (Yusura)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The signature dishes are wheat-based — the katsu sandwich uses bread and a breaded cutlet, Hunton rice is topped with a wheat-flour omelette and ketchup/demi sauce, and the menu leans heavily on curry, chiffon/gateau cakes and warabi mochi. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented, and Japanese curry roux and katsu breading both contain wheat. A celiac diner should not consider this a safe option.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-oriented. The menu centers on a pork-cutlet sandwich, omelette-topped Hunton rice and meat curry, and the sweets (gateau chocolat, pudding, chiffon cake, soft serve) are dairy- and egg-based. No vegan or vegetarian dishes are advertised. A plant-based diner would have very limited options here; ask in advance.
places.hours_raw: Cafe 10:00–17:00; Bar 18:00–23:00 by reservation only. Closed Wednesdays.
places.neighborhood: 野町・にし茶屋街 (Nomachi / Nishi Chayagai)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Yusura is a cafe set inside a traditional Kanazawa machiya townhouse in the atmospheric Nishi Chayagai geisha district, a short walk from Nomachi Station. The building previously housed a sushi restaurant that operated for around twenty years; it reopened as a cafe in the wave of

chef_bio.background: Yusura is a cafe set inside a traditional Kanazawa machiya townhouse in the atmospheric Nishi Chayagai geisha district, a short walk from Nomachi Station. The building previously housed a sushi restaurant that operated for around twenty years; it reopened as a cafe in the wave of interest that followed the Hokuriku Shinkansen reaching Kanazawa in 2015. Behind a preserved historic facade, the interior is bright and modern, with counter and table seating, a small tsubo-niwa courtyard garden, and a private tatami room.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature is the 'tea-house katsu sandwich' (茶屋のカツサンド) — fluffy bread around a crisp pork cutlet with a house sauce and butter. The kitchen also serves local Kanazawa comfort dishes including Hunton rice and Kaga/Kanazawa curry, alongside coffee and house-made sweets such as a not-too-sweet gateau chocolat finished with gold leaf, custard pudding, chiffon cake, and gold-leaf soft serve.

anecdotes.text: The cafe has served as a filming location for a national TV drama and is frequented by geisha working in the surrounding tea-house district.
Its walls display numerous celebrity autographs, and it offers a discount for junior- and senior-high-school students dining alone.
Set in a former sushi restaurant of about twenty years, it reopened as a cafe after the 2015 Shinkansen extension to Kanazawa.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed townhouse cafe in the tea-house district where a friendly greeting and pointing at the menu will get you by; daytime cafe service is casual and tourist-friendly, though the reservation-only evening bar may expect more Japanese.

places.name: 薬屋カフェ (Kusuriya Cafe)
places.gf_detail: No documented gluten-free program. The cafe serves yakuzen (medicinal) soups with stone-cooked Ishikawa rice, homemade tsukudani and pickles, and herbal teas. While rice-based dishes may be naturally wheat-light, no celiac-safe handling, dedicated equipment, or gluten-free menu is documented; spice cakes and other baked goods contain wheat. Celiac travelers must ask directly and treat it as not confirmed safe.
places.vegan_detail: Plant-forward menu built around seasonal medicinal vegetable soups, soy-milk potages, Ishikawa rice, pickles, and herbal teas — many items are vegetable-based and the philosophy is "anything you eat becomes medicine." However, no item is explicitly labeled vegan, and soups may use dashi or animal-derived stock; the cafe does not document a dedicated vegan/vegetarian protocol. Vegan diners should confirm soup base and ingredients on the day.
places.hours_raw: 10:00-18:00; closed Tuesday and Wednesday
places.neighborhood: Nomachi / Teramachi (corner of Nomachi Hirokoji intersection, near the Teramachi temple district)
places.notes: Kusuriya Cafe opened in April 2017 on the ground floor of the historic Ando Hoendo Building at the Nomachi Hirokoji intersection, near the temple district of Teramachi. It is run by Ando Pharmacy (安藤薬局), an apothecary with roots reaching back to the Edo period — hence the name 'K

roles: owner-operated by Ando Pharmacy (安藤薬局)
chef_bio.background: Kusuriya Cafe opened in April 2017 on the ground floor of the historic Ando Hoendo Building at the Nomachi Hirokoji intersection, near the temple district of Teramachi. It is run by Ando Pharmacy (安藤薬局), an apothecary with roots reaching back to the Edo period — hence the name 'Kusuriya' (the medicine shop). The space took over the furniture and chandeliers of a long-running coffeehouse that previously occupied the spot, giving it a nostalgic, European-tinged elegance.
chef_bio.philosophy: The cafe embraces the idea that 'anything you eat becomes medicine,' offering approachable yakuzen rather than intimidating formal medicinal cuisine — eating seasonally and according to what the body needs.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature is the seasonal yakuzen (medicinal) soup lunch (around 1,200 yen): a generous vegetable-rich soup paired with seasonal medicinal tea, stone-cooked Ishikawa rice, and homemade tsukudani and pickles. The monthly-changing menu has featured turnip-and-taro minestrone for warming the body in cold months and winter-melon soups for cooling in summer. Sweets include a yakuzen spice cake with ginger preserves, goji berries and walnuts, and rose-raisin tea.

anecdotes.text: The interior doubles as a book cafe with roughly 500 curated volumes on architecture, travel, literature and coffee, and a quiet 'no-conversation' rear section for contemplation — visitors are said to linger for hours.
The cafe is housed in the Ando Hoendo Building and operated by the Edo-period-rooted Ando Pharmacy, tying its medicinal-food concept to a real apothecary heritage.

positives.text: Menu is built around vegetable-forward seasonal soups, soy-milk potages, rice, pickles and herbal teas, so plant-based and lighter dishes are part of the regular offering.

cultural_comfort.note: A calm, quiet book-cafe where a few words of Japanese and a relaxed, respectful manner go a long way; English support is not documented, so point at the menu and a smile will carry you.

places.name: いも屋cafe 楽 (Imoya Cafe Tano)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. While roasted sweet potatoes themselves are naturally gluten-free, the café also serves items such as imo curry and various sweets in a single tiny kitchen with no stated allergen handling, so it should not be considered celiac-safe.
places.vegan_detail: The base ingredient is sweet potato, and plain stone-baked/roasted sweet potato and the sweet potato chips are likely plant-based, but the signature まるごと焼きいもブリュレ uses vanilla ice cream and custard (dairy/egg), the smoothie and soft cream use milk, and coffee can be had black. There is no documented vegan menu, so plant-based diners can find a few items but should ask. Not formally vegan.
places.hours_raw: 10:00-18:00, closed Tue (plus occasional sudden/irregular closures noted humorously on the shop sign as "totsuzen" / "suddenly")
places.neighborhood: Teramachi / Nomachi (near Hirokoji bus stop, toward the Teramachi temple district)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Imoya Cafe Tano is a tiny counter cafe in Kanazawa's Teramachi temple district, opened by Nishikawa Masahiro, who previously worked as a French chef in hotels and restaurants. After earlier restaurants closed, he was invited to take over this small Teramachi space, which he fitte

chef_bio.background: Imoya Cafe Tano is a tiny counter cafe in Kanazawa's Teramachi temple district, opened by Nishikawa Masahiro, who previously worked as a French chef in hotels and restaurants. After earlier restaurants closed, he was invited to take over this small Teramachi space, which he fitted out himself with his own cooking tools and interior.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop name 楽 is read 'tano' rather than 'raku'. It comes from advice a French service manager once gave him that food is not truly nourishing without laughter and enjoyment; he wanted to run the place 'amusingly and joyfully' for both himself and his guests.
chef_bio.specialty: Sweet potato everything: the signature まるごと焼きいもブリュレ (a whole roasted sweet potato with vanilla ice cream and custard, finished with caramelized sugar), plus an imo smoothie topped with gold-leaf cream, imo soft cream, sweet potato chips, chilled stone-baked sweet potato, and freshly ground coffee. He rotates several premium sweet potato varieties such as Beni Haruka, Goro-shima Kintoki (a Kaga heirloom vegetable), Silk Sweet and Annou Imo.

anecdotes.text: The chef draws on his French training to develop the desserts through trial and error, plating each one fresh in front of the guest at the counter.
The shop sign humorously lists irregular closures as 'とつぜん' (suddenly), reflecting the relaxed, personality-driven spirit behind the name 'tano'.

positives.text: Desserts are made to order at the counter in front of you, so you can see the ingredients and speak directly with the owner-chef about what goes into each dish.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny owner-run counter café where the chatty master cooks desserts right in front of you, so a foreign student with little Japanese will be most comfortable bringing a Japanese-speaking friend or guide to enjoy the conversation that is part of the experience.

places.name: 善隣カフェ（ZenrinCafe） (Zenrin Cafe)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free offering is documented. The signature "Tanto Curry" is a Japanese-style curry, which is made with a wheat-flour roux, and the cafe also serves coffee/tea and light fare in a casual community setting. There is no indication of gluten-free preparation or allergen handling, so this is not safe for celiacs. Ask directly if you have a medical need.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu is documented. The kitchen focuses on local-ingredient home-style lunches like the Tanto Curry (650 yen); Japanese curry typically contains meat and/or dairy and wheat. The cafe is small and home-style, so a vegetable-based plate may be possible on request, but nothing is confirmed in available sources. Vegetarians/vegans should ask in advance.
places.hours_raw: 10:00～16:00. Closed Sundays, public holidays, and year-end/New Year. (Phone reachable Mon–Fri 10:00–16:00.)
places.cuisine: Community machiya cafe / Japanese curry and
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Zenrin Cafe is a community cafe set in a restored Kanazawa machiya (traditional townhouse) at the foot of the Teramachi temple district, run by the social-welfare corporation Daiichi Zenrin-kan. Beyond serving visitors, it operates as a workplace that provides continued-employmen

chef_bio.background: Zenrin Cafe is a community cafe set in a restored Kanazawa machiya (traditional townhouse) at the foot of the Teramachi temple district, run by the social-welfare corporation Daiichi Zenrin-kan. Beyond serving visitors, it operates as a workplace that provides continued-employment support for people with disabilities, in partnership with the NPO Fureai Kobo Tanto and the general incorporated association Life PALM.
chef_bio.philosophy: A place where neighbors and travelers mingle casually over a cup of coffee, built on community welfare and local production for local consumption (chisan-chisho) using Kanazawa ingredients.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature lunch is the home-style Tanto Curry (650 yen) made with local Kanazawa ingredients; coffee starts at 300 yen and there is royal milk tea around 350 yen. Guests can relax in tatami rooms (shoes off) during a break between temple visits.

anecdotes.text: The cafe doubles as an off-site employment-support workplace, so visiting for a coffee directly supports work opportunities for people with disabilities.
Travelers can take off their shoes and rest in a quiet tatami room mid-sightseeing, with the budget-friendly Tanto Curry made from local ingredients as the recommended lunch.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a warm, very local neighborhood community cafe with little to no English, so a foreign student should come with a Japanese-speaking guide or translation app and a relaxed, friendly attitude.

places.name: 和食ダイニング ばん莱 (Washoku Dining Banrai)
places.gf_detail: This is a wheat- and dashi-heavy Japanese restaurant — fried horse mackerel (aji fry), sushi, oden, and soy-based seasonings dominate the menu. No gluten-free accommodation is documented, and it is not safe for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: Menu is centered on seafood (sashimi, sushi, fried aji), Noto pork, eel, roast beef, and dashi-based dishes. No vegan options are documented.
places.neighborhood: Nomachi / Nishi-chaya district (にし茶屋街)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Banrai opened on April 18, 2023 in Kanazawa's quieter Nishi-chaya teahouse district, occupying an upper floor of a townhouse building it shares with a sister seafood restaurant, 町家食堂 晴れやか, which opened the same day on the ground floor.

chef_bio.origin: Set in the Nishi-chaya district, about ten minutes by car from Kanazawa Station, in a renovated machiya-style building.
chef_bio.background: Banrai opened on April 18, 2023 in Kanazawa's quieter Nishi-chaya teahouse district, occupying an upper floor of a townhouse building it shares with a sister seafood restaurant, 町家食堂 晴れやか, which opened the same day on the ground floor.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen emphasizes local, seasonal ingredients (chisan-chisho) and aims to bring out the natural appeal of each ingredient.
chef_bio.specialty: Hearty, affordable set meals such as the aji fry teishoku (fried horse mackerel) and the Noto pork loin teishoku, alongside sushi, sashimi, oden, and lunch rice bowls (seafood chirashi, premium eel, roast beef).

anecdotes.text: A visiting blogger praised the Nishi-chaya district as a calmer alternative to Kanazawa's busier teahouse areas and enjoyed both the aji fry set and the Noto pork loin set here, noting the meals came with assorted small sides.
Banrai and its ground-floor seafood sibling 町家食堂 晴れやか opened together on April 18, 2023 in the Nishi-chaya district.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The signature aji fry is breaded and deep-fried (wheat batter), and the menu includes sushi and soy-seasoned dishes, so gluten and cross-contact are pervasive.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: A sushi and washoku restaurant; standard wheat-containing soy sauce is used and no gluten-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Kitchen is seafood-, pork-, and dashi-based; no vegan preparation or separation is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A small local washoku spot where a foreign student should be comfortable pointing at the lunch set menu, though staff likely speak mainly Japanese.

places.name: 海鮮丼 ゆたか水産 (Kaisendon Yutaka Suisan)
places.gf_detail: This is a seafood-bowl specialist with no gluten-free provision. The official menu lists no gluten-free, vegan, or vegetarian options. Bowls are built on sushi rice seasoned with citrus-juice vinegar plus ingredients like kanpyo, and every bowl is served with aosa miso soup (dashi/miso typically contains wheat), and nigiri/seafood would be served with soy sauce. There is no documentation of a dedicated fryer or any allergen-handling protocol. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: A dedicated seafood-bowl restaurant — every signature item is built on raw fish, shellfish, roe, or meat (e.g., the flagship bowl piles on 15 kinds of seafood; other bowls feature nodoguro, crab, or wagyu roast beef). Bowls come with miso soup made with seafood-based dashi. No vegan or vegetarian dishes are documented.
places.hours_raw: 7:00-15:00; closed Wed (open if Wed is a public holiday). An early-morning breakfast bowl is available from 7:00.
places.neighborhood: Omicho Market (Musashigatsuji / inside Omicho Market)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The shop is run by chef Toyoshi Nishimura, formerly the head chef of the Katamachi restaurant 'Shoujuku' (笑宿), which closed beloved by its regulars. He trained under Rokusaburo Michiba, the celebrated 'Iron Chef' of Japanese cuisine. Opening a seafood-bowl shop inside Omicho Mark

chef_bio.background: The shop is run by chef Toyoshi Nishimura, formerly the head chef of the Katamachi restaurant 'Shoujuku' (笑宿), which closed beloved by its regulars. He trained under Rokusaburo Michiba, the celebrated 'Iron Chef' of Japanese cuisine. Opening a seafood-bowl shop inside Omicho Market had long been his dream.
chef_bio.philosophy: A fine-dining craftsman applying kappo techniques to the humble rice bowl — the shop's own line is 'what happens when a fishmonger sources the ingredients and a ryotei chef builds the bowl.' Each ingredient is treated with selective grilling and precise knife work; the rice is seasoned with shiitake, kanpyo, sesame and ginger and finished with citrus juice instead of vinegar for a clean aftertaste.
chef_bio.specialty: Lavish seafood rice bowls built on fish bought each morning at the market auction. The flagship 'Yutaka Saikyo-don' heaps on roughly 15 kinds of seafood — nodoguro (blackthroat seaperch), bluefin tuna, sea urchin, salmon roe, crab, yellowtail, sweet shrimp, Noto pufferfish and more. Other bowls include a nodoguro bowl, buri-crab bowl, and a wagyu roast-beef bowl.

anecdotes.text: The owner-chef trained under Rokusaburo Michiba, Japan's famous 'Iron Chef,' and previously ran the head kitchen at Katamachi's 'Shoujuku' before opening his long-dreamed-of Omicho seafood-bowl shop.
There is a 1,000-yen morning-only 'asa-don' breakfast bowl served from the 7am opening — a generous deal noted by diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Bowls are served with miso soup and seafood/nigiri are eaten with soy sauce; no gluten-free soy sauce or wheat-free dashi is documented, so wheat exposure should be assumed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Every dish centers on raw seafood, roe, or meat and bowls come with seafood-dashi miso soup; no vegan items exist on the menu.

cultural_comfort.note: A bustling, no-frills market seafood-bowl counter where a smile, a greeting, and pointing at the photo menu will get you a long way, though staff may have limited English during the morning rush.

places.name: 近江町海鮮丼家ひら井 本店 (Omicho Kaisendonya Hirai Honten)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. This is a seafood-bowl specialist where dishes are served with wheat-based soy sauce, and alternatives include tempura and unagi (eel) bowls that use wheat batter and wheat-containing tare sauce. No gluten-free menu, no dedicated preparation, and dashi/seasonings are wheat-and-soy based. Not safe for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians. The entire menu is built around raw seafood rice bowls (tuna, salmon roe, sea urchin, crab, sweet shrimp, nodoguro) and fish-based alternatives. Rice is typically vinegared and dishes use fish dashi and soy sauce. No plant-based options are offered.
places.hours_raw: Weekdays 11:00-15:00; weekends/holidays 10:30-15:00. Closed Wednesdays (and New Year's Day).
places.neighborhood: 近江町市場 (Omicho Market, Musashigatsuji)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Hirai is a seafood rice-bowl specialist set inside Omicho Market, Kanazawa's bustling 300-year-old central market. The honten (main shop) sits among the market's fish stalls, sourcing its catch from the very vendors around it. It now operates additional branches (an Ichiba-kan lo

chef_bio.origin: Omicho Market (近江町市場), Kanazawa's historic central food market
chef_bio.background: Hirai is a seafood rice-bowl specialist set inside Omicho Market, Kanazawa's bustling 300-year-old central market. The honten (main shop) sits among the market's fish stalls, sourcing its catch from the very vendors around it. It now operates additional branches (an Ichiba-kan location and a Kenrokuen-area shop), but the main shop remains the original Omicho counter.
chef_bio.philosophy: A 'ganko' (stubborn/uncompromising) approach to portions and freshness — large, abundant bowls built from carefully selected morning catch and arranged with care.
chef_bio.specialty: Generously heaped seafood bowls (kaisendon) featuring fish from the Sea of Japan. Around 20 varieties are offered; signatures include the 近江町海鮮丼 (Omicho seafood bowl), 金沢三味丼 (Kanazawa three-taste bowl), and ウニ・イクラ丼 (sea urchin and salmon-roe bowl), plus a 'special selection' bowl loaded with tuna, crab, sweet shrimp and sea urchin. Smaller bowls and cooked alternatives like tempura or eel bowls are available for those who prefer not to eat raw fish.

anecdotes.text: The shop describes its bowls as colorful and piled high with the bounty of the Sea of Japan (彩り美しく、日本海の幸をたっぷり盛りつけた様々な海鮮丼).
Located in Omicho Market within a 15-minute walk of Kenrokuen, the 21st Century Museum and the Higashi Chaya district.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Seafood bowls are eaten with standard Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat; no gluten-free soy sauce is mentioned.

cultural_comfort.note: A friendly market kaisendon counter where pointing at the photo menu works fine; a few words of Japanese help, but it is used to tourists and is welcoming to foreigners.

places.name: 海鮮丼ひかりや 近江町市場店 (Kaisendon Hikariya Omicho Ichiba-ten)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. The signature まるひ丼 is dressed with a house sesame-soy sauce (ごま醤油だれ) and finished by pouring hot dashi broth to make chazuke; standard Japanese soy sauce and dashi typically contain wheat. No gluten-free menu or accommodations are documented. Celiacs should avoid.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. This is a dedicated seafood donburi specialist — the entire concept is raw fish/shellfish bowls (ikura, snow crab, sweet shrimp, tuna, octopus, scallop) over rice with fish-based dashi. No plant-based options are documented.
places.hours_raw: 10:00-16:00 (L.O. 15:30); occasional evening service 17:30-21:00. Closed Mondays (or following Tuesday if Monday is a holiday). Sources vary slightly (9:00/10:00 open; 15:30/16:00 close).
places.neighborhood: Omicho Market (Musashigatsuji)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 海鮮丼ひかりや is billed as the only original seafood-bowl specialty shop inside Kanazawa's historic Omicho Market, the city kitchen that has fed locals for centuries. It sits on the market's ground floor in the Fruit Murahata Omicho building, a few shops in from the Kaga-Hachiban-machi

chef_bio.background: 海鮮丼ひかりや is billed as the only original seafood-bowl specialty shop inside Kanazawa's historic Omicho Market, the city kitchen that has fed locals for centuries.
