It sits on the market's ground floor in the Fruit Murahata Omicho building, a few shops in from the Kaga-Hachiban-machi entrance, with a compact counter-and-table layout that draws solo travelers, market workers, and families alike.
chef_bio.philosophy: The whole bowl is built to be eaten twice: first the seafood is enjoyed over rice with the shop's special sesame-soy sauce (ごま醤油だれ), then hot dashi is poured over the leftover rice and fish to finish it as a clean, savory だし茶漬け (dashi chazuke) — 'one bowl, twice delicious.' Reviewers note the dashi is tuned so the hot broth never brings out any fishiness from the raw seafood.
chef_bio.specialty: The house signature is the まるひ丼 (Maruhi-don), offered in three tiers — 梅 (¥1,280), 竹 (¥1,780), and 松 (¥2,380). The premium 松 piles on seven toppings including ikura, snow crab, sweet shrimp, toro bincho tuna, minced maguro, octopus, and baby scallops.

anecdotes.text: The bowl is designed as a two-act meal — eat it first as a sesame-soy seafood don, then pour the provided hot dashi over the rest to turn it into dashi-chazuke for a second flavor in the same bowl.
A reviewer praised that the hot-dashi finish, which often draws out a fishy smell from raw seafood, produced none here — 'まったく出てこなかったのでとっても美味しかった.'
Described as Omicho Market's only original kaisen-don specialty shop, with the premium 松 まるひ丼 layering seven toppings including ikura, snow crab, sweet shrimp and tuna.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Bowls are seasoned with a house sesame-soy sauce (特製ごま醤油だれ) and finished by pouring hot dashi to make chazuke; standard soy sauce and dashi typically contain wheat, making the dish unsafe for celiacs.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is entirely raw fish and shellfish bowls (ikura, crab, shrimp, tuna, octopus, scallop) over rice with fish-based dashi; no plant-based options documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual, tourist-friendly seafood-bowl counter inside the bustling Omicho Market; a few basic Japanese greetings and pointing at the picture menu will get you a warm welcome, though detailed English may be limited.

places.name: 海鮮丼いちば (Kaisendon Ichiba)
places.gf_detail: A seafood donburi and sushi shop built around rice bowls served with wheat-based soy sauce; no gluten-free menu, labeling, or dedicated prep is documented, so this is not safe for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: The entire concept is raw fish and seafood rice bowls (sashimi over rice). There are no documented vegan or vegetarian options.
places.hours_raw: 09:00-15:00 (until early evening). Irregular days off.
places.neighborhood: Omicho Market (Musashigatsuji)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 海鮮丼いちば sits right in the heart of Kanazawa's Omicho Market, the 300-year-old warren of fishmongers and grocers that locals call their kitchen. It is run as part of the Omicho Ichiba Zushi family of shops and is one of the market's tiniest seafood-bowl counters, the kind of place

chef_bio.background: 海鮮丼いちば sits right in the heart of Kanazawa's Omicho Market, the 300-year-old warren of fishmongers and grocers that locals call their kitchen. It is run as part of the Omicho Ichiba Zushi family of shops and is one of the market's tiniest seafood-bowl counters, the kind of place that fills to capacity within minutes of opening.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop emphasizes seasonal and local ingredients (旬の食材・地物ネタ) bought fresh from the surrounding market stalls each day.
chef_bio.specialty: Seafood rice bowls heaped with the morning's catch: a seasonal omakase bowl, a 'daimyo' bowl piled with almost everything in the case, and a Hokuriku luxury bowl featuring local treasures like nodoguro (rosy seaperch) and Toyama Bay white shrimp. Aburi (lightly seared) nodoguro and gas-ebi shrimp are signatures.

anecdotes.text: Described on the official Omicho Market site as one of the one-or-two most popular tiny restaurants in the whole market, so small that seats vanish fast.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Raw fish over rice is served with standard wheat-based soy sauce; no gluten-free accommodation is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a sashimi/donburi shop, standard Japanese soy sauce (which contains wheat) is the default condiment.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Every documented dish is seafood-based; raw fish is the core ingredient, so vegan cross-contact is unavoidable.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, bustling market counter where you point at the seafood-bowl photos and pay by card or cash; a few words of Japanese or a translation app go a long way, so bring a guide or be ready to gesture.

places.name: そば やまの葉 (Soba Yamanoha)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. While juwari (十割) soba is 100% buckwheat, the kitchen also makes wheat-containing soba (外二/外二八, roughly 20% wheat flour) and Shinshu oyaki dumplings whose dough uses wheat, so there is heavy shared wheat handling and flour in the air. The dipping broth (tsuyu) is made with soy sauce, which standardly contains wheat. No dedicated gluten-free preparation or celiac protocol is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-oriented. The tsuyu and broths are dashi-based (fish/bonito), and signature dishes include negitoro (raw tuna) bowls and tempura. The walnut-miso (kurumi) soba and nameko-oroshi soba may appear plant-based but are served with fish-dashi tsuyu. No vegan menu or vegan dashi is documented; a vegetarian would need to ask, and even then dashi makes it unlikely.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-14:00, 18:00-20:30. Closed Wednesdays and the 2nd Tuesday of each month.
places.neighborhood: 泉が丘（犀川以南・野町駅方面） Izumigaoka, south of the Saigawa near Nomach
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The owner trained as a soba maker in Shinshu (Nagano), Japan's most celebrated buckwheat region, and brings a Japanese-cuisine chef's discipline to the craft. He opened the shop in December 2011 in the Izumigaoka neighborhood south of the Saigawa river.

chef_bio.chef_name: Yasumura Hiroshi (安村弘 / やすむら)
chef_bio.background: The owner trained as a soba maker in Shinshu (Nagano), Japan's most celebrated buckwheat region, and brings a Japanese-cuisine chef's discipline to the craft. He opened the shop in December 2011 in the Izumigaoka neighborhood south of the Saigawa river.
chef_bio.philosophy: Showcasing the bounty of the Shinshu satoyama (foothill countryside) through careful, from-scratch soba craft.
chef_bio.specialty: Two styles of hand-cut soba are offered: a juwari (100% buckwheat) made with locally milled Torigoe-area buckwheat flour, prized for its aroma and clean throat-feel, and a sotoni/soto-nihachi (roughly 80% buckwheat, 20% wheat) made with Hokkaido flour that pairs well with the house tsuyu. The signature is kurumi-soba (walnut soba): fragrant roasted walnuts blended with white miso into a dipping sauce that diners loosen with broth using a small whisk. Other favorites include nameko-oroshi soba topped with large Yoshino-area nameko mushrooms, tempura and ten-don sets, sobagaki, and a multi-course soba tasting menu (around 4,500 yen) ending with house-made soba-tea ice cream.

anecdotes.text: The kurumi-soba is rooted in a regional specialty of Komoro in Nagano: roasted walnuts ground with sweet white miso, dissolved into the dipping broth before the noodles go in.
The shop also serves Shinshu-style oyaki, savory dough dumplings wrapped around fillings such as miso-seasoned nozawana pickled greens.
A four-course soba tasting lets guests experience buckwheat in several forms, finishing with fragrant soba-tea ice cream.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen handles wheat-containing soba (soto-nihachi, ~20% wheat) and wheat-dough oyaki alongside the 100% buckwheat juwari, so shared flour and boiling water make cross-contact very likely even for the juwari noodles.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soba is served with a soy-sauce-based dipping broth (tsuyu); standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths are dashi-based and the menu features fish (negitoro) and tempura; no separate vegan stock is documented.

positives.text: A genuine juwari (100% buckwheat) soba is on the menu, milled from local Torigoe buckwheat, for those avoiding wheat in the noodle itself (though shared equipment and the soy-based tsuyu remain concerns).

cultural_comfort.note: A small owner-run neighborhood soba shop where ordering in basic Japanese (or by pointing at the menu photos) is the easiest path; staff are unlikely to speak much English but the atmosphere is calm and welcoming.

places.name: 和栗白露 (Waguri Shiratsuyu)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature Noto-chestnut Mont Blanc is built on wheat-based components (sponge cake and pastry cream layers), and there is no documented gluten-free menu or celiac handling. Cross-contamination from flour in a small dessert kitchen is likely. Not safe for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The flagship Mont Blanc and related desserts contain dairy throughout (mascarpone, custard/creme patissiere, unsweetened fresh whipped cream) and egg-based components such as meringue and sponge. No vegan dessert option is documented despite the chestnut paste itself being sugar-free.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-17:00 (last order 16:30), open daily with irregular closures
places.neighborhood: Higashi Chaya District / Kannon-machi (near the Asano River,
places.cuisine: Japanese chestnut specialty cafe and sweets
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Waguri Shiratsuyu is a Japanese-chestnut specialty cafe set inside a renovated machiya townhouse more than a century old, on a quiet lane just off the main street of Kanazawa's Higashi Chaya district. The owner, who comes from Noto, opened the shop to champion the region's chestn

chef_bio.background: Waguri Shiratsuyu is a Japanese-chestnut specialty cafe set inside a renovated machiya townhouse more than a century old, on a quiet lane just off the main street of Kanazawa's Higashi Chaya district. The owner, who comes from Noto, opened the shop to champion the region's chestnut growers and the idea of local production for local tables, personally overseeing sourcing and keeping direct relationships with the orchards.
chef_bio.philosophy: Local production and local consumption (chisan-chisho) — using the cafe as a way to connect visitors with Noto and to support chestnut farmers who struggle with sustainability.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature is the 'Harizuri' premium Mont Blanc made from Noto aged roasted chestnuts. The chestnut paste carries no added sugar, drawing its sweetness entirely from naturally fallen Noto chestnuts that are aged and pressure-roasted; it is piped to order into ultra-thin roughly 1mm strands over layers of mascarpone, sponge, meringue, custard, and unsweetened fresh cream.

anecdotes.text: The chestnut grower the shop highlights, Matsuo of Wajima, developed an aging-and-roasting technique after discovering Noto chestnuts online in 2005; the cafe was created in part to bring that work to a wider audience.

Guests can watch and photograph the chestnut paste being extruded through a custom press into delicate roughly 1mm strands, a piece of theater meant to heighten the aroma and texture of each made-to-order Mont Blanc.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Desserts are assembled in a small kitchen using wheat-based sponge and pastry cream, so flour is present and cross-contamination is likely; no gluten-free handling is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The signature Mont Blanc and related sweets contain dairy (mascarpone, custard, fresh cream) and egg (meringue, sponge); no separate vegan preparation is documented.

positives.text: The chestnut paste itself uses only Noto aged roasted chestnuts with no added sugar, so the chestnut element is naturally simple.

cultural_comfort.note: A calm machiya cafe slightly off the main Higashi Chaya street where pointing to the signature Mont Blanc and a smile will carry you, though detailed English explanations may be limited.

places.name: 能加万菜 市場屋 近江町総本店 (Noukabanzai Ichibaya Omicho Sohonten)
places.gf_detail: This is a seafood-bowl, sushi and Kanazawa-oden market eatery where soy sauce, wheat-based seasonings (including local ishiru fermented-fish sauce) and dashi are used heavily across the menu. There is no documented gluten-free program, no dedicated preparation area, and no allergen handling information published. Sushi and kaisen-don are served with regular wheat-based soy sauce, and oden broth is not gluten-free. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: The entire concept is fresh seafood sourced directly from Omicho Market — seafood rice bowls (kaisen-don), nigiri sushi, crab dishes and fish-based Kanazawa oden. Dashi and fish-based seasonings (ishiru) are foundational. No vegan or vegetarian dishes are documented, and the kitchen is built around raw and cooked fish. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
places.hours_raw: 7:00–22:00 (L.O. food 21:30), open daily. Note: hours have varied around a 2025 renovation; older listings showed 7:00/8:00–15:00/16:00/18:00. Confirm directly before visiting.
places.neighborhood: Omicho Market (Musashigatsuji / right by the Jukkenmachi entrance)
places.notes: Ichibaya is the fifth shop of the Kanazawa-based Noukabanzai (能加万菜) brand, which centers on Ishikawa's local ingredients and food culture. Set right at the Jukkencho entrance of Omicho Market, it serves fresh seafood bought directly from the market that morning, with breakfast se

chef_bio.background: Ichibaya is the fifth shop of the Kanazawa-based Noukabanzai (能加万菜) brand, which centers on Ishikawa's local ingredients and food culture. Set right at the Jukkencho entrance of Omicho Market, it serves fresh seafood bought directly from the market that morning, with breakfast service starting early so visitors can eat a seafood bowl from the start of the day.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop aims to offer 'delicious food and good time' that becomes an enjoyable page in visitors' memories of Kanazawa, showcasing Ishikawa's ingredients, seasonings (such as ishiru) and craft tableware.
chef_bio.specialty: Signature dishes are the 特選近江町市場屋丼 (premium seafood bowl) and generously topped nigiri sushi, alongside other kaisen-don, a crab Kaga-temari bowl, and Kanazawa oden — all served in regional ceramics such as Kutani porcelain and Yamanaka lacquerware.

anecdotes.text: It is the fifth location of the Noukabanzai brand, positioned so guests can enjoy seafood bowls, sushi and Kanazawa oden together right inside the market.
The kitchen highlights Ishikawa craft tableware — Kutani porcelain and Yamanaka lacquerware — and traditional seasonings like ishiru fermented-fish sauce.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Sushi and seafood bowls are served with standard wheat-based soy sauce, and local ishiru (fermented-fish) seasoning and dashi are used throughout the menu.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is built entirely around fresh fish and shellfish from Omicho Market, including raw seafood bowls, nigiri sushi, crab dishes and fish-based Kanazawa oden; no vegan items are documented.

positives.text: An English menu is available, which can help travelers identify ingredients.

cultural_comfort.note: Very approachable for a foreign student: it sits right inside the bustling Omicho Market, offers an English menu, and the casual point-and-order seafood-bowl format makes it easy even with little Japanese.

places.name: 乙女寿司 (Otome Sushi)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional Edomae-style sushi counter where nigiri is brushed with nikiri (wheat-based soy sauce) before serving, and cooked appetizers rely on dashi and soy-based seasonings. There is no gluten-free menu and no indication of allergen accommodation. Not safe for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: An omakase sushi restaurant built entirely around Noto seafood — sweet shrimp, nodoguro (blackthroat seaperch), white-fleshed fish, sea urchin, anago and similar. There are no vegan options; this is not a destination for plant-based diners.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Thu, Fri, Sat: 12:00-14:00, 17:00-22:00 (last entry 19:30). Closed Wed, Sun, and holidays.
places.neighborhood: Katamachi / Kiguramachi (near Korinbo intersection)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Otome Sushi opened in 1995 on a quiet back lane in Kanazawa's Kibura-machi, just off the lively Katamachi district near the Korinbo crossing. The master, Kazuhiko Tsurumi, was born in Kanazawa and began his apprenticeship at age sixteen, bringing roughly four decades of craft to

chef_bio.background: Otome Sushi opened in 1995 on a quiet back lane in Kanazawa's Kibura-machi, just off the lively Katamachi district near the Korinbo crossing. The master, Kazuhiko Tsurumi, was born in Kanazawa and began his apprenticeship at age sixteen, bringing roughly four decades of craft to his counter. The shop has grown into one of the city's most celebrated sushi houses, earning two Michelin stars in the 2021 Michelin Guide Hokuriku.
chef_bio.philosophy: Tsurumi is known for meticulous sourcing and understated, balanced seasoning, letting the quality of Noto ingredients lead while quietly focusing on his work rather than performing for guests. Small touches, such as hiding a layer of uni sauce beneath the sea urchin, reflect his attention to detail.
chef_bio.specialty: An omakase course rooted in the seasonal seafood of the Noto Peninsula — sweet shrimp, nodoguro (blackthroat seaperch), white-fleshed fish, squid scored with fine knife work, sea urchin and anago — moving through cooked appetizers into a procession of nigiri. The chef brushes each piece with nikiri sauce before it is served.

anecdotes.text: On arrival the master greets each guest with a simple choice — whether to start with appetizers (tsumami) or go straight to nigiri — setting the tone for a personal, conversational meal.
Writers consistently note the shop serves mostly local regulars rather than tourists and offers exceptional value, with prices well below comparable Tokyo counters.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Nigiri is brushed with nikiri (a soy-sauce-based glaze) before serving, and standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat — there is no indication of a gluten-free alternative.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire omakase is built around fish, shellfish, and seafood from the Noto Peninsula, with no plant-based course described.

cultural_comfort.note: This is an intimate, reservation-only counter where the master serves a set omakase flow and mostly hosts regulars, so a foreign student should book by phone two months ahead and be comfortable with a quiet, Japanese-language, high-end atmosphere.

places.name: 麺屋大河 (Menya Taiga)
places.gf_detail: This is a wheat-based ramen and tsukemen shop. Noodles are made from wheat, and miso/pork-bone broths typically contain wheat-derived soy and miso ingredients. Not safe for celiacs; no gluten-free options documented.
places.vegan_detail: A miso ramen specialist built on pork-bone (tonkotsu) broth with miso; no vegan options documented. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
places.neighborhood: Around Kanazawa Stn (Hokutetsu Kanazawa Stn)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Menya Taiga is a small, much-loved miso ramen specialist near Kanazawa Station (steps from Hokutetsu Kanazawa Station). It built a regional reputation as a Hokuriku miso ramen destination and grew into a tiny group with a flagship, a Takayanagi branch, and a tanmen-focused shop.

chef_bio.background: Menya Taiga is a small, much-loved miso ramen specialist near Kanazawa Station (steps from Hokutetsu Kanazawa Station). It built a regional reputation as a Hokuriku miso ramen destination and grew into a tiny group with a flagship, a Takayanagi branch, and a tanmen-focused shop.
chef_bio.philosophy: A wellness-minded touch sets it apart: guests are served a complimentary vegetable juice before the meal, framed as helping moderate blood-sugar spikes. The miso broth is praised as mellow and balanced rather than oily, finished with aonori, fresh ginger, and yuzu for layered flavor.
chef_bio.specialty: Specially blended red miso (described in sources as Hokkaido red miso) simmered into an aromatic, rich yet clean broth. Signature bowls include the special miso ramen, red miso ramen, black miso ramen, and miso tsukemen, with seasonal limited bowls such as oyster or crab miso ramen.

anecdotes.text: Ranked at the top for Ishikawa Prefecture on a major ramen database and known to draw weekday lunch queues, with the shop sometimes opening early yet still unable to clear the line.
Widely celebrated as one of western Japan’s standout ramen bowls.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles are boiled in the same kitchen; no gluten-free preparation documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Miso-based broths at ramen shops typically contain wheat-derived miso and soy ingredients; no allergen-free claims documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is pork-bone based with pork toppings; no separate vegan preparation documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, busy counter ramen shop where you order quickly and slurp; basic Japanese or pointing helps, and expect to line up at peak times.

noodles.notes: Medium-thick wavy noodles chosen to grab and carry the roasted-miso broth; they cling to the soup with a firm, satisfying bite.

ramen.signature_bowl: Miso Ramen (味噌ラーメン) — the house standard built on iron-pot roasted miso; black miso (squid-ink) and red miso (spicy) are the popular variations, with miso tsukemen for dipping.
ramen.chashu: Noto-pork chashu, prized for its quality; braised pork served as the bowl's centerpiece.

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui ramen noodles plus a miso-and-soy tare; no rice or konjac noodle option is documented, so this bowl is not celiac-safe.

vegan.note: The broth is built on chicken bones, pork bones, chicken feet and dried sardine, finished with pork fat and pork chashu — no plant-based bowl is offered.

ramen.pantheon: A modern Hokuriku miso specialist where the soul is the miso itself: each serving is finished in its own iron pot so the paste chars and caramelizes, sending up a smoky, deeply aromatic fragrance over a chicken-and-pork broth lifted with niboshi and kombu. Bright accents of grated ginger, yuzu and green laver keep the rich, roasted miso from ever turning heavy, so the flavor shifts as you eat.

places.highlight: A modern Hokuriku miso specialist where the soul is the miso itself: each serving is finished in its own iron pot so the paste chars and caramelizes, sending up a smoky, deeply aromatic fragrance over a chicken-and-pork broth lifted with niboshi and kombu. Bright accents of grated ginger, yuzu and green laver keep the rich, roasted miso from ever turning heavy, so the flavor shifts as you eat.

places.name: 海鮮市場料理 市の蔵 (Kaisen Ichiba Ryori Ichi no Kura)
places.gf_detail: This is a seafood and Kaga-cuisine restaurant built around fish, crab, tempura, grilled dishes, and rice bowls. Cooking relies heavily on Japanese soy sauce, miso, and dashi — all of which typically contain or are processed with wheat — so it cannot be considered celiac-safe. No gluten-free menu, gluten-free soy sauce, or dedicated preparation is documented, and signature dishes (jibuni stew, crab miso shell-grill, set meals) are wheat- and dashi-based. A celiac traveler should not rely on this restaurant.
places.vegan_detail: The entire concept is seafood-market cuisine: live crab, seasonal fish, sashimi, rice bowls, and duck jibuni. Dashi (bonito/fish stock) underlies most dishes. No vegan or vegetarian menu is documented and the kitchen is fundamentally fish- and meat-based. Not a viable option for vegans or vegetarians.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:30). Open year-round (some listings note closed only on New Year's Day).
places.neighborhood: Omicho Market (Ichiba-kan 2F)
places.notes: Ichi no Kura sits on the second floor of the Ichiba-kan building right inside Omicho Market, the centuries-old marketplace locals call 'Kanazawa's kitchen.' The restaurant draws directly on that bustling market energy, sourcing fish and crab from just downstairs and serving them

chef_bio.origin: 近江町市場（いちば館2F）, central Kanazawa
chef_bio.background: Ichi no Kura sits on the second floor of the Ichiba-kan building right inside Omicho Market, the centuries-old marketplace locals call 'Kanazawa's kitchen.' The restaurant draws directly on that bustling market energy, sourcing fish and crab from just downstairs and serving them in a warm, wood-filled dining room.
chef_bio.philosophy: To let visitors enjoy the lively spirit of Omicho Market over a meal — pairing Kanazawa sightseeing with fresh local seafood — and notably to offer crab dishes all year rather than only in season.
chef_bio.specialty: Seafood market cuisine and Kaga regional dishes: seasonal fish set meals and kaisen-don rice bowls at lunch, and at dinner traditional Kaga fare alongside crab served year-round. Signature plates include crab miso grilled in the shell (かに味噌甲羅焼き), crab salad, duck jibuni stew (鴨の治部煮), and squid-liver miso grill (いかわた味噌焼き).

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's defining feature is that you can enjoy crab dishes year-round, not just during winter crab season, thanks to its place inside the market.
Lunch focuses on seasonal fish in set meals and rice bowls, while evenings turn to traditional Kaga cuisine and dishes using local meat.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Cuisine is built on soy sauce, miso, and dashi (jibuni stew, grilled dishes, set meals), which in standard Japanese cooking contain or involve wheat; no gluten-free alternatives are documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: A seafood- and meat-centered kitchen using fish dashi throughout; no vegan or vegetarian preparation is mentioned.

cultural_comfort.note: A busy, tourist-friendly market restaurant in the heart of Omicho where pointing at the picture menu and basic greetings will get you a warm welcome, though deep allergy questions may be hard to convey in English.

places.name: 能登牛 串焼き・炙り寿司 たくみ (Notogyu Kushiyaki Aburizushi Takumi)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The seared nigiri and skewers are served with the shop's own soy-based sauce (Japanese soy sauce normally contains wheat), and rice/sushi is central. No gluten-free menu, no dedicated handling, and no allergen information published. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. This is a dedicated Noto beef stall — every menu item (seared beef nigiri, beef skewers, seared beef rolls) centers on A5-grade beef. There are no plant-based options.
places.hours_raw: 10:00–16:00 (per Omicho Market official listing). Tabelog lists Fri–Sun 09:00–16:00, closed Mon–Thu. Effectively a weekend-focused operation; weekends can see lines and early sell-outs.
places.neighborhood: Omicho Market (Omicho Ichiba-kan 1F / Seseragi-dori), centra
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small standing-counter stall tucked into the Omicho Ichiba-kan building on the first floor of Kanazawa's lively Omicho Market, Takumi specializes in Noto beef — a prized local brand. Noto beef is raised in the nature and sea breeze of the Noto Peninsula and is so scarce (only a

chef_bio.background: A small standing-counter stall tucked into the Omicho Ichiba-kan building on the first floor of Kanazawa's lively Omicho Market, Takumi specializes in Noto beef — a prized local brand. Noto beef is raised in the nature and sea breeze of the Noto Peninsula and is so scarce (only around 930 head shipped a year, almost all consumed within Ishikawa) that locals call it a phantom brand.
chef_bio.philosophy: Making premium, hard-to-find Noto beef approachable — grilled fresh and eaten immediately, standing right there in the market, so visitors can taste a regional luxury without a formal restaurant.
chef_bio.specialty: Seared Noto beef nigiri (aburi sushi) and hand-skewered, grilled Noto beef. The kitchen sources A5-grade-or-higher Noto beef, skewers each stick by hand, and grills to order. For the seared nigiri it uses Ishikawa Koshihikari rice, lightly torches the surface of the beef, and finishes it with the shop's own soy-based sauce. The lineup also includes seared tuna and seared gunkan rolls.

anecdotes.text: The seared Noto beef nigiri (from around 700 yen) is described by visitors as filling the mouth with umami and sweetness, with a melting texture; the convenience of eating rare premium beef on the spot has made it especially popular with tourists.
The shop uses a golden cow as its signboard mascot and frames itself as supporting local Kanazawa and Ishikawa tourism.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Seared nigiri and skewers are finished with the shop's own soy-based sauce; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, so the sauce should be assumed to contain gluten.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Entirely a Noto beef stall — all items are beef-based and grilled on shared equipment; no plant-based options.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny standing stall in the bustling market where you point, pay cash, and eat on the spot — easy for a foreign student, though there is no English menu and staff likely speak limited English.

places.name: フルーツむらはた 本店 (Fruit Murahata Honten)
places.gf_detail: A traditional fruit parlor serving parfaits with cake, cookies, cereal/granola and wheat-based components; no gluten-free menu or celiac handling is documented, so it is not safe for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: Parfaits are built on dairy ice cream, cream and milk; although the fresh fruit is plant-based, no vegan or dairy-free options are documented.
places.neighborhood: Musashimachi (right by Omicho Market / across from Meitetsu M'za)
places.notes: Murahata is a long-established greengrocer founded in 1914 (Taisho 3) that has grown into a fruit specialist with over a century of history. Its flagship in Musashi-cho keeps a fresh-fruit shop on the ground floor and a beloved fruit parlor upstairs.

chef_bio.origin: Kanazawa, Musashi-cho (steps from Omicho Market, across from Meitetsu M'za)
chef_bio.background: Murahata is a long-established greengrocer founded in 1914 (Taisho 3) that has grown into a fruit specialist with over a century of history. Its flagship in Musashi-cho keeps a fresh-fruit shop on the ground floor and a beloved fruit parlor upstairs.
chef_bio.philosophy: As a specialist fruit company, Murahata pursues the possibilities of fruit and aims to make fruit gifts an indispensable part of Japanese life, insisting that its quality cannot be spoken of without its hundred-year history.
chef_bio.specialty: Seasonal fruit parfaits made with whatever fruit is at its peak — from the classic fruit parfait to honey-peach (suimitsuto), Miyazaki mango and matcha versions — plus a takeout 'Kanazawa Parfait.'

anecdotes.text: The shop began in 1914 as a greengrocer and is now a century-old fruit specialist whose parfaits draw regular queues in central Kanazawa.
The classic fruit parfait is praised for the careful cut and quality of each piece of fruit at an approachable price, a hallmark of a dedicated fruit shop.

positives.text: Built around fresh seasonal fruit, so naturally fruit-forward, though parfaits include dairy and wheat-based items.

cultural_comfort.note: A bustling, walk-in-only fruit parlor where you can simply point at the seasonal parfait you want, so a foreign student can enjoy it comfortably even with no Japanese, though English support is not guaranteed.

places.name: 四十萬谷本舗 本店（金沢町家発酵カフェ） (Shijimaya Honpo Honten (Kanazawa Machiya Hak)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. The shop's core trade is fermented foods (kaburazushi/turnip sushi, koji pickles) and the cafe's signature items are amazake (sweet rice-koji drink), amazake soda, and koji-based gelato. Amazake and gelato can be naturally wheat-free, but there is no published allergen handling, no dedicated equipment information, and koji/fermented products often involve shared production with wheat-containing items. Not confirmed celiac-safe — a sensitive traveler must ask in person about each item.
places.vegan_detail: The cafe's headline offerings are plant-friendly by nature: amazake (made from rice koji), amazake soda in straight/shiso/yuzu/ginger, and koji-based gelato including amazake and amazake-yuzu-basil flavors, plus Kaga-vegetable gelato lines (e.g., Kaga roasted-tea). These are rice/fruit/vegetable based rather than dairy-forward, so there are vegan-friendly choices. However, the cafe does not advertise a certified vegan menu, some gelato may contain dairy, and the parent shop's main products (turnip and daikon sushi cured with fish) are not vegan. Confirm dairy/egg content of individual gelato flavors before ordering.
places.hours_raw: Shop 9:00-19:00 (Sun/holidays 9:00-18:00); Cafe approx 9:00-17:00 (L.O. 16:30). Tourism listing notes shop 9:00-18:00, closed Sundays. Hours vary by source.
places.neighborhood: Yayoi (Teramachi / Nomachi area, south of the Sai River)
places.notes: Shijimaya Honpo was founded in 1875 (Meiji 8) and has spent roughly 150 years making Kanazawa's traditional fermented foods, most famously kaburazushi (turnip cured with yellowtail and rice koji) and daikon-zushi. The Yayoi main store occupies a Kanazawa machiya (townhouse) build

chef_bio.origin: Kanazawa (Yayoi district, near the Teramachi temple area south of the Sai River)
chef_bio.background: Shijimaya Honpo was founded in 1875 (Meiji 8) and has spent roughly 150 years making Kanazawa's traditional fermented foods, most famously kaburazushi (turnip cured with yellowtail and rice koji) and daikon-zushi. The Yayoi main store occupies a Kanazawa machiya (townhouse) building that has kept its traditional appearance for about 140 years, in an area recognized for its preserved historic streetscape.
chef_bio.philosophy: The company frames its work around valuing local tradition and contributing to healthy, enriched living through food and fermentation.
chef_bio.specialty: In one renovated section of the historic shop the family opened a 'fermentation cafe,' turning the koji expertise behind their pickles into gentle, body-friendly sweets and drinks: amazake (sweet rice-koji drink), amazake soda in shiso, yuzu and ginger, and koji-based gelato including amazake, amazake-yuzu-basil and Kaga-vegetable flavors.

anecdotes.text: The cafe sits inside a Meiji-era machiya and looks onto a traditional inner courtyard garden, so visitors sip amazake or eat koji gelato surrounded by the old shop's architecture.
The shop also offers hands-on pickling experiences for kaburazushi and koji pickles, and the family even keeps historic Japanese swords that are explained on certain tour courses.

positives.text: Signature cafe items are rice-koji amazake, amazake soda and koji-based gelato, which are plant-friendly and naturally wheat-free options rather than dairy- or meat-forward dishes.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a historic family fermented-food shop with a quiet cafe corner; staff likely speak limited English, so bring a translation app or a few Japanese phrases and point to the amazake or gelato you want.

places.name: 洋食屋 グリルニュー狸 (Yoshokuya Grill New Tanuki)
places.gf_detail: This is a classic yoshoku kitchen built on wheat — demi-glace, fried cutlets (panko), gratin and soy-seasoned fried rice all feature heavily, and the demi-glace base is simmered for about a week. No gluten-free menu, no dedicated equipment, and no documented allergen handling. Not safe for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: A meat-and-seafood Showa-era Western diner: the signatures are tongue stew, hayashi rice with a week-simmered beef-tendon demi-glace, hamburger steak, salmon steak and chashu-and-shiitake fried rice. Sauces and dressings are house-made but built on meat stock; there are no documented vegan or vegetarian options.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:00-14:30, Dinner 17:00-20:30 (L.O. 20:30). Closed Tuesdays and the 3rd Monday of each month.
places.neighborhood: 小立野 (Komatsuno), near the Ishikawa Prefectural Library, on t
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Founded in 1967 (Showa 42) by Norihiko Yada, who had trained at a Kanazawa Western-style eatery called Tanuki Chaya and borrowed the kanji 狸 (tanuki) from his old employer as a tribute when he opened his own place. The restaurant later settled in its current Komatsuno location ne

chef_bio.chef_name: 矢田 紀彦 (Norihiko Yada), founder; with son 佳弘 (Yoshihiro) and grandson 勇紀 (Yuki)
chef_bio.background: Founded in 1967 (Showa 42) by Norihiko Yada, who had trained at a Kanazawa Western-style eatery called Tanuki Chaya and borrowed the kanji 狸 (tanuki) from his old employer as a tribute when he opened his own place. The restaurant later settled in its current Komatsuno location near the prefectural library, where it is run today by three generations of the Yada family working the same open kitchen together.
chef_bio.philosophy: Everything from sauces to dressings is made in-house, with the family describing the use of unexpected hidden ingredients to give each dish depth and individuality. The third generation has spoken of wanting to faithfully protect the old-shop yoshoku flavors while gradually adding his own touch.
chef_bio.specialty: Its most famous dish is the dark, soy-seasoned 'yakimeshi' fried rice — born from the idea of cooking in a 'tanuki color' (a deep brown) and made with chashu pork and dried shiitake. Also iconic are the hayashi rice over a demi-glace simmered roughly a week from beef tendon and aromatic vegetables and finished with a runny egg, the tongue stew (tan-shichu), hamburger steak, and an Omicho-Market salmon steak.

anecdotes.text: The name comes from the founder's former workplace, the Western eatery 'Tanuki Chaya'; Norihiko Yada took the tanuki (狸) character with him as a homage when he went independent in 1967.

Second-generation Yoshihiro has said the three generations all share the wish to make the shop better — 'we sometimes clash, but I respect my son's fresh ideas and am gradually handing things over' — while third-generation Yuki has said he wants to protect the old-shop flavors and eventually add his own character.
The signature dark fried rice is described locally as 'black yakimeshi,' and the tongue stew has fans who say they could eat its carrots every day.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu is wheat-dominated (panko-fried cutlets, gratin, demi-glace, soy-seasoned fried rice) in a single small kitchen; no gluten-free provision is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature yakimeshi is explicitly a soy-sauce-seasoned fried rice (醤油ベース), and standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Core dishes are beef tongue stew, beef-tendon demi-glace, hamburger steak, salmon and chashu — the kitchen is meat- and fish-centered with no documented vegan separation.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, much-loved local yoshoku counter run by three generations of one family; there is no indication of an English menu or English service, so a foreign student will be most comfortable with a Japanese-speaking friend or a translation app and some patience.

places.name: サニーベルコーヒー 尾張町喫茶 (Sunny Bell Coffee Owaricho Kissa)
places.gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. The signature desserts (tiramisu/panna cotta with biscuit, macarons) and the general café context mean wheat is present. No gluten-free handling or dedicated preparation is documented, so this is not celiac-safe; plain black coffee is naturally GF.
places.vegan_detail: This is a specialty coffee and dessert café built around dairy-heavy items: café latte, panna cotta, tiramisu, and soft serve ice cream. No vegan menu or plant-milk options are documented. A black drip coffee or espresso would be the only incidental vegan order; ask about milk alternatives, as none are confirmed.
places.hours_raw: 10:00-17:00 (Fri from 09:00 per one source); Tabelog lists Fri 09:00-16:00, Tue/Wed/Sat/Sun 10:00-16:00; closed Mon & Thu
places.neighborhood: Owaricho (between Higashi Chaya District and Omicho Market)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] This is the second location of Sunny Bell Coffee, a self-roasting specialty coffee shop regarded as one of Kanazawa's representative coffee houses, whose flagship sits in Kasaichi-machi. The Owaricho branch opened on April 17 (2024), tucked between the Higashi Chaya geisha distri

chef_bio.background: This is the second location of Sunny Bell Coffee, a self-roasting specialty coffee shop regarded as one of Kanazawa's representative coffee houses, whose flagship sits in Kasaichi-machi. The Owaricho branch opened on April 17 (2024), tucked between the Higashi Chaya geisha district and Omicho Market.
chef_bio.philosophy: Where the flagship is a coffee-first counter, the Owaricho location was conceived as a place to slow down: its stated concept is for guests to relax and savor sweets and drinks at leisure, and a dedicated pastry chef was brought on to broaden the dessert lineup.
chef_bio.specialty: Self-roasted drip coffee and graceful latte art carry over from the original shop, but this branch leans into sweets: a tiramisu panna cotta with espresso-soaked biscuit and mascarpone-cream cheese, seasonal mini panna cotta parfaits (mango, blueberry), and house soft serve ice cream (with an affogato option).

anecdotes.text: The space is designed in a deliberately minimalist style with restrained, muted color tones, built around an espresso machine and a pastry showcase.
A summer-season tiramisu soft serve was introduced as a branch-exclusive twist on the shop's signature dessert.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed, design-forward café where you order coffee and dessert at the counter; a few words of Japanese or pointing at the pastry case will get you through comfortably.

places.name: サニーベルコーヒー (Sunny Bell Coffee)
places.gf_detail: A specialty coffee stand, not a gluten-free destination. The food offerings are wheat-based pastries such as canelé and tiramisu, and no gluten-free menu or celiac-safe handling is documented. Black coffee and the bean selection are naturally gluten-free, but there is no dedicated GF preparation, so it is not suitable for strict celiacs beyond plain coffee.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan venue. The signature sweets (tiramisu, soft-serve, pudding, parfaits) are dairy- and egg-based, and milk-based lattes feature heavily. Black drip coffee and whole-bean purchases are plant-based, but no plant-milk option or vegan food is documented; a vegan visitor would need to ask and would largely be limited to black coffee.
places.hours_raw: 11:00-18:00 (Tue, Wed, Fri, Sat, Sun) / closed Mon and Thu
places.neighborhood: Kasaichimachi (between Kanazawa Stn east exit and Omicho Market, toward Higashi Chaya District)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Sunny Bell Coffee is the flagship roastery of Masashi Takamatsu, an award-winning latte artist who has placed in multiple latte-art competitions. He opened the shop in 2013 and moved it to its current Kasaichi-machi premises in 2017, building a five-seat space where vintage roast

chef_bio.background: Sunny Bell Coffee is the flagship roastery of Masashi Takamatsu, an award-winning latte artist who has placed in multiple latte-art competitions. He opened the shop in 2013 and moved it to its current Kasaichi-machi premises in 2017, building a five-seat space where vintage roasting equipment is on full view to customers.
chef_bio.philosophy: Takamatsu emphasizes the individual characteristics of each bean, monitoring color and aroma closely to preserve fragrance and aims to deliver a special cup that is hard to reproduce at home.
chef_bio.specialty: House-roasted single-origin specialty coffee (typically 5-7 varieties on offer) brewed by meticulous hand pour, alongside signature sweets such as canelé and tiramisu. He roasts on a Fuji Royal R-105 semi-hot-air 5kg roaster, favoring light-to-medium roasts that highlight each bean's individual character.

anecdotes.text: The roasting aroma is said to spread across the whole surrounding area, drawing people to the tiny five-seat stand.
Cafes across Japan source Takamatsu's beans, and he opened a second shop, Owari-machi Kissa, in April 2024 between Higashi Chaya District and Omicho Market.

positives.text: Whole-bean purchases and plain black drip coffee are naturally free of gluten, dairy, and animal products.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny five-seat, cash-only roastery stand where a few words of Japanese and patience go a long way, though pointing at the bean lineup and ordering a drip coffee is easy enough for a foreign student.

places.name: 蕎麦処 大藪 (Sobadokoro Ooyabu)
places.gf_detail: This is a buckwheat soba shop, but the signature noodle is described as "九割そば" (nine-tenths buckwheat), meaning roughly 10% wheat flour binder is used — so the soba itself is NOT gluten-free. The dashi-based broths (soba tsuyu, oyakodon sauce) are made with kombu and bonito but typically also contain wheat-based soy sauce/mirin. There is no documented gluten-free menu, no dedicated preparation, and no allergy protocol on the official site or any fetched source. Not safe for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: A traditional soba and izakaya-style shop. Broths are built on bonito (katsuo) and kombu dashi, and signature dishes feature charcoal-grilled chicken and eggs (oyakodon, tenmori with fried shrimp). No documented vegan menu or plant-based options. Not suitable for vegans; a vegan would need to ask, and even plain soba is served with fish-based dashi tsuyu.
places.hours_raw: Weekdays 11:00-14:00 / 18:00-21:30 (L.O. 21:00). Sat, Sun & holidays 11:00-14:30 / 17:30-21:30 (L.O. 21:00). May close early if soba sells out. Closed Mon (including holidays) and the 4th Sun.
places.neighborhood: Futatsuchi-machi (near Wakamiya Ohashi bridge / toward Kanazawa Stn east exit / Eki-nishi)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Founded in 1996, this neighborhood soba shop sits near Wakamiya Bridge in the Futakuchi-machi district on the station-west side of Kanazawa. It is now run by the second-generation proprietor, who carries forward the traditions inherited from the founder while applying, in the sho

chef_bio.background: Founded in 1996, this neighborhood soba shop sits near Wakamiya Bridge in the Futakuchi-machi district on the station-west side of Kanazawa. It is now run by the second-generation proprietor, who carries forward the traditions inherited from the founder while applying, in the shop's own words, 'old wisdom with new thinking.'
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop describes itself as 'a place to enjoy soba and sake together' (蕎麦と日本酒を楽しむ場), emphasizing painstaking effort and quality ingredients.
chef_bio.specialty: The house specialty is coarsely-ground nine-tenths buckwheat soba (粗挽き九割そば) with a rich, rustic flavor and a distinctive texture, served with a dashi built primarily on bonito and kombu. The most famous dishes are the tenmori (soba with lightly fried shrimp and seasonal vegetables) and a premium oyakodon made with charcoal-grilled chicken and rich 'Ranoh' (蘭王) eggs from Oita Prefecture over a house dashi-based sauce.

anecdotes.text: The proprietor is known for an exceptional sake program — the shop stocks well over 50 (and by some accounts more than 100) varieties of Japanese sake from across the country, including rare and seasonal bottles, with appetizers designed specifically as sake pairings.

Locals praise the coarse-ground, wind-flavored buckwheat noodles and the meticulous boiling technique that highlights the texture unique to roughly milled soba, alongside an unusually deep sake list for a soba shop.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The signature soba is '九割そば' (nine-tenths buckwheat), meaning it contains roughly 10% wheat flour as a binder — the noodles are not gluten-free, and a shared soba-boiling kitchen makes cross-contamination with wheat likely.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes such as the oyakodon and soba tsuyu use a dashi/kaeshi base; traditional kaeshi is made with soy sauce and mirin, which normally contain wheat. No wheat-free seasoning is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths are based on bonito (katsuo) and kombu fish/seaweed dashi, and signature dishes feature chicken, egg, and shrimp, so plant-based diners cannot avoid animal-derived stock.

cultural_comfort.note: A welcoming local soba shop with tatami, table, and counter seating that suits solo diners and families, but there is no sign of English support, so a few basic Japanese phrases (or a translation app) will help you order comfortably.

places.name: 珈琲館 長田亭 (Coffee-kan Nagatatei)
places.gf_detail: This is a classic Showa-era Japanese coffeehouse (junkissa) whose menu centers on toast, sandwiches and lunch sets built on bread, plus coffee. The core offerings are wheat-based and there is no indication of gluten-free preparation or any allergen accommodation. Not suitable for celiacs or strict gluten avoidance.
places.vegan_detail: A traditional kissaten focused on coffee, toast and lunch sets. Black coffee is plant-based, but most food items (toast with butter, sandwiches, lunch sets) typically involve dairy, egg or meat, and no vegan menu is documented. A vegan visitor would need to ask and would likely be limited to a black coffee or similar drink.
places.hours_raw: 9:00-13:30, 15:30-20:00 (closed Sun and holidays)
places.neighborhood: Honmachi (~5 min walk from Kanazawa Stn east exit) / Hokutetsu Kanazawa Stn
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A long-running Showa-retro junkissa (pure coffeehouse) tucked into a residential pocket of Honcho, about a five-minute walk from Kanazawa Station behind the hotel district. It has been in operation for over 30 years; the Honcho shop is actually the second location, the original h

chef_bio.background: A long-running Showa-retro junkissa (pure coffeehouse) tucked into a residential pocket of Honcho, about a five-minute walk from Kanazawa Station behind the hotel district. It has been in operation for over 30 years; the Honcho shop is actually the second location, the original having stood near what is now Musashi's studio street. The building predates the cafe itself and was reinforced with sturdy pillars to keep it standing.
chef_bio.philosophy: Preserves an increasingly rare junkissa culture: an unhurried, understated space for savoring a cup of coffee, the kind of place that would be nearly impossible to recreate today.
chef_bio.specialty: A clean, refreshing blend coffee well suited to a quiet morning, alongside classic kissaten fare like toast and lunch sets, plus nostalgic touches such as a sprinkle-topped coffee float.

anecdotes.text: The ceiling holds decorative lead lamps and vintage lampshades once used in cabarets; they normally stay dark, but the welcoming mama-san will sometimes light them up for guests who show genuine interest.
Behind a striking black awning and tiled roof that can look almost forbidding from the street, the interior opens into a calm, wood-scented retreat with counter seating and a hidden sofa nook.

cultural_comfort.note: A quiet, old-school neighborhood coffeehouse run by a welcoming "mama-san"; there is no English menu and little tourist traffic, so a few words of Japanese and a relaxed attitude go a long way.

places.name: カフェフレール（ギャラリー三田内） (Cafe Frere (inside Gallery Mita))
places.gf_detail: A Showa-era kissaten serving cakes, curry and hayashi rice — all wheat-based — with no documented gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or allergy accommodation. Not suitable for anyone with celiac disease. Coffee and plain hot drinks are the only items unlikely to contain gluten, but cross-contamination cannot be ruled out and staff allergy handling is undocumented.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan offering is documented. The menu centers on coffee/tea, cakes (fruit cake, granola cheesecake — contain dairy/egg) and light meals like curry and hayashi rice. A vegan could likely have black coffee or tea, but any solid food would need to be confirmed with the owner; nothing on the documented menu is verified plant-based.
places.hours_raw: 10:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:30). Lunchtime discount 11:30-14:00. Irregular closures a few times a year. Note: the adjoining Gallery Mita's exhibitions themselves are open 10:00-18:00.
places.neighborhood: Owaricho (near Kanazawa Phonograph Museum / Izumi Kyoka Memorial Museum, inside the nationally registered tangible cultural property "former Mita Shoten" brick building)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The café sits inside Gallery Mita, the former Mita drapery shop (旧三田商店) — a reinforced-concrete building raised in 1930 (Showa 5) with scratch-tile walls and Art Deco detailing, designated a National Registered Tangible Cultural Property in 1997 and counted among the finest survi

chef_bio.chef_name: 髙 隆秀（タカ タカヒデ）
chef_bio.background: The café sits inside Gallery Mita, the former Mita drapery shop (旧三田商店) — a reinforced-concrete building raised in 1930 (Showa 5) with scratch-tile walls and Art Deco detailing, designated a National Registered Tangible Cultural Property in 1997 and counted among the finest surviving modern buildings in Kanazawa. A tearoom first opened in the space around 1967 as 'Kissaten Luault,' closed for a time, and was revived in 2005 as Café Frère under the current owner, who carefully preserved the original interior.
chef_bio.philosophy: Preserve and share a piece of Showa romance — letting guests linger over a quiet cup beneath original early-Showa stained glass while honoring the heritage of the cultural-property building.
chef_bio.specialty: Blended coffee (a light, refreshing cup with gentle acidity and mild bitterness) alongside homemade sweets such as fruit cake and granola cheesecake, plus light meals like curry and hayashi rice; beer and whiskey are also served.

anecdotes.text: The retro stained glass dates to the building's 1930 construction: the former Mita drapery was a regional agent for Kansai Stained Glass Manufacturing, so original Art Deco geometric and plant-motif panels — made with natural stone powder — were preserved and reused when the tearoom opened. On sunny days the outside light pours beautifully through them.

The owner is known as conversational and knowledgeable; first-time visitors are encouraged to take the counter for an unhurried experience, and the room is steadily filled with local office workers and neighbors into the evening.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed neighborhood kissaten where a friendly owner chats with regulars; you'll be warmly received with a few words of Japanese and a smile, though English menus are not guaranteed.

places.name: 四季のテーブル (Shiki no Table)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature jibu-ni dredges duck/chicken in wheat flour and simmers it in a soy-sauce-based dashi, and the kaiseki courses rely on wheat-containing soy sauce; no gluten-free accommodation is documented. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. The cuisine centers on duck/chicken jibu-ni, Sea of Japan fish, and bonito/seafood dashi; no vegan menu is documented. Vegetable-forward Kaga dishes exist but are not animal-free.
places.hours_raw: Cafe 9:30-11:00, lunch 11:00-15:00, afternoon cafe 15:00-17:00 (Mon/Tue/Thu/Fri), dinner from 17:00 (kitchen close around 20:30 L.O., to ~21:30). Hours vary with reservation status. Closed Wednesdays.
places.neighborhood: Nagamachi (Nagamachi Samurai District / near Nishi Chaya, wa
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Etsuko Aoki opened the Nagamachi Cooking Salon (later Aoki Cooking School) in 1957 and spent more than 50 years researching and reviving Kanazawa's traditional home and regional cooking. In December 1992 she opened Shiki no Table on the ground floor of her cooking school in the N

chef_bio.background: Etsuko Aoki opened the Nagamachi Cooking Salon (later Aoki Cooking School) in 1957 and spent more than 50 years researching and reviving Kanazawa's traditional home and regional cooking. In December 1992 she opened Shiki no Table on the ground floor of her cooking school in the Nagamachi samurai district so visitors could taste the dishes she had documented for decades.
chef_bio.philosophy: Her cooking emphasizes 'food and health,' using Hakusan water, local seasonal ingredients and hand preparation to keep authentic Kanazawa home cooking alive.
chef_bio.specialty: Kaga regional cuisine: the city's signature jibu-ni (duck or chicken dusted in wheat flour and simmered with sudare-fu, lily bulb and shiitake in a soy-mirin dashi), winter kabura-zushi (cured yellowtail and turnip), and dishes built around Kaga vegetables and Sea of Japan fish.

anecdotes.text: Aoki published a book of Kanazawa regional cuisine in 1982 and that year received the 3rd Kanazawa City Cultural Activity Award.
She served as culinary supervisor for the 2013 film 'A Tale of Samurai Cooking' (武士の献立), starring Aya Ueto, which is set among Kaga-domain kitchen samurai.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Signature jibu-ni coats the meat in wheat flour, so wheat is integral to the kitchen's core dish.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Regional kaiseki dishes are built on soy-sauce and mirin based dashi, which in standard Japanese cooking contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes rely on duck/chicken and Sea of Japan fish with seafood/bonito dashi; no vegan preparation is documented.

positives.text: Run by a veteran cuisine researcher and cooking-school principal, so the kitchen is knowledgeable about ingredients; reservations are recommended and allow diners to discuss needs in advance.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional, refined regional-cuisine restaurant where some Japanese helps; staff are welcoming but English support is not guaranteed, so calling ahead or asking your guide to reserve is wise.

places.name: 金沢おでん 地酒地魚 新近江町商店 (Kanazawa Oden Jizake Jizakana Shin-Omicho Sh)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe and no gluten-free menu is documented. Kanazawa oden is built on a fish/dashi broth simmered with wheat-containing fish cakes (練り物) and seasoned with regular (wheat-based) soy sauce, and the kitchen also serves sushi, tempura-style and grilled rice-bowl dishes, so cross-contact is unavoidable. Celiac travelers should avoid; others with mild sensitivity should ask staff directly.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian offering. The menu is centered on fresh seafood (sashimi, nodoguro and seafood rice bowls, sushi), Noto beef, and Kanazawa oden made with fish-based dashi and seafood/meat ingredients. No plant-based dish is documented; vegans cannot eat here in a meaningful way.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Fri, Sat, Sun, holidays: 11:00-15:00 (L.O. 14:30) and 17:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:30). Closed Wednesday and Thursday. Irregular closures may also occur. (Note: the Omicho market page lists different daytime-focused hours; Tabelog and HotPepper agree on the 11:00-15:00 / 17:00-21:00 schedule used here.)
places.neighborhood: Omicho Market (Omicho Ichiba-kan 2F / Musashigatsuji)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A compact market eatery on the second floor of the Omicho Ichiba building, right inside Kanazawa's famous Omicho fresh-food market near Musashigatsuji. The shop brings the market's catch straight upstairs, pairing it with the city's beloved oden in one casual spot built for both

chef_bio.background: A compact market eatery on the second floor of the Omicho Ichiba building, right inside Kanazawa's famous Omicho fresh-food market near Musashigatsuji. The shop brings the market's catch straight upstairs, pairing it with the city's beloved oden in one casual spot built for both locals on a lunch break and visitors exploring the market.
chef_bio.philosophy: Freshness and local sourcing first: fish bought direct from the surrounding market, Noto-grown rice, and regional sake and ingredients combined so visitors can taste several Kanazawa specialties in one small space.
chef_bio.specialty: Kanazawa oden served alongside market-fresh seafood: sashimi and seafood rice bowls (including a nodoguro/throat-perch don), sushi, and Noto beef dishes such as a grilled Noto-beef rice bowl, with rice from Noto Koshihikari and local sake to match.

anecdotes.text: Every seat has a touchscreen ordering system that displays prices, so solo diners and tourists can order easily and know the cost up front.
The shop opened inside the Omicho Ichiba building as a place combining fresh seafood and oden, noted by a local Kanazawa info site when it launched.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Kanazawa oden is simmered as a shared pot with wheat-based fish cakes and other items, so broth and ingredients are not gluten-free and share cooking surfaces with other dishes.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Regional dishes (sushi, sashimi, oden, rice bowls) are served with standard Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat; no gluten-free tamari is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is built around seafood, fish dashi, and Noto beef; oden broth is fish-based, so even vegetable items would share fish/meat broth and surfaces.

positives.text: Touchscreen ordering at every seat shows item names and prices, helping diners review what they are ordering, and English menus are available.

cultural_comfort.note: Very easy for a foreign student: it sits inside Omicho market, uses touchscreen ordering at every seat with prices shown, and English menus are available, so you can order seafood bowls and oden without speaking Japanese.

places.name: 鮨 えのめ (Sushi Enome)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional sushi and seafood-bowl counter where soy sauce (which normally contains wheat) is used throughout, and sushi rice is seasoned with vinegar that may also contain wheat-derived ingredients. There is no documented gluten-free menu, no dedicated preparation area, and no indication of celiac-safe practices. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: The entire concept is fresh fish and shellfish delivered daily from the family's fishing operation on Noto Island — seafood rice bowls, sushi, sea urchin, salmon roe, crab and nodoguro. There are no documented vegan dishes; this is not a destination for plant-based diners.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–15:00 (closed Tuesdays). Note: some listings show 10:00–16:00 with irregular days off.
places.neighborhood: Ōmichō Market (Musashigatsuji / Hokutetsu Kanazawa Stn)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 鮨 えのめ is a small family-run sushi counter tucked inside Kanazawa's lively Omicho Market. Its defining feature is a direct line to the sea: the owner's family are fishermen (網元, amimoto — heads of a fishing operation) at Enome harbor on Noto Island, and the catch is delivered fres

chef_bio.origin: Noto Island (能登島), Ishikawa — Enome harbor
chef_bio.background: 鮨 えのめ is a small family-run sushi counter tucked inside Kanazawa's lively Omicho Market. Its defining feature is a direct line to the sea: the owner's family are fishermen (網元, amimoto — heads of a fishing operation) at Enome harbor on Noto Island, and the catch is delivered fresh each morning straight from that family operation, supplemented by direct buying at Kanazawa Port.
chef_bio.philosophy: Direct sourcing from the family's own Enome fishing harbor and Kanazawa Port, served casually so visitors can enjoy top-grade local seafood amid the open, energetic atmosphere of the market.
chef_bio.specialty: Its signature is a seafood rice bowl (海鮮丼) heaped with eleven kinds of seasonal fish and shellfish, alongside sushi featuring snow crab, sea urchin (uni), salmon roe (ikura) and grilled nodoguro (blackthroat seabream). The rice is grown in the owner's home region of Noto.

anecdotes.text: The fish is brought in each morning directly from the owner's family, who work as fishermen (網元) at Enome harbor on Noto Island, and even the sushi rice comes from the owner's Noto hometown.
The signature seafood bowl piles eleven kinds of seasonal fish and shellfish into a single dish, sourced directly from Enome harbor.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a traditional sushi and seafood-bowl counter, soy sauce is used throughout for sushi and donburi; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat. No gluten-free soy sauce or wheat-free preparation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is entirely seafood-focused, with all dishes built around fish, shellfish, uni and roe, so cross-contact with animal products is unavoidable.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, casual 10-seat counter inside the bustling Omicho Market where pointing at the seafood-bowl photos and a friendly konnichiwa will get you far; bring cash, as cards are not accepted.

places.name: 閼伽 (Aka (Kissashitsu Aka))
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free in any way. The menu centers on wheat-heavy dishes — baked curry (roux), sandwiches, fried udon, spaghetti — and there is no documented gluten-free preparation, dedicated equipment, or allergen handling. Not safe for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan offerings documented. The food is classic Japanese-Western café fare (curry with meat, egg sandwiches, jako/dried-fish fried rice, café au lait) and coffee is the staple. No plant-based menu or substitutions are noted.
places.hours_raw: 08:00-16:30 (closes around 16:30); closed Sundays and public holidays
places.neighborhood: Omicho Market (Emza entrance) / Shimotsutsumi-machi, Musashi
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Aka is a retro kissaten hidden up a small staircase between a butcher and a rice-ball shop near the Emza entrance of Omicho Market. The owner, Kaoru Fukue, opened it here roughly four decades ago — and has now run it for some 41 years — despite having no prior ties to the market;

chef_bio.background: Aka is a retro kissaten hidden up a small staircase between a butcher and a rice-ball shop near the Emza entrance of Omicho Market. The owner, Kaoru Fukue, opened it here roughly four decades ago — and has now run it for some 41 years — despite having no prior ties to the market; he was simply drawn to the room's unusual trapezoidal shape. Though it sits in one of Kanazawa's busiest tourist areas, most of the regulars are people who work in the market.
chef_bio.philosophy: The menu grew organically out of listening to customers. As Fukue puts it, the shop originally served little more than fried udon and sandwiches, but expanded over time in response to regulars saying what they wanted to eat.
chef_bio.specialty: Drip coffee is the heart of the place, alongside its much-loved homemade baked curry (yaki-curry). Over the years the kitchen has added light meals like jako (dried-fish) fried rice, sandwiches and spaghetti.

anecdotes.text: Fukue says he opened in Omicho with no connection to the area, won over simply by the room's distinctive trapezoidal shape about 40 years ago.
On how the menu grew: "Originally we mostly did fried udon and sandwiches. But as we kept answering 'I want to eat that,' it just kept growing."

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, smoke-friendly old-school café full of market regulars and run by its longtime owner — wonderful atmosphere but no English and no allergy accommodation, so a foreign student is best off going with a guide or a Japanese-speaking friend.

places.name: ラーメン みなと屋 (Ramen Minatoya)
places.gf_detail: A wheat-noodle ramen shop whose broth is built on chicken, fish, and kombu with shoyu/miso seasonings, so wheat-based noodles and soy sauce are central. There is no documented gluten-free menu, no dedicated fryer, and no stated allergen handling. Not suitable for celiacs or anyone needing strict gluten avoidance.
places.vegan_detail: The menu lists vegetable ramen (野菜ラーメン) variations, but the house broth is made from chicken and fish (plus kombu and vegetables), so the standard soups are not vegan and the vegetable ramen is not confirmed to use a plant-only stock. No vegan-specific preparation is documented; a strict vegan would need to ask, and the kitchen is fundamentally a meat/fish dashi operation.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–19:00 (official / Hot Pepper). Some listings such as Gurunavi show 11:00–16:00; last order may be earlier or hours shortened. Irregular days off.
places.neighborhood: Musashigatsuji / Ōmichō Market (Ōmichō Ichiba-kan B1)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small ramen counter tucked into the basement of the Omicho Ichiba-kan in the heart of Kanazawa's Omicho Market, the city's centuries-old fresh-food bazaar. It serves old-school chuka soba (Chinese-style noodle soup) to market shoppers and visitors.

chef_bio.background: A small ramen counter tucked into the basement of the Omicho Ichiba-kan in the heart of Kanazawa's Omicho Market, the city's centuries-old fresh-food bazaar. It serves old-school chuka soba (Chinese-style noodle soup) to market shoppers and visitors.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen builds a clear soup from natural ingredients — chicken, fish, kombu, and vegetables simmered gently just below a boil — and describes its approach as using ingredients with controlled additives so guests can taste the natural umami.
chef_bio.specialty: Clear, broth-driven chuka soba in shoyu, shio, and miso, alongside local-flavored bowls such as Kaga sake-lees ramen (加賀酒粕ラーメン吟醸) and a jibuni-style ramen (治部煮ラーメン) that nods to Kanazawa's signature simmered dish.

anecdotes.text: The shop frames its bowls around a clear, natural-ingredient broth of chicken, fish, kombu, and vegetables simmered at a careful low temperature.
Local-color menu items include Kaga sake-lees ramen and a jibuni ramen referencing Kanazawa's famous jibuni stew.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles and shoyu/miso seasonings are central to every documented bowl; broth uses chicken, fish, and kombu. No gluten-free option is mentioned.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Shoyu and miso chuka soba are core menu items, indicating wheat-containing soy sauce/miso in the seasoning base.

vegan_cross_contact.text: House broth is made from chicken and fish; even vegetable ramen variations are not confirmed to use a plant-only stock.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny 12-seat counter ramen stand inside the Omicho Market food hall where a smile, a point at the menu, and a few words of Japanese will get you served comfortably; bring cash as cards are not accepted.

noodles.notes: Wheat-and-kansui noodles kept simple and restrained so as not to overpower the delicate, gently sweet broth. One cold variant was noted as medium-thickness wheat noodles with good firmness and bite.

ramen.signature_bowl: 中華そば (chūka soba) — the classic clear shoyu bowl; the menma-forward メンマラーメン showcases the house bamboo shoots, and the Kaga sake-kasu ramen (加賀酒粕ラーメン) is the local specialty worth seeking.
ramen.chashu: Lean, lightly braised pork chashu — a "healthy," restrained cut chosen to suit the gentle soup rather than a fatty, rich preparation.
notable_toppings: house-made menma (bamboo shoots reconstituted over three days, hand-kneaded and slow-seasoned — crisp yet tender)

gf.note: Wheat-and-kansui noodles and a soy-based (wheat) shoyu tare; no rice- or konjac-noodle or gluten-free tare option is documented.

vegan.note: The broth is built on chicken and fish (plus kombu and vegetables), and bowls carry pork chashu and egg; no documented plant-only (no katsuo/no animal) bowl.

ramen.pantheon: A quiet, old-school chūka soba in the heart of Kanazawa's market: chicken, fish, kombu and vegetables coaxed just below a boil into a crystal-clear, naturally sweet broth that tastes of restraint rather than power. It leans toward the gentle, gobō-clear lineage of Hokuriku market ramen, with a local twist in its Kaga sake-kasu specialty.

places.highlight: A quiet, old-school chūka soba in the heart of Kanazawa's market: chicken, fish, kombu and vegetables coaxed just below a boil into a crystal-clear, naturally sweet broth that tastes of restraint rather than power. It leans toward the gentle, gobō-clear lineage of Hokuriku market ramen, with a local twist in its Kaga sake-kasu specialty.

places.name: 近江町どてや (Oumicho Doteya)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. The signature 金沢どて焼き is simmered in Kaga miso (wheat-fermented) and Ono soy sauce (wheat-based), and the menu centers on skewers, fried items (ふぐのからあげ, 塩からあげ — wheat-flour batter) and market dishes. Beer is also served. No gluten-free accommodation is documented and the kitchen is wheat-heavy.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. The menu is built around grilled skewers, doteni (simmered offal/motsu), fried pufferfish and chicken, and market seafood. No vegan options are documented.
places.hours_raw: 9:00–19:00 (irregular days off). Note: a separate listing shows 8:30–20:00 daily; hours are best confirmed before visiting.
places.neighborhood: Ōmichō Market (Musashigatsuji)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 近江町どてや is a small standing-bar (立ち飲み) tucked into a passageway of Kanazawa's Omicho Market, the city's centuries-old central food market. It opened in March 2025 as an easy place to stop in mid-shopping for a skewer and a drink, where local shoppers and visitors mingle through th

chef_bio.background: 近江町どてや is a small standing-bar (立ち飲み) tucked into a passageway of Kanazawa's Omicho Market, the city's centuries-old central food market. It opened in March 2025 as an easy place to stop in mid-shopping for a skewer and a drink, where local shoppers and visitors mingle through the morning, midday and evening bustle of the market.
chef_bio.philosophy: Carrying on the traditional flavors of Ishikawa: the doteyaki is prepared and simmered in-house every day by hand, leaning on ingredients sourced right from the surrounding market so each bite tastes of Kanazawa.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature dish is 金沢どて焼き (Kanazawa doteyaki) — skewers slowly simmered each day in 108-year-old Kaga miso and the mild, sweet Ono soy sauce of the region (2 skewers for 500 yen). The menu also features ふぐのからあげ (fried pufferfish) and のと塩からあげ (Noto salt fried chicken), paired with local sake, draft beer and highballs.

anecdotes.text: The doteyaki is made with a 108-year-old Kaga miso and mellow, sweet Ono soy sauce, slowly simmered by hand each day to bring out a deep, aromatic flavor that the shop describes as distinctly Kanazawa.
Sitting right by the market passage, it is the kind of counter you drift into at the end of a market stroll for a light drink of local sake while shoppers and tourists cross paths around you.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen fries pufferfish and chicken (wheat-flour batter) and grills skewers in a tiny shared counter space, so cross-contact with wheat is effectively unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature doteyaki is simmered in Kaga miso and Ono soy sauce, both of which are wheat-fermented and not gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes center on meat skewers, doteni (simmered offal) and seafood; even non-meat items share the same simmering and frying station, so no dish can be considered free of animal products.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny standing counter inside the bustling market where a few words of Japanese and a point at the skewers go a long way; it is cash-only and casual, so it is welcoming if you are comfortable ordering simply and standing among locals.

places.name: 海鮮つるや (Kaisen Tsuruya)
places.gf_detail: This is a seafood-bowl and Kanazawa-oden restaurant where wheat-based soy sauce, dashi, and other seasonings are central; nothing in the sources indicates gluten-free accommodation or awareness, so it is not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: The restaurant is built around raw fish, seafood bowls, and dashi-based dishes. The official Omicho Market listing notes that vegetarian-friendly options are available, but there is no documented dedicated vegan menu and fish/dashi are pervasive, so vegans should ask carefully and expect very limited choices.
places.hours_raw: Sun & weekdays 9:00-17:00; Sat & day before holidays 9:00-20:00 (editorial source). Note: the official Omicho Market listing states 7:00-17:00, with Sat/day-before-holiday until 20:00. Open hour varies between sources.
places.neighborhood: Omicho Market (Musashigatsuji)
places.notes: 海鮮つるや is a seafood-bowl restaurant inside Kanazawa's Omicho Market, the centuries-old food market locals call 'Kanazawa's kitchen.' A relative newcomer, it opened around 2022 and quickly became a popular stop for fresh kaisendon. It is sized deliberately large for the market so g

chef_bio.background: 海鮮つるや is a seafood-bowl restaurant inside Kanazawa's Omicho Market, the centuries-old food market locals call 'Kanazawa's kitchen.' A relative newcomer, it opened around 2022 and quickly became a popular stop for fresh kaisendon. It is sized deliberately large for the market so guests can be seated quickly: '極力待たせないように、と80席のキャパシティで客を迎えている' (the place welcomes guests with 80 seats so as to keep waiting to a minimum).
chef_bio.philosophy: A commitment to Kanazawa's local flavors paired with quick, comfortable seating that suits everyone from solo diners at the counter to larger groups.
chef_bio.specialty: Seafood rice bowls are the draw, from the lavish 特上海鮮丼 piled with nodoguro (blackthroat seaperch), tuna, white shrimp, salmon roe, octopus, sea bream, sweet shrimp, squid, salmon, crab claw and steamed shrimp, to the value おまかせ市場丼. Signature single-topping bowls include the のど黒丼 (nodoguro bowl), いくら丼 (salmon roe bowl) and かんぱち丼 (amberjack bowl). Beyond bowls, the kitchen leans into local Kanazawa flavors with 金沢おでん simmered in a well-developed bonito broth, regional sake from local breweries, and northern delicacies such as Noto sea mozuku and Toyama firefly squid in oil pickle.

anecdotes.text: Find it by heading straight in from the Emza-side entrance of Omicho Market and turning right at Tadamura Suisan; it has been popular with many diners since opening about two years before a May 2024 feature.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a kaisendon and Kanazawa-oden restaurant, dishes rely on standard (wheat-based) soy sauce and bonito dashi; no gluten-free soy sauce or accommodation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu centers on raw fish and seafood prepared in a shared kitchen; vegans should expect heavy cross-contact with fish and dashi.

positives.text: The official Omicho Market listing notes that vegetarian-friendly options are available and the restaurant is child-friendly, with counter seats welcoming solo diners.

cultural_comfort.note: A busy, tourist-friendly seafood-bowl spot inside Omicho Market where pointing at the picture menu and a few words of Japanese will get you by, but bring cash since cards are not accepted.

places.name: あまつぼ じもの亭 (Amatsubo Jimonotei)
places.gf_detail: This is a seafood-rice-bowl and Kanazawa-oden eatery built around soy sauce, dashi, and shared simmering broth. Sashimi bowls are served with wheat-based soy sauce, and oden uses a kombu/bonito broth seasoned with sake and salt but served alongside wheat-containing items and dipping sauces. There is no indication of gluten-free preparation or precautions, so it is not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: The menu centers on fresh fish (sweet shrimp, yellowtail, sea bream, crab, salmon roe, white shrimp, firefly squid) and oden simmered in a bonito-based dashi. No vegan or vegetarian dishes are documented; this is a seafood and local-cuisine destination, not suitable for vegans.
places.hours_raw: 8:00-16:00 (last order 15:30), closed Wednesdays. Note: some listings cite an earlier 7:00 open and sake is advertised from 8:00.
places.neighborhood: Ōmichō Market (Kami-Ōmichō / Hokutetsu Kanazawa Stn)
places.notes: When the long-loved Jimonotei seafood-bowl shop inside Omicho Market closed at the end of 2024 after years in business, it didn't simply disappear. Amatsubo, a respected Kanazawa oden house and a member of the city's Kanazawa Oden 50-Year Veterans Association, stepped in to take

roles: market food stop with local sake
chef_bio.origin: Kanazawa, inside Omicho Market (上近江町)
chef_bio.background: When the long-loved Jimonotei seafood-bowl shop inside Omicho Market closed at the end of 2024 after years in business, it didn't simply disappear.
