Amatsubo, a respected Kanazawa oden house and a member of the city's Kanazawa Oden 50-Year Veterans Association, stepped in to take over the spot and reopened it in early February 2025 under the name Amatsubo Jimonotei, blending the old market institution with their own oden heritage.
chef_bio.philosophy: A market food stop where you can sit from early morning, order oden and a one-plate dish, and enjoy them with a glass of celebrated local sake while the market bustles around you.
chef_bio.specialty: The headline dish is the Kanazawa-don (around 2,980 yen), a seafood bowl piled with eleven kinds of fish bought that morning in the market just outside the door, including sweet shrimp, baigai whelk, yellowtail, sea bream, crab, and salmon roe. Alongside it the shop serves Kanazawa oden, with a set of fifteen carefully chosen ingredients simmered in a kombu-and-bonito broth seasoned only with sake and salt, plus market small plates like fried white shrimp and marinated firefly squid.

anecdotes.text: The oden broth follows a traditional recipe built on kombu and bonito and seasoned with nothing but sake and salt, a nod to Amatsubo's place in Kanazawa's Oden 50-Year Veterans Association.
Because it opens early, you can pair Kanazawa oden and small plates with morning sake from celebrated breweries such as the Noguchi Naohiko Sake Institute, Kuruma Brewery, and Kazuma Brewery.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Sashimi seafood bowls are served with standard Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All featured dishes are seafood-based and oden shares a bonito-dashi simmering broth, so there is no separation from animal ingredients.

cultural_comfort.note: A friendly, casual market eatery where pointing at the photo menu and a smile will get you a great seafood bowl, though staff mainly speak Japanese so a few basic phrases help.

places.name: 金沢茶屋 割烹つづみ (Kanazawa Chaya Kappo Tsuzumi)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The cuisine centers on soy-sauce-based Kaga dishes, and the signature jibuni is explicitly made by dusting duck with wheat flour; dashi, soy sauce, and wheat are pervasive. No gluten-free menu or allergy protocol is documented, so this is not safe for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The menu is built on seafood (sashimi, fugu, swordfish), eel, wagyu, duck (jibuni), and fish/meat dashi. No vegan or vegetarian course is advertised; any plant dishes would still use bonito/kombu-meat-based stocks.
places.hours_raw: 07:00–09:00 / 11:30–14:30 (L.O. 14:00) / 17:00–22:00 (L.O. 21:30). Open daily (may close for facility reasons).
places.neighborhood: Honmachi (~5 min walk from Kanazawa Stn East Exit)
places.notes: Kappo Tsuzumi is the dining heart of Kanazawa Chaya, a culinary ryokan in the Kagaya hospitality group, set a short walk from Kanazawa Station. It serves Kaga cuisine — the refined castle-town cooking of the old Kaga domain — in a space that blends traditional Kanazawa wa aesthet

roles: dining restaurant within a culinary ryokan
chef_bio.origin: Kanazawa (Honmachi, near the station east exit)
chef_bio.background: Kappo Tsuzumi is the dining heart of Kanazawa Chaya, a culinary ryokan in the Kagaya hospitality group, set a short walk from Kanazawa Station. It serves Kaga cuisine — the refined castle-town cooking of the old Kaga domain — in a space that blends traditional Kanazawa wa aesthetics with quiet comfort.
chef_bio.philosophy: The house follows Kagaya-style hospitality summed up in five principles — flavor, sincerity of welcome, comfort, traditional craftsmanship, and approachability — aiming to pair a refined setting with seasonal gastronomy.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature is jibuni, Kanazawa's hallmark dish: duck (and seasonal ingredients) lightly dusted in wheat flour and simmered, which gives a soft, silky texture and a gentle, rounded flavor. Seasonal courses also feature high-quality domestic eel grilled Kaga-style, natural fugu in winter and spring, sashimi, and wagyu, all built around carefully selected seasonal Kanazawa fish and vegetables.

anecdotes.text: The jibuni here is described as made the traditional way using only wheat flour to coat the duck, producing the dish's characteristically soft texture and mild, gentle taste.
The restaurant belongs to the Kagaya group and presents itself as offering Kagaya-style 'taste and hospitality,' carrying the welcome of its hot-spring heritage into a station-side Kanazawa setting.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Signature jibuni is coated in wheat flour and the cuisine is soy-sauce and dashi based; wheat and gluten are pervasive across the menu.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Kaga cuisine here relies on standard Japanese soy sauce (wheat-containing) and soy-based seasonings; no wheat-free tamari is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes center on seafood, eel, fugu, duck, and wagyu with fish/meat-based dashi, so plant items would share animal-based stocks and prep.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a refined, ryokan-style kappo with formal multi-course Kaga cuisine and dinner courses around 10,000+ yen, so a foreign student would likely feel most comfortable visiting with a guide or for a special lunch rather than dropping in casually.

places.name: 近江町市場 杉本水産 近江町店 (Ohmicho Ichiba Sugimoto Suisan Ohmicho-ten)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature unagi and dojou kabayaki are grilled and repeatedly basted with a tare (glaze) made from local Oono soy sauce and malt syrup — soy sauce here is wheat-based, so the core product contains gluten. No gluten-free preparation or accommodation is documented, and there is no dedicated fryer/equipment for allergen separation. Celiac travelers should avoid the kabayaki.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. This is a fish-shop counter whose entire offering is animal-based: charcoal-grilled freshwater eel (unagi) and loach (dojou), plus fresh-fish prepared deli items and, in winter, local snow crab (Kano-gani). There are no documented plant-based dishes.
places.hours_raw: 9:00–16:00 (until sold out); Wednesdays 9:00–13:00. Closed Sundays and public holidays. (The market website also shows 9:00–17:30.)
places.neighborhood: Ōmichō Market (Aokusa-machi / inside Ōmichō Ichiba-kan)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Sugimoto Suisan is a fresh-fish shop in Kanazawa's Ohmicho Market with more than 80 years and four generations of history. The founder's great-grandfather started in the market, and the father and grandfather worked the same stalls before the current manager, Mr. Sugimoto, took o

chef_bio.background: Sugimoto Suisan is a fresh-fish shop in Kanazawa's Ohmicho Market with more than 80 years and four generations of history. The founder's great-grandfather started in the market, and the father and grandfather worked the same stalls before the current manager, Mr. Sugimoto, took over. He once dreamed of becoming a pilot or truck driver, but was drawn into the family trade after watching his relatives' passion for market life.
chef_bio.philosophy: The work begins at the company factory around 3 a.m. each morning, where eel and loach are split, skewered, and grilled over charcoal — repeatedly basted with a house tare made from local Oono soy sauce and malt syrup, with each piece adjusted for the fish and the weather. Mr. Sugimoto says what he values most is hearing customers tell him directly that the food he made is delicious, and he is working to extend eel-eating beyond the peak summer season and to improve conditions for the early-rising market crew.
chef_bio.specialty: Charcoal-grilled freshwater eel (unagi) and loach (dojou) kabayaki are the signature. The Kanazawa-style unagi is split down the back and grilled Kansai-style; the small dojou kabayaki skewers are a local specialty sold year-round (around 150 yen each). In winter the shop also sells local Kano snow crab.

anecdotes.text: During Doyou no Ushi no Hi (the midsummer eel day), the factory turns out more than 1,500 kabayaki skewers.

The kabayaki is grilled in three traditional stages — saki (splitting), kushi-uchi (skewering), and yaki (charcoal grilling) — with the eel basted in tare made from local Oono soy sauce and malt syrup between grillings.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The kabayaki tare (basting glaze) is made with local Oono soy sauce and malt syrup, and the eel is repeatedly coated with it during grilling — standard soy sauce is wheat-based, so the signature product contains gluten.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The shop is entirely a fish and shellfish operation (eel, loach, fresh seafood, winter crab); no plant-based options are offered and all grilling equipment is used for fish.

cultural_comfort.note: A bustling fish-market stall where you point and order at the counter; very little English, so it is easiest if you come with a guide or a Japanese-speaking friend, though pointing and gestures work fine for grabbing a skewer.

places.name: 金沢近江町市場 みやむら (Kanazawa Omicho Ichiba Miyamura)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core products are kabayaki — eel and loach grilled with a soy-sauce-and-mirin-based tare that contains wheat. No gluten-free preparation, dedicated equipment, or accommodation is documented. Not celiac-safe.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. This is a specialist eel/loach and seafood shop — every signature item is animal-based (grilled eel, loach, crab, shrimp). There are no documented vegan or vegetarian options.
places.hours_raw: 9:00–17:00 (Tabelog lists 9:00–17:30). Closed Sundays and public holidays (no closures in November–December; Sundays 9:00–14:00).
places.cuisine: Kabayaki (grilled eel & loach) / specialty shop for seafood such as nodoguro (blackthroat seaperch) and crab
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Miyamura grew out of one man's devotion to a single dish. Owner Yamauchi Hiroto had loved the loach kabayaki (dojou no kabayaki) of an old Kanazawa shop in the Kanaiishi district since childhood — a shop that had grilled loach for more than fifty years before its aging owner fina

chef_bio.background: Miyamura grew out of one man's devotion to a single dish. Owner Yamauchi Hiroto had loved the loach kabayaki (dojou no kabayaki) of an old Kanazawa shop in the Kanaiishi district since childhood — a shop that had grilled loach for more than fifty years before its aging owner finally closed it. When it shut, Yamauchi tracked the retired master down and begged to taste the dish one more time. The old man wouldn't reopen, but he agreed to teach. He passed on his filleting and skewering technique, his charcoal-grilling method, his original iron skewers and grill, and the closely guarded secret tare. Yamauchi started making it as a hobby for friends, and their encouragement pushed him to open his own shop at the former location.
chef_bio.philosophy: Everything is made in-house from live fish to sauce — domestic wild loach and live eel, natural salt and purified water, real charcoal, and quality tare ingredients. Yamauchi puts taste and repeat customers above cheap pricing, and labels the origin of his fish on the packaging.
chef_bio.specialty: Charcoal-grilled freshwater eel (unagi kabayaki), white-grilled eel (shirayaki), eel rolls (unagi-maki), and the signature grilled loach (dojou no kabayaki) — fired each morning when the charcoal is hottest. The stall also sells local seafood: Kaga and Toyama Bay zuwai crab, hair crab, and local shrimp (amaebi, shiroebi, gasuebi).

anecdotes.text: When the original kabayaki master refused to reopen his shuttered shop, Yamauchi persuaded him to teach instead, inheriting the old iron skewers, grill, and secret tare that had been used for over fifty years.

A wholesale order from within Omicho Market kick-started rapid growth; within about eight years of going independent the shop was selling on the order of 250,000 skewers a year, becoming the prefecture's top seller of loach kabayaki.
Yamauchi developed his own vacuum-sealing method (patent pending) that extended shelf life from a few days to about two months, letting the kabayaki travel nationwide as a Kanazawa souvenir.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All signature items are kabayaki glazed with a soy-and-mirin tare, which contains wheat; there is no documented gluten-free preparation.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The kabayaki tare is a soy-sauce-based glaze made in-house; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The shop deals exclusively in eel, loach, crab, and shrimp grilled on shared charcoal — no vegan items and pervasive animal-product contact.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a busy local market stall where you point and order grilled eel or loach to take away; bring a guide or translation app, as little English is expected and it is not a sit-down restaurant experience.

places.name: かばやきの浅田 (Kabayaki no Asada)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The single product is loach glazed in a soy-sauce-based tare (soy sauce, sugar, mirin, sake), and Japanese soy sauce and mirin typically contain wheat. There is no gluten-free preparation, so this is not safe for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. The shop exclusively sells grilled loach (dojo, a freshwater fish), so the entire menu is animal-based with no plant-based options.
places.neighborhood: Yokoyama-cho (near the Asano River and Higashi Chaya distric
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A third-generation family business in Kanazawa's Yokoyama-cho neighborhood, near the Asano River and the Higashi Chaya geisha district. The current proprietor, Asada Hiroko, carries on a shop her parents built; she is described as one of the last keepers of a vanishing craft, as

chef_bio.background: A third-generation family business in Kanazawa's Yokoyama-cho neighborhood, near the Asano River and the Higashi Chaya geisha district. The current proprietor, Asada Hiroko, carries on a shop her parents built; she is described as one of the last keepers of a vanishing craft, as Kanazawa once had dozens of dedicated dojo kabayaki specialists and now only a handful remain.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop pointedly rejects being labeled cheap B-grade gourmet, insisting on careful ingredient selection and technique worthy of a formal restaurant. The family uses larger farmed loach (avoiding wild fish over pesticide and supply concerns) and aims for a tare that is refined rather than cloyingly sweet.
chef_bio.specialty: Dojo kabayaki — freshwater loach, butterflied open along the back, skewered one to one-and-a-half fish per stick, and grilled until the surface turns crisp while the flesh stays tender, then finished in a long-standing sweet-savory tare of premium soy sauce, sugar, mirin and sake. It is Kanazawa's classic summer stamina food, often given as a gift for recovery from illness or after childbirth.

anecdotes.text: The shop tells the dish's origin story: Christians exiled from Nagasaki's Urakami were imprisoned on Utatsuyama in Kanazawa during the Meiji era and caught loach to grill with soy sauce as nourishment — and the loach is split along the back rather than the belly to avoid evoking seppuku, considered inauspicious in this castle town.
The owner's mother, who is said to have lived to 100, believed that loach are a source of vitality.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature tare is built on premium soy sauce, sugar, mirin and sake; Japanese soy sauce and mirin typically contain wheat, so the glaze is a gluten source.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The shop sells only grilled loach (a freshwater fish); there are no plant-based items, so it is unsuitable for vegans.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, traditional family-run takeout counter where little or no English is spoken — easiest to visit with a local guide or someone who can order in Japanese.

places.name: 源左ェ門 片町店 (Genzaemon Katamachi-ten)
places.gf_detail: This is a seafood- and dashi-heavy Kaga cuisine izakaya. Signature dishes like jibuni and grilled fish rely on soy sauce and dashi (typically wheat-containing soy and bonito stock), and tempura uses wheat batter. No gluten-free menu or handling is documented, so it is not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: The kitchen is built around fresh local seafood (sashimi, nodoguro, crab) and meat (Noto beef), with dashi-based seasoning throughout. No vegan or vegetarian dishes are documented. Kaga vegetable items exist but are not prepared vegan (dashi/soy). Not a vegan destination.
places.hours_raw: 17:00-23:00 (L.O. 22:30), closed Mondays
places.neighborhood: Katamachi (near Kōrinbō / Saigawa River)
places.notes: Genzaemon Katamachi-ten is the Katamachi branch of the Genzaemon izakaya-kappo, set in the lively Katamachi entertainment district near Korinbo and the Saigawa river in central Kanazawa. The intimate space is decorated with Kaga Yuzen, the local kimono-dyeing tradition, giving a

chef_bio.background: Genzaemon Katamachi-ten is the Katamachi branch of the Genzaemon izakaya-kappo, set in the lively Katamachi entertainment district near Korinbo and the Saigawa river in central Kanazawa. The intimate space is decorated with Kaga Yuzen, the local kimono-dyeing tradition, giving a calm, refined feel that mirrors the regional cuisine.
chef_bio.specialty: Near-shore seafood and Kaga regional dishes: salt-grilled nodoguro (blackthroat seaperch), sashimi platters, duck jibuni (the classic Kanazawa simmered dish), squid kurozukuri, and seasonal specialties like rock oysters, kobako crab and zuwai crab. Around 50 kinds of local sake are stocked alongside multi-course kaiseki options.

anecdotes.text: The dining room is finished with Kaga Yuzen textile motifs so guests can feel Kanazawa's craft culture while eating the region's seafood and Kaga vegetables.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Core dishes such as jibuni and grilled fish are seasoned with soy sauce and dashi, which normally contain wheat; no gluten-free soy sauce is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional Kaga-cuisine izakaya-kappo where a friendly greeting goes a long way; menus may be Japanese-only, so a little Japanese or a translation app will make ordering smoother.

places.name: 魚匠庵 (Gyoshoan)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. This is a sushi and seafood restaurant whose signature eel is glazed in a wheat-based soy tare, and dashi/soy seasonings run through the menu. No gluten-free accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The menu is built around fresh local fish, sushi, sashimi and grilled eel, all using fish-based dashi and seafood. No vegan options are documented.
places.neighborhood: Ekinishi Shinmachi (near Kanazawa Stn)
places.cuisine: Sushi and local seafood kaiseki / izakaya
places.notes: 魚匠庵 is a sister restaurant produced by the Kanazawa Maimon Sushi group, set near Kanazawa Station in Eki-nishi-shinmachi. It is a spacious, calm-toned restaurant built for gatherings, with counter, table, raised zashiki and tatami rooms designed for everything from family celebra

chef_bio.background: 魚匠庵 is a sister restaurant produced by the Kanazawa Maimon Sushi group, set near Kanazawa Station in Eki-nishi-shinmachi. It is a spacious, calm-toned restaurant built for gatherings, with counter, table, raised zashiki and tatami rooms designed for everything from family celebrations and first-meal (お食い初め) and coming-of-age rites to memorial dinners and banquets.
chef_bio.philosophy: To convey the full deliciousness of just-caught local fish, served in a tranquil space offering an out-of-the-ordinary sense of relaxation.
chef_bio.specialty: The kitchen centers on fresh local fish landed at Kanazawa and Nanao ports, served as sushi, sashimi, grilled and steamed dishes, with seasonal stars like winter buri (yellowtail) shabu-shabu and Kaga crab. A signature draw is its thick-cut Aichi-prefecture eel, grilled in a continuously replenished house tare for an exceptionally meaty cut with a fluffy interior and rich, melting fat.

anecdotes.text: For Doyo no Ushi (the midsummer eel day, around July 26), the restaurant grills its large whole eel over charcoal in a demonstration sale from its own parking lot, offering a master's whole-eel kabayaki as a limited takeout.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature eel is glazed in a continuously replenished house soy-based tare, which in Japan is virtually always made with wheat-containing soy sauce; sushi and grilled dishes likewise rely on standard soy sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is built around fresh fish, sushi and seafood with fish-based dashi throughout, so any non-seafood dish would carry a high risk of seafood and dashi cross-contact.

cultural_comfort.note: A polished, spacious banquet-style sushi and seafood restaurant aimed at family gatherings and formal occasions; a foreign student would be welcome but would do best coming with a Japanese-speaking guide or a clear plan, as it is not a casual drop-in spot.

places.name: 大衆酒場魚八 (Taishu Sakaba Uohachi)
places.gf_detail: A seafood-focused izakaya built around sashimi, fried local dishes, and grilled fish — soy sauce, tempura/karaage batter, and dashi are pervasive, and no gluten-free accommodation is documented. Not safe for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: The menu centers on fresh fish from Kanazawa Port (boat-style sashimi platters, grilled nodoguro, fish-and-lotus-root fritters). No vegan or vegetarian options are documented; dashi and seafood run through nearly everything.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Thu 17:30–23:30 (L.O. 23:00) / Fri, Sat & days before holidays 17:30–24:00 (L.O. 23:30) / Sun & holidays 16:30–22:00 (L.O. 21:30)
places.neighborhood: Kanazawa Stn West Exit / Hirooka
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A casual seafood izakaya a five-minute walk from the west exit of Kanazawa Station, built around fish delivered directly from Kanazawa Port.

chef_bio.background: A casual seafood izakaya a five-minute walk from the west exit of Kanazawa Station, built around fish delivered directly from Kanazawa Port.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop describes itself around reliable, affordable pricing that welcomes everyday patrons — an unpretentious neighborhood drinking spot rather than a formal restaurant.
chef_bio.specialty: Boat-style sashimi platters (舟盛), grilled and seared nodoguro (rosy seabass), and Kaga-style dishes such as Kaga lotus root and megisu fritters (加賀れんこんとめぎすの挟み揚げ).

anecdotes.text: The signature 舟盛 (boat platter) showcases the day's catch from Kanazawa Port, and nodoguro is highlighted for its rich fat, deep umami, and elegant, non-fishy taste.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Fried Kaga lotus root and megisu fritters share the kitchen; no dedicated gluten-free preparation is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Sashimi and seared fish are served with soy sauce, which in Japan is typically wheat-based; no gluten-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is seafood-centric (sashimi, grilled fish, dashi-based dishes), so vegan cross-contact risk is high and no plant-only preparation is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A lively, smoking-permitted local izakaya near Kanazawa Station with no documented English menu — comfortable for a foreign student who knows some Japanese or can point at dishes, but expect a Japanese-language setting.

places.name: 大衆酒場 魚八 はなれ (Taishu Sakaba Uohachi Hanare)
places.gf_detail: This is a soy-sauce and dashi-heavy izakaya — oden simmered in dashi, fried items (tempura/fry), and sushi-style dishes all involve wheat-containing soy sauce or batter. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented, so it is not safe for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: The menu is built around fresh fish, sashimi, oysters, nodoguro and seafood, and the oden and dishes are made with fish/kombu dashi. No vegan options are documented; even vegetable items rely on dashi or animal-based seasoning.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Fri 18:00-00:30; Sat 18:00-01:00; Sun closed
places.neighborhood: Horikawa-cho, near Kanazawa Station East Exit
places.notes: Uohachi Hanare opened on October 15, 2024 in Horikawa-cho, a five-minute walk from Kanazawa Station's east exit. It is the second outpost of the popular Taishu Sakaba Uohachi, whose original location sits two minutes from the station's west (port) side. The shop leans into Kanaza

chef_bio.background: Uohachi Hanare opened on October 15, 2024 in Horikawa-cho, a five-minute walk from Kanazawa Station's east exit. It is the second outpost of the popular Taishu Sakaba Uohachi, whose original location sits two minutes from the station's west (port) side. The shop leans into Kanazawa's harbor, with fresh fish delivered from Kanazawa Port displayed in a counter-side case.
chef_bio.philosophy: A casual, high-value neighborhood drinking spot — small plates start at 299 yen — built on daily fresh fish and the theatre of an open counter where guests watch the fish case, oden pot and robata grill at work.
chef_bio.specialty: Fresh seafood and Kanazawa oden. Signature plates include the five-piece Kanazawa oden assortment (金沢おでん盛り合わせ5種, ~1,098 yen) simmered in a light-yet-deep dashi, nodoguro and other local fish, sashimi platters, the 'toro-taku nokke' sushi (トロたくのっけ寿司), and gando fry with house tartar-ponzu (がんどフライ 特性タルタルポン酢).

anecdotes.text: A standout is the Kanazawa oden assortment, described as 'oden infused with dashi that tastes light yet deeply flavorful' (出汁がしみしみのおでんは、あっさりながら深みのある味わい).
Appetizers begin at 299 yen, earning it a reputation as an exceptional-value (コスパ抜群) station-front izakaya.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu includes fried items (fries, tempura-style dishes) and is a general izakaya kitchen; no dedicated gluten-free preparation is documented, so cross-contact with wheat is likely.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Sushi, sashimi and oden are served/seasoned with standard Japanese soy sauce and dashi, which contain wheat; no wheat-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Oden and dishes use fish/kombu dashi and the kitchen centers on seafood; no vegan preparation is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A lively, affordable local izakaya where second-floor tables use touch-panel ordering, so a few words of Japanese and a willingness to point at the menu will carry you through comfortably.

places.name: 金茶寮 本店 (Kincharyo Honten)
places.gf_detail: This is a traditional Kaga-cuisine kaiseki ryotei where multi-course menus lean heavily on dashi, soy-based seasonings, and wheat-containing components typical of Japanese fine dining. No gluten-free program, dedicated preparation, or celiac accommodation is documented. Not suitable for strict gluten avoidance; a serious celiac would need to discuss needs in advance and accept high uncertainty.
places.vegan_detail: The kaiseki courses center on seasonal mountain and sea ingredients (sashimi, grilled fish, simmered seafood) and dashi, so the standard menus are not vegan or vegetarian. As an upscale ryotei serving private rooms by reservation, a plant-based course might be arranged if requested well ahead, but no vegan offering is documented. Vegans should inquire directly before booking.
places.hours_raw: Lunch seating 11:00-14:00 / Dinner seating 17:00-21:00 (reservation required)
places.neighborhood: Teramachi / Nomachi
places.notes: Kincharyo opened in 1933 (Showa 8), taking over the villa of Baron Yokoyama, a former chief retainer of the Maeda family who ruled the million-koku Kaga domain. Within its roughly 1,000-tsubo garden stand the main hall, built in the Meiji era, and detached sukiya-style annexes. It has built up its history as one of the leading ryokan-restaurants of the castle town of Kanazawa.

roles: Kanazawa guest house for Kaga cuisine
chef_bio.origin: Kanazawa, Ishikawa (寺町), on the site of a former Maeda-clan retainer's villa
chef_bio.background: 金茶寮は昭和8年（1933年）、加賀百万石・前田家の元家老であった横山男爵の別邸を譲り受けて開業した料亭旅館。約1,000坪の庭園のなかに、明治期に建てられた本館と数寄屋風の離れが点在する。城下町金沢を代表する料理旅館として歴史を重ねてきた。
chef_bio.philosophy: 創業以来『一客一亭』のおもてなしを旨とし、客をもてなす料亭旅館としての文化の継承と発信を使命に掲げる。
chef_bio.specialty: 加賀の郷土料理に京都の懐石の手法を取り入れた会席料理。山海の幸をふんだんに用いた多皿構成のコースを、庭園を望む座敷・離れで供する。

anecdotes.text: 皇族や、吉田茂・佐藤栄作といった歴代首相をもてなしてきた由緒ある料亭として知られる。
2016年に『ミシュランガイド富山・石川（金沢）2016 特別版』で一つ星を獲得した。
京都の懐石料理の手法に地元の豊富な山海の幸を取り入れた料理を、約1,000坪の庭園と数寄屋造りの建物のなかで提供している。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a Kaga-cuisine kaiseki ryotei, menus rely on dashi and soy-based seasonings; no allergen or gluten information is published, so wheat-containing soy sauce should be assumed present.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Courses are built around sashimi, grilled fish and simmered seafood alongside dashi, so animal ingredients and cross-contact are pervasive on the standard menus.

positives.text: Meals are served in private rooms by reservation, so dietary requests can in principle be discussed directly with the kitchen when booking ahead.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a formal, reservation-only historic ryotei with kaiseki courses starting around 12,000 yen at lunch and far higher at dinner, so a foreign student would be most comfortable visiting with a Japanese-speaking guide or host who can handle booking and etiquette.

places.name: そば処 はしもと (Soba-dokoro Hashimoto)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free or celiac-safe. Echizen soba noodles are typically a buckwheat-wheat blend, and the cold soba is served with a wheat-based soy/dashi tsuyu poured over grated daikon; the recommended dish also adds aburaage (fried tofu) prepared with soy. No dedicated gluten-free preparation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan destination. The signature oroshi soba is dressed with a dashi-based tsuyu (typically containing bonito/kombu), so it is not reliably vegan. A plain soba could in theory be eaten without the fish-based broth, but no documented vegan preparation exists, so vegans must ask in advance and may find options limited.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–17:00 (L.O. 16:00); closes early when the day's soba sells out. Closed Wednesdays and the 2nd and 4th Thursdays.
places.neighborhood: Nomachi / Nishi Chaya district (toward Teramachi, central Kanazawa)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The owner hails from Fukui Prefecture and brings authentic Echizen-style soba to Kanazawa's historic Nishi Chaya-gai geisha district. The shop sits in a traditional machiya townhouse, with an elegant ground-floor counter and a second-floor tatami room. It originally opened under

chef_bio.background: The owner hails from Fukui Prefecture and brings authentic Echizen-style soba to Kanazawa's historic Nishi Chaya-gai geisha district. The shop sits in a traditional machiya townhouse, with an elegant ground-floor counter and a second-floor tatami room. It originally opened under the name 手打ち蕎麦 はなれ (Teuchi Soba Hanare) and now operates as そば処 はしもと.
chef_bio.philosophy: Hand-making the soba fresh each morning starting at 5 AM using Fukui-sourced buckwheat flour, bringing the rare Echizen soba style to Kanazawa.
chef_bio.specialty: Echizen oroshi soba. The signature dish is おろしそば (oroshi soba), where diners combine grated spicy daikon radish and dashi to coat 100% Fukui buckwheat noodles; the chef's recommendation is 油揚げ入りおろしそば (oroshi soba topped with crispy aburaage sourced from the long-established Fukui maker Taniguchiya / 谷口屋).

anecdotes.text: The owner begins hand-making the day's soba at 5 AM, and the shop closes once the soba sells out.
Uses 100% Fukui Prefecture buckwheat flour and sources its aburaage directly from Taniguchiya, a famous long-established fried-tofu maker in Fukui.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Cold oroshi soba is served with a soy/dashi tsuyu, which in Japan is normally made with wheat-containing soy sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The standard tsuyu is dashi-based and the recommended dish adds aburaage; no fish-free or animal-free preparation is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny owner-run soba counter in a traditional machiya where ordering is simple by pointing at the soba dish, but little to no English is documented, so a few Japanese words help.

places.name: 玄米菜食 明制（てんてん・野町） (Genmai Saishoku Meisei (Tenten Nomachi))
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free claim is documented. This is a brown-rice macrobiotic vegan kitchen, not a celiac-safe operation. Documented dishes include a soba (buckwheat) galette made with soy milk and soy-based meat alternatives, and Japanese plant-based cooking that typically relies on wheat-containing soy sauce. There is no mention of gluten-free soy sauce, a dedicated fryer, or cross-contamination controls. A celiac traveler should not assume safety here.
places.vegan_detail: Fully vegan. The kitchen uses no meat, fish, eggs, dairy, chemical seasonings, or additives, centering on local pesticide-free vegetables, brown rice, and traditional seasonings. Vegetable-forward dishes (steamed and roasted vegetables, soy-meat curry, soy-milk buckwheat galette) and vegan cakes are served. Macrobiotic in approach, so the whole menu is plant-based by default.
places.hours_raw: By reservation (Fri, Sat, Sun) from 11:30, timed to the guest (other days can be booked on request)
places.neighborhood: Nomachi (southwest part of central Kanazawa)
places.notes: A small, intimate brown-rice macrobiotic kitchen in Kanazawa's Nomachi district, serving entirely plant-based Japanese cooking by reservation. Beyond meals, the owner shares the same lifestyle through cooking classes and traditional kimono-dressing lessons held at the same place.

chef_bio.background: A small, intimate brown-rice macrobiotic kitchen in Kanazawa's Nomachi district, serving entirely plant-based Japanese cooking by reservation. Beyond meals, the owner shares the same lifestyle through cooking classes and traditional kimono-dressing lessons held at the same place.
chef_bio.philosophy: Cooking with no meat, fish, eggs, dairy, chemical seasonings, or additives — letting local organic vegetables and traditional condiments carry the meal so that, in the spirit one visitor described, both stomach and heart feel at ease.
chef_bio.specialty: Macrobiotic vegan Japanese plates built around local pesticide-free vegetables and traditional seasonings: steamed vegetables (with carrot cut into heart shapes and tofu standing in for egg), roasted carrots described as surprisingly sweet with concentrated umami, a soy-based 'meat' curry seasoned with spices that wins over non-vegetarians, and a thick, chewy buckwheat galette made with soy milk. Vegan cakes round out the menu.

anecdotes.text: A visitor wrote that even the bold, impactful soy-meat curry satisfied non-vegetarians dining alongside vegetarians, and that the food left both the stomach and the heart feeling relieved and at ease.
Guests may bring their own alcohol for a 300-yen corkage, lending the small room a relaxed, social feel.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No gluten-free or celiac protocols are documented; the kitchen is macrobiotic vegan, not allergen-controlled for wheat.
A signature dish is a buckwheat (soba) galette made with soy milk; buckwheat galettes are commonly blended with wheat flour, and no wheat-free claim is made.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Traditional Japanese seasonings are used; no gluten-free soy sauce is mentioned, so assume standard wheat-containing shoyu.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire kitchen is animal-product-free (no meat, fish, eggs, or dairy), so vegan cross-contact with animal ingredients is not a concern.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Service is fully reservation-based and tailored to the guest, which suggests dietary requests can be discussed when booking, though no specific allergy-handling protocol is documented.

positives.text: Completely vegan kitchen using local pesticide-free vegetables, brown rice, and no chemical seasonings or additives — an easy, reliable choice for vegans and vegetarians.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a tiny, reservation-only home-style macrobiotic kitchen run on a personal schedule, so a foreign student should book ahead and expect to manage in Japanese, but the plant-based welcome is warm.

places.name: 加賀麸司 宮田・鈴庵 (Kaga Fuzukasa Miyata Suzuan)
places.gf_detail: This restaurant's entire cuisine is built on 麩 (fu), which is wheat gluten — the signature product is essentially concentrated wheat protein. Courses are fu-centered (生麩 sashimi, fried fu, fu steak, fu desserts), and the famous local dish 治部煮 is thickened and seasoned with wheat-based ingredients. This is one of the least gluten-free-safe restaurants imaginable for a celiac diner; it should be avoided entirely by anyone who must exclude gluten.
places.vegan_detail: Much of the fu cuisine is plant-based in origin (wheat gluten), and several preparations are vegetarian-leaning, but the menu is not vegan: courses include 治部煮 (jibuni, which uses chicken/duck and dashi) and egg dishes, and dashi-based seasoning is used throughout. No documented vegan course. A vegan diner would need to inquire in advance about which fu dishes can be served without animal products; this is not confirmed possible.
places.hours_raw: 11:30–15:00 (Tue, Thu, Fri, Sat, Sun, Mon), closed Wednesdays. By reservation. Note: the official site also shows 9:00–16:00; confirm your booking time before visiting.
places.neighborhood: Higashi Chaya district area (Higashiyama)
places.notes: Suzuan is the restaurant arm of Kaga Fuzukasa Miyata, a long-established Kanazawa fu (wheat gluten) maker founded in 1875 (Meiji 8). Set in a quiet residential pocket within walking distance of the Higashi Chaya District, the dining room has a traditional wooden interior, a view

chef_bio.background: Suzuan is the restaurant arm of Kaga Fuzukasa Miyata, a long-established Kanazawa fu (wheat gluten) maker founded in 1875 (Meiji 8). Set in a quiet residential pocket within walking distance of the Higashi Chaya District, the dining room has a traditional wooden interior, a view onto a courtyard garden, and distinctive lapis-lazuli-toned walls.
chef_bio.philosophy: To carry forward the heritage of Kaga fu — a craft refined in the Edo period by Funaki Den'nai, a chef to the ruling Maeda clan, who perfected fu that would not fall apart when simmered (the 'sudare-fu' used in jibuni) — while also demonstrating that fu can pair with Western ingredients and modern techniques.
chef_bio.specialty: Multi-course fu cuisine that shows off the versatility of 麩 — raw fu (生麩) served sashimi-style, glazed and fried fu, fu 'steak', vinegared and simmered preparations, and fu-based sweets finished with black honey, alongside the Kaga classic 治部煮 (jibuni). Three set courses are offered (roughly 3,850-6,050 yen).

anecdotes.text: Kaga fu traces to 1725, when the Maeda-clan chef Funaki Den'nai-kanehaya perfected simmer-proof fu and created the 'sudare-fu' still used in the local dish jibuni.

A reviewer's 'Course Three' ran to eight items, from raw-fu sashimi through fu steak and fried fu to rice with chestnut and millet and a fu dessert with black honey, illustrating how the kitchen builds an entire meal around a single ingredient.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The restaurant's core ingredient, fu, is wheat gluten; virtually every dish on the fu courses is wheat-based, so the entire menu is unsafe for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Traditional Kaga preparations such as jibuni rely on Japanese soy sauce and broth, which standardly contain wheat; no wheat-free seasoning is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Courses include jibuni (chicken/duck and dashi) and egg dishes, and dashi-based seasoning is used across the menu, so plant-only fu dishes are not guaranteed and would need to be requested in advance.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a quiet, reservation-only traditional fu-cuisine restaurant where little to no English is likely; book ahead (ideally with help) and be ready to dine in a calm, formal atmosphere.

places.name: 百薬キッチン (Hyakuyaku Kitchen)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature dish "百薬御膳" features Kaga-fu (a wheat-gluten product) in its dessert, and the kitchen uses miso, sauces and likely soy sauce throughout. There is no dedicated gluten-free menu, no dedicated fryer, and no documented celiac-safe handling. This is not a safe choice for celiac travelers.
places.vegan_detail: Vegetable-forward and health-oriented, but not vegan. The popular set's herb "hamburger" combines tofu with chicken, and other menu items include chicken and natto/avocado dishes; fish/dashi presence elsewhere is not documented. Some plant-based vegetable side dishes exist, but full vegan meals are not advertised — vegans should call ahead (076-255-1893) to ask what can be prepared without animal products.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:00-15:00 (L.O. 14:30); some morning service reported 7:30-10:00 and occasional dinner on weekends/Mondays/holidays. Closed Tuesdays (open if a holiday); irregular closures occur — call to confirm.
places.neighborhood: Nagamachi samurai district area (Nagamachi, near Kōrinbō)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A husband-and-wife team who previously ran an ethnic-cuisine restaurant in Uchinada before relocating to Kanazawa, where they reimagined their cooking around traditional Japanese medicinal herbs. They opened 百薬キッチン in January 2010 in a hidden-gem space reached through a narrow pa

chef_bio.background: A husband-and-wife team who previously ran an ethnic-cuisine restaurant in Uchinada before relocating to Kanazawa, where they reimagined their cooking around traditional Japanese medicinal herbs. They opened 百薬キッチン in January 2010 in a hidden-gem space reached through a narrow passage beside a residential building in the Nagamachi samurai-house district.
chef_bio.philosophy: Built on the idea that 'everything we eat becomes medicine' (食べるものすべてが薬になる) — extremely healthful food that is still everyday-delicious. Medicinal herbs (loquat, goji, mugwort, etc.) are sourced from Shirakane specialists and organic vegetables from local and Miyagi farmers.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature lunch '百薬御膳' (Hyakuyaku Gozen) built around a medicinal-herb tofu-and-chicken hamburger with herb sauce, served with 10+ organic vegetables, brown rice from contract farmers, miso soup, a mugwort/Kaga-fu sweet from the old confectioner Miyata, and a daily herbal tea. Other dishes include avocado-natto donburi, herb-fried chicken, and mugwort-fu ginger carpaccio; evenings feature playful 'medicinal junk food' like herb tacos and bagna cauda.

anecdotes.text: Co-owner Megumi says they set out to make food that is 'extremely healthful yet enjoyable to eat normally,' noting the medicinal-herb tofu hamburger is especially popular with children.

The dessert mugwort sweet is made with Kaga-fu from the traditional Kanazawa confectioner Miyata, and the open kitchen with glass jars of herbs invites guests to chat with the owners about ingredients.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Kaga-fu (a wheat-gluten product) is used in the signature set's dessert, and wheat/gluten ingredients are present in the kitchen, so cross-contact is likely.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Miso soup and house sauces/dressings are standard parts of the menu; conventional (wheat-containing) soy sauce/miso use is typical and no gluten-free substitution is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The signature herb hamburger blends tofu with chicken, and chicken/egg/dairy-based items appear across the menu, so plant-only dishes share a kitchen with animal products.

positives.text: Strong emphasis on organic local vegetables, brown rice and medicinal herbs, with owners openly discussing ingredient sourcing — a good fit for plant-forward eaters who can ask about specific dishes.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny owner-run spot where the friendly husband-and-wife team are happy to chat about ingredients, but service is in Japanese, so a few basic phrases (and pointing at the set menu) will make a foreign student feel welcome.

places.name: グリーンノート（光明食養研究所） (Green Note (Komyo Shokuyo Kenkyusho))
places.gf_detail: This is a macrobiotic / brown-rice (玄米) place, so rice-based dishes are naturally a good base, but documented menu items include brown-rice nut BREAD (玄米木の実パンセット) which contains wheat, and macrobiotic cooking commonly uses miso, shoyu, and tamari that often contain wheat. No dedicated gluten-free program or cross-contamination controls are documented. A celiac traveler should ask directly about each item.
places.vegan_detail: A macrobiotic / 食養 (food-as-medicine) cafe whose documented offerings — brown-rice fermented foods, brown-rice nut bread set, natural sweets, health teas — are plant-based in character. Macrobiotic cuisine is typically vegan, but the menu is not formally certified vegan and items are not individually documented, so confirm specific dishes (especially sweets) when ordering.
places.hours_raw: Hours not formally published. Described as essentially open with no fixed closing day, but the proprietor frequently runs outside events / food-education sessions, so advance reservation is recommended before visiting to eat.
places.neighborhood: 瓢箪町 (Hyotanmachi), near Omicho Market and Hashiba-cho, centr
places.notes: Green Note is a natural-food shop and food-education space in Kanazawa's Hyotanmachi, a short walk from Omicho Market. It also goes by 光明食養研究所 (Komyo Shokuyo Kenkyusho), framing itself as a research and teaching practice around 食養 (shokuyo, food as medicine) and macrobiotics as m

chef_bio.background: Green Note is a natural-food shop and food-education space in Kanazawa's Hyotanmachi, a short walk from Omicho Market. It also goes by 光明食養研究所 (Komyo Shokuyo Kenkyusho), framing itself as a research and teaching practice around 食養 (shokuyo, food as medicine) and macrobiotics as much as a place to buy and eat.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop's motto is 『台所は家庭の薬局、健体康心』 — roughly, 'the kitchen is the family pharmacy; a healthy body, a peaceful heart.' It centers brown rice, fermented foods and additive-free macrobiotic eating as everyday medicine.
chef_bio.specialty: An attached brown-rice cafe with a traditional irori sunken hearth serves macrobiotic plates and snacks: the food-nutrition basic set (食養基本セット) and the brown-rice nut bread set (玄米木の実パンセット), plus brown-rice fermented foods, natural sweets and health teas. It also stocks Osawa Japan and other additive-free macrobiotic products.

anecdotes.text: The cafe is built around an irori (sunken hearth) and is named '奄美' (Amami), serving brown-rice fermented foods, natural sweets and health teas alongside the shop's macrobiotic groceries.
Affordable tasting-style sets such as the brown-rice nut bread set and a food-nutrition basic set are offered, along with samples of enzyme/fermented foods.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Macrobiotic cooking here relies on miso/shoyu/tamari seasonings, which commonly contain wheat; no wheat-free guarantee is documented.

positives.text: Menu centers on brown rice and additive-free macrobiotic, largely plant-based foods, which is a friendly base for vegan diners.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a tiny owner-run shop and food-education space rather than a regular restaurant, so it helps to call ahead in Japanese (or have a Japanese-speaking friend reserve) and be ready for a personal, conversation-heavy visit.

places.name: Cafe 甘 本店 (Cafe Kan (Honten))
places.gf_detail: The cafe's own site promotes "low-calorie menus with minimal allergens and many gluten-free options," reflecting its health-focused fermented-adzuki concept. However this is a wagashi cafe and several signature items contain wheat (fresh dorayaki plate, certain accompaniments), so it is not a dedicated gluten-free kitchen and no celiac cross-contamination protocol is documented. A coeliac should ask staff which specific items (e.g. parfaits, anmitsu without wheat components) are safe and be aware cross-contact is possible.
places.vegan_detail: The menu is built around adzuki beans, kinako, matcha and Kaga hojicha, but parfaits and many sweets include dairy (vanilla ice cream, whipped cream) and the cafe makes no vegan claim. Some plant-based components (fermented adzuki, mochi/shiratama dumplings, agar-based anmitsu jelly, black tea/roasted tea) exist, so a vegan could likely assemble a limited option by asking staff to omit dairy, but nothing is marketed as vegan.
places.hours_raw: 10:30-18:00 (last order 17:30). Closed Wednesdays and the 1st and 3rd Tuesdays per the cafe's own site; the Tabelog listing shows closed Mondays and Wednesdays. Closures may vary, so confirm before visiting.
places.neighborhood: 尾張町 (Owaricho), between Omicho Market and the Higashi Chaya
places.notes: Cafe Kan is the townhouse cafe of Koshiyama Kanseido, a long-established Kanazawa wagashi (Japanese confectionery) house. The cafe moved from Musashi-machi to a renovated Edo-period machiya in Owaricho, reopening on 10 February 2023 with a fuller menu, placing it on the walking r

chef_bio.background: Cafe Kan is the townhouse cafe of Koshiyama Kanseido, a long-established Kanazawa wagashi (Japanese confectionery) house. The cafe moved from Musashi-machi to a renovated Edo-period machiya in Owaricho, reopening on 10 February 2023 with a fuller menu, placing it on the walking route between Omicho Market and the Higashi tea district.
chef_bio.philosophy: Themed 'delicious and healthy,' the cafe runs an in-house 'AZUKI de HAKKO' (fermented-legume) research effort, fermenting adzuki and other beans with rice koji to draw out natural sweetness with roughly half the usual sugar while keeping nutritional density.
chef_bio.specialty: Signature items center on the Kaga hojicha (roasted barley tea) and kinako parfait, the matcha and pistachio parfait developed by the proprietor, fermented-adzuki cream anmitsu, and a seasonal fresh dorayaki plate. Guests can choose standard or fermented adzuki for their parfaits.

anecdotes.text: The matcha and pistachio parfait is described as a creation refined through the seventh-generation owner's own experimentation.
On relocating in February 2023, the shop set up an 'AZUKI de HAKKO Research Institute' to develop fermented-bean sweets under a delicious-and-healthy concept.

positives.text: The cafe's own site states it offers low-calorie menus with minimal allergens and many gluten-free options, reflecting its fermented-adzuki health concept.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed townhouse sweets cafe used to tourists from the nearby tea district; a few words of Japanese and pointing at the picture menu will carry you through comfortably.

places.name: 兼六園 三芳庵 (Kenrokuen Miyoshian)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is documented. This is a traditional Kaga cuisine restaurant built around soy sauce, dashi, and wheat-based seasonings; signature dishes such as jibu-ni and kabura-zushi rely on wheat-containing ingredients. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian menu is documented. The menu centers on seafood (e.g., botan-ebi, kombu-jime), jibu-ni (typically duck or chicken), and fish-based dashi. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
places.hours_raw: 9:30-16:30 (extended during cherry blossom season and Kenrokuen garden illumination events). Seasonal variation noted; the garden tea-house listing also cites 8:00-17:00 in winter and 7:00-18:00 in summer for garden access.
places.neighborhood: Inside Kenrokuen, beside Hisago Pond (Kenrokumachi)
places.notes: Miyoshian was founded in 1875 (Meiji 8), the year after Kenrokuen Garden first opened to the public, and stands on the shore of Hyoike Pond, often called the birthplace of the garden. Its name reflects its three original structures: a main building (本館) that also manages the adja

chef_bio.background: Miyoshian was founded in 1875 (Meiji 8), the year after Kenrokuen Garden first opened to the public, and stands on the shore of Hyoike Pond, often called the birthplace of the garden. Its name reflects its three original structures: a main building (本館) that also manages the adjacent tea room, a water pavilion (水亭) set over Hyoike Pond, and a villa built above Suiryaku Waterfall.
chef_bio.philosophy: The house frames the experience around the garden ideals of 蒼古 (ancient verdure) and 幽邃 (profound, tranquil depth), emphasizing quiet enjoyment of food and tea within refined natural surroundings.
chef_bio.specialty: Kaga regional cuisine including jibu-ni and winter-only house-made kabura-zushi, seasonal bento boxes, and matcha tea served with seasonal wagashi. Diners eat by the pond with views of the changing seasons and the sound of Suiryaku Waterfall.

anecdotes.text: Since its founding it has hosted members of the imperial family and many notable figures.
The venue also hosts tea-style weddings (茶婚式) and receptions overlooking the garden.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Traditional Kaga dishes such as jibu-ni and kabura-zushi rely on soy sauce and other wheat-containing seasonings; no wheat-free preparation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is built on seafood, meat-based jibu-ni, and fish dashi, with no vegan options documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A historic, formal Kaga-cuisine tea house inside Kenrokuen Garden where some etiquette and Japanese ability help; a foreign student would be most comfortable visiting with a guide or Japanese-speaking companion, especially for a full meal reservation.

places.name: インドめし ティルパティ (Indomeshi Tirupati)
places.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The kitchen is built around naan baked in a tandoor oven (wheat flour), and the vegetarian curries use soy meat dumplings; Indian curries here are also commonly thickened/seasoned with wheat-containing ingredients. Rice (Koshihikari) is available as an alternative to naan, but there is no documented gluten-free protocol, no dedicated fryer or prep area mentioned, and cross-contamination from a flour-heavy kitchen is likely. A celiac diner should not assume safety; rice-based dishes would need to be confirmed individually with staff.
places.vegan_detail: Vegetarian-friendly rather than strictly vegan. There is a vegetable curry (with soy-meat dumplings) and a vegetable set aimed at vegetarians, plus a choice of naan or rice. However, dairy is heavily used in Indian cooking here (naan typically contains milk/yogurt/butter; kulfi dessert and likely paneer/cream-based curries are present), so dishes are not confirmed dairy-free or egg-free. A vegan diner can find plant-based mains but must ask specifically about ghee, butter, milk, yogurt and cream in each dish.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Thu, Fri, Sat, Sun: 11:00–14:30, 17:00–21:00 (L.O. 20:30); closed Wednesdays
places.neighborhood: Fujie-kita (prefectural office / Ekinishi area)
places.notes: Tirupati opened on 27 July 2000, timed with the relocation of the Ishikawa Prefectural Government to the nearby Fujie/駅西 district, and has run continuously in the same Fujie-kita spot for around 25 years. Head chef Mohan Kumar brings roughly 40 years of cooking experience and ble

chef_bio.background: Tirupati opened on 27 July 2000, timed with the relocation of the Ishikawa Prefectural Government to the nearby Fujie/駅西 district, and has run continuously in the same Fujie-kita spot for around 25 years. Head chef Mohan Kumar brings roughly 40 years of cooking experience and blends the shop's own spice mixes by hand.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen leans toward lighter, health-conscious Indian cooking: Himalayan rock salt and Koshihikari rice are used, and the chef deliberately cuts back on the oil typical of Indian curry while keeping authentic flavor. The name 'Tirupati' is taken from a famous Indian temple, chosen with the wish that the restaurant be a place where many people gather and happiness and abundance circulate.
chef_bio.specialty: Curries with naan baked in a traditional tandoor oven, vegetable curry with soy-meat dumplings, chicken kofta, biryani and tandoori chicken; six levels of spice heat from mild to extra-hot, plus a kulfi (cardamom and saffron ice cream) dessert. Set meals include a vegetable set and a dessert lunch.

anecdotes.text: The shop opened the same year the prefectural government moved nearby and has served the 駅西 area's families and office workers ever since, with lunchtimes drawing local families.
Chef Mohan Kumar says: 塩はヒマラヤ岩塩、お米はコシヒカリを使用しています。通常のインドカレーは、油を多く使いますが、なるべく使わないようにヘルシーに仕上げています。
The restaurant has run an online shop for about 20 years selling its curries, naan, tandoori chicken, biryani, original spice blends, Himalayan salt and chai packs.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen centers on a tandoor oven baking wheat-flour naan, so airborne flour and shared surfaces make cross-contact likely; no gluten-free handling is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The vegetarian vegetable curry uses soy-meat dumplings and the menu relies heavily on dairy (naan, kulfi, likely cream/paneer dishes); no vegan-specific separation is documented, so confirm ghee/butter/milk per dish.

positives.text: Offers a dedicated vegetable curry and vegetable set aimed at vegetarians, plus a choice of Koshihikari rice instead of naan.
Uses Himalayan rock salt and reduces oil for a lighter, more health-conscious style of Indian cooking.

cultural_comfort.note: A relaxed, family-friendly neighborhood curry house where a friendly greeting in Japanese goes a long way; the chef is Indian and the menu is approachable, but staff communication is mainly in Japanese so basic phrases help.

places.name: 一笑 (Issho)
places.gf_detail: This is a roasted-tea (hojicha) tea house; the tea itself is naturally gluten-free, but the food offering is seasonal wagashi (traditional Japanese sweets) which commonly contain wheat flour or are dusted with it, and there is no documented gluten-free handling. Celiac travelers should ask about the specific sweet of the day; treat as not celiac-safe by default.
places.vegan_detail: Hojicha is vegan, and many Japanese wagashi are plant-based (bean paste, rice, sugar), but the seasonal confections rotate and some may contain egg or dairy. No vegan labeling is documented, so vegan guests should confirm the day's sweet.
places.hours_raw: 12:00-17:00 (L.O. 16:30); closed Mondays and Tuesdays
places.neighborhood: Higashiyama (Higashi Chaya district)
places.notes: Issho is a hojicha specialty tea house in the heart of Kanazawa's historic Higashi Chaya District, opened and directly operated by Maruhachi Seicha, a Kaga roasted-tea (kaga boucha) producer founded in 1863 in Kaga City, Ishikawa. The shop occupies a renovated traditional machiya

chef_bio.background: Issho is a hojicha specialty tea house in the heart of Kanazawa's historic Higashi Chaya District, opened and directly operated by Maruhachi Seicha, a Kaga roasted-tea (kaga boucha) producer founded in 1863 in Kaga City, Ishikawa. The shop occupies a renovated traditional machiya townhouse that blends a tea service area, a rotating monthly gallery, and an upstairs coworking space.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop's concept is 'kaoru, ajiwau, tsunagaru' — to be fragrant, flavorful, and connected — framing the tea house as a place to work, think, and take a breather over Japanese tea, where new ideas and conversation arise.
chef_bio.specialty: Hojicha (roasted green tea) paired with seasonal wagashi. Guests are first served a water-brewed cup of the prized 'kensho kaga boucha' (a roasted tea made from first-harvest stems), then choose from a small selection of hojicha after smelling the leaves; the food menu is limited to roasted tea and seasonal sweets.

anecdotes.text: On arrival, customers are welcomed with a complimentary cup of water-brewed kensho kaga boucha, and pick their hojicha by smelling the different tea leaves before ordering.
The menu deliberately consists only of hojicha and seasonal Japanese sweets, reflecting the shop's focus on showcasing roasted tea.

positives.text: Hojicha roasted tea, the shop's core offering, is naturally gluten-free and vegan.

cultural_comfort.note: A calm, tourist-friendly tea house in the Higashi Chaya District where you write your name on a waiting list and choose a hojicha after smelling the leaves; a few words of Japanese help, but the simple tea-and-sweets format makes it easy to enjoy.

places.name: YAE 東山（ヤエ） (YAE Higashiyama)
places.gf_detail: Galettes are made from buckwheat (soba) flour, which is naturally wheat-free, and that is the core of the menu. However, this is NOT a dedicated gluten-free kitchen: there is no documented allergen protocol, no stated dedicated equipment, and savory galettes are built with cheese, cured meats and sauces whose ingredients are not published. Celiacs should not assume safety — confirm the batter is 100 percent soba (no wheat blend) and ask about cross-contamination directly before relying on it.
places.vegan_detail: The menu centers on meat, seafood, cheese and butter (Noto pork bacon, halibut, duck, four-cheese, etc.), so it is not a vegetarian or vegan destination. No vegan options are documented. Sweet galettes and coffee may offer something workable, but you would need to ask in person; assume no reliable plant-based main.
places.hours_raw: Open 11:00–17:00 (galettes served 11:00–14:00, café & desserts thereafter). Closed Mondays.
places.neighborhood: Higashiyama / Kannon-machi (around Higashi Chaya district)
places.notes: YAE opened on August 22, 2022 in Kanazawa's Higashiyama / Kannon-cho area, just around the corner from the famous Higashi Chaya geisha district. The shop occupies a traditional machiya townhouse with an attached storehouse (kura) that has been strikingly renovated — glass-fronted

chef_bio.origin: 金沢市東山・観音町（ひがし茶屋街周辺）
chef_bio.background: YAE opened on August 22, 2022 in Kanazawa's Higashiyama / Kannon-cho area, just around the corner from the famous Higashi Chaya geisha district. The shop occupies a traditional machiya townhouse with an attached storehouse (kura) that has been strikingly renovated — glass-fronted contemporary design set against exposed old wooden beams — so the space itself is a draw, blending the city's history with a modern café feel.
chef_bio.philosophy: The concept is 'a full course meal experienced on a single plate of galette' (1皿のガレットで味わうコース料理) — using Hokuriku and seasonal produce layered onto each crepe.
chef_bio.specialty: Buckwheat (soba) galettes in the French Breton style. The signature is the Noto pork house-made bacon galette complète; seasonal savory galettes feature Hokuriku ingredients such as halibut with mushrooms or roasted duck with raspberry sauce, alongside dessert galettes and coffee in the afternoon.

anecdotes.text: The buckwheat is a heritage (zairai) variety grown in Echizen City, Fukui Prefecture, chosen for its rich aroma; lightly roasted, it is described as drawing out a deep, subtle umami.
The café fills a renovated storehouse and townhouse, prized as much for its photogenic interior as for the food.

positives.text: Galettes are made with buckwheat (soba) flour from a heritage Echizen variety, a grain that is naturally free of wheat.

cultural_comfort.note: A stylish, welcoming café near the Higashi Chaya district where a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu will get you a long way; staff are used to tourists but English may be limited.

places.name: ル・マルス（LE MARS） (Le Mars)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is documented. The menu centers on French country cooking (choucroute with sausages, cassoulet, breaded pork, brownies, petit fours) and beer, all of which typically contain wheat. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian menu is documented. This is a meat-forward French bistro (pork, sausages, cassoulet). Lunch courses do include a vegetable appetizer, but a vegan would need to call ahead to ask whether anything can be prepared without animal products; nothing specific is promised.
places.hours_raw: Dinner 18:00-22:30 (L.O. ~21:00). Lunch on Saturdays/Sundays 12:00-14:00; weekday lunch by reservation made the previous day. Thursdays dinner only (no lunch). Closed Wednesdays (official site notes Tuesday closures around year-end).
places.neighborhood: Kōrinbō
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Le Mars is a small, hidden French bistro tucked into a back alley of Kanazawa's Korinbo district. It is an independent, personally run establishment that celebrated its 20th anniversary in April, having opened around 2005. The dining room is intimate with an antique, distinctly E

chef_bio.background: Le Mars is a small, hidden French bistro tucked into a back alley of Kanazawa's Korinbo district. It is an independent, personally run establishment that celebrated its 20th anniversary in April, having opened around 2005. The dining room is intimate with an antique, distinctly European feel and warm lighting that locals describe as nostalgic and inviting.
chef_bio.philosophy: Bringing rustic regional French food and an unhurried European atmosphere to a quiet Kanazawa side street.
chef_bio.specialty: French country home cooking such as choucroute and cassoulet, paired with French wines and a selection of Belgian beers. A documented lunch featured a vegetable appetizer, a pork main with a crispy aromatic exterior and tender interior, a chocolate brownie, and coffee with petit fours.

anecdotes.text: The bistro marked its 20th anniversary in April, a milestone for a tiny owner-run spot in a Korinbo back alley.
Visitors describe stepping inside as feeling completely transported to Europe, with a warm, nostalgic interior.

positives.text: Reservations (ideally the previous day) are encouraged and can be made by phone or via Facebook/Instagram direct message, which gives diners a chance to discuss requests in advance.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny 16-seat back-alley bistro run as a personal labor of love, so it helps to have a few words of Japanese or a translation app, and calling ahead (ideally the day before) is appreciated, especially for weekday lunch.

places.name: 欧風料理酒場 カミイ (Ofu Ryori Sakaba Kamii)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free program. This is a European-style bistro serving homemade pizza, pork ribs and wine-pairing dishes; wheat, soy and standard seasonings are pervasive and there is no documented allergen handling. Not suitable for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The menu is built around fresh seafood from Omicho Market, Noto pork spare ribs and other meat/fish dishes; no vegan menu is documented. Vegetable dishes appear on the daily blackboard but are not prepared as vegan.
places.hours_raw: 18:00–00:00 (L.O. 23:30), weekdays, Saturdays and holidays. Closed Sundays (open if a Monday is a holiday).
places.neighborhood: 片町 (Katamachi)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Chef-owner Yasunori Kamii honed his craft over roughly 18 years at the long-established beer hall Pilsen and at bistros around Kanazawa before opening his own counter restaurant in Katamachi in October 2017.

chef_bio.background: Chef-owner Yasunori Kamii honed his craft over roughly 18 years at the long-established beer hall Pilsen and at bistros around Kanazawa before opening his own counter restaurant in Katamachi in October 2017.
chef_bio.philosophy: Kamii shops Omicho Market each day and builds the menu around 'what he finds exceptional,' favoring seasonal seafood and local vegetables over a fixed card, served across an intimate counter where he cooks and hosts single-handedly.
chef_bio.specialty: Ingredient-driven European cooking from a daily blackboard menu, with signatures including Noto pork spare ribs and homemade pizza, plus a wine list curated entirely by the chef's own palate.

anecdotes.text: The shop is a hideaway reached by a narrow staircase to the second floor, with counter seating facing the kitchen so guests interact directly with the chef.
Kamii spent about 18 years at the long-standing beer hall Pilsen before striking out on his own in 2017.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny chef-run counter bistro where the owner cooks and serves solo in Japanese; warm and approachable, but with only a handful of seats and a blackboard-only menu, comfort comes from basic Japanese and a willingness to chat with the chef.

places.name: 金澤 かが美 (Kanazawa Kagami)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. This is a Kanazawa oden kaiseki restaurant where dishes are simmered in dashi-based broths and seasoned with soy sauce, which in Japan almost always contains wheat. There is no documentation of gluten-free accommodation, dedicated equipment, or wheat-free options. A celiac traveler should not consider this restaurant safe.
places.vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians. The cuisine centers on Kanazawa oden and Kaga regional kaiseki built around bonito/kombu dashi, seafood (including items such as hotaruika/firefly squid and seasonal fish), and animal-based ingredients. No vegan or vegetarian course is documented. A plant-based diner could at best ask, but the menu is fundamentally fish- and dashi-based.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-14:00 (L.O. 13:30); Dinner 17:30-22:00 (L.O. 21:30). Closed Sundays (or the following day if Sunday is a public holiday).
places.neighborhood: Honmachi (~5 min walk from Kanazawa Stn East Exit)
places.cuisine: Kaga cuisine, regional dishes & oden kaiseki
places.notes: Kanazawa Kagami opened in 2019 as the oden-kaiseki sister restaurant of Amatsubo, a long-established Kaga regional-cuisine and izakaya group with more than half a century of history in the city. It occupies a renovated roughly hundred-year-old machiya (traditional Kanazawa townho

chef_bio.background: Kanazawa Kagami opened in 2019 as the oden-kaiseki sister restaurant of Amatsubo, a long-established Kaga regional-cuisine and izakaya group with more than half a century of history in the city. It occupies a renovated roughly hundred-year-old machiya (traditional Kanazawa townhouse) a short walk from Kanazawa Station, where aged wooden beams and traditional architecture are paired with a calm, modern interior.
chef_bio.philosophy: The restaurant works from the idea of 食の温故知新 — drawing on tradition while evolving it — so that guests can taste both the heritage and the modern evolution of Kanazawa oden in a single meal.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature is 'oden kaiseki' — Kanazawa oden reimagined and served course-style, plate by plate, with different broths matched to individual ingredients. The flagship Kenroku course showcases seasonal local produce sourced from nearby markets such as Omicho, alongside seasonal seafood, and an extensive list of Ishikawa sake (including Kuroobi, Tengu Mai, and Gorin).

anecdotes.text: The dining room is set inside a restored century-old Kanazawa townhouse, keeping its old timber framing while creating a quiet space where each oden course arrives one dish at a time.
Reviewers note dishes such as firefly squid and seasonal fish prepared with delicate technique, reflecting the kaiseki framing of what is normally a casual oden tradition.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Oden is cooked by simmering ingredients together in a shared dashi broth, so even naturally gluten-free items absorb the wheat-containing soy seasoning and share a common pot — high cross-contamination risk for celiac diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The cuisine is built on Kanazawa oden and Kaga kaiseki seasoning, which relies on soy sauce; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat and no wheat-free alternative is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes are based on bonito/kombu dashi and feature seafood throughout the oden and kaiseki courses, so plant-only items would still be cooked in fish-based broth.

cultural_comfort.note: This is a refined reservation-based kaiseki restaurant where some Japanese and a clear sense of dining etiquette help; a foreign student should book ahead and be ready for a higher-end, formal experience rather than a casual drop-in.

places.name: リョーモン (Ryomon)
places.gf_detail: A French bistro built around dishes like veal cutlet (breaded/fried), pâté de campagne, and pasta. These are wheat-heavy and there is no documented gluten-free menu or handling, so it is not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: A meat- and fish-forward French bistro (signature veal cutlet, pâté de campagne); no vegan menu or vegan handling is documented.
places.hours_raw: 18:00-22:00 (last seating around 20:30); closed Wednesdays
places.neighborhood: Higashiyama (Higashi Chaya district)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Ryomon is a machiya (traditional townhouse) bistro tucked one street behind the main thoroughfare of Kanazawa's Higashi Chaya geisha district, where it blends seamlessly into the historic teahouse streetscape. Its latticed entrance offers glimpses of guests nursing wine glasses a

chef_bio.background: Ryomon is a machiya (traditional townhouse) bistro tucked one street behind the main thoroughfare of Kanazawa's Higashi Chaya geisha district, where it blends seamlessly into the historic teahouse streetscape. Its latticed entrance offers glimpses of guests nursing wine glasses along a striking 8.3-meter single-slab African bubinga counter, lit in a moody, intimate glow.
chef_bio.philosophy: Restraint and technical precision over heavy saucing, with portion sizes thoughtfully adjusted to the size of the party.
chef_bio.specialty: Owner-chef Yamada Hisato is known for a reserved, artisan temperament and meticulous craftsmanship. The signature veal cutlet (仔牛のカツレツ) is cooked to a tender, fine-grained doneness and served with only salt to let the veal's natural sweetness come through; pâté de campagne, pasta, and seasonal fish and vegetable dishes round out the menu, paired with a serious wine selection.

anecdotes.text: The chef is described as a taciturn craftsman whose precise work and refined sensibility earn consistent trust, adjusting servings so couples and solo diners alike eat comfortably.
The veal cutlet is served with salt alone, no sauce, so the pure sweetness of the veal is intensified rather than masked.

cultural_comfort.note: This is an intimate, reservation-only owner-chef bistro where some Japanese and advance booking will help; it is a quiet adult dining spot rather than a casual drop-in.

places.name: 角の店 (Kado no Mise)
places.gf_detail: Wheat is central to the entire menu — okonomiyaki batter, yakisoba noodles, takoyaki, and taiyaki are all wheat-flour based and finished with wheat-based sauces. This is not a celiac-safe environment and there is no documented gluten-free handling.
places.vegan_detail: A teppan diner built around egg-batter okonomiyaki, seafood toppings (e.g., Noto oysters, octopus takoyaki), beef-tendon stew, and dashi/sauce — no documented vegan options. Not suitable for strict plant-based diners.
places.hours_raw: Mon/Tue/Thu 11:00-14:00 & 16:00-19:00; Fri 11:00-14:00 & 16:00-21:00; Sat 11:00-21:00; closed Wed & Sun (plus occasional irregular closures).
places.neighborhood: Nagamachi
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Kado no Mise occupies the spot of 'Shima' (島), a beloved okonomiyaki shop that ran for roughly 50 years and served as an after-school hangout for neighborhood kids in the 1960s-70s before closing for over a decade following its elderly owner's passing. Owner Kakura used to come h

chef_bio.background: Kado no Mise occupies the spot of 'Shima' (島), a beloved okonomiyaki shop that ran for roughly 50 years and served as an after-school hangout for neighborhood kids in the 1960s-70s before closing for over a decade following its elderly owner's passing. Owner Kakura used to come here as a schoolboy, buying simple okonomiyaki for around 15 yen and cooking it himself on the griddle. After a 2016 alumni reunion at the nearby elementary school, talk turned to the shuttered shop; when the building came up for sale, Kakura bought it and reopened it as Kado no Mise, recreating Shima's recipes with help from the original owner's daughter and her suppliers.
chef_bio.philosophy: Kakura, a former company head who once trained at a German hotel school and dreamed of opening a bakery, runs the place as 'doraku' (a labor of love / hobby) rather than a serious business, and has declined offers to expand so the shop stays small and manageable. The name 'corner shop' happens to echo the German 'An der Ecke' (at the corner) he once imagined for that bakery.
chef_bio.specialty: Yakisoba served the old-school way in a donburi bowl eaten with a fork (inherited from Shima), griddle okonomiyaki (from about 220 yen, with a seasonal Noto-oyster version), takoyaki, taiyaki, beef-tendon stew, and a wall of nostalgic dagashi (penny candy). In summer, dojo kabayaki appears, using loach from Kakura's own eel farming in Nanao.

anecdotes.text: A wall holds a neighborhood map where regulars past and present pin where they used to live, turning the shop into a living memory board of the old merchant quarter.

Despite classmates' enthusiasm at the reunion that inspired the revival, Kakura jokes that 'nobody comes' — yet the toys, manga, picture books and penny candy still draw solo daytime drinkers and weekend dads with their kids.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Okonomiyaki batter, yakisoba noodles, takoyaki and taiyaki are all wheat-flour based and prepared on a shared teppan griddle, so cross-contact with gluten is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes are finished with okonomiyaki/yakisoba sauce, which is typically wheat-based; no gluten-free sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Egg batter, seafood (Noto oysters, octopus), dashi and beef-tendon stew are core to the menu and share the same griddle, so plant-based dishes cannot be reliably separated.

cultural_comfort.note: A warm, chatty husband-and-wife place where you order verbally and pay after; the friendly proprietress (originally from Tokyo) puts first-timers at ease, but basic Japanese will make the experience much smoother.

places.name: 大衆割烹 魚吟 (Taishu Kappo Uogin)
places.gf_detail: A seafood izakaya built around soy-sauce and dashi-heavy Japanese cooking. Signature dishes like sashimi, seared nodoguro and grilled nodoguro sushi rely on soy sauce (wheat), and the Kanazawa oden is simmered in ishiru (fish sauce) and includes wheat gluten (fu). There is no documented gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or allergen handling. Not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: This is a fish- and seafood-centered kappo/izakaya — nodoguro, sashimi platters, smoked mackerel, and oden simmered in fish sauce (ishiru) are the core of the menu. Dashi and fish stock run through the dishes. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented; not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
places.hours_raw: 17:00-23:30 (L.O. 23:00), open daily including holidays
places.neighborhood: 片町（Katamachi）
places.notes: A casual yet refined kappo-style seafood izakaya in Kanazawa's Katamachi entertainment district, set in a restored traditional wooden building with gunjo (indigo) walls — a historically prestigious Kanazawa color — and decorative candy molds. The ground floor has an open kitchen

chef_bio.background: A casual yet refined kappo-style seafood izakaya in Kanazawa's Katamachi entertainment district, set in a restored traditional wooden building with gunjo (indigo) walls — a historically prestigious Kanazawa color — and decorative candy molds. The ground floor has an open kitchen with counter seating so guests can watch the chefs work, while upper floors offer private tatami rooms.
chef_bio.philosophy: The house motto is '大衆の値、割烹の味' — people's prices with kappo-quality cooking — built on locally sourced seafood and regional ingredients from the sea and mountains of Hokuriku.
chef_bio.specialty: Nodoguro (rosy seabass), called 'the king of white fish' for its rich, melting fat: served as seared sashimi, grilled, and as seared nodoguro sushi rolls. Also known for abundant 'bucket' sashimi platters of seafood landed that morning, and Kanazawa oden simmered in Noto ishiru (fish sauce), with more than 15 local Ishikawa sake varieties.

anecdotes.text: The Kanazawa oden is simmered in ishiru, a traditional fermented fish sauce from the Noto Peninsula, and served with local ingredients including daikon, wheat gluten (fu), and white shrimp dumplings.
