The restored building features indigo (gunjo) walls, a color historically reserved for prestigious use in Kanazawa, with antique candy molds as decor.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Soy-sauce- and dashi-based seafood cooking throughout; sashimi and grilled dishes are served with soy sauce, and oden is simmered in fish sauce — high gluten exposure with no documented gluten-free handling.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese soy sauce (wheat-containing) is used for sashimi and grilled dishes; no gluten-free tamari mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is built on fish, seafood, and fish-based dashi/ishiru stock; no vegan or vegetarian dishes documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A lively, popular local izakaya where a friendly greeting and pointing at the menu work fine, though staff may speak limited English and the room can be bustling.

places.name: 川端鮮魚店 片町店 (Kawabata Sengyoten Katamachi-ten)
places.gf_detail: This is a wheat- and soy-sauce-heavy seafood izakaya with no documented gluten-free accommodation. Sashimi and grilled fish are served with regular soy sauce (which contains wheat), and tempura/fried items and izakaya sauces are common. Not safe for celiacs without bringing your own tamari and confirming each dish; no dedicated GF handling is documented.
places.vegan_detail: A seafood-focused izakaya built around fresh fish, shellfish, sashimi and charcoal grilling; dashi (bonito/fish stock) is pervasive. There is no documented vegan menu or vegan-friendly preparation. Not a suitable destination for vegans.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Thu 17:00-23:00 (food L.O. 22:00); Fri 17:00-23:30 (food L.O. 22:30, drink L.O. 23:00); Sat & day-before-holiday 17:00-00:00 (food L.O. 23:00, drink L.O. 23:30); Sun closed (open if day before a holiday)
places.neighborhood: Katamachi
places.notes: Run by the Kawabata family, who hail from Nanao in the Noto region. The Katamachi flagship opened in 2009 in Kanazawa's main entertainment district, with a later branch added in Kikuracho around the time of the Hokuriku Shinkansen's arrival. Rather than a polished seafood restaur

chef_bio.background: Run by the Kawabata family, who hail from Nanao in the Noto region. The Katamachi flagship opened in 2009 in Kanazawa's main entertainment district, with a later branch added in Kikuracho around the time of the Hokuriku Shinkansen's arrival. Rather than a polished seafood restaurant, it deliberately adopts a working fishmonger aesthetic — fishing gear, flags and sake-brewery aprons decorate the room, staff dress in fishmonger uniforms, and display cases and tanks of live fish and shellfish greet you at the entrance.
chef_bio.philosophy: Bring the freshness and value of a fish market straight to the table — sourcing whole tuna and catch delivered fresh from Ishikawa's fishing ports, and letting diners grill it themselves over charcoal.
chef_bio.specialty: Five-piece sashimi platters of regional catch, shichirin (tabletop charcoal) grilling of fresh fish and shellfish, buri-shabu (yellowtail hot pot), and seasonal items such as natural Noto rock oysters in summer; paired with local sake.

anecdotes.text: The shop styles itself as a fishmonger rather than a restaurant: the interior is hung with fishing equipment, flags and sake-brewery aprons, staff wear fishmonger garb, and live tanks and display cases of the day's catch sit at the entrance.
The owners come from Nanao in Noto, and the Katamachi original opened in 2009; fish is procured fresh-caught directly from Ishikawa Prefecture's fishing ports.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Sashimi and grilled seafood are served with standard Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat; no wheat-free tamari is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is centered on fish and shellfish with fish-based dashi; no vegan-safe preparations are documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A lively, locally packed seafood izakaya where a few words of Japanese and pointing at the fish display go a long way; little to no English is expected, so a translation app helps.

places.name: 酒の大沢 (Sake no Ozawa)
places.gf_detail: This is a sake shop centered on Japanese sake (a brewed rice product) with a kakuuchi standing-drink counter rather than a kitchen. Sake itself is generally wheat-free, but the shop is not a food establishment with gluten-free provisioning, has no allergen handling, and any accompanying snacks (otsumami) are not controlled for gluten. Not suitable as a celiac-safe destination.
places.vegan_detail: A liquor retailer / standing-drink sake bar, not a food destination. There is no vegan menu; any nibbles served alongside drinks are incidental and unverified. Not relevant as a vegan dining choice, though the sake itself is plant-based.
places.hours_raw: Omicho Market listing: 9:00-16:00, closed Sundays, holidays and Wednesdays (subject to change). Other listings give weekday 8:00-18:00 and Sun/holiday 9:00-17:00; hours vary by source.
places.neighborhood: Omicho Market (Kami-Omicho), near Musashigatsuji / Meitetsu
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Sake no Ozawa has stood inside Omicho Market - long called the kitchen of Kanazawa - for more than a hundred years, and it is the only liquor merchant within the market. Generations of local cooks, fishmongers and residents have relied on it, giving rise to the local saying that

roles: the only liquor shop in Omicho Market
chef_bio.background: Sake no Ozawa has stood inside Omicho Market - long called the kitchen of Kanazawa - for more than a hundred years, and it is the only liquor merchant within the market. Generations of local cooks, fishmongers and residents have relied on it, giving rise to the local saying that when it comes to regional sake, you think of Sake no Ozawa.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop describes itself as having been born and raised within the market, growing through the support of its community and remaining devoted to serving its customers.
chef_bio.specialty: Roughly 500 varieties of sake centered on Ishikawa jizake (local sake), rounded out with selections from neighbouring Fukui and Toyama, plus seasonal regional recommendations. A small standing-drink (kakuuchi) counter lets locals sample a pour on the spot.

anecdotes.text: The shop is the sole liquor merchant in Omicho Market and has operated there for over a century, earning the reputation that for local sake, it's Sake no Ozawa.
It stocks about 500 varieties, primarily Ishikawa local sake alongside Fukui and Toyama, with seasonal recommendations.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional neighborhood sake shop inside the bustling market where the staff speak Japanese; a foreign student comfortable pointing and using basic Japanese can enjoy a casual standing drink, but little English is likely.

places.name: 岡久 (Okahisa)
places.gf_detail: A traditional yakiniku/hormon house where grilled meats and offal are served with wheat-based soy and tare marinades; there is no documented gluten-free handling and the kitchen is not celiac-safe.
places.vegan_detail: The menu is built almost entirely around beef, pork, offal (hormon), and horse-meat sashimi, with no documented vegan or vegetarian dishes.
places.hours_raw: 18:00–00:00 (Tuesday closed)
places.neighborhood: Nomachi / Nagamachi (toward Katamachi; Hokutetsu Nomachi Stn / south of Kōrinbō area)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Okahisa is a long-running neighborhood grill tucked into the streets between Nomachi and Nagamachi, south of the Korinbo/Katamachi area. It is run by an older married couple, and the small, well-worn room carries a retro Showa-era feel — the kind of smoky, lived-in yakiniku house

chef_bio.background: Okahisa is a long-running neighborhood grill tucked into the streets between Nomachi and Nagamachi, south of the Korinbo/Katamachi area. It is run by an older married couple, and the small, well-worn room carries a retro Showa-era feel — the kind of smoky, lived-in yakiniku house where regulars keep coming back.
chef_bio.specialty: Carefully curated grilled meats and offal (hormon): beef heart, beef tongue, beef ribs, liver, pork cheek and pork soft-bone (nankotsu), plus horse-meat sashimi and yukhoe.

anecdotes.text: The couple, said to be in their sixties, run the place themselves at a counter-centered room of about 14 seats, drawing a loyal crowd of regulars who come for good meat at an approachable price.
Diners describe it simply as the go-to spot when you are craving good meat, with the dishes praised as uniformly delicious.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Yakiniku/hormon grill where meats are coated in wheat-based soy and tare sauces and cooked on shared grills; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard wheat-containing soy sauce and tare marinades are typical for this style of grill; no wheat-free tare is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Effectively an all-meat and offal menu cooked on shared grills, with no vegan items documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, smoky old-school grill run by an elderly couple where some basic Japanese (or a translation app) and an easygoing attitude will help a foreign student feel welcome.

gf.note: Not applicable; this establishment does not serve ramen.

vegan.note: Not applicable; this establishment does not serve ramen.

ramen.pantheon: This is a charcoal-grill house for beef and offal, not a noodle shop, so it has no place in the ramen world. A homey, old-school counter spot built around grilled cuts and offal rather than a bowl of soup.

places.highlight: This is a charcoal-grill house for beef and offal, not a noodle shop, so it has no place in the ramen world. A homey, old-school counter spot built around grilled cuts and offal rather than a bowl of soup.

places.name: 焼肉ホルモンくろまる (Yakiniku Horumon Kuromaru)
places.gf_detail: This is a charcoal/grill yakiniku and offal house whose flavor centers on house-made tare (marinade/dipping sauce), which in Japanese grill houses is almost always soy-sauce-based and therefore contains wheat. Marinated meats, shared grills, and sauces make this unsuitable for celiacs; no gluten-free menu, dedicated equipment, or allergen handling is documented. Treat as not celiac-safe.
places.vegan_detail: A meat-focused restaurant specializing in beef and pork offal (horumon) and grilled meat. The only notably plant-based item documented is the complimentary mound of cabbage (お通しの山盛りキャベツ), but everything is cooked around and with meat on shared grills. There is no vegan menu and the concept is fundamentally non-vegan.
places.hours_raw: Mon-Sat, holidays & day-before-holiday: 18:00-24:00 (Fri/Sat L.O. 23:30). Sunday: 16:30-22:00 (L.O. 21:30).
places.neighborhood: Near Kanazawa Stn (Horikawa-machi / Nanatsuya)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A back-alley grill house tucked into the streets just a few minutes' walk from Kanazawa Station's east exit, Kuromaru bills itself as a 'meishten in an alleyway' (路地裏に佇む名店) built around fresh, carefully sourced offal and grilled meat sold at friendly prices.

chef_bio.origin: Kanazawa (堀川町, near Kanazawa / Hokutetsu-Kanazawa / Nanatsuya stations)
chef_bio.background: A back-alley grill house tucked into the streets just a few minutes' walk from Kanazawa Station's east exit, Kuromaru bills itself as a 'meishten in an alleyway' (路地裏に佇む名店) built around fresh, carefully sourced offal and grilled meat sold at friendly prices.
chef_bio.philosophy: Fresh, well-prepped offal and select cuts at accessible prices, with house-made sauces as the signature.
chef_bio.specialty: House specialties center on offal: the 'white mix' and 'red mix' pork plates (トントロ, ほっぺ, ガツ) at around 748 yen, and コプチャン (beef small intestine, ~825 yen) described as プリプリで濃厚な甘み — springy with a rich sweetness. Domestic-beef horumon is given thorough prep so it carries no off-smell, finished with the house's secret tare.

anecdotes.text: The complimentary starter is a heaping mound of cabbage (お通しの山盛りキャベツ) that's a hit even with women diners, and the mix-horumon plates are the house's calling card.
Described as a name-brand spot hidden in an alley a 3-minute walk from Kanazawa Station's east exit.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Yakiniku/horumon served on shared grills with soy-based house tare; no gluten-free accommodation or allergen handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The restaurant's signature is its house-made tare (秘伝の特製ダレ), which in Japanese grill houses is virtually always soy-sauce-based and thus contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: An all-meat grill concept; the only plant item noted is a complimentary cabbage starter, and all cooking happens on grills shared with offal and meat.

cultural_comfort.note: A lively, locally-loved grill-it-yourself horumon shop with counter seats for solo diners, but the menu and signage are in Japanese and no English support is documented, so some basic Japanese or a translation app will help a foreign student a lot.

noodles.notes: Not a ramen shop. The only noodle dishes are Korean-style cold noodles (naengmyeon: white/red/special cold noodles) served as a palate-cleansing side to grilled meat, not a ramen bowl.

gf.note: Not a ramen establishment. The cold noodles served are Korean-style wheat/buckwheat naengmyeon, not gluten-free, and no GF ramen option exists.

vegan.note: This is a grilled-meat and offal restaurant; no plant-based ramen or broth is offered.

ramen.pantheon: This is a charcoal-grilled offal and yakiniku house, not a ramen shop, so it sits outside the ramen world entirely. Its kitchen is built around house-marinated horumon and tongue with a mountain of complimentary cabbage, with only Korean-style cold noodles as a finishing side rather than any bowl of ramen.

places.highlight: This is a charcoal-grilled offal and yakiniku house, not a ramen shop, so it sits outside the ramen world entirely. Its kitchen is built around house-marinated horumon and tongue with a mountain of complimentary cabbage, with only Korean-style cold noodles as a finishing side rather than any bowl of ramen.

places.name: 立ち飲み餃子 大笑 (Tachinomi Gyoza Daisho)
places.gf_detail: The whole concept is gyoza (wheat-flour dumpling wrappers) plus Chinese small plates and Chinese-style oden, almost certainly cooked with wheat-based soy sauce. There is no gluten-free menu and the kitchen is built around wheat — not safe for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: A meat-and-drink standing bar: gyoza, stamina gyoza, Chinese oden and izakaya snacks are the draw, typically pork/meat-filled and made with soy/meat-based seasonings. No vegan menu is documented; not a fit for vegans.
places.hours_raw: 17:00–24:00 (Mon–Thu, Sun); 17:00–01:00 (Fri, Sat, and days before holidays). Open daily. Per official group site.
places.neighborhood: Honmachi, near Kanazawa Station / Hokutetsu-Kanazawa Station
places.cuisine: Gyoza standing bar / casual Chinese izakaya
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A friendly standing-bar gyoza joint a short walk from Kanazawa Station, in the Honmachi neighborhood. It belongs to a homegrown Kanazawa company, Tachinomiya Co., which began in 2009 with a skewer bar called Kushi Sakaba Daisho; the founder fell in love with Osaka's standing-bar

chef_bio.origin: Kanazawa (Honmachi), part of the locally founded Tachinomiya / Daisho family of standing bars
chef_bio.background: A friendly standing-bar gyoza joint a short walk from Kanazawa Station, in the Honmachi neighborhood. It belongs to a homegrown Kanazawa company, Tachinomiya Co., which began in 2009 with a skewer bar called Kushi Sakaba Daisho; the founder fell in love with Osaka's standing-bar culture during an apprenticeship there and brought that easygoing, shoulder-to-shoulder spirit back home. The gyoza shop is one of several 'Daisho' standing bars the group runs around the city.
chef_bio.philosophy: The parent company sums up its outlook as keeping smiles alive on both sides of the noren — a town where everyone can smile equally — and sees standing-bar culture as a way to connect customers, producers, the region and staff. The shop's own catchphrase plays on this: 'gyoza and beer are Japanese culture.'
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-made gyoza is the headliner, with several varieties on offer (including a house Daisho gyoza, coriander/pakuchi gyoza and a stamina gyoza), alongside soup dumplings, simple izakaya snacks and a Chinese-style oden. Reviewers praise the dumplings for a crisp exterior and juicy filling.

anecdotes.text: The venue is described as an at-home standing-bar setting where hand-made dumplings and small plates come paired with the owner's warmth and hospitality — welcoming even for a quick solo drink and a plate of gyoza.
Within the group's lineup, the gyoza shop is cast as the one embodying 'the charm of a father's human warmth.'

gf_cross_contamination.text: A gyoza-focused kitchen working constantly with wheat-flour wrappers; flour and wheat-based sauces are pervasive, so cross-contamination is effectively unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No specific information published; as a Chinese-style izakaya, standard wheat-containing Japanese/Chinese soy sauce should be assumed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Meat-centric menu (meat-filled gyoza, stamina gyoza, Chinese oden); no vegan options documented and shared cooking surfaces make plant-only dining impractical.

cultural_comfort.note: A warm, casual standing bar where a friendly greeting and pointing at the gyoza menu will carry you; little to no English, so a few Japanese words go a long way.

places.name: 第六ギョーザの店 (Dairoku Gyoza no Mise)
places.gf_detail: This is a gyoza and town-Chinese izakaya: gyoza wrappers are wheat, and dishes lean on soy sauce and dashi. There is no gluten-free menu or documented accommodation, so it is not safe for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: The menu centers on meat/vegetable gyoza, cream croquettes, beef-tendon stew, sashimi, and Kanazawa oden — all involving meat, seafood, and animal- or fish-based dashi. No documented vegan options.
places.hours_raw: 17:00-23:30 (L.O. 23:00), Monday-Saturday; closed Sundays and year-end/New Year holidays.
places.neighborhood: Nomachi / Kanda (south of central Kanazawa, south from Katamachi / Kōrinbō)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] One of Kanazawa's beloved 'numbered gyoza' shops, 第六ギョーザの店 sits quietly in a residential pocket near Nomachi and is run as a snug, counter-only family operation — the proprietor working alongside his daughter and her husband. The numbered gyoza shops are part of a local Kanazawa

chef_bio.background: One of Kanazawa's beloved 'numbered gyoza' shops, 第六ギョーザの店 sits quietly in a residential pocket near Nomachi and is run as a snug, counter-only family operation — the proprietor working alongside his daughter and her husband. The numbered gyoza shops are part of a local Kanazawa tradition said to trace back to a postwar gyoza street stall.
chef_bio.specialty: Pan-fried gyoza — both the regular slightly-small, crisp-bottomed dumplings and a refreshing shiso (perilla) variety — alongside cream croquettes, beef-tendon stew, and affordable Kanazawa oden.

anecdotes.text: A solitary, compact shop tucked into a residential neighborhood, run by the proprietor with his daughter and her husband; the younger man handles the wrapping and cooking, and the shiso gyoza in particular won over visitors with its fresh aroma.
A long-time regular noted the place keeps its warm, at-home hospitality even after 15 years.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Gyoza wrappers are wheat-based and prepared in a shared kitchen; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes and dipping rely on standard soy sauce (wheat-containing) and dashi typical of town-Chinese/izakaya cooking.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is built around meat, seafood, and animal/fish dashi; no vegan preparation or separation is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, homey family-run counter shop in a residential neighborhood — friendly but Japanese-only is likely, so a few words of Japanese (or a translation app) will make ordering smoother for a foreign student.

places.name: ホルモンゆうちゃん (Horumon Yu-chan)
places.gf_detail: This is a grilled-offal and yakiniku restaurant. Yakiniku dipping sauces (tare) and marinades are soy-sauce based and contain wheat, and braised/seasoned offal dishes use the same. There is no documented gluten-free menu, no dedicated fryer, and grilling is done on a shared gas grill. Not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: The menu is built around grilled beef offal (hormone), wagyu cuts, pork, and fresh seafood. There are no documented vegan or vegetarian dishes; even side items would be cooked on shared meat surfaces. Not a vegan destination.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Sat & holidays: 19:00–05:00 next day (food L.O. 04:00, drinks L.O. 04:30); closed Sundays. (Hot Pepper lists 18:00 open; Tabelog lists 19:00.)
places.neighborhood: Katamachi / Nomachi (~12 min walk from Hokutetsu Nomachi Stn; south of Katamachi / Kōrinbō)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A long-running, family/individually operated hormone (offal) yakiniku spot in Kanazawa's Katamachi entertainment district, just south of Korinbo and a short walk from Hokutetsu Nomachi station. It has been grilling in the neighborhood for over 20 years, with a cozy, home-like Sho

chef_bio.background: A long-running, family/individually operated hormone (offal) yakiniku spot in Kanazawa's Katamachi entertainment district, just south of Korinbo and a short walk from Hokutetsu Nomachi station. It has been grilling in the neighborhood for over 20 years, with a cozy, home-like Showa-retro room of a few counter seats and a small tatami corner.
chef_bio.philosophy: The shop emphasizes that how the offal is cut affects its quality, so each order is hand-cut fresh on the spot to maximize flavor and texture.
chef_bio.specialty: Beef offal (hormone) and wagyu cuts grilled at the table, with offal cut by hand to order so it arrives at its freshest. Dishes mentioned include wagyu harami (skirt), pork belly, and braised tongue (tan nikomi); fresh Kanazawa seafood is also served alongside the meat.

anecdotes.text: Described as an established yakiniku shop carrying on for more than 20 years in Katamachi, where offal is hand-cut after you order for exceptional freshness.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All meat and offal is grilled on a shared tabletop gas grill, and yakiniku tare/marinades are soy-sauce based (wheat-containing). No gluten-free menu is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Yakiniku dipping sauces and braised offal seasonings are soy-sauce based and contain wheat; no wheat-free sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is meat-, offal-, and seafood-focused with grilling on shared surfaces; no separate vegan preparation is documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny, late-night neighborhood offal-grill run by locals with a Showa-retro feel; staff likely speak little English, so basic Japanese and a relaxed attitude help, and offal/meat dishes mean it is not suited to vegan or strictly gluten-free diners.

noodles.notes: No ramen is served. This is a charcoal-grilled offal (horumon yakiniku) and yakiniku restaurant; no noodle dish is documented on its menu.

gf.note: Not a ramen shop; no noodle dish exists to assess. As a grilled-offal yakiniku spot, tare and marinades typically contain wheat-based soy sauce.

vegan.note: Not a ramen shop. The menu centers on grilled offal, meat, and fresh seafood; no plant-based bowl is documented.

ramen.pantheon: This is not a ramen establishment at all but a long-running, late-night charcoal-grilled offal counter in Kanazawa's Katamachi nightlife quarter, where the cuts are sliced to order and paired with fresh local seafood. There is no bowl to place in the ramen world.

places.highlight: This is not a ramen establishment at all but a long-running, late-night charcoal-grilled offal counter in Kanazawa's Katamachi nightlife quarter, where the cuts are sliced to order and paired with fresh local seafood. There is no bowl to place in the ramen world.

places.name: かわら亭 (Kawaratei)
places.gf_detail: This is a grilled-meat and offal joint where dishes are seasoned with wheat-based soy/tare and served alongside dipping sauces, so it is not safe for celiacs. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
places.vegan_detail: The entire concept is hormone (offal) and beef yakiniku; there are no documented plant-based meals beyond incidental sides like kimchi. Not a viable option for vegans.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Sat 18:30–00:00 / closed Sundays
places.neighborhood: 片町 (Katamachi)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, locally loved hormone (offal) and yakiniku counter tucked into Kanazawa's Katamachi nightlife district, with just 15 seats including a row of counter stools and a small raised tatami nook. It is the kind of intimate, owner-run grill that draws regulars for late-night eat

chef_bio.background: A small, locally loved hormone (offal) and yakiniku counter tucked into Kanazawa's Katamachi nightlife district, with just 15 seats including a row of counter stools and a small raised tatami nook. It is the kind of intimate, owner-run grill that draws regulars for late-night eating and drinking.
chef_bio.specialty: Charcoal-style grilled offal and beef cuts: documented menu items include salted upper tongue (上タン塩), wagyu ribeye, outside skirt (ハラミ系), and side dishes such as kimchi natto.

cultural_comfort.note: A tiny 15-seat late-night counter grill where almost no English is spoken, so some Japanese (or a translation app) and comfort with ordering offal yakiniku will help a foreign student feel at ease.

noodles.notes: Not applicable. This establishment is a yakiniku/horumon (grilled meat and offal) restaurant, not a ramen shop. No ramen noodles are documented.

gf.note: Not a ramen shop, so no ramen GF assessment applies. As a tabletop grill specializing in offal and yakiniku, marinades and dipping sauces typically contain soy/wheat; ask staff directly about any dietary needs.

vegan.note: Not a ramen shop. This is a grilled-meat and offal restaurant; the menu is built around animal proteins and is not suited to a vegan diet.

ramen.pantheon: This is not a ramen establishment but a Katamachi tabletop grill devoted to yakiniku and horumon (offal), where the draw is fresh-grilled meat over fire rather than a bowl of soup. There is no ramen lineage to place here.

places.highlight: This is not a ramen establishment but a Katamachi tabletop grill devoted to yakiniku and horumon (offal), where the draw is fresh-grilled meat over fire rather than a bowl of soup. There is no ramen lineage to place here.

places.name: 手打ちそば処 くら (Teuchi Soba-dokoro Kura)
places.gf_detail: A hand-cut soba house — but not celiac-safe by default. Soba is usually cut nihachi-style with ~20% wheat flour, the dipping tsuyu is built on wheat soy sauce, and udon (pure wheat) is made in the same kitchen, so cross-contact is likely. Ask directly before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: The soba tsuyu and broths use katsuo/fish dashi, and the signature bowl is duck (kamo). A plain cold seiro soba might be possible but the dipping sauce is fish-based — confirm with staff.
places.hours_raw: Mon 11:00–15:00 (no dinner); Tue closed; Wed–Sun 11:00–15:00 & 17:30–20:00. Closes early once the day’s soba sells out.
places.neighborhood: 下新町 (Shimoshinmachi) — east of Omichō Market, near the Izumi Kyōka Memorial Hall
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A quiet hand-pulled soba shop on a back street in the historic Shimoshinmachi district, east of Omichō — a locals’ favorite known among those in the know, balancing serious craft with everyday prices.

chef_bio.background: A hand-pulled soba shop tucked down a quiet back street in Shimoshinmachi, one of Kanazawa’s historic neighborhoods near the Izumi Kyōka Memorial Hall, a short walk east of Omichō Market. The buckwheat is milled in-house from whole grain (genshu) sourced in Fukui and Hokkaidō.
chef_bio.philosophy: Serious craft at an everyday price — the kind of unshowy neighborhood soba-ya that locals quietly champion.
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-cut soba with a firm chew and pronounced buckwheat aroma; the signature bowl is kamo-nanban (warm soba with duck and leek). Udon is also on the menu.

anecdotes.text: The shop mills its own buckwheat from whole grain grown in Fukui and Hokkaidō, and is known for closing early once the day’s soba runs out.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat-flour udon is made in the same kitchen and soba is typically cut with some wheat, so gluten cross-contact is likely.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soba tsuyu / dipping sauce is based on wheat-containing soy sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths and tsuyu use fish (katsuo) dashi; the signature bowl is duck.

cultural_comfort.note: A quiet neighborhood soba-ya loved by locals; a little Japanese (or pointing at the kamo-nanban) and an arrive-early-before-soba-sells-out mindset will serve you well.

places.name: Conconto Kashiten (コンコント菓子店)
places.gf_detail: A fully plant-based patisserie and cafe that makes every item from rice flour, beans and other plant ingredients — no wheat is used or baked anywhere on site. The shop states 「全商品、卵や乳製品、小麦粉、そば、落花生など食物アレルギー物質の特定原材料7品目は使わず、米や豆など植物性の食材のみでつくる」 (all products use none of the 7 major allergens — egg, dairy, wheat flour, soba, peanut, etc. — only plant-based ingredients like rice and beans). Because wheat, buckwheat and peanuts are never present in the kitchen, cross-contact risk is very low; still worth confirming with staff on the day for the most sensitive celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: Everything is vegan by design — the shop describes itself as making sweets 「米粉と植物性食材のみでつくる」 (only from rice flour and plant-based ingredients). Dairy is replaced with soy-milk-based ice cream and custard; eggs are not used at all. Whole menu is safe for vegans.
places.hours_raw: 10:30–18:00; closed Wednesdays, Tuesdays irregular (parfaits from ~12:30)
places.neighborhood: 横川 (Yokogawa), Kanazawa
places.notes: [GF-dedicated] A rare 100% wheat-free patisserie in Kanazawa: all cakes, tarts, Mont Blanc and parfaits are built from rice flour and beans, with no wheat, buckwheat or peanut anywhere in the kitchen. This is one of the safest cafe stops in the region for celiacs, and it doubles as a plant-based cafe (soy-milk ice cream, custard). Sit-in seating (~8 seats) means you can enjoy a parfait or cake with Kaga bōcha tea on the spot.

chef_bio.background: Yamazaki worked in Western pastry manufacturing and production management before joining his family's Yamanaka-lacquerware wholesale business. In 2014 he launched the project unit 'Conconto' to promote traditional Ishikawa crafts, and the patisserie opened in May 2017 as a food-culture arm of that project. The shop occupies a converted 1960s fiber-machinery building near the Yokogawa intersection in southern Kanazawa. His stated aim is to 'convey the food culture handed down through generations by making authentic Western sweets from the rice and beans Japanese have eaten since ancient times.'
chef_bio.philosophy: Make authentic Western-style confectionery entirely from rice, beans and other plant ingredients — kind to allergy sufferers and the body alike, while minimizing food additives.
chef_bio.specialty: Rice-flour and bean-based cakes, tarts, Mont Blanc and seasonal parfaits (all vegan and free of the 7 major allergens).

anecdotes.text: The pastry shop grew out of Yamazaki's 'Conconto' craft-promotion project (est. 2014), tying Ishikawa's lacquerware heritage to a new food venture.
From 2025 the shop began incorporating kōji (米麹) fermentation techniques into its sweets.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 金沢経済新聞 and まんぷく金沢 confirm the shop is run by 山崎弘二, opened May 2017 in 横川, and makes all products from rice flour and plant ingredients with none of the 7 major allergens (卵・乳・小麦・そば・落花生 etc.).

gf_cross_contamination.text: 全商品、卵や乳製品、小麦粉、そば、落花生など食物アレルギー物質の特定原材料7品目は使わず、米や豆など植物性の食材のみでつくる — no wheat, soba or peanut is present anywhere in production, so cross-contact risk is minimal.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dessert-only patisserie; no soy sauce used in the sweets menu.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Entirely plant-based kitchen — dairy replaced by soy-milk ice cream and custard, no eggs used, so vegan cross-contact is not a concern.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Shop is explicitly built around allergy safety, making items suitable for people with allergies as well as those without.

positives.text: 100% wheat-free, buckwheat-free and peanut-free dedicated plant-based patisserie with sit-in cafe — a standout safe option in Kanazawa.

places.name: Kamuna (しぜんおやつ工房カムナ)
places.gf_detail: A tiny container-house 'natural sweets' workshop making 米粉・もち粉 (rice-flour/mochi-flour) treats like the signature カムナどらやき (fermented-anko dorayaki). Most days are wheat-free, but LOWERED from options because the owner intentionally uses wheat: her site states 「普段小麦粉は使用いたしませんが」 and 「グルテンフリー（日曜日はスペルト小麦使用）」— 「日曜日のみ不定期で『スペルト小麦（古代小麦）』を使用した『小麦に感謝の日』を開催いたします」(on some Sundays she holds a 'day of gratitude to wheat' using spelt). Spelt IS wheat/gluten and is baked in the same small shared kitchen, so cross-contact is a real risk and no wheat-free-kitchen claim or protocol is published. The shop's 'グルテンフリー' wording is a health-lifestyle framing, not a celiac guarantee — ask directly about same-day/same-equipment wheat handling before eating.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan. Products rely on egg for leavening — 「卵の力で膨らませられるようレシピを作りました」(uses Noto/Ishikawa cage-free eggs) — and use butter: 「乳製品はバターのみ使用」. No vegan line is offered, though white sugar is often omitted (「お砂糖不使用です！」on some items).
places.hours_raw: Tue/Thu/Sat 11:00–18:00; Sunday irregular ~9:00–13:00 (often the spelt 'wheat gratitude day' — avoid for celiacs); closed Mon/Wed/Fri plus irregular closures. Only ~3 days/week — check Instagram @kamuna357 / LINE calendar before visiting.
places.neighborhood: 直江南 (Naoe-minami), Kanazawa
places.notes: [GF] Rice-flour natural-sweets shop, NOT celiac-safe as-is: the owner deliberately bakes spelt (古代小麦) on some Sundays in the same tiny kitchen and publishes no cross-contact controls. Products also contain egg and butter (not vegan). If visiting, avoid Sundays, and message @kamuna357 or LINE beforehand to confirm the day's items are wheat-free and how equipment is handled. Open only ~3 days/week; check the daily calendar. Phone 070-8948-3496.

chef_bio.background: The owner is a former nurse (元看護師) and mother of three who started Kamuna during the pandemic (opened Jan 11, 2022) from a preventive-medicine motivation — wanting healthy snacks she would feed her own children, priced so kids can buy them with pocket money (「身体にいいものは高いから…とならないよう」). She cites rising childhood allergies and illness (「子供たちのアレルギー・精神疾患・癌などはどんどん増えています」) as a driver. She uses Ishikawa pesticide-free rice flour (無農薬米粉), mochi flour, Noto/Ishikawa cage-free eggs, reduced/no white sugar and no additives.
chef_bio.philosophy: Affordable, additive-free, mostly rice-flour 'natural sweets' for children's health — while keeping 'a relationship with wheat as an occasional indulgence' (「時々食べる嗜好品として小麦とお付き合いしていきます」).
chef_bio.specialty: カムナどらやき — fermented-anko dorayaki with rice-flour/shiratamako skin; sesame-paste brownies, sablé butter sandwiches and rice-flour tarts.

anecdotes.text: The shop name 'Kamuna' combines カタツムリ (snail, a happiness symbol) with 直江 (Naoe, the neighborhood); a snail logo is painted on the small container-house shop.
Owner is a former nurse who launched the shop so healthy snacks would be cheap enough for kids to buy with pocket money.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: manpuku and kaname-kanazawa cover the former-nurse owner, preventive-medicine motivation, snail/Naoe name origin and pesticide-free rice-flour sourcing; the shop's own Wix site states the spelt-Sunday 'wheat gratitude day' and egg/butter usage.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Owner intentionally bakes spelt wheat (スペルト小麦/古代小麦) on some Sundays in the same small kitchen: 「日曜日のみ不定期で『スペルト小麦（古代小麦）』を使用した『小麦に感謝の日』を開催いたします」. Spelt is wheat/gluten; shared-equipment cross-contact is a real, uncontrolled risk.
No wheat-free-kitchen claim, no 'may contain' warning and no cross-contact protocol are published. Absence of a warning is not evidence of safety.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Sweets shop; no soy sauce noted. Other allergens present: egg, butter (dairy), sesame, almond, cocoa, matcha.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Not vegan — egg is used for leavening and butter for dairy; no plant-based separation.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No published allergy-handling policy; owner reachable via Instagram @kamuna357 / LINE for questions.

positives.text: Most days' products are rice-flour/mochi-flour based (Ishikawa 無農薬米粉), additive-free with reduced/no white sugar.
Owner is health-focused (former nurse) and transparent about when wheat is used.

places.name: Komekura Coffee (こめ倉珈琲)
places.gf_detail: A multi-purpose cafe run by the ~120-year-old Kurayama rice merchant in Hakusan City (just outside Kanazawa). Its flagship rice waffle is marketed as 「米粉を100%使用した完全グルテンフリースイーツ」(a completely gluten-free sweet using 100% rice flour), made with 白山市産米粉100% (100% Hakusan-grown rice flour). However this is a general cafe with heavy wheat use — four wheat pastas, hot sandwiches, bread/chili dog, and hayashi-rice roux (conventionally wheat-thickened). No allergen protocol, dedicated GF prep area or cross-contact statement is published anywhere, so the '完全グルテンフリー' claim refers to the waffle recipe, not a controlled process. Treat the venue as high cross-contamination risk and confirm handling with the shop before a celiac eats.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan program or labeling. The flagship rice waffle is served with butter and honey; chocolate-banana waffle and cheesecake contain dairy. A few onigiri (塩 salt, 梅 umeboshi, 昆布 kombu) are incidentally plant-based but carry no allergen/ingredient guarantee (nori/seasonings unverified); the miso-soup set likely uses fish dashi.
places.hours_raw: 9:30–17:00, Mon–Sat; closed Sundays and public holidays. Onigiri available from ~10:30.
places.neighborhood: 横町 (Yokocho), Hakusan City — adjacent to Kanazawa
places.cuisine: Rice-merchant cafe — onigiri, rice-flour waffles, coffee (also pasta/bread)
places.notes: [GF] The 100% rice-flour waffle is a genuine GF-recipe treat and the reason to come, but this is a wheat-heavy kitchen (pasta, hot sandwiches, bread, hayashi roux) with no published cross-contact controls. For a diagnosed celiac, phone ahead (076-259-1100) to confirm how the waffle is prepared and whether utensils/griddle are shared. Do not assume the venue itself is gluten-free; avoid the hayashi rice (roux is wheat-based). ~5–6 min walk from Mattō Station; 13-car parking.

chef_bio.background: Komekura Coffee is operated by 倉山米穀 (Kurayama Beikoku), a long-established (~120-year) family rice merchant. The cafe is framed as 「お米屋さんが提案する、魅力あふれる多目的カフェ」(an appealing multi-purpose cafe proposed by a rice merchant). It employs 石川県唯一の五ツ星お米マイスター — Ishikawa Prefecture's only Five-Star Rice Meister — whose expertise drives ingredient and menu selection. The shop is certified under Hakusan City's chisan-chisho (local-production) program.
chef_bio.philosophy: Showcase premium local rice: primarily Koshihikari (switching to new-crop/seasonal varieties), with self-made onigiri as the signature and 白山市産米粉100% sweets. Committed to core ingredients 'rice and bread'.
chef_bio.specialty: Hand-made onigiri (~6 kinds) and 100% Hakusan rice-flour waffles; premium rice and goods sold downstairs.

anecdotes.text: Two-floor wood-and-steel cafe with a 2nd-floor billiards/carom table (cues on display), floor-to-ceiling windows, barrier-free with elevator, Wi-Fi and power outlets; downstairs sells premium rice plus Volkswagen-themed merchandise.
Menu selection is guided by the prefecture's only Five-Star Rice Meister employed by the shop.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: weekend-kanazawa establishes the 120-year rice-merchant heritage, five-star meister and 米粉100% completely-gluten-free waffle; Hakusan City government confirms 白山市産米粉100%; kanazawabiyori and ishikabakun cover the multi-purpose-cafe concept, Koshihikari/onigiri focus and the billiards-table interior.

gf_cross_contamination.text: General cafe with heavy wheat use — 4 wheat pastas, hot sandwiches, bread and a chili dog — sharing the same kitchen as the rice waffle; no dedicated GF prep area or utensil separation is published.
No allergen or cross-contact statement appears in any source; the '完全グルテンフリー' label describes the waffle recipe only.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Hayashi rice roux conventionally contains wheat flour — treat as NOT gluten-free despite being served over rice. Onigiri/miso-set seasonings and dashi are unverified for wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Not vegan; butter, honey and dairy used across sweets. Any plant-based onigiri share a kitchen with meat fillings and wheat items.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No published allergy-handling policy; verify by phone (076-259-1100).

positives.text: Flagship waffle uses 白山市産米粉100% and is marketed as 「米粉を100%使用した完全グルテンフリースイーツ」.
Hakusan City government confirms the sweets use 100% Hakusan-grown rice flour (chisan-chisho certified).

places.name: Meringue (お好み焼き 鉄板バル メレンゲ)
places.gf_detail: Novelty okonomiyaki bar whose batter uses beaten egg-white meringue instead of flour; the shop states 「小麦粉を一切使わず、メレンゲなどを使ったオリジナルの生地」 and reviewers confirm 「生地にメレンゲ使ってて小麦粉不使用でグルテンフリー！」 — the pancake batter itself is genuinely wheat-flour-free. Two open questions keep this at ask: (1) the finishing okonomiyaki ソース/オリジナルソース is not documented, and commercial okonomiyaki/tonkatsu-style sauce almost always contains wheat and soy sauce, so the sauce's celiac-safety is unverified; (2) it is a 鉄板バル that also cooks やきそば (wheat noodles) and offers a 「金澤野菜のサラダそば」 (soba, normally wheat-blended) on the same teppan, so shared-griddle cross-contact is real. Also note: Tabelog now tags the shop 【移転】 (relocated) and the official domain was unreachable at last check — verify the current location and sauce before visiting.
places.vegan_detail: The signature is an egg-white-meringue okonomiyaki topped with 能登豚 (Noto pork), seafood (えび/かき) and dashi; the batter is egg-based by definition. No vegan offering.
places.hours_raw: Approx. 11:30–14:30 and 17:30–21:00 (Tue 11:30–14:30; Sat 11:30–21:00; Sun/holidays 11:30–17:00); closed Wednesdays. Hours vary by day — shop advises checking Instagram (@meringue_1204). Older Tabelog listing shows 11:00–16:00.
places.neighborhood: 下堤町 (Shimotsutsumi-cho, near Omicho Market, Kanazawa)
places.notes: [GF] The okonomiyaki batter is genuinely flourless (meringue, 小麦粉不使用), a rare celiac-friendly twist. But the finishing sauce is undocumented (okonomiyaki sauce usually contains wheat), the same teppan cooks やきそば/そば, and Tabelog flags the shop as relocated. Confirm current address, ask for the sauce ingredients (or bring/skip sauce), and request a clean griddle spot away from wheat noodles.

chef_bio.background: お好み焼き 鉄板バル メレンゲ opened on 4 December 2020 near the entrance to Omicho Market at 下堤町34-2, running as an okonomiyaki lunch spot by day and a 鉄板バル by night in an all-counter room. Its hook is a 'new-sensation' okonomiyaki that swaps wheat flour for whipped egg-white meringue, plated with Noto-brand pork and Kaga vegetables bought each morning at Omicho. The shop has since been tagged as relocated on Tabelog (reportedly toward the Katamachi area), so its current base should be confirmed.
chef_bio.philosophy: Flourless, additive-free 'fluffy' okonomiyaki using local Ishikawa produce.
chef_bio.specialty: Meringue okonomiyaki (ぶた玉/えび玉/かき玉); teppan bar plates

anecdotes.text: The batter is whipped into a meringue so the okonomiyaki comes out unusually light and fluffy — reviewers describe it as 小麦粉不使用でグルテンフリー.
Daytime is an okonomiyaki shop; at night it flips to a teppan bar where all daytime drinks (including beer) are ¥300.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: weekend-kanazawa.com and kanazawadays.com confirm a meringue-based, 小麦粉不使用 okonomiyaki (ぶた玉/えび玉 ¥1,000, かき玉 ¥1,200, dessert set +¥350) and that a set includes 本格蕎麦 (real soba); tacsp.net notes やきそば on the teppan and cheap lunchtime drinks; Tabelog lists the shop as 【移転】 relocated with the former address 下堤町34-2.

gf_cross_contamination.text: It is a 鉄板バル cooking やきそば (wheat noodles) and a 「金澤野菜のサラダそば」 (soba, normally wheat-blended) on the same teppan as the flourless okonomiyaki — shared-griddle cross-contact is likely. All-counter room; no clean-surface protocol published.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The finishing okonomiyaki ソース/オリジナルソース ingredients are undocumented. Standard okonomiyaki sauce contains wheat and soy sauce; no tamari/GF sauce is confirmed, so sauced okonomiyaki cannot be assumed gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Egg-white-meringue batter plus pork/seafood — not vegan.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Shop markets the okonomiyaki as グルテンフリー based on the flourless batter, but no allergen/sauce/cross-contact statement is published; Tabelog flags the venue as relocated and the official domain was unreachable at last check.

positives.text: Genuinely flourless okonomiyaki — batter uses egg-white meringue instead of wheat flour (小麦粉不使用), a rare celiac-friendly base.

places.name: Miron Rice-Flour Bakery (ふぁすとふーど＆お米パン miron)
places.gf_detail: A rice-flour (米粉) bakery, sweets and bento shop where 「パンはすべてグルテンフリー」 (all bread is gluten-free) — no wheat flour is used on the premises, per Japan's national all-GF bakery guide (glutenfree.empacede.co.jp), which lists it as a wheat-free bakery. Products are built on 特別栽培米コシヒカリ (specially cultivated Koshihikari rice), 国産米粉, rice oil, organic soy milk and unrefined sugar. The kitchen is wheat-free, so cross-contact risk is low; because the shop also prepares cooked bento (hamburger, karaage, etc.), the most sensitive celiacs should confirm that a specific bento item is fully rice-based before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: Most breads are egg-free, and there are several vegan (dairy-free) breads plus items free of all 28 major/minor allergens; sweets use organic soy milk rather than dairy. However the shop also sells bento with fish and meat (grilled salmon, stewed hamburger, fried chicken), so it is not fully vegan — pick the labelled vegan breads/sweets.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–17:00 (until sold out); closed Sundays, Mondays & public holidays
places.neighborhood: 津幡町 (Tsubata-cho), near JR Naka-Tsubata Station, Kahoku-gun, Ishikawa (~20 min by train from central Kanazawa)
places.notes: [GF-dedicated] A wheat-free rice-flour bakery just outside Kanazawa (about a 20-minute train ride to JR Naka-Tsubata, ~28 m from the station), founded by a mother after her child was diagnosed with egg and milk allergies. All breads are gluten-free and it is listed in Japan's national all-GF bakery directory as a no-wheat-on-premises shop. Great for stocking up on rice bread, chiffon and cookies; if you want the cooked bento, confirm the specific item is rice-based (hamburger patties in particular). Closes early / when sold out, and shut Sun–Mon.

chef_bio.background: The shop's manager, Ms. Hiramura, began self-teaching gluten-free baking after her own child was diagnosed with egg and milk allergies. Through extensive trial-and-error she developed rice-flour breads and sweets that are both allergy-safe and genuinely tasty. She opened ふぁすとふーど＆お米パン miron on 14 May 2022 right beside JR Naka-Tsubata Station, offering rice bread, rice-flour chiffon, cookies, onigiri and handmade bento. Rice is sourced from locally grown specially-cultivated Koshihikari.
chef_bio.philosophy: Provide food that children with allergies and health-conscious customers alike can eat with peace of mind, using carefully selected domestic ingredients (国産米粉, rice oil, organic soy milk, unrefined sugar).
chef_bio.specialty: Fluffy 米粉シフォン (rice-flour chiffon) and moist お米パン (rice bread), plus gluten-free bento.

anecdotes.text: 店長の平村さんは、子どもが卵や牛乳のアレルギーだと分かったことがきっかけで、グルテンフリーのお菓子やパンを独学で作り始めた。
Opened 14 May 2022, about 28 m from JR Naka-Tsubata Station.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 週末、金沢 と まんぷく金沢 によれば、店長・平村さんが子どもの卵・牛乳アレルギーをきっかけに独学で開店。パンはすべてグルテンフリーで、国産米粉・米油・有機豆乳・きび砂糖を使用。glutenfree.empacede.co.jp は小麦不使用のGFパン店として掲載。

gf_cross_contamination.text: パンはすべてグルテンフリー — no wheat flour is used on premises; listed in Japan's national all-GF bakery guide as a wheat-free bakery (★★★★).
Rice-flour and specially-cultivated Koshihikari rice are the base for bread, bento, onigiri, chiffon and cookies.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Bento items (e.g. stewed hamburger, karaage) may involve seasonings/coatings — as an allergy-focused rice-flour shop these are intended to be wheat-free, but confirm the specific bento before ordering if highly sensitive.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Most breads are egg-free and several are dairy-free vegan; a few items are free of all 28 major/minor allergens.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Shop was founded specifically to serve a child's egg/milk allergy, and its concept is food that allergy families can eat with confidence.

positives.text: Wheat-free rice-flour bakery listed in the national GF bakery directory; egg-free and partly vegan; run by an allergy-parent owner.

places.name: Oyama Saryo (を山茶寮)
places.gf_detail: General wa-cafe with two wheat-free hero items: a 米粉100% (100% rice-flour) pancake and a curry the shop bills as 「小麦粉、化学調味料、保存料は一切使用しないグルテンフリーのカレー」 (no wheat flour, no chemical seasonings, no preservatives). Important trap: that curry is built on a 「自家製のかえし醤油」 (house kaeshi soy-sauce base), and ordinary Japanese kaeshi/shōyu contains wheat — the shop only claims 小麦粉 (wheat FLOUR) is absent, not gluten, and does not state a tamari or GF soy sauce, so celiac-safety of the curry is unconfirmed. The same kitchen and copper griddle also turn out pizza, gratin, doria and other wheat dishes, so cross-contact is high. Treat as ask: confirm the kaeshi/soy sauce is tamari-based and ask about a clean cook surface.
places.vegan_detail: Not a vegan-oriented kitchen. The rice-flour pancake uses 卵 (egg), whipped butter and maple syrup (only the milk component is 豆乳/soy milk), so it is vegetarian but not vegan. The GF curry is described as spice-and-vegetable based over a kaeshi soy base with no stated animal stock, but this is unconfirmed. Vegans should ask item by item.
places.hours_raw: Mon 11:00–21:00; Tue, Thu–Sun 8:00–21:00 (morning service 8:00–10:45, curry lunch from 11:00, café from 14:00, dinner 17:00–); closed Wednesdays
places.neighborhood: 有松 (Arimatsu, Kanazawa)
places.notes: [GF] Two dedicated wheat-free dishes (100% rice-flour pancake, wheat-flour-free curry) in an otherwise wheat-heavy café that also makes pizza, gratin and doria. The curry's 「かえし醤油」 base is a soy-sauce that normally contains wheat, so confirm it is tamari/GF before relying on it; ask for a clean pan for the rice-flour pancake given the shared copper griddle.

chef_bio.background: を山茶寮 opened on 9 September 2022 in Kanazawa's Arimatsu (有松) district and later reopened under the banner 「京都珈琲 を山茶寮」, pairing Kyoto-style coffee with an all-day wa-café format spanning morning service, lunch, café and dinner. The kitchen leans on seasonal ingredients and gentle 麹・味噌 (koji/miso) seasoning while trying to minimise additives. The rice-flour pancake and the 小麦粉不使用 curry are the house's signature 'kind on the body' items.
chef_bio.philosophy: Additive-minimal cooking with seasonal, gentle seasonings; a small wheat-free line for guests avoiding wheat flour.
chef_bio.specialty: 米粉100% pancake; wheat-flour-free (grated-off) curry; Kyoto-style coffee

anecdotes.text: The rice-flour pancake is baked slowly on a copper griddle (銅板) for a fluffy texture and served with whipped butter and maple syrup.
The GF lunch curry is capped at 30 servings a day and priced from about ¥900.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Official site (oyamasaryou.owst.jp) states the curry uses no wheat flour, chemical seasonings or preservatives and a house かえし醤油 base; weekend-kanazawa.com confirms a 米粉100% pancake (plain ¥900) using contract-farm eggs, additive-free sugar and soy milk, and notes the café also serves pizza/gratin/doria.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Full-menu café that also serves pizza, gratin and doria — wheat dishes share the kitchen and copper griddle with the rice-flour pancake; no cross-contamination protocol is mentioned.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The GF curry base is a house 「かえし醤油」 (kaeshi soy sauce). Kaeshi is made from ordinary shōyu, which normally contains wheat; the shop only claims 小麦粉 (wheat flour) is absent and does not state a tamari/GF soy sauce, so celiac-safety of the curry is unconfirmed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Rice-flour pancake contains egg and whipped butter; not vegan.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No published allergen-handling or celiac protocol found; wheat-free claim is worded around 小麦粉 (wheat flour) only.

positives.text: Explicit 米粉100% pancake and a 「小麦粉・化学調味料・保存料一切不使用」 curry — a rare wheat-free lunch option in Arimatsu.

places.name: Petit pas (お米の粉の研究所 プティパ)
places.gf_detail: A fully wheat-free rice-flour bakery-cafe: the owners were diagnosed with wheat allergy and converted the entire kaffee-konditorei Y kitchen to gluten-free, reopening the 米粉 lab 'Petit pas' in April 2021 using 100% domestic 山田錦 rice flour. The shop states 「当店でお出しするパンメニューはすべて国産米粉パンになります」 (all bread served is domestic rice-flour bread) and 「当店のケーキ・デザートはすべて小麦粉不使用です」 (all cakes and desserts are wheat-flour-free), with the tagline 「本当においしいグルテンフリーをかたちに」. No wheat flour is baked anywhere on premises, so cross-contact risk is low; it is a working cafe rather than a certified celiac facility, so confirm shared-equipment handling if highly sensitive.
places.vegan_detail: Focus is gluten-free rather than vegan. Cakes and desserts (rare cheesecake, pudding) use dairy and eggs, and sandwiches include egg salad; the soy-milk chai (豆乳チャイ) and some rice-flour breads may be plant-based. Ask which breads avoid butter/egg.
places.hours_raw: 9:00–17:00; closed Tue & Wed
places.neighborhood: 大額 (Ōgaku), south Kanazawa
places.cuisine: Gluten-free rice-flour bakery & self-roast coffee cafe
places.notes: [GF-dedicated] The whole kaffee-konditorei Y / Petit pas kitchen is wheat-free - all bread is 100% 山田錦 rice flour and all cakes are wheat-flour-free, run by celiac owners. Practically celiac-safe for a cafe, though it also serves coffee and non-baked items, so mention celiac needs and confirm no shared-fryer/toaster issues. Closed Tue & Wed; ~20 shared parking spaces.

chef_bio.background: The owners of kaffee-konditorei Y (カフェコンディトライ ワイー), a Kanazawa self-roast coffee and homemade bread/cake shop, discovered a wheat allergy and shifted to a gluten-free lifestyle. They studied rice-flour baking and converted the entire cafe away from wheat, reopening the dedicated 米粉 workshop 'お米の粉の研究所 プティパ' (Rice Flour Research Lab, Petit pas) in April 2021 at 大額 3-182. All bread and confectionery are now made from 100% domestic 山田錦 rice flour.
chef_bio.philosophy: 「本当においしいグルテンフリーをかたちに」- to make genuinely delicious gluten-free food a reality, using natural ingredients such as beet sugar and maple syrup rather than positioning GF as a compromise.
chef_bio.specialty: Daily hand-made 米粉 食パン (rice-flour loaf), カンパーニュ and 丸パン, plus rice-flour cakes and desserts.

anecdotes.text: After the owners were diagnosed with wheat allergy they converted the whole cafe to gluten-free and reopened the rice-flour lab in April 2021.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Venue site (saloon.jp / kaffee-konditorei.com) states the entire shop is wheat-free with all bread as 国産米粉パン and all cakes 小麦粉不使用; local GF coverage corroborates the celiac-owner conversion story.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No wheat flour is used anywhere in the shop - all bread is 100% rice flour and all cakes are wheat-flour-free, so ambient flour cross-contact is essentially eliminated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dairy and eggs are used in cakes and some sandwiches; shared prep with vegan items not documented.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Owners are wheat-allergic themselves and built the shop around gluten-free baking; phone 076-298-1181 to confirm specifics.

positives.text: 「当店のケーキ・デザートはすべて小麦粉不使用です」- all cakes and desserts are wheat-flour-free; 「すべて国産米粉パン」- all bread is domestic rice flour.

places.name: SHIROM Creperie (シロームクレープリー)
places.gf_detail: Solo-run Brittany-style creperie near Oyama Shrine built around Ishikawa ingredients; the shop uses the hashtag #グルテンフリー and press quotes its aim as 「全てグルテンフリーに対応した商品にする」(make all products gluten-free-compatible). The dessert crepes are the best-documented GF option — 「100%石川県産米粉」/「米粉[使用]、小麦粉不使用のグルテンフリー」(100% Ishikawa rice flour, no wheat flour). The savory galette uses 白山麓鳥越そば粉 (Torigoe buckwheat) but NO source confirms it is 十割/そば粉100% — a wheat-cut (二八) is common in Japan, so ask whether the galette batter is 100% buckwheat. No cross-contact protocol is published (single vs. dedicated griddle unknown); one celiac blogger (米粉とひのことひつじ) reported eating there with essentially no reaction, which is encouraging but anecdotal.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan items or claim. Galettes contain egg, pork/sausage and cheese; the rice crepes are made with French butter (Échiré/Bordier), fresh cream, custard and cheese cream. The focus is celiac/wheat-oriented, not plant-based.
places.hours_raw: Approx. 11:00–16:00 eat-in / until sold out; closed Sundays. Hours officially irregular — solo owner with a young child. Bar service Fri–Sat. DM @shirom.kanazawa to confirm same-day opening and reserve (~7 seats).
places.neighborhood: 尾山町 (Oyama-cho, near Oyama Shrine), Kanazawa
places.cuisine: Creperie — buckwheat galettes & rice-flour dessert crepes
places.notes: [GF] The 100% Ishikawa rice-flour dessert crepes are explicitly 小麦粉不使用 and the safer documented choice for celiacs. Treat the buckwheat galette as unconfirmed — ask directly whether it is そば粉100%/十割 or a wheat-blend before eating. No cross-contamination handling is published; because it is a tiny solo operation, DM @shirom.kanazawa in advance to confirm opening, reserve a seat, and ask about dedicated griddle/prep.

chef_bio.background: Per 金沢経済新聞, the owner is a woman named Nana who previously worked as a cook at a local café and opened SHIROM Creperie on March 31, 2021, during the COVID-era state-of-emergency lull. She runs the shop solo (ワンオペ) and has young children, which is why hours are irregular. Other write-ups describe her simply as the female proprietor.
chef_bio.philosophy: 「大人の休日」— an adult's unhurried day off: a refined Brittany-style creperie where galettes and crepes are enjoyed as a leisurely course with drinks, using Ishikawa ingredients (Torigoe buckwheat, Ishikawa rice flour, Amami cane sugar).
chef_bio.specialty: Buckwheat galettes and 100% Ishikawa rice-flour dessert crepes; French butter and 素焚糖 (sudaki cane sugar).

anecdotes.text: The shop's mascot is a giraffe (キリン), inspired by the giraffe-like spotted pattern the galette batter forms as it cooks on the griddle; the grey-and-yellow interior has only ~7 seats.
Opened March 31, 2021 during the pandemic lull, aiming for a 'cidre once restrictions lift' Brittany creperie concept.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 金沢経済新聞 names the owner Nana and dates the March 31 2021 opening and gluten-free-compatible concept; food-japon and weekend-kanazawa cover the giraffe motif, seating, ingredients and the both-galette-and-crepe-gluten-free point.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No source addresses shared vs. dedicated griddle, separate prep or flour-dust handling — cross-contact status is unpublished and unknown.
A celiac/GF blogger (米粉とひのことひつじ) reported eating there with essentially no reaction — encouraging but a single anecdote, not a protocol.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Galettes are savory (Noto pork ragù, egg/cheese); no source specifies soy sauce or tamari use. Assume standard wheat-based seasonings unless confirmed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Not vegan; butter, cream, cheese and egg are used throughout, so plant-based cross-contact is unmanaged.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No published allergy-handling policy; owner directs customers to Instagram DM for reservations and questions.

positives.text: Dessert crepes are documented as 100% Ishikawa rice flour, 小麦粉不使用 (no wheat flour) gluten-free.
Press states the shop aims to make all products gluten-free-compatible: 「全てグルテンフリーに対応した商品にする」.

places.name: SOYA (宗屋)
places.gf_detail: A dedicated 100% gluten-free bakery in Nomi City that uses no wheat at all: 「小麦、乳、卵不使用のパン生地」 (bread dough with no wheat, dairy or egg) and cookies that are 「特定原材料27品目不使用」 (free of all 27 designated allergens). It mills its own organic, pesticide-free brown rice into 玄米粉/米粉 in-house, using only flour milled within three days. Because the entire facility is wheat-free and largely plant-based, cross-contact risk is minimal. It sits ~40 min south of Kanazawa (Nomi City, 5 min from JR Nomi-Neagari Sta), so plan a day-trip or use the mail-order shop (frozen shipping).
places.vegan_detail: Products are made without wheat, egg or dairy and are described as vegan-friendly, using organic brown rice flour, vegetables and 餡 (bean paste). Effectively a plant-based bakery - confirm any honey-sweetened item if strictly vegan.
places.hours_raw: 10:00–18:00; closed Sun & Mon
places.neighborhood: 大成町 (Ōnarimachi), Nomi City (能美市), ~40 min S of Kanazawa
places.notes: [GF-dedicated] Entirely gluten-free bakery - no wheat on premises, in-house-milled organic brown-rice flour, and cookies free of all 27 designated allergens; also egg- and dairy-free (vegan-friendly), making it very celiac-safe. Trade-off is distance: it is in Nomi City ~40 min south of Kanazawa (5 min from JR Nomi-Neagari Sta), so treat as a day-trip or order frozen via mail-order (yasapan.shop-pro.jp). Closed Sun & Mon.

chef_bio.background: Owner Motoda Sōshirō comes from a family that runs a farm and a rice shop; concerned by Japan's declining rice consumption, he began making rice-flour bread. When his second son developed egg and dairy allergies, he set out to create gluten-free bread and sweets that whole allergy-affected families could safely eat, founding SOYA 宗屋 in Nomi City.
chef_bio.philosophy: To make safe, reassuring rice and rice-flour foods that also work for those with allergies; grow rice and vegetables without pesticides or chemical fertilizers and mill flour fresh in-house.
chef_bio.specialty: In-house-milled organic 玄米粉 (brown-rice flour) breads: brown-rice sourdough loaf, soy-salt rolls, organic anpan, plus 27-allergen-free rice-flour cookies and sponge cake.

anecdotes.text: Brown rice is soaked overnight and de-bittered before milling to make a gentler flour than ordinary brown-rice flour; only flour milled within three days is used.
Founded after the owner's second son developed egg and dairy allergies, to give allergy families safe bread.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Venue site (soya-come.com) documents the celiac/allergy-driven founding, in-house organic brown-rice milling, and no-wheat/egg/dairy recipes; empacede's fully-GF bakery guide lists it as a dedicated gluten-free bakery.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Entirely wheat-free facility with in-house brown-rice milling; no wheat flour is handled on site, so cross-contact is minimal.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Bakery items; savory rolls use soy but products are labelled 小麦・卵・乳不使用 (no wheat). Confirm any tamari/soy component if highly sensitive.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Products are wheat-, egg- and dairy-free and vegan-friendly; whole bakery is plant-based.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Founded by an allergy parent specifically for allergy families; cookies made free of all 27 designated allergens.

positives.text: 「小麦、乳、卵不使用のパン生地」 and cookies 「特定原材料27品目不使用」 - dedicated GF, allergen-conscious bakery listed in empacede's fully-GF guide.

places.name: CAFE&BAR SQOL (スコール金沢)
places.gf_detail: A dietitian-supervised wellness cafe/bar that markets its food as fully wheat-free: 「料理は全てグルテン・カゼイン・エッグフリー」 (all dishes gluten-, casein- and egg-free) with all sweets made from 米粉 rice flour. However Japanese coverage notes a celiac trap - the kitchen uses mostly wheat-free seasonings but its soy sauce contains a minute amount of wheat (醤油に微量の小麦), and wheat-allergic diners are told to phone ahead (076-224-8787) to confirm safe dishes. For that reason a celiac should treat it as extensive-options-with-caveats rather than fully safe: rice-flour sweets and plate meals are broadly GF, but confirm any savory dish that may use standard soy sauce, and note goat cheese appears on some plates.
places.vegan_detail: Casein-free (dairy-free) across the menu, but dishes include meat, fish and goat cheese, so it is not a vegan venue. Smoothies and some plant-based plates/sweets can suit vegans - ask per dish.
places.hours_raw: Mon 7:00-17:00; Tue/Fri/Sat 7:00-21:30; Wed/Thu 10:00-21:30; Sun 7:00-10:00 (food L.O. 20:45). Morning 7-10, Lunch 11-15, Cafe from 10, Bar/Dinner 18-21:30. Closed public holidays.
places.neighborhood: 広岡 (Hirooka), ~5-6 min walk from Kanazawa Sta west exit
places.cuisine: Wellness cafe & bar - balanced plates, curry, salads, rice-flour sweets
places.notes: [GF] Nearly the whole menu is built gluten-free (all sweets are 米粉, plates are wheat-free-seasoned) and it is one of the few full-meal GF spots by Kanazawa Station - but the venue's own soy sauce carries trace wheat, so a celiac must call ahead (076-224-8787) and confirm which savory dishes avoid it. No dedicated fryer is documented; it is also a bar, so alcohol/beer is on premises. Casein-free throughout; watch for goat cheese if avoiding dairy.

chef_bio.background: SQOL is a wellness/medical fitness & spa complex in Hirooka, near Kanazawa Station's west exit; its CAFE&BAR (also branded DOCTOR'S CREATION CAFE87) is supervised by a physician and registered nutritionist from the affiliated Urata Clinic. The menu is designed as anti-inflammatory, gluten/casein/egg-free health food with carbohydrate counts shown per dish.
chef_bio.philosophy: 「美と健康を重視した管理栄養士監修メニュー」- dietitian-supervised food for beauty and health: no chemical seasonings, additives or preservatives; premium oils (perilla, avocado) instead of ordinary vegetable oil and margarine.
chef_bio.specialty: Daily balance plates and a thrice-weekly changing 腸活 (gut-health) plate; freshly baked gluten-free casein-free 米粉 bread around midday; rice-flour cakes and parfaits.

anecdotes.text: Fresh gluten-free casein-free rice-flour bread is baked daily around noon and described as mochi-mochi, moist and fluffy when warm.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: Venue site claims all dishes are gluten-, casein- and egg-free with rice-flour sweets; Japanese review (ishikawa19) confirms dietitian supervision and rice-flour bread but flags trace wheat in the soy sauce and a call-ahead recommendation for wheat allergy.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu is designed wheat-free and sweets are all 米粉, but it is a shared working kitchen/bar with beer on premises; no dedicated GF equipment documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Kitchen uses mostly wheat-free seasonings, but its soy sauce contains a minute amount of wheat; wheat-allergic diners are advised to phone ahead (076-224-8787) to confirm safe options.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Casein-free but includes goat cheese, meat and fish; not a vegan kitchen.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Dietitian-supervised, allergy-responsive menu; staff invite wheat-allergic guests to call ahead to confirm safe dishes.

positives.text: 「料理は全てグルテン・カゼイン・エッグフリー」- all dishes gluten-, casein- and egg-free; all sweets are rice flour.

places.name: Chiffon Grace (シフォン グレイス)
places.gf_detail: A rice-flour chiffon specialist that opened 27 Sept 2025 in front of the Terai Budokan in Nomi. The shop states 「シフォン生地は小麦粉や白砂糖不使用、米粉使用」(the chiffon batter uses no wheat flour or white sugar, and is made with rice flour), also using rice oil, kibi (unrefined cane) sugar, soy milk and 地養卵 eggs; the orange-sandwich flavour includes handmade rice-flour cookies. Note: the wheat-free claim is worded specifically about the シフォン生地 (batter) and there is NO explicit whole-kitchen / no-wheat-on-premises statement, so it is rated fully gluten-free but not certified dedicated — cross-contact risk is unconfirmed and a sensitive celiac should ask directly.
places.vegan_detail: The chiffon uses 地養卵 (specially-fed eggs) throughout, so the cakes are not vegan, though soy milk (not dairy) is used. No vegan items were identified.
places.hours_raw: Tue & Sat only, 11:00 until sold out (often sells out by midday)
places.neighborhood: 寺井町 (Terai), 能美市 (Nomi) — in front of Terai Budokan
places.notes: [GF] Rice-flour chiffon specialist (opened Sept 2025). The batter is stated 「小麦粉や白砂糖不使用、米粉使用」and even the orange-sandwich cookies are handmade from rice flour, but the wheat-free wording covers the batter specifically and there is no dedicated-kitchen declaration — cross-contact is unconfirmed. Uses 地養卵 eggs (not vegan). Open only Tue & Sat 11:00 until sold out, and sells out by early afternoon, so arrive early.

chef_bio.background: Owner Nagai Tomoko (52) opened Chiffon Grace on 27 September 2025 in a lodge-style shop opposite the Terai Budokan in Nomi. She previously developed sweets at welfare-facility cafés and became devoted to the depth of chiffon cake; after her own health issues she resolved to offer 「誰もが安心して食べられるケーキ」(cake everyone can eat safely), leading to the gluten-free, rice-flour approach. The shop has been so busy she reportedly did not expect the crowds, and it often sells out by midday.
chef_bio.philosophy: Make body-friendly cake that everyone, including those avoiding wheat and white sugar, can eat with peace of mind.
chef_bio.specialty: Ultra-fluffy rice-flour chiffon cakes (~10 daily-rotating flavours)

anecdotes.text: Nagai was surprised by the crowds — 'I never expected this many customers' — and the shop frequently sells out by early afternoon.
She turned to safe-to-eat cake after her own health issues, wanting cake everyone could enjoy.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 週末、金沢。, 北陸中日新聞 and local blogs describe chiffon grace as a 米粉シフォン専門店 opened 2025/9/27 in 能美市寺井町, owner 永井智子 (52), 「シフォン生地は小麦粉や白砂糖不使用、米粉使用」, eggs are 地養卵, open Tue & Sat until sold out.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat-free claim is worded for the シフォン生地 (batter) specifically; no dedicated/certified whole-kitchen statement was found, so cross-contact is unconfirmed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Not applicable — dessert/bakery shop.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Uses 地養卵 eggs throughout; not vegan.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Owner explicitly aims for cake 'everyone can eat safely,' driven by her own health experience.

positives.text: Rice flour throughout, including handmade rice-flour cookies in the orange sandwich; no wheat flour or white sugar in the batter.

places.name: Comecobito (こめこびと／椿原製菓)
places.gf_detail: A brown-rice-flour (玄米粉) chiffon and white-rice-flour (白米粉) muffin maker run by Tsubakihara Confectionery in Tsubata. The shop describes its sweets as 「小麦粉や乳製品不使用で、安心の無添加」(no wheat flour or dairy, reassuringly additive-free) using prefecture-grown rice flour and mostly organic ingredients. FLAG RESOLVED: the tea-flavoured chiffon/muffin is 加賀棒茶 (Kaga bōcha) — a roasted green stem tea (a hōjicha-type roasted green tea), NOT 麦茶/barley tea — so no barley is involved. No dedicated/certified wheat-free-kitchen wording was found, so it is rated fully gluten-free rather than certified dedicated; confirm cross-contact if highly sensitive.
places.vegan_detail: The white-rice-flour muffins are described as 「小麦粉も乳製品も卵も不使用のヴィーガンおやつ」(no wheat, dairy or egg — vegan treats). The玄米粉 chiffon cakes are not confirmed egg-free, so vegans should stick to the muffins or ask.
places.hours_raw: Wed, Fri, Sun 11:00–17:00 (until sold out)
places.neighborhood: 南中条 (Minami-Chūjō), 津幡町 (Tsubata), Kahoku District
places.notes: [GF] Brown-rice-flour chiffon + white-rice-flour muffins, 「小麦粉や乳製品不使用、無添加」, prefecture-grown rice flour, mostly organic. The tea flavour is 加賀棒茶 (Kaga bōcha), a roasted green stem tea — NOT barley tea — so it is celiac-safe. Muffins are fully vegan; chiffon may contain egg. Open only Wed/Fri/Sun 11:00–17:00, and sells out; no certified dedicated-kitchen claim, so confirm cross-contact if very sensitive.

chef_bio.chef_name: Tsubakihara (椿原) — given name not disclosed
chef_bio.background: Comecobito is the sweets brand of 椿原製菓 (Tsubakihara Confectionery) in 津幡町南中条, run by an owner who is a former Chinese-cuisine chef. The shop focuses on brown-rice-flour chiffon and white-rice-flour muffins made with Ishikawa-grown rice flour and mostly organic ingredients, additive-free. It opens only Wednesday, Friday and Sunday and often sells out.
chef_bio.philosophy: Additive-free, wheat- and dairy-free rice-flour sweets that are gentle on the body, using local and organic ingredients.
chef_bio.specialty: 玄米粉 (brown-rice-flour) chiffon cakes and vegan white-rice-flour muffins

anecdotes.text: The owner is a former Chinese-cuisine chef who moved into gluten-free rice-flour confectionery.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: 週末、金沢。 describes こめこびと（椿原製菓）as a 玄米粉/白米粉 sweets shop, 「小麦粉や乳製品不使用、無添加」, muffins 「ヴィーガンおやつ」, tea flavour 加賀棒茶; owner is a former 中華料理 chef.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All sweets are brown-/white-rice-flour based and stated 「小麦粉不使用」, but no certified dedicated-kitchen claim was found.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Not applicable — dessert/bakery shop.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Muffins are vegan; chiffon may contain egg — made in the same kitchen.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Positions products as additive-free and wheat/dairy-free; check Instagram for allergen details per batch.

positives.text: Tea flavour confirmed as 加賀棒茶 roasted green stem tea, not barley tea (麦茶) — no barley gluten risk.

places.name: Komeko Chiffon Kokone (米粉シフォン 心寧)
places.gf_detail: A rice-flour chiffon-cake specialty shop (米粉シフォンケーキ専門店) that uses 100% domestic rice flour and no wheat. The owners' own page states 「調理場含め、作業場等に小麦粉は一切置いていないので小麦アレルギーの方も安心してお召し上がり頂けます。」 (no wheat flour is kept anywhere on the premises, kitchen included, so those with a wheat allergy can eat with confidence), and 「米粉なのでグルテンフリー」. It is a sweets-and-coffee-only shop, so no wheat is baked on-site and there is no soy-sauce/miso wheat risk. The claim is self-declared with no third-party certification or ppm testing, so a strict celiac should confirm cross-contact practices via Instagram/phone before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: Every item is egg-based chiffon (eggs from a Kaga-city farm, ひらおかの卵) and the shop leans on a baking-powder-free, egg-driven technique. Base cakes use rice oil and cane sugar rather than butter, but the cheese flavor and the カップシフォン (topped with fresh whipped cream) contain dairy. No egg-free or vegan option is offered.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Fri 10:00–16:00, Sat 10:00–15:00; closed Sun, national holidays, plus irregular closures
places.neighborhood: 小菅波町 クロスガーデン加賀2F (Kaga, Ishikawa — approx. 5 min from Kaga-Onsen Station)
places.cuisine: Rice-flour chiffon cakes, baked sweets & coffee
places.notes: [GF-dedicated] A rice-flour-only chiffon specialty shop where the owners state no wheat flour is kept anywhere on the premises, kitchen included — effectively a dedicated gluten-free operation, and being sweets-only there is no soy-sauce/miso wheat trap. Note it is self-declared with no certification, so a highly sensitive celiac should confirm cross-contact directly. Roughly 4–5 flavors are made daily and popular ones sell out before closing (some gone shortly after 13:00); check @kokone_chiffon for the day's open calendar as 不定休 closures apply.

chef_bio.chef_name: Yoshie Yamaguchi (山口芳恵) & Naoko Takahashi (高橋直子)
chef_bio.background: Kokone is run by two sisters born and raised in Kaga City — Yoshie Yamaguchi, a former nurse (看護師), and Naoko Takahashi, a former nursery-school teacher (保育士). After experiencing childbirth they reconsidered what 'truly safe and secure food' means, drawing on their mother's teachings, and decided to open a shop together. They opened the rice-flour chiffon specialty shop on 20 January 2024 on the 2nd floor of Cross Garden Kaga.
chef_bio.philosophy: 「本当に安心安全なものを届けたい。身体と心に優しいシフォンケーキを食べてたくさんの人に笑顔になってほしい」 — to deliver genuinely safe food that is gentle on body and mind, using 100% domestic rice flour, no additives/colorings/preservatives, and local Ishikawa ingredients (地産地消). The shop name 心寧 (kokone) combines 心 (heart) and 寧 (calm), expressing 「真心込めて丁寧に」 (made with sincerity and care).
chef_bio.specialty: Rice-flour (米粉) chiffon cake, baking-powder-free for an ultra-fluffy egg-driven texture

anecdotes.text: The two sisters — a former nurse and a former childcare worker — turned to allergen-conscious baking after childbirth made them rethink food safety.
「口から入った物が血となり骨となり…食べることは生きること」 — the sisters' stated belief that what you eat becomes your body, from their own 私たちの思い page.

chef_bio.japanese_sources_summary: The shop's own BASE こだわり/思い pages establish the 100% rice-flour, no-wheat-on-premises, additive-free claims first-person; weekend-kanazawa and ishikawa-style corroborate the sisters' backstory (ex-nurse + ex-nursery teacher), the post-childbirth food-safety motivation, and the name meaning.

gf_cross_contamination.text: 「調理場含め、作業場等に小麦粉は一切置いていないので小麦アレルギーの方も安心してお召し上がり頂けます。」 — no wheat flour is kept anywhere on the premises, kitchen included, so wheat-allergic guests can eat safely.
「小麦粉を使わず国産米粉100％で丁寧に作る、米粉シフォンケーキ専門店」 — a rice-flour-only chiffon specialty shop that uses no wheat flour; corroborates a wheat-free operation. Self-declared, no certification/ppm testing published.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Not applicable — sweets-and-coffee-only shop with no savory dishes, so no soy sauce or miso is used in any product.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All items contain egg; cheese and cup-chiffon items contain dairy. No vegan/egg-free preparation exists, so vegan cross-contact is moot.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Run by the two sister-owners themselves (a former nurse and a former childcare worker); the whole business is built around allergen-conscious food, but no formal documented allergen-handling protocol was found beyond the no-wheat-on-premises statement.

positives.text: Dedicated rice-flour-only shop: 100% domestic 米粉, no wheat kept on premises, additive/coloring/preservative-free, and no wheat baked on-site.
Sweets-only menu removes the soy-sauce/miso wheat trap entirely.

places.name: My Chiffon (マイシフォン)
places.gf_detail: A rice-flour (米粉) chiffon specialist that opened Oct 2024 beside Johoku Hospital; the owner developed the recipes for a child with allergies, and everything is built on gluten-free ingredients (rice flour, oatmeal). The shop states its焼き菓子 are made 「小麦粉や乳製品、卵不使用」(no wheat flour, dairy or eggs). No explicit 'no wheat anywhere in the kitchen' declaration was found, so it is treated as fully gluten-free rather than a certified dedicated facility. Caveat: oats/oatmeal are naturally GF but frequently cross-contaminated with wheat unless certified, and the chiffon cakes contain eggs — celiacs should ask about the oat sourcing.
places.vegan_detail: The chiffon cakes use fresh Noto eggs so are not vegan, but several baked goods (rice-flour salt snowballs, rice-flour maple cookies) are made 「小麦粉や乳製品、卵不使用」— no wheat, dairy or eggs — making them vegan-friendly.
places.hours_raw: Wed & Fri 10:00–16:00; Sat irregular hours; closed Mon, Tue, Thu, Sun
places.neighborhood: 京町 (Kyōmachi), Kanazawa
places.notes: [GF] Rice-flour chiffon specialist — the whole shop is built on gluten-free ingredients (米粉 rice flour, oatmeal) and the owner created the recipes for an allergic child.
